text:
Thousands of people protested on AustraliaÕs beaches against a shark cull in Western Australia. They asked the stateÕs prime minister to stop the cull, and RSPCA Australia and Virgin Atlantic owner Richard Branson criticized it. Catching and killing sharks longer than three metres began after a number of shark attacks on Western AustraliaÕs coast. A 35-year-old surfer, who was killed in November 2013, was the sixth person to die from a shark attack intwo years.

But the whole of Australia has had, on average, just one shark-related death a year for the last 50 years. Kate Faehrmann, of the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society, said, from a protest in Perth, the state capital of Western Australia: ÒWeÕve always said that this idea wonÕt work. Drumlines, which they use to catch the sharks, kill sharks if theyÕre one, two, three metres or more, and also dolphins, turtles and other things. ThatÕs why we donÕt want the cull.Ó

Thousands of people protested on beaches in the cities of Perth, Sydney and Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests showed that Australians wanted sharks to be protected: ÒWhatÕs amazing is that so many people in Australia love sharks. This has shown that people are scared but thousands of people are coming out across the country to say, ÔThatÕs the sharksÕ ocean. We respect them, we love them and we donÕt want you to kill them.Õ Anthony Joyce, a surfer who once had his foot caught in a sharkÕs mouth, said: ÒThe number of sharks they are going to kill is going to make no difference.Ó

The state government will not say how many sharks they have killed, but some people say that sharks smaller than three metres were released after they were caught on drumlines,  oating drums  xed to the sea bed with bait on hooks underneath them. Conservationists say there is no evidence the cull will reduce the number of shark attacks on humans, because no other cull has only used drumlines. Researchers at the University of Western Australia say the increased number of shark attacks in the state may be because Western Australia has the fastest-growing population in Australia, not because of a rising number of sharks.

Richard Peirce, of the UK-based conservation charity, the Shark Trust, said that the cull would not work and could bring more predators towards the coast. ÒThe activity in Western Australia is adding tothe human tragedy of shark attacks. It is very sad that a government has ignored the best advice and chosen a method that is ineffective and counterproductive,Ó he said, Òand, even if they monitor drumlines through the day, leaving the lines in at night could attract other predators to the area, attracted by those sharks and other species hooked and injured.Ó

Worldwide, in 2012, there were 80 attacks by sharks. Seven of the attacks were fatal. This is compared to nearly 100 million sharks killed by humans each year. RSPCA Australia says it believes the cull is wrong. ÒThere is no evidence that the increasein attacks is a result of increasing shark numbers. We think it is the result of a changing population and changing human behaviour; that is, there are more people in the water,Ó it said.

Richard Branson said the idea was not working. ÒIÕm sure one of the reasons Western Australia Premier, Colin Barnett,did it was because he was thinking itwould encourage tourism. ItÕs going to do the opposite, I think. YouÕre advertising a problem that doesnÕt exist in a big way and youÕre going to stop people from wanting to come to Perth and your beautiful countryside around it. All youÕre going to achieve, I think, is to worry people.Ó

However, according to the Shark Attack File, Australia as a whole has averaged one shark- related fatality a year for the last 50 years.Kate Faehrmann, a board member at the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society, said from a protest in Perth: ÒWeÕve been saying all along that this policy wonÕt work. Drumlines, used to catch the sharks, are indiscriminate killers. TheyÕll kill sharks whether theyÕre one, two, three metres or more, as well as dolphins, turtles and other things. ThatÕs why the community doesnÕt want it.Ó

Thousands of people protested on PerthÕs Cottesloe Beach and SydneyÕs Manly Beach,as well as hundreds at Glenelg, in south-west Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests had shown Australians wanted sharks protected: ÒWhatÕs amazing is so many people in Australia love sharks. This has demonstrated something about the national psyche, that, despite Jaws, despite all the fear, people are coming out in their thousands across the country to say, ÔThatÕs their ocean. We respect them, we love them and we donÕt want them killed.ÕÓ Anthony Joyce, a surfer who once had his foot caught in a sharkÕs mouth, said: ÒThe number of sharks they are going to kill is going to make no difference in the schemeof things.Ó

The state government has refused to provide a running tally of sharks killed, though there have been reports of sharks smaller than three metres being released after getting caught on drumlines,  oating drums anchored to the sea bed with bait hanging on hooks beneath them. Richard Peirce, chairman of the UK-based conservation charity, the Shark Trust, said that the cull would be ineffective and potentiallylure more predators towards the coast. ÒThe activity in Western Australia is compoundingthe human tragedy of shark attacks. It is very sad that a government that could be seen totake positive initiatives with regards to sharkÐ human interactions by trialling alternatives to indiscriminate killing has ignored the best advice and opted for an approach that is ineffective and counterproductive,Ó he said. ÒThe indiscriminate nature of drumlines is often overlooked Ð evenif monitored through the day, leaving the linesin overnight has the potential to attract other predators into the area, attracted by those sharks and other species hooked and injured.Ó

RSPCA Australia released a statement saying it believes the cull is unjusti ed. ÒThere is no evidence that the increase in attacks is a result of increasing shark numbers. Rather,it is consistent with a changing population and human behaviour; that is, there are greater numbers of people in the water,Ó it said.

Richard Branson told Fairfax Radio the policy was back ring. ÒIÕm sure one of the reasons Western Australia Premier, Colin Barnett, did it was because he was thinking it would encourage tourism. ItÕs going to do quite the reverse, I think. YouÕre advertising a problem that doesnÕt existin a major way and youÕre deterring people from wanting to come to Perth and your beautiful countryside around it. text:
Race engineerA race engineer takes information from the driver and gives it to the mechanics.

Typical salary: You start at £25,000 and very soon earn more than £40,000 with just a few yearsÕ experience. Senior race engineers earn £50,000 to £90,000.

What the job involves: ÒA race engineer is the interpreter between the race-car mechanics and the driver,Ó says race engineer Jamie Muir. ÒThe engineer takes feedback from the driver, analyses the data and gives this to the mechanics.Ó

Quali cations: A university degree, usually in automotive/mechanical engineering or motorsport technology. Hands-on experience is essential.

To succeed as a race engineer, you need ... to be able to work under pressure.

Worst thing about the job: The long hours. ÒRace engineers work 24/7,Ó says Chris Aylett, CEO of the Motorsport Industry Association.

Ethical hackerTypical salary: A newly quali ed hacker will usually have a minimum salary of £35,000 to £50,000. This rises to £60,000 to £90,000 when they become team leader.

What the job involves: A company pays an ethical hacker to hack into its computer system to see how well it might  ght a real attack.

Quali cations: You donÕt need a degree in computer science. The industry accepts people with a very wide range of quali cations and skills.

To succeed as an ethical hacker, you need ...a passion for technology and detail. You should also enjoy solving dif cult problems.

Worst thing about the job: When you are testing the security of a new customerÕs network and you  nd that they have already been hacked.

Bomb-disposal diverTypical salary: In the private sector, you can earn up to £100,000 working just two months out of every three.

What the job involves: You descend to the sea bed and look for unexploded bombs and mines. Then, you safely collect the weapons or safely dispose of them.

Quali cations: To dive offshore, you must have diving quali cations. To be able to dispose of the bombs safely, youÕll also need a special quali cation and years of experience.

To succeed as a bomb-disposal diver, you need ... to be calm in stressful situations. You work alone under water, with zero visibility and, if you donÕt like living in small spaces with lots of other people, this job is not for you.

Worst thing about the job: You will be away from home for at least six months of the year.

Social engineerTypical salary: Graduates start on £25,000. Yoursalary will rise to between £50,000 and £80,000.

The job: Companies pay a social engineer to try to trick their employees and make them give the engineer secret information.

Quali cations: Usually, social engineers have a degree in IT but an understanding of psychology is also useful.

To succeed as a social engineer, you need ...to be a good liar. You also need to have strong personal ethics and to understand the law.

Worst thing about the job: Other people may misunderstand your job: social engineers are not spies but most people think they are.

Power-line helicopter pilot Typical salary: £65,000

The job: To  y close to power lines in a helicopter so that someone can check the lines with a camera.

Quali cations: A private-helicopter-pilot licence, a commercial pilotÕs licence and around 2,000 hours of experience  ying at low levels.

To succeed as a power-line helicopter pilot, you need ... a steady hand and to stay calm in dif cult situations. Pilots often have to  y next to the power line, sometimes as little as 20 feet away and just 30 feet off the ground.

Worst thing about the job: ÒThere are no negatives,Ó says helicopter pilot Robin Tutcher.

Private butlerTypical salary: £60,000 to £90,000

The job: An employer can ask a private butler to do anything from managing other staff, serving at every meal, running errands and looking after guests to booking restaurants, house security, housekeeping and cooking.

Quali cations: You donÕt need any quali cations but you can do a special course.

To succeed as a butler, you need ... to enjoy looking after other people.

Worst thing about the job: Long hours and an unpredictable work schedule mean itÕs dif cult to have a family life.

text:
More than one million British workers might be employed on zero-hours contracts. This number comes from a poll of more than 1,000 employers by the Chartered Institute of Personnel and Development (CIPD).

Recently, some UK organizations Ð from shops to Buckingham Palace Ð have been criticized for employing staff without a guarantee of work and pay each week. Employees on zero-hours contracts often get no holiday or sick pay and have to ask permission before looking for extra work with another company.

The CIPD found that 38% of zero-hours contract workers describe themselves as employed full-time. They say they typically work 30 hours or more a week. One-third of voluntary sector employers use the contracts and one in four public sector organizations.

The retail company Sports Direct employs around 20,000 of its 23,000 staff on zero-hours contracts. Other companies using the contracts include cinema chain Cineworld and Buckingham Palace, which uses the contracts for its 350 summer workers. Pub group J D Wetherspoon has 24,000 of its staff Ð 80% of its workforce Ð on zero-hours contracts.

Vidhya Alakeson, from the Resolution Foundation, said: ÒIf itÕs true that there are around one million people on zero-hours contracts, then that would be a big part of the workforce.Ó

Unions say that employers put pressure on staff to sign the contracts. In this way, the employers can avoid their responsibilities to employees and reduce staff bene ts.

Dave Prentis, of the trade union Unison, said: ÒThe majority of workers are only on these contracts because they haveno choice.Ó

Workers on zero-hours contracts are often only told how many hours they will work when weekly or monthly rotas are created. But they have to be available for extra work at short notice. They may get holiday pay, but they do not get sick pay.

The charity National Trust, which employs many of its seasonal workers on zero-hours contracts, said it gives the same pay and bene ts to workers on zero-hours contracts as to full-time staff.

ÒWe believe zero-hours contracts are essential in our organization, because we are very weather-dependent,Ó the National Trust said. ÒItÕs important to be able to reorganize staff rotas quickly to respond to the weather and zero-hours contracts allow us to do this.Ó

Politician Chuka Umunna said, ÒWhilesome employees welcome the  exibility of zero-hours contracts, for many, zero-hours contracts leave them insecure and unsure of when work will come,Ó he said.

The poll shows that 17% of employers in the private sector use zero-hours contracts, lower than the 34% of organizations in the voluntary sector and 24% in thepublic sector.

Industries where employers were most likely to have at least one person on a zero-hours contract were hotels, catering andleisure (48%), education (35%) and healthcare (27%).

text:
More than one million British workers might be employed on zero-hours contracts, a new poll shows. This suggests that British businesses are using the controversial employment contracts far more than previously thought.

After the results Ð which come from a poll of more than 1,000 employers by the Chartered Institute of Personnel and Development (CIPD) Ð people began asking the government to launch a full inquiry into the use of the contracts. Recently, many organizations Ð from retail chains to Buckingham Palace Ð have been criticized for hiring staff but offering no guarantee of workand pay each week. Employees on zero-hours contracts often get no holiday or sick pay and have to ask permission before looking for additional work elsewhere.

The CIPD found that 38% of zero-hours contract workers describe themselves as employed full-time, typically working 30 hours or more a week. One-third of voluntary sector employers use the contracts and one in four publicsector organizations.

Peter Cheese, from the CIPD, said: ÒThere does need to be a closer look at what is meant by a zero-hours contract. And this needs to consider both the advantages and disadvantages for businesses and employees.Ó

Retailer Sports Direct recently became the focus of controversy on zero-hours when it was reported that the company employs around 20,000 of its 23,000 staff on the contracts. The retailerÕs use of the contracts was followedby details of many other companies using the contracts, including cinema chain Cineworld and Buckingham Palace, which uses them for its 350 summer workers. Pub group J D Wetherspoon has 24,000 of its staff Ð 80% of its workforce Ð on zero-hours contracts.

Vidhya Alakeson, from the Resolution Foundation, added: ÒIf itÕs true that there are around one million people on zero-hours contracts, then that would be a substantial portion of the workforce Ð this could no longer be dismissed as an issue affecting only atiny minority.Ó

Unions and poverty campaign groups have accused employers of pressuring staff into signing the contracts so that they can avoid their responsibilities and reduce staff bene ts.

Dave Prentis, of the trade union Unison, said: ÒThe vast majority of workers are only on these contracts because they have no choice. They may give  exibility to a few, but the contracts favour the employers and make it hard for workers to complain.Ó

Workers on zero-hours contracts are often only told how many hours they will work when weekly or monthly rotas are created, but are expected to be on call for extra work at short notice. They should get holiday pay, but they do not getsick pay.

The charity National Trust, which employs many of its seasonal workers on zero-hours contracts, said it offered the same pay and bene ts to those workers, pro rata, as full-time staff, but needed some workers to be on more  exible contracts.

ÒWe believe zero-hours contracts are essential in our organization, as we are very weather-dependent,Ó a spokeswoman said. ÒItÕs important to be able to reorganize staff rotas quickly to respond to the weather and zero-hours contracts allow us to do this.Ó

Politician Chuka Umunna said the contracts should be the exception to the rule. ÒWhile some employees welcome the  exibility of such contracts, for many, zero-hours contracts leave them insecure and unsure of when work will come,Ó he said.

Some people have argued that the  exibility of zero-hours contracts may have allowed the UK to avoid higher levels of unemployment during the economic crisis.

Figures from the poll suggest that 17% of employers in the private sector use zero-hours contracts, considerably lower than the 34% of organizations in the voluntary sector and 24% in the public sector.

Industries where employers were most likelyto have at least one person on a zero-hours contract were hotels, catering and leisure (48%), education (35%) and healthcare (27%).

text:
Poorer countries will be most affected
by climate change in the next century.
Sea levels will rise, there will be stronger
cyclones, warmer days and nights, more
rainfall, and larger and longer heatwaves,
says a new report.

The last big United Nations (UN) report, in
2007, said there would be temperature rises
of 6 °C or more by the end of the century.
Scientists now think this will not happen,
but average land and sea temperatures will
probably continue rising during this century,
possibly becoming 4 °C hotter than now.
That rise would ruin crops and make life in
many cities too hot.

As temperatures rise and oceans become
warmer, there will be big changes in annual
rainfall in tropical and subtropical regions,
says the Intergovernmental Panel on
Climate Change (IPCC) report, released in
Stockholm and published online
in September 2013.

East Africa can expect more short rainfalls
and west Africa should expect heavier
monsoons. Burma, Bangladesh and India
can expect stronger cyclones; elsewhere in
southern Asia, there will probably be heavier
summer rainfalls. Indonesia may receive
less rainfall between July and October, but
the coastal regions around the south China
Sea and Gulf of Thailand can expect more
rainfall when cyclones hit the land.

‘Rainfall patterns will change. Northern
countries, for example in Europe or North
America, will probably receive more rainfall,
but many subtropical, dry regions will likely
get less rain,’ said the report.

The report also said that the monsoon
season will probably start earlier and
last longer.

Scientists in developing countries are happy
with the report. “The IPCC says that climate
change is real and happening much more
strongly than before. We are already seeing
the effects of climate change in Bangladesh
and across south Asia. It’s not news to us.
Most developing countries are experiencing
climate change now. They do not need
the IPCC to tell them that the weather is
changing,” said Saleemul Huq, director of
the International Centre for Climate Change
and Development.

Scientists have also lowered their
predictions for sea-level rises. Sea levels will
probably rise an average of 40–62 cm by
2100. But many millions of people living in
the developing world’s great cities, including
Lagos and Calcutta, are in danger.

Weather disasters are also more likely in a
warmer world, the report says. The number
of tropical cyclones will probably not change,
but they may become more intense, with
stronger winds and heavier rainfall.

Life in many developing country cities could
become very diffi cult, especially because
city temperatures are already higher than
those in the countryside. Much higher
temperatures could reduce the length of the
growing period in some parts of Africa by up
to 20%, the report said.

The charity Oxfam said that world hunger
would get worse because climate changes
hurt crop production. They said the number
of people at risk of hunger might increase by
10% to 20% by 2050.

“The changing climate is already hurting the
fi ght against hunger, and it looks like it will
get worse,” said Oxfam. “A hot world is a
hungry world”.

text:
Low-income countries will continue to be
the most affected by human-induced climate
change over the next century. They will
experience gradual sea-level rises, stronger
cyclones, warmer days and nights, more
unpredictable rainfall, and larger and longer
heatwaves, according to a recent report.

The last major United Nations (UN)
assessment, in 2007, predicted temperature
rises of 6 °C or more by the end of the
century. That is now thought unlikely
by scientists, but average land and sea
temperatures are expected to continue rising
throughout this century, possibly reaching
4 °C above present levels – enough to
devastate crops and make life in many cities
unbearably hot.

As temperatures rise and oceans warm,
tropical and subtropical regions will see
sharp changes in annual rainfall, says the
Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change
(IPCC) report, released in Stockholm and
published online in September.

East Africa can expect increased short
rainfalls and west Africa should expect
heavier monsoons. Burma, Bangladesh
and India can expect stronger cyclones;
elsewhere in southern Asia, heavier summer
rains are anticipated. Indonesia may receive
less rainfall between July and October,
but the coastal regions around the south
China Sea and Gulf of Thailand can expect
increased rainfall extremes when cyclones
hit the land.

“In the long term, rainfall patterns will
change. Northern countries, such as those
in Europe or North America, are expected to
receive more rainfall, but many subtropical
arid and semi-arid regions will likely
experience less rain,” said the
report’s authors.

They added that the monsoon season is
likely to start earlier and last longer.

Scientists in developing countries and
commentators have welcomed the report,
which they said supported their
own observations.

“The IPCC says that climate change is
real and happening much more strongly
than before. We are already seeing the
effects of climate change in Bangladesh
and across south Asia. It’s not news to
us. Most developing countries are facing
climate change now. They do not need
the IPCC to tell them that the weather is
changing,” said Saleemul Huq, director of
the International Centre for Climate Change
and Development.

Scientists have also lowered their
projections of sea-level rises. Depending
on future greenhouse gas emissions, sea
levels will rise an average of 40–62 cm by
2100. Nevertheless, there will be signifi cant
geographical variations; many millions of
people living in the developing world’s
great cities, including Lagos and Calcutta,
are threatened.

Weather disasters are also more likely in a
warmer world, the report suggests. Although
the global frequency of tropical cyclones is
expected to decrease or remain unchanged,
they may become more intense, with
stronger winds and heavier rainfall.

Life in many developing-country cities could
become unbearable, especially as urban
temperatures are already far above those
in surrounding countryside. Much higher
temperatures could reduce the length of the
growing period in some parts of Africa by up
to 20%, the report said.

The charity Oxfam predicted that world
hunger would worsen because climate
changes inevitably hurt crop production and
reduce incomes. They said the number of
people at risk of hunger might rise by 10% to
20% by 2050.

“The changing climate is already
jeopardizing gains in the fi ght against
hunger, and it looks like it will get worse,”
said Oxfam. “A hot world is a hungry world”.

text:
Low-income countries will remain on the front
line of human-induced climate change over the
next century, experiencing gradual sea-level
rises, stronger cyclones, warmer days and
nights, more unpredictable rainfall, and larger
and longer heatwaves, according to the most
thorough assessment of the issue yet.

The last major United Nations (UN)
assessment, in 2007, predicted runaway
temperature rises of 6 °C or more by the end
of the century. That is now thought unlikely
by scientists, but average land and sea
temperatures are expected to continue rising
throughout this century, possibly reaching 4 °C
above present levels – enough to devastate
crops and make life in many cities unbearably
hot.

As temperatures climb and oceans warm,
tropical and subtropical regions will face
sharp changes in annual rainfall, says the
Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change
(IPCC) report, released in Stockholm and
published online in September.

East Africa can expect to experience increased
short rains, while west Africa should expect
heavier monsoons. Burma, Bangladesh and
India can expect stronger cyclones; elsewhere
in southern Asia, heavier summer rains are
anticipated. Indonesia may receive less rainfall
between July and October, but the coastal
regions around the South China Sea and
Gulf of Thailand can expect increased rainfall
extremes when cyclones hit land.

“It is virtually certain that, in the long term,
global precipitation will change. High latitude
countries, such as in Europe or North America,
are expected to receive more rainfall, but many
subtropical arid and semi-arid regions will likely
experience less precipitation. Over wet tropical
regions, extreme precipitation events will very
likely be more intense and more frequent in a
warmer world,” said the report’s authors.

They added: “Monsoon onset dates are likely
to become earlier or not to change much while
monsoon withdrawal rates are very likely to
delay, resulting in a lengthening of the season.”

Scientists in developing countries and
commentators have welcomed the report,
which they said backed their own observations.

“The IPCC makes the case that climate
change is real and happening much more
strongly than before. We are already seeing
the effects of climate change in Bangladesh
and across south Asia. It’s not news to us.
Most developing countries are facing climate
change now. They do not need the IPCC to
tell them that the weather is changing,” said
Saleemul Huq, director of the International
Centre for Climate Change and Development,
based in Dhaka.

Scientists have also lowered projections
of sea-level rises. Depending on future
greenhouse gas emissions, sea levels
will rise an average of 40–62 cm by 2100.
Nevertheless, there will be signifi cant
geographical variations; many millions of people
living in the developing world’s great cities,
including Lagos and Calcutta, are threatened.

Weather disasters are also more likely in a
warmer world, the report suggests. Although
the global frequency of tropical cyclones is
expected to decrease or remain essentially
unchanged, they may become more intense,
with stronger winds and heavier rainfall.

Life in many developing-country cities could
become practically unbearable, given that
urban temperatures are already well above
those in surrounding countryside. Much higher
temperatures could reduce the length of the
growing period in some parts of Africa by up to
20%, the report said.

Dr Camilla Toulmin, director of the International
Institute for Environment and Development,
says: “Climate models are not yet robust
enough to predict impacts at local and
regional scales, but it is clear that everybody is
vulnerable in some way.”

The charity Oxfam predicted that world hunger
would worsen as climate changes inevitably
hurt crop production and disrupt incomes.
They suggested the number of people at risk
of hunger might climb by 10% to 20% by 2050,
with daily per-capita calorie availability falling across the world.

“The changing climate is already jeopardizing
gains in the fi ght against hunger, and it looks
set to worsen,” said Oxfam. “A hot world is
a hungry world. If the remainder of the 21st
century unfolds like its fi rst decade, we will
soon experience climate extremes well outside
the boundaries of human experience.”

text:
When you hear the word Amazon, what do you think of first: the huge rainforest, the long river, or the big online store? Which one do you think is most important?

This question is part of an important argument about internet addresses. Brazil and Peru do not want the US company Amazon to get the internet ending “.amazon”.

The company in Seattle wants to use “.amazon” as a top-level domain name, like “.com”. But Brazil and Peru say this could stop people from using “.amazon” for things like protecting the environment, supporting indigenous rights, and other public uses.

This is one of many fights about new internet endings, including “.patagonia” and “.shangrila”. It is about who controls the internet and what it is for.

Before, internet endings were easy to understand. For example, “.com” was for businesses and “.gov” was for governments. There were about 20 other types too.

Now these endings, called generic top-level domains (gTLDs), will grow a lot more than ever before.

A non-profit group in the US called the Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers (ICANN) helps manage these internet names. ICANN has received requests for hundreds of new gTLDs, adding to the 22 that already exist. Each request is reported to cost almost $200,000.

Amazon has asked for many new endings, such as “.shop”, “.song”, “.book”, and “.kindle”. But the most argued-about request is “.amazon”.

Brazil and Peru want the “.amazon” request to be taken back. They say a private company should not get a name that is also a very important place name. The Amazon area crosses their countries, and the name is used for some regions and groups that work across borders.

Brazil’s Ministry of Science and Technology said it does not help the public when private companies use place names as gTLDs to support their brand or make money.

Brazil said other members of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty agree with them: Bolivia, Colombia, Ecuador, Guyana, Suriname, and Venezuela.

There have also been many other protests about new internet endings that use place names, cultural names, or names that people argue about.

Argentina is unhappy that a US outdoor clothing company, Patagonia, wants “.patagonia”. Argentina says Patagonia is a well-known region with its own parliament. Argentina says the region is important to its economy because it has oil, fishing, mining, and agriculture. It also has an active local community and many tourists.

China has also argued about “.shangrila”, which a hotel group called Shangri-La wants. Officials in Beijing say “shangri-la” is the name of a region in Yunnan province. But the region was only renamed in 2001, long after the hotel group started. The name was used so the local community could make money from the famous made-up paradise in the novel Lost Horizon by British writer James Hilton.

At a conference in Beijing in April, ICANN’s Governmental Advisory Committee, which represents national governments, suggested stopping disputed requests for now. They are expected to talk about them again at a meeting in Durban in July. The first approved new domain names should be used before the end of 2013.

text:
To tourists, Amsterdam still seems like a liberal city. The Labour Mayor recently said the city’s marijuana-selling coffee shops would stay open, even though there is a new national law to fight drug tourism. But Amsterdam may lose its name for tolerance because of plans to send very troublesome neighbours to places called “scum villages,” made from shipping containers.

The Mayor, Eberhard van der Laan, says his new policy costs £810,000. He says it is meant to stop antisocial behaviour and to protect victims of abuse and homophobia from harassment. People who behave very badly could be moved for three to six months. Some people call these places “scum villages” because the idea sounds like a plan from Geert Wilders, a far-right populist. Last year, Wilders said “repeat offenders” should be “sent to a village for scum.”

The Mayor’s spokesman, Bartho Boer, says the plan is not against liberal values. He says Amsterdam wants everyone to be who they are, including gay and lesbian people, and wants to protect people who are harassed. Boer says the plan is not for normal neighbour problems, like loud music on a Saturday night. He says it is for people who are extremely violent and intimidating, when a victim is repeatedly harassed.

People found guilty of causing “extreme havoc” will be evicted. They will be put in temporary homes that are very basic. These may be converted shipping containers in industrial areas of the city. Boer says they call it a “living container.” The units have showers and kitchens and have been used for student housing. Boer says the goal is to make sure these people are not “rewarded” by being moved to better housing.

Dutch newspaper the Parool said that in the 19th century, troublemakers were moved to villages in Drenthe and Overijssel, and these quickly became slums. But Boer says the city has learned from the past and will not put antisocial people together.

Boer says “scum houses” is a more accurate name than scum villages, because the city does not want to put more than one of these families in the same area. After up to six months in these houses, which will be spread around the city, the tenants will get permanent homes. The city expects to move about ten families a year into this programme, starting in 2013.

The temporary homes will have heavy police presence. But the tenants will also be able to get help from doctors, social workers, and parole officers. Boer says they will be supported so the same problems do not happen again in their new homes.

text:
Anitta is a famous Brazilian funk singer. She has gained millions of fans by bringing music from the favelas into the mainstream. But people are debating her skin colour.

Some anti-discrimination groups and writers say she had to give up part of her black identity to become popular with Brazil’s mostly white middle-class market.

The debate grew after “before and after” photos were shared. In the first photo, from when she was not well known, she looks darker. In the second photo, a marketing picture after she became famous, she looks lighter. People wonder if this happened because of skin-whitening products, cosmetic surgery, or more likely Photoshop. The photos started a new discussion about whether people need to be pale to succeed in Brazil.

Jarid Arraes, a psychology student and blogger, wrote that Anitta’s image change shows hidden discrimination in media and marketing. She said many people do not admit they are racist and think Brazil is a country where all races are treated equally. But she said the numbers show that is not true. She wrote that society often connects white skin with being rich and successful, and black skin with the opposite. She also said this can hurt people’s self-esteem. She gave examples like feeling forced to straighten curly hair or change a nose with surgery.

Anitta’s real name is Larissa de Macedo Machado. As a child, she sang in a church choir. As a teenager, she became known in Rio de Janeiro’s baile funk scene as a dancer and singer.

She became very famous with an album and her hit song “Show das Poderosas.” The song reached the top of the charts and got 52 million views on YouTube.

Many fans love her as a pop star with strong messages and catchy songs. The people who support her also present her as a bridge between the mostly black and mixed-race shanty towns on Rio’s hills and the richer and whiter areas below.

She has also changed her style. She toned down sexual dancing, gangsta references, and explicit lyrics from baile funk. Now some people ask if she, or her marketing team, changed her image too much to attract a richer audience.

Race and colour are sensitive topics in Brazil. Brazil was one of the last big countries to ban slavery. It has the largest population of African descent outside Africa. In Brazil, skin colour often matters more than ancestry. There is also a clear link between skin tone and inequality.

In Brazilian cities, white workers earn about twice as much as workers of African descent. Until 2011, black or mixed-race students spent about two fewer years in school on average.

The government says the gap is getting smaller because of university quotas and other programs. But big differences still remain. Most business and government leaders are white, while many lower-paid jobs are done by black and mixed-race workers. In rich areas like Ipanema and Gávea, it is common to see black nannies pushing strollers with white children.

Colour categories can be complicated. In the 2010 census, people who called themselves white were a minority for the first time. Out of 197 million people, 82 million said they were “pardu” (mixed race), 15 million said black, two million said Asian, and 0.5% said indigenous.

Sylvio Ferreira, a psychology lecturer at the Federal University of Pernambuco, said Anitta won middle-class fans by taking a rebellious sound and making it calmer and easier for everyone to accept. He said it was not racial whitening. He said it was a change in where she made her art, from the periphery to the centre.

Some others also say the colour issue is too big. Maycon de Mattos Batista, a financial analyst who knew Anitta when she was an intern, said her image changed a lot but not her colour. He said makeup, hairstyling, and clothing can explain the difference. He also said he did not think she was being pressured, because she always liked to sing, dance, and show off, and that natural style helped her succeed.

Leandro Silva de Souza, a racial equality activist in Salvador, said the problem is not the public but music producers and media leaders. He said people showed they care about music itself when they chose Ellen Oléria, a black lesbian, to win the talent show The Voice Brazil.

The Guardian could not reach Anitta for a comment. But in a recent interview, she said identity should be decided by the person. She said a powerful woman does not need to be beautiful. She said she tries to show people that we can be who we want.

text:
Google has mapped many places, like the world’s highest mountains, the ocean floor, and the Amazon rainforest. It even showed some of North Korea. But Google has mostly avoided mapping the cold Arctic.

Now Google is starting a big new mapping project in the Arctic. It wants to help millions of people understand what life is like on frozen ground called permafrost. Google flew a small team to Iqaluit, the largest town in Nunavut, Canada. They brought very warm winter clothes, laptop computers, and an 18kg backpack with a camera on it.

With help from an Inuit mapping expert, and watched by curious local people, the team walked for four days. They collected photos and information. This will help the community on Baffin Island get the same kind of map many city people already have.

Iqaluit has about 7,000 people. It will appear in Google Street View in July 2013.

Aaron Brindle, the project leader, said he lives in Toronto. He said he is used to having everything clearly shown on a map, but people in the north have not had that for a long time.

In most places, Google uses a camera on top of a car. But in Iqaluit, the team had to walk on snowy roads and on trails that are not well known. Some trails are made of ice and disappear during the short summer.

The team also went along part of a 15km dead-end road called the Road to Nowhere. They did this even though there were warnings about polar bears and other animals.

Mayor John Graham said the mappers were followed by excited and curious local people, sometimes called Google stalkers.

Graham said the excitement made sense. He said Street View continues a history of exploration in the area. In 1576, the English explorer Martin Frobisher sailed into the bay where Iqaluit is now, while looking for the Northwest Passage. In 1941, Captain Elliott Roosevelt, a reconnaissance officer and son of the US President, flew over the area. This helped the site get chosen for a military airbase. That exploration led to the founding of the modern town of Iqaluit. Today, it is the seat of government and also a transport and communications hub for Nunavut.

One early problem for Google was getting the basic information to finish its map. The satellite map was mostly correct. But because the town has been growing quickly, helped by a mining boom, Google missed one road built in the past year. This was said by Arif Sayani, the town’s Director of Planning.

Another problem was locating many businesses and homes. Many people get mail at the post office, not at their home address. If Google used only PO box locations, many places like companies, banks, and schools would all look like they were at the Canada Post building in the middle of town.

About 30 Inuit elders, business owners, and high-school students came one night to help fix these problems. They used laptop computers and learned how to make sure their homes, stores, and meeting places appeared in the correct locations.

The project is not only for fun or charity. Sayani, 32, said Iqaluit can use the maps to attract people who want to visit or move there. It may also help the town make faster planning decisions about how Iqaluit will grow.

Google tested this kind of mapping last summer in Cambridge Bay. Cambridge Bay is another Nunavut town with about 1,500 people. It is 1,700km west of Iqaluit and in a different time zone.

But the online pictures of gravel roads and muddy puddles do not show what life is usually like there, because the land is often covered in snow. That is one reason Google chose March, a harsher month, to visit Iqaluit.

Brindle said he hopes the work will continue in other northern towns. But shipping costs and airfares to move people and equipment around the huge Arctic area may be limiting Google’s plans.

Google has not chosen the next northern place yet. When it does, Brindle said the company might send one high-tech backpack and let volunteers do the work to put their town on the map.

Brindle said he hopes that in three, four, or five years, the map of Canada’s north will look very different.

text:
A controversial auction of a Banksy mural was stopped just before it was going to be sold.

The artwork is called Slave Labour. It is spray-painted and shows a child making Union Flag bunting. Many people see it as social criticism about last year’s diamond jubilee. It was expected to sell for about $700,000 at a street and contemporary art auction in Florida.

The auctioneer, Frederic Thut, owns Fine Arts Auction Miami. All week he would not say who the seller was or how the artwork was put up for sale. Then he announced that Slave Labour, and a second Banksy work, were withdrawn from the auction.

Thut did not give a reason. Community leaders in Haringey were very happy. They had campaigned to stop the sale of the artwork, which had been taken from the wall of a Poundland in Wood Green.

Alan Strickland, a Haringey councillor, said, “One of our two demands was that it doesn’t sell and the other was that we get it back again, so we’re halfway there.” He said he would write to the auction house quickly to find out what happened and what will happen next. He said the pressure and strong views from people in Wood Green helped a London campaign affect what happened in the US.

Claire Kober, Leader of Haringey Council, wrote to Arts Council England and the Mayor of Miami, Tomás Regalado, asking them to help stop the sale. But it seemed the decision to withdraw the item came from the gallery owners after talking with their lawyers. The FBI would not confirm reports that they were asked to investigate.

After the auction ended, the auction house released a short statement. It said it had convinced the owners of the two Banksys to pull them from the sale. The statement said there were no legal issues with selling the two Banksy lots.

About 30 possible buyers attended the auction. There were 106 lots listed. The three-hour auction continued, and some other items sold for more than their asking prices.

Critics accused the auction house of selling stolen property. Thut said earlier that the seller was a “well known collector,” was the rightful owner, and that the sale was legal.

Thut said his gallery received many emails and phone calls from the UK, and some were abusive or offensive. He said he supported including the pieces in the sale because it would preserve them.

The second Banksy was a 2007 artwork called Wet Dog. It was removed from a wall in Bethlehem and was estimated to be worth up to $800,000. It disappeared from the auction house’s online catalogue at lunchtime on Saturday. But Slave Labour was still listed for sale up to the 3pm start time.

Thut said the two pieces came from different owners, and neither owner was British. He said the works were important in street art and should go to buyers who care about art and preserving it.

Thut said he would keep the seller’s identity private. He said, “We respect our clients and their confidentiality. It’s not our decision to have [the Banksy] returned. We only sell it. We do not have control of it.”

A spokesperson for Poundland said it did not know who removed the 4ft x 5ft slab from the side of the shop it rents in Turnpike Lane. Lawyers for the building owner, Wood Green Investments Ltd, would not say if the company was involved.

Banksy has not commented on the Slave Labour situation. But he has criticized people who try to sell his work. Before a planned 2011 auction in New York with five of his pieces, he spoke out. None of those pieces sold.

Stephan Keszler, the dealer behind that 2011 auction, believes it is legal to sell Banksy’s works without his permission. He said Banksy makes art on other people’s property without asking, and the property owner can do what they want with it.

text:
Oxfam says the very large fortunes of the world’s richest 100 billionaires are increasing inequality and making it harder to fight poverty.

Oxfam says wealth and income are growing for a few people on a scale never seen before. This often happens while poor people lose secure jobs and fair wages. This makes it harder for people who live on aid or low pay to improve their lives and escape poverty.

Oxfam says the world’s poorest people could be lifted out of poverty several times if the richest 100 billionaires gave away the money they made in 2012. The charity says the $240bn net income these 100 billionaires made in 2012 could end extreme poverty four times over.

Charities rarely criticize rich people, because rich people often give money to charities. Bill Gates and Warren Buffett are among 40 US billionaires who promised to give much of their wealth to aid projects. But there is little information about how much they donate each year. Oxfam says Russian, Middle Eastern, and Chinese billionaires have not done the same.

In a report called The Cost of Inequality: How Wealth and Income Extremes Hurt Us All, released just before the World Economic Forum in Davos, Oxfam asks world leaders to limit extreme incomes and reduce inequality to at least the levels of 1990.

The report says the richest 1% increased their incomes by 60% in the past 20 years. It says the financial crisis made this happen faster, not slower.

Barbara Stocking, Oxfam’s Chief Executive, says extreme wealth is bad for the economy, politics, society, and the environment. She says studies show countries have lower investment and growth when workers get a smaller share of total income. She says we can no longer believe that wealth for a few will always help everyone else, because often the opposite happens.

The report says inequality is a problem all over the world. In the UK, inequality is quickly returning to levels not seen since the time of Charles Dickens. In China, the top 10% now get nearly 60% of the income. China’s inequality is now similar to South Africa’s. South Africa is the most unequal country on Earth and is much more unequal than at the end of apartheid.

In the US, the share of national income going to the top 1% has doubled since 1980, from 10% to 20%. For the top 0.01%, their share is higher than it was in the 1920s.

The World Bank and the International Monetary Fund say extreme income inequality slows growth. They have tried to connect their loans to programmes that limit the growth of inequality.

Oxfam says the richest 1% may use up to 10,000 times more carbon than the average US citizen.

Oxfam says world leaders should learn from countries like Brazil, which has grown quickly while reducing inequality.

Stocking says the world needs a “global new deal” to reverse many years of rising inequality. She says as a first step, world leaders should formally promise to reduce inequality to the levels seen in 1990.

She also says tax havens should be closed. The Tax Justice Network says tax havens hold as much as $31 trillion, which may be as much as a third of all global wealth. She says closing them could bring in $189bn more in tax money.

text:
In 2005, BlackBerry brought instant messaging to mobile phones. The company was starting to do very well. The iPhone did not exist yet, and BlackBerry phones became one of Canada’s big exports.

Six years later, in summer 2011, there were riots in London and also in Birmingham, Nottingham, Liverpool, and Manchester. BlackBerry Messenger (BBM) helped people organize very quickly. Some politicians said BBM should be shut down for a short time. But two years later, many users were leaving BBM on their own.

Fewer people were buying BlackBerry phones, so fewer people were using BBM. Many other messaging apps appeared. These include messaging from Facebook and Apple, and other apps like WhatsApp and Kik (which is also Canadian). These apps are free to download and use. They use the internet to send text messages, pictures, voice clips, stickers, and even videos (in WhatsApp).

BlackBerry tried to keep users by making BBM available on Android and Apple phones. Many people downloaded it, and BlackBerry said there were more than 20 million downloads. But some people think this came too late. James Gooderson, an 18-year-old student who writes a technology blog, says bringing BBM to the iPhone is four or five years too late. He says WhatsApp has taken away the reason young people used a BlackBerry.

BBM says it has 80 million users each month after an upgrade, but WhatsApp has 300 million. Other apps can do things BBM cannot. BBM does not yet have video or voice calls like Skype and Viber. It does not share your location like Path. It does not have video sharing like iMessage. It also does not have stickers, which many kids like. Even features BBM had on BlackBerry phones, like sharing contacts and calendars, are not on the Apple and Android versions.

Messaging is changing from mostly words to more pictures. Photos on Instagram get many comments. Snapchat lets people send pictures that delete after ten seconds. Like BBM, these services are free on any phone with internet. But in 2011, BBM was seen as very powerful. Some people said it helped start a revolution in Egypt. During the London riots, BBM could spread news faster than television.

Jean-Pierre Moore, 28, manages a youth club in Stockwell, south London. He said they could see messages about where rioters were going next, while TV news was four hours behind. He mostly uses an iPad now. He does not think stopping BBM would have stopped the looting. He says people were angry for a long time. He says that if you mention “stop and search” in his area, young men quickly get angry.

A research firm called Enders Analysis says almost 80% of young smartphone owners regularly use a social networking app. But two-thirds use more than one. Among 16- to 24-year-olds, 60% use Facebook every day, but 46% use other options too. Benedict Evans, a digital media specialist at Enders, says the phone itself is now the main platform. Apps connect to your contacts and photos. Apps can become popular and then lose popularity quickly. Some, like Instagram, stay popular, and others disappear.

A 13-year-old named Bennett has three devices, all given by family members. He keeps his BlackBerry for messaging. He uses an iPhone on wi-fi to play games. He uses an HTC Android phone to make calls. His friends still use BBM. There have been four phone thefts at his school this term, and all were BlackBerrys. With BBM, one message can be sent to everyone in your contacts, which can be several hundred people. On WhatsApp, a broadcast message can go to 50 people. But Bennett says Instagram is now very important. He says Instagram is like Facebook without parents. He says older people have taken over Facebook. He says that when he saw his mum on Facebook, he deleted his account.

For families who may struggle to pay heating bills this winter, BlackBerry can still seem good because it is cheap. With a second-hand device and a £7-a-month deal from T-Mobile, people can send unlimited BBM messages. But people trust BBM privacy less now. Many business people, revolutionaries, demonstrators, and rioters believed BBM messages were encrypted and could not be traced. After the riots, prosecutions ended that belief.

In South Kensington, outside a private school, older students all have phones with Apple logos. They all use WhatsApp. For many of them, BBM is something from the past. One teenager says he still has a BlackBerry, but he is the only one. He says that makes him feel isolated.

text:
A big international argument has started about Bolivia’s indigenous people and their right to chew coca leaves. Coca leaves are the main plant used to make cocaine. Bolivia got a special exception to the 1961 Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, an international agreement about drug rules. This exception lets indigenous people in Bolivia chew coca leaves.

Bolivia said the agreement went against its new constitution from 2009. The constitution says Bolivia must “protect native and ancestral coca as cultural patrimony” and says coca “in its natural state … is not a narcotic”.

Indigenous people in South America have chewed coca leaves for hundreds of years. People say the leaves give energy and may help as medicine. People who support Bolivia said it was protecting indigenous rights. Danny Kushlick from the Transform Drug Policy Foundation said Bolivia’s action was “inspirational and groundbreaking”. He said countries can change how they deal with UN drug agreements.

But the UN’s International Narcotics Control Board (INCB) said Bolivia is harming the world drug control system. Several countries, including the UK, the US, Italy, Sweden, the Netherlands, and Russia, opposed Bolivia’s request.

The UK told the UN that it respects the coca leaf’s cultural importance in Bolivia. But it said it is worried the exception could increase coca growing and increase the amount used for cocaine. The UK said this could weaken international law and the world effort to stop the drug trade.

The right to chew coca leaves in the Andean region was removed in 1964. At that time Bolivia was under a dictatorship and signed the agreement. Bolivia was given 25 years to start the ban. That time ended in 1989, and the issue has been argued about since then.

In 2011, Bolivia told the UN it was leaving the agreement. Bolivia’s President, Evo Morales, is a former coca producer. On Friday Bolivia joined the agreement again, but with an exception that allows coca chewing.

This is the first time something like this has happened in UN drug agreements. Some people worry other countries will ask for changes too. Russia said the move will increase illegal cocaine trade and said it sets a “dangerous precedent” for more liberal drug rules.

The UK parliament’s Home Affairs Select Committee said the UK government should support Bolivia’s request. It said it is important for countries to stay inside the agreement. Bolivia’s return could be blocked only if one third or more of the 184 countries in the agreement oppose the request. Some people suspect the US and UK are strongly trying to persuade other countries to block Bolivia.

Nancie Prud’homme from the International Centre on Human Rights and Drug Policy criticized the organized opposition. She said the objections are legally doubtful. She said they support a rule that is too broad and ignore cultural and indigenous rights that support Bolivia’s efforts.

The ban on coca chewing was based on a 1950 UN report about the coca leaf. Supporters of drug law change say the report did not have strong evidence. In 1949, before the inquiry started, the head of the group, Howard B Fonda, said he was against coca chewing. He said it was harmful and spoke about “racial degeneration” and wanted the habit abolished.

In Bolivia, growing coca leaves is legal and licensed. Some people say this policy helped reduce cocaine production in the country. Some experts think Bolivia’s model could help other countries.

text:
Male bosses get bonuses that are about twice as big as the bonuses for women doing the same jobs. Over a whole working life, this can mean men get £141,500 more than women in extra pay.

The Chartered Management Institute (CMI) shared these numbers. It said that in 2012, men in UK management jobs got an average bonus of £6,442. Women got £3,029.

In the most senior jobs, female directors got bonuses of £36,270 in the past 12 months. Male directors got £63,700.

These figures led equality campaigners to call for action.

Ann Francke, the CMI chief executive, said this issue should be part of normal management. She said workplaces should have more flexibility, less “masculine” culture, more focus on results instead of time in the office, and clearer information about performance and rewards. She said fixing this could improve how organizations work and help people feel better at work.

Dr Ruth Sealy, a senior research fellow at Cranfield School of Management, said this is not surprising. She said bonuses can be given based on personal choice, so the system should be more open and clear.

Some statisticians warned that some data may be affected by things like women working in jobs where bonuses are less common. But the bonus differences seem to make the UK pay gap worse. The government says the pay gap is getting smaller, but full-time men still earn 10% more than women.

Maria Miller, the Minister for Women and Equalities, said the CMI figures show women still do worse than men at work, and things are not equal yet. She said the pay gap is closing, but more work is needed.

She said the government has taken steps. It made pay secrecy clauses illegal. It gave tribunals power to make employers who break equal pay laws do equal pay audits. It also got 120 companies to join the Think, Act, Report scheme, which encourages companies to improve how they recruit, promote, and pay women. She also said the government has tried to help with work and family duties by introducing shared parental leave and the right to request flexible working for all employees.

Big companies like Tesco, BT, Unilever, and the international law firm Eversheds have joined Think, Act, Report. The scheme has 120 supporters after almost two years. It had 54 participants in November 2012.

CMI data also gave some support to Miller’s idea that the overall pay gap is getting smaller. The difference between the average salaries of male and female bosses seemed to drop from £10,060 in 2012 to £8,502 in the most recent CMI figures.

But the institute warned that the 2012 and 2013 groups are not the same, even though each survey asked about 40,000 managers.

A smaller group of 17,000 managers was tracked over several years. It showed that for the first time in five years, men’s earnings are rising faster than women’s. When salary and bonuses are added together, men’s total earnings rose by 3.2%, compared with 2.8% for women.

At the highest level, male directors’ earnings rose by 5.3% in the past 12 months, compared with 1.1% for female directors.

text:
Brazil had one of its biggest protest nights in many years. More than 100,000 people went into the streets across the country. They were angry about tough police actions, bad public services, and the high cost of the World Cup.

Big protests happened in Rio de Janeiro, São Paulo, Brasilia, Belem, Belo Horizonte, Salvador, and other places. The protests began peacefully, but some turned into fights with police. Some people also set cars and buses on fire.

The number of protesters and how many cities joined showed the protests were growing fast. Earlier protests were smaller and were about higher bus prices. People said police reacted too strongly, using rubber bullets, tear gas, and violent beatings.

The protests happened as the Confederations Cup started, a test event for the World Cup. Many different kinds of people joined. They were upset about rising costs, poor public services, big spending on sports events, low health care standards, and worries about inequality and corruption.

In Rio, most protests were peaceful. But some police were hurt in clashes at the city’s legislative assembly. At least one car was flipped over and burned. Windows were broken at banks and notary offices.

Later that night, a large crowd set several fires outside the legislative assembly, broke the building’s windows, and wrote graffiti that said “Revolution”, “Down with Paes, down with Cabral [the mayor and state governor]” and “Hate police”. Police inside used pepper spray, and maybe more. The Guardian saw one protester passed out and bleeding badly from a wound in the upper arm.

Protesters had many reasons. Oscar José Santos, 19, from a group of hooded youths from the Rocinha favela, said: “We are here because we hate the government. They do nothing for us.”

Nadia al Husin said: “I’m an architect but I have been unemployed for six months. There must be something wrong with this country.” She held a banner asking the government to do more for education.

In Brasilia, a smaller group broke through police lines and entered the high-security area of the national congress. Several people climbed onto the roof.

In Belo Horizonte, police fought with protesters who tried to get past a barrier around a football stadium. The stadium was hosting a Confederations Cup match between Nigeria and Tahiti.

In Porto Alegre, people set a bus on fire. In Curitiba, protesters tried to force their way into the office of the state governor. There were also rallies in Belem, Salvador, and other places.

In São Paulo, where clashes had been very serious the week before and many people accused police of violence, large crowds gathered again. Early reports said the marches were peaceful.

Social networks helped spread news about the protests. In São Paulo, where the crowd was estimated between 30,000 and 100,000, some people carried signs saying, “We come from Facebook”.

Most protesters were young, and for many it was their first very large rally. Student Thiago Firbida said: “My generation has never experienced this. Since the dictatorship, Brazilians never bothered to take over the streets. They did not believe they had a reason to. But now Brazil is once again in crisis, with a constant rise in prices, so people are finally reacting.”

Some people compared Brazil’s protests to rallies in Turkey and other places. A few protesters wore Guy Fawkes masks, linked to Anonymous and the Occupy Wall Street protests.

People called the protests the “vinegar revolution” because police arrested people for carrying vinegar to reduce the effects of tear gas. They also called it the “20-cent revolution” because of the bus price rise, and Passe Livre because of the demand for free public transport.

Some people said the protests did not feel like Brazil, but they also felt free. Deli Borsari, a 53-year-old yoga instructor, said: “Our politicians need to see the strength we have as one people. Brazilians tend to be too nice sometimes – they enjoy partying rather than protesting – but something is changing.”

After many news reports about the cost of new and rebuilt stadiums, the Confederations Cup became an important focus of the protests. Before the opening match in Brasilia, riot police pushed crowds away. Video showed scared Japanese supporters running away with their children. People heard the sound of shots, maybe rubber bullets or tear gas.

Another march near Rio’s Maracana Stadium faced a similar strong police response.

Most rallies seemed peaceful at first, until they met security forces. These forces are mostly organized by region.

President Dilma Rousseff accepts the protests, her aides said. Helena Chagas from the president’s office said: “The president believes peaceful protests are legitimate and proper for a democracy, and that it is natural for young people to demonstrate.”

However, people booed the president at the opening ceremony of the Confederations Cup. The economy was doing badly and social unrest was growing. She faced a serious political challenge, now and in 2014, when Brazil would host the World Cup and also have a presidential election.

text:
After a vote in the European Parliament, new EU rules on tobacco are closer.

The plan includes big health warnings on cigarette packs and a ban on menthol cigarettes across the EU. Menthol and other flavours will be banned from 2022.

But MEPs decided that most electronic cigarettes (e-cigarettes) do not need to be regulated like medicines. This was bad news for the UK government.

Health officials and the e-cigarette industry in Britain want to understand what the vote means. For example, they want to know if e-cigarette companies will face the same bans on sponsorship and promotion at sports events as tobacco companies.

The Department of Health said it would not comment on advertising until it had studied the decision. It said it was pleased about tougher action on tobacco, including a ban on flavoured cigarettes and stricter health warnings on the front of packs. But it said it was disappointed that the plan to regulate nicotine-containing products (NCPs), including e-cigarettes, as medicines was rejected. The Department of Health said it still believes these products should be treated as medicines.

The Department of Health also said smoking levels in England are at their lowest since records began: 19.5%. It said it wants to reduce smoking even more.

The UK e-cigarette industry mostly welcomed the vote. It said it was already talking with the Advertising Standards Authority. It also said it would not be sensible to ban all advertising.

MEPs said e-cigarettes should only be treated as medical products if companies claim they can stop people from smoking tobacco. The government’s main medicines regulator criticized this decision.

By 2014, an estimated 1.3 million people in Britain were expected to use these products. Some people want e-cigarettes to be treated like nicotine gum, patches, and mouth sprays that help smokers quit. But the industry says the licensing process would be expensive and could push alternatives to tobacco off the shelves.

MEPs voted for health warnings to cover 65% of each cigarette pack, not 75% as first suggested. Right now, warnings cover at least 30% on the front and 40% on the back. The UK government has delayed deciding whether to copy Australia’s standardized packaging, until there is evidence it reduces tobacco use.

This was the first reading of a draft EU tobacco directive. It could become law in 2014. Next, there will be negotiations with the EU Council of Ministers.

The Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Authority had asked manufacturers to choose voluntary regulation in June 2013. It still hopes regulation will become compulsory across Europe. A spokesman said the law-making process is not finished, and the UK still believes regulating NCPs as medicines is best for public health.

Linda McAvan, a Labour MEP for Yorkshire and the Humber, said children start smoking, not adults. She said World Health Organization figures show worrying increases in young smokers in some EU countries. She said tobacco companies should not be allowed to target young people with gimmicky products, and packs should have strong warnings.

Martin Callanan, a Conservative MEP for North East England, said forcing e-cigarettes off the shelves would be crazy. He said they have helped many people stop smoking more harmful cigarettes, and some MEPs wanted to make e-cigarettes harder to make than tobacco.

Katherine Devlin, president of ECITA, said the decision not to support medicines regulation was very important, and meant it was now off the table.

British American Tobacco said the larger health warnings go beyond what is needed to inform consumers about health risks. It also said banning menthol cigarettes would increase demand for black-market goods.

text:
Coal may become as important as oil as the world’s biggest energy source in the next five years. This could be very bad for the climate, says a top energy economics group.

A big reason for more coal use worldwide is the huge rise of shale gas use in the United States.

New research from the International Energy Agency (IEA) says coal use is rising around the world, even in places that want to cut carbon. The main exception is the US, where shale gas has replaced coal. When the US used less coal, coal prices fell worldwide. This made coal seem cheaper and more attractive, even in Europe, where the Emissions Trading Scheme was meant to discourage coal.

Maria van der Hoeven, Executive Director of the IEA, said coal’s share of world energy keeps growing. She said that if current policies do not change, coal will catch up to oil within a decade.

Coal is common in many parts of the world. It is often cheaper to get than normal oil and gas. Because of this, coal provided almost half of the growth in world energy demand in the past ten years. The IEA says China and India will push coal use higher in the next five years. India will likely pass the US to become the world’s second biggest coal user. China is the biggest coal importer. Indonesia is the biggest exporter and has briefly passed Australia.

The IEA’s Medium-Term Coal Market Report says the world will burn 1.2 billion more tonnes of coal each year by 2017 than it does today. This is about the same as the current coal use of Russia and the US together. By 2017, world coal use is expected to reach 4.3 billion tonnes of oil equivalent. Oil use is expected to reach 4.4 billion tonnes by that time.

Coal makes more carbon emissions than other major fossil fuels. It adds a lot to climate change, especially when burned in old, inefficient power stations. If power stations do not have special “scrubbing” machines to remove chemicals, coal can also release sulphur, which causes acid rain, and other pollution like mercury and soot.

Van der Hoeven said coal use will not fall much unless there is a high price on carbon to push people away from coal and toward cleaner technology, such as renewable power. She said only cheaper gas could realistically lower coal demand. This happened in the US because shale gas production grew very fast in the last five years.

She said the US shows that a better gas market, with flexible prices and local unconventional resources made in a sustainable way, can reduce coal use, cut carbon dioxide, and lower electricity bills. She said Europe, China, and other places should pay attention.

To do that, much more shale gas would need to be produced, because easy-to-reach normal gas is running out.

In Europe, the Emissions Trading Scheme was meant to reduce high-carbon power by putting a price on carbon dioxide emissions. Power companies and heavy-using industries were given a set number of permits. They had to buy more if they wanted to emit more than allowed. But too many permits were given out. Also, the financial crisis and recession reduced emissions. This created a big extra supply of permits and made permit prices fall sharply. At today’s low prices, only a few euros per tonne of carbon, there is little reason to choose lower-carbon fuels, and coal use is rising again in Europe.

This means one of the world’s main market rules for cutting greenhouse gas emissions is not reaching its main goals. Because coal has the highest carbon emissions, this growing use of coal increases the risk of climate change.

text:
Author Philip Pullman says illegal downloading is “moral squalor” and is theft. He says it is as much theft as reaching into someone’s pocket and stealing their wallet. Pullman wrote this in an article for Index on Censorship. He is the president of the Society of Authors. He strongly supports copyright laws. He is very critical of people who think it is OK to download music or books without paying.

Pullman writes that the technology is so impressive that some people do not see that what they are doing is wrong. He says it is outrageous that someone can steal an artist’s work and not be punished. He repeats that it is theft, like stealing a wallet.

His article came after music industry leaders met British Prime Minister David Cameron at Downing Street. They talked about web piracy, which means illegal downloading.

Pullman is the writer of the His Dark Materials trilogy. He says authors and musicians often work for years in poverty and without attention to create work that delights audiences. Then, when they finally succeed, their chance to make a living can be taken away. He ends with a simple idea: if we want to enjoy someone’s work, we should pay for it.

In the next issue of the group’s magazine, Pullman is in a dialogue with Cathy Casserly. She is the chief executive of Creative Commons. Creative Commons offers open content licences that “lets creators take copyright into their own hands”. Casserly says there are many problems with copyright, because it was created “in an analogue age”. She says copyright can block many ways people can share, build on, and remix other people’s work, ways that were not imagined when the laws were made.

Casserly says artists need to think in new ways about how they share and make money from their work. She quotes the science fiction writer Cory Doctorow, who said: “My problem is not piracy, it’s obscurity.”

Index on Censorship agrees with her. The magazine’s editor, Rachael Jolley, said: “Existing copyright laws don’t work in the digital age and risk criminalizing consumers. We need new models for how artists, writers and musicians earn a living from their work.”

The debate is active, and illegal downloading is very large. Data collected by Ofcom (the independent regulator and competition authority for the UK communications industries) suggests that, between November 2012 and January 2013 in the UK, 280 million music tracks were pirated. It also suggests 52 million TV shows, 29 million films, 18 million ebooks, and 7 million software or games files were pirated.

Ofcom has said 18% of internet users aged over 12 admit they have recently pirated content, and 9% say they fear getting caught. Pullman writes that the speed and ease of getting music as MP3 downloads is still surprising to people who have been building their iTunes list for some time.

After the Downing Street meeting, Cameron chose the Conservative MP Mike Weatherley to be his adviser on this issue. A spokesman for the BPI, the record industry trade body, said: “Mike Weatherley is a strong champion of copyright and the artists and creative producers it’s there to protect. We hope his influence and the prime minister’s endorsement of copyright will be brought to bear on the approach of the UK’s intellectual property office.”
