text:
This invention could be very useful, like Trevor Baylis’s wind-up radio in the 1990s before the internet. It is a cheap light that uses free power from gravity. It could help stop people in Africa and India from using dangerous kerosene lamps.

British designer Patrick Hunt tried to get money from banks and venture capital to start selling his invention. But it was hard. He said getting funding was slow and complicated, and people did not want to take a risk.

So he tried crowdfunding on a US website called Indiegogo, which had just opened in the UK. In five days, he reached his goal and raised £36,200. By the end of 2012, after 40 days, he had raised £400,000.

The LED light works with a dynamo. A 10kg bag of rocks is attached to the light. The bag is lifted to about 2m high. As it slowly falls to the ground, it makes enough power for 30 minutes of light.

Hunt is getting ready to make the light in China. He will test it again by sending 1,000 lights to Africa before making many more. He hopes to make millions.

Hunt is part of a new group of entrepreneurs who use crowdfunding to get money. Another new website is InvestingZone. It matches rich people with start-up entrepreneurs.

Indiegogo does not give shares. Instead, people who donate can get “perks” at different donation levels. People who helped fund Hunt’s light could feel good about helping poorer people, and they also got their own light.

Danae Ringelmann, a co-founder of Indiegogo, says the “gravity light” shows how crowdfunding can work well. She says it is fast and fair because it is not about knowing people at a bank or a venture capital company. It is about showing customers and fans that your idea is good, and getting them to support it.

She says it is still useful to test ideas even if they do not get enough funding. Then you find out sooner that the idea was not good.

Ringelmann lives in the US. She first worked as a Wall Street analyst. In 2008, she quit and used her skills to help friends in the arts raise money.

At first, the site focused on films. It launched at the Sundance Film Festival that year.

Five years later, the site was raising about $2m each week for new businesses that are starting or growing. In December 2012, it started services in euros and pounds to grow in Europe. It says Britain is its third biggest market. International activity has gone up 41% since December.

There are many competing sites, like Kickstarter, Seedrs, and Funding Circle. But Ringelmann says Indiegogo is different because anyone can start a campaign. No project is too strange.

Indiegogo charges a 4% fee for successful campaigns. If a campaign does not reach its target, users can choose to refund all money with no charge, or keep the money raised but pay a 9% fee.

A British woman, Lauren Pears, raised £100,000 on the site to open a “cat café” in London. It is called Lady Dinah’s Cat Emporium. It has not opened yet, but it is described as a place where people can sit in a comfortable wingback chair, drink hot tea, read a book, and spend time with a cat.

Ringelmann says some campaigns are rejected by venture capitalists. The investors say they do not know if people really want the product. But an Indiegogo campaign can show proof that there is a market. Even the campaign itself can be enough proof.

She says crowdfunding helps you test your market, price, and features, and get important feedback.

Ringelmann says ideas are common. What matters is doing the work to make them real. She says if you are afraid someone will steal your idea and do it better and faster, then you may not be the right person to do it. She says you need confidence to move fast and learn.

She says expanding into Europe and working with a web transaction provider is part of a dream to make finance more open to everyone. This will allow payments through local card services like Maestro in the UK and Carte Bleue in France, as well as PayPal. More than 7,000 finance campaigns are live on the site.

Crowdfunding has grown as a choice instead of banks, but it does not attract many big investors. Big investors usually want a stake in a business. This may start to change in the UK with the launch of InvestingZone.

text:
Emmanuel Limal is a man who dated many people. He was tired of meeting women who did not want to start a family, or did not say they wanted one. Limal is a 43-year-old actor from France. He lived in Copenhagen for 20 years and looked for love because he wanted more children. He tried online dating, but he did not like what he saw.

He said many people tried to look very busy. They wrote about travelling a lot, going to the gym, and having many hobbies, but they did not write about children. Some websites had a box to click that said, “I’d like kids someday.” But when he read the profile, he thought the person would never have time for a family. He said that if someone goes to the gym eight times a week and travels every month, they are not putting a family first.

Limal has a six-year-old daughter from an earlier relationship. His father is one of 11 children, so Limal comes from a big family. He has always wanted more children. He said it was hard to know when to tell someone new that he wanted kids. He called it a big dating taboo. One day he read a profile from a 38-year-old woman. She said she knew it was “really bad to admit,” but she wanted children. Limal thought she should not feel ashamed.

Limal remortgaged his apartment to pay for a new dating website called Babyklar.nu. In English, the name means “baby-ready now.” It works like a normal dating site, but it asks people to be honest about wanting to start a family soon. The site asks if people are OK with dating someone who already has children and also wants another baby. But it does not ask people to say how many children they want. Limal said that would feel too much like shopping online.

He said many people joined the site. When it started in June, it got 50 sign-ups an hour. He said some couples have already met through the site and are now together. He expects the first Babyklar.nu baby by next summer. More men joined than women: 53% men and 47% women. Some people wrote messages like, “It’s so lovely to be able to say this out loud,” and “I finally dare to be honest about what I want.”

The site came at a good time for Denmark, a country of five million people. A report from the Copenhagen hospital Rigshospitalet says Danes are not having enough babies. The rate is now 1.7 children per family, and that is not enough to keep Denmark’s population the same size. Some people blame women for waiting “too late.” Others blame couples who live together and wait to start families.

Limal hopes the site will help men and women who want a family but have not met the right person. He says it is not only about making babies. He wants it to be about children and love. His goal is to match people who want a family and a partner, and who will stay together. He says he is a romantic.

There are plans to bring the site to France and the UK later in 2013. But for now, it is helping people in Denmark. Limal said Danes do not mind having children before marriage, so things can move fast. He also said Denmark is small, so a person from Jutland can date someone from Copenhagen without too much travel. Limal also said he has finally found love. He met a nice woman, and she wants a baby too, so he said they will see.

text:
Kenton Cool usually likes to talk, but now he can hardly speak. Climbing high mountains has made his voice rough. He is in Kathmandu, the capital city of Nepal. He flew down from Everest base camp that morning.

Cool is thinking about three big climbs he finished last weekend. Early on Saturday, he reached the top of Nuptse. Nuptse is the first and lowest of three main peaks in the Everest “horseshoe.” This horseshoe is around an icy valley called the Western Cwm.

On the same day, he climbed to the top of Everest. He reached the summit in total darkness early on Sunday. Then he and his climbing partner continued. On Monday morning, they reached the top of Lhotse, the third peak.

Cool says it was a rare chance. For the first time since the late 1990s, there were fixed ropes on all three mountains. He says this does not remove the hard physical work he did. But he thinks the next person will set a higher goal and do it without ropes or bottled oxygen.

Sixty years after Everest was first climbed, many stories look back to Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay. They talk about an earlier time, before today’s business tourism and damage to the environment. The writer asks Cool to look ahead and imagine what top climbers might do 60 years from now.

Cool says he does not like to think about it, but he talks about the Swiss climber Ueli Steck. Steck left the mountain in April after what Cool calls “an altercation” with a group of Sherpas at Camp 2. Cool says Steck planned a very hard climb: he wanted to climb Everest’s west ridge, first climbed in 1963, then go down to the South Col, and then climb Lhotse right away on a new route. He planned to do this without fixed ropes. Cool says Steck trained extremely hard. He says Steck was special, with great skills and very strong fitness. Cool says it would have been amazing to see what Steck could do.

Tourism is another issue. One sign of change is a helicopter rescue by Simone Moro, Steck’s climbing partner. Moro’s angry language helped cause the conflict at Camp 2. On Tuesday, Moro flew a powerful helicopter back to Everest. He rescued a very sick climber at 7,800 metres.

This was the highest rescue ever done on Everest. It also shows that helicopter flights have increased very fast in recent years. By 2073, the mountain could even have a helipad on the South Col. Tourists could arrive while using bottled oxygen. For now, helicopters are changing how people can rescue climbers and also many trekkers who go as far as base camp.

Mountain geographer and environmentalist Alton Byers says it is not clear if the Everest region can handle fast-growing tourism. He says climate change and tourism together are causing new problems for the Sherpa homeland. Glaciers are shrinking, and some are disappearing. He says this is already having a big effect. He says people everywhere talk about having less water. There is less water for farming and less water for the new lodges being built.

Byers says that in the Sherpa town of Namche Bazaar, workers are building a new five-mile pipeline to bring in water. This is to meet the growing tourist demand for showers and flush toilets. The local stream has been polluted by human waste. It also does not have enough water during the busy season, when the town is packed. Byers says every village digs a pit just outside the houses for garbage. He says Khumbu has the highest landfill sites in the world. At base camp, human waste is now handled well and taken away in plastic barrels. But Byers says the barrels are emptied into a huge pit a few hours down the valley, and it could leak into the region’s streams and rivers.

Byers says these problems can be fixed, but people must take them seriously. He says one climber can spend $85,000 to climb Everest, and that is fine. But he says people must also focus on other needs. He says for half a million dollars a year, most of these problems could be solved.

Byers says climate change is harder. He works with local conservation groups to plan for climate change. They often look for new water sources or collect rainwater. He says fast-growing glacial lakes are always dangerous. Some lakes might burst and flood the Sherpa homeland. He says there will be a time when people will have to move away.

Changing weather also affects tourism. More clouds during times that are usually clear are closing Lukla Airport more often. Lukla Airport is the main entrance to the Everest region. A new road for 4x4 vehicles is being built to Lukla to keep tourists and money coming. But Byers worries that Nepal is building roads cheaply and too fast. He says this can cause serious soil erosion and landslides.

Byers says everyone knows Everest. He calls it “the canary in the coalmine” that everyone understands. He says it is the best place to study how to deal with problems like climate change and tourism.

text:
Get fit in four minutes sounds like a silly promise. But when you try Dr Izumi Tabata’s workout, you learn it is very hard. You work as hard as you can for 20 seconds, rest for 10 seconds, and do this eight times. You will be out of breath and very tired.

Tabata has seen this before. He laughs about the first time he made his university students do it in the early 1990s. After four minutes they were exhausted. But after six weeks they saw results and were surprised.

Tabata studied Japan’s speed skating team in the early 1990s with coach Irisawa Koichi. They noticed that short, very hard exercise could work as well as hours of medium exercise. Tabata tested this with students.

One group rode a stationary bike for one steady hour, five times a week. The other group did a 10-minute warm-up, then four minutes of Tabata intervals, four times a week. They also did one 30-minute steady session with two minutes of intervals.

After six weeks, the Tabata group exercised only 88 minutes a week. Their anaerobic capacity went up by 28%. Their VO2 max went up by 15%. VO2 max is a key sign of heart and fitness level. The other group trained five hours a week. Their VO2 max went up by 10%, but their anaerobic capacity did not change.

Tabata also says they measured bigger heart size after three weeks of doing the protocol. He also says there is research coming that shows it lowers the risk of diabetes in humans. This is something they already showed in rats.

This workout is not something you do slowly while reading a magazine. It needs short bursts of very hard work on a bike or rowing machine, or with sprints and bodyweight exercises. Think of a 100m sprint at school. Now imagine doing eight of them with only a 10-second break.

Tabata says the rule is all-out effort at 170% of your VO2 max. If you feel fine afterward, you did not do it right. The first three rounds may feel easy, but the last two feel almost impossible. In the first plan the goal was eight rounds, but some people could only do six or seven.

One person on the exercise forum T-Nation says that if you do it correctly, you should “meet God.” They say most people cannot do it correctly and should not try.

Tabata does not fully agree. He says everyone can do it, but beginners should start with trained experts. This helps them work at the right intensity for them. He says he will soon publish research showing that doing it only twice a week, less than half of the original plan, still gives important health benefits.

Tabata also describes another new finding. It shows the protocol burns an extra 150 calories in the 12 hours after exercise, even while resting. This happens because of extra oxygen use after exercise. So it can help people get fit, and it can also burn fat.

It is surprising that the plan is still mostly used by serious athletes and gym fans. This may change because Tabata has made a deal with Universal Studios. This will create a network of instructors and a DVD range released near the end of the year. Tabata says he did this because he often sees people on YouTube doing it wrong and not understanding how hard they must work.

Should everyone add this to their workouts? Richard Scrivener, a former Assistant Strength and Conditioning Coach at Northampton Saints Rugby Club, says the benefits are clear. But Tabatas should be added, not used instead of a sport or training method. He says runners need good running economy, which comes from learning skill and running many miles. But runners could reduce long runs and total mileage by adding Tabata training. This can reduce stress on the skeleton and let joints rest and recover, especially for people with small pains or past injuries. Then they may get more benefit from long runs when they do them.

People who lift weights can do three strength sessions and three Tabatas each week. Other people can build up slowly, session by session and week by week. But it will not get easier, because each session needs maximum effort. That is why the protocol is so tough, and why it works.

story_id: Facebook deserted by millions of users
story_key: facebook deserted by millions of users
text:
Independent data suggests Facebook is losing millions of users each month in its biggest markets. Other social networks are getting attention from people who want something new.

Facebook is about to tell investors how it did in the first three months of the year. Analysts expect revenue to be 36% higher than last year. But studies say Facebook’s growth in the US, UK, and other big European countries may have reached its top point.

In the last month, Facebook lost 6m visitors in the US, a 4% drop, according to Socialbakers. In the UK, 1.4m fewer users visited in March, a 4.5% drop. These drops have continued. Over the last six months, Facebook has lost nearly 9m monthly visitors in the US and 2m in the UK.

Users are also leaving in Canada, Spain, France, Germany, and Japan. These are countries where Facebook has many users. A Facebook spokeswoman did not comment.

Ian Maude, a new media specialist at Enders Analysis, said, “In the US and UK, most people who want Facebook have already signed up.” He also said some people get bored and want to try something new. He asked if Facebook could end up like MySpace. He said the risk is small, but it still exists.

Other social networks are growing. Instagram, a photo-sharing app, gained 30m users in 18 months before Facebook bought it. It has become more popular with younger people.

Path is a social network for mobile phones. It was started by Dave Morin, a former Facebook employee. It limits users to 150 friends. Path is gaining 1m users each week. It recently passed 9m users. In one weekend, 500,000 people in Venezuela downloaded the app.

Facebook is still growing fast in South America. In Brazil, monthly visitors rose 6% in the last month to 70m, according to Socialbakers. In India, monthly visitors rose 4% to 64m. That is still only a small part of India’s population, so there is room to grow.

But in developed markets, other trackers also report declines. Analysts at Jefferies bank made an algorithm that connects directly to Facebook software. It suggests user levels in the first quarter may have dropped from their peak.

Jefferies saw global monthly numbers peak at 1.05bn in January, then fall by 20m in February. Numbers rose again in April. In the UK, Facebook has lost nearly 2m visitors since December, according to Nielsen. Nielsen says Facebook has about 27m total there, about the same as a year earlier.

Americans also seem to be spending less time on Facebook. comScore says people spent 121 billion minutes on Facebook in December 2012, but only 115 billion minutes in February.

Facebook has warned that time spent on Facebook pages from personal computers is dropping fast. This is because people are using smartphones and tablets more.

Smartphone minutes have doubled in a year to 69 a month. But this growth may not fully make up for people using desktop computers less.

Facebook is the main source for its own user numbers, and it will update investors on the quarter. Wall Street expects revenue of about $1.44bn, up from $1.06bn in 2012.

Shareholders especially want to know how fast Facebook’s mobile user base is growing. They also want to know if advertising revenue is growing just as fast.

At the end of 2012, mobile use made up nearly a quarter of Facebook’s advertising income. In December, Facebook had 680m mobile users each month.

In recent stockmarket filings, the company warned it might be losing “younger users” to “other products and services similar to, or as a substitute for, Facebook”.

Mark Zuckerberg, Facebook’s founder, is worried about competition from services made for mobile phones instead of PCs. He has pushed new projects to attract smartphone users. The biggest is Facebook Home. It is software that some Android phone owners can download. It puts news, photos from friends, and advertising onto the phone’s locked home screen.

text:
Scientists put a false memory into the brains of mice. They did this to help understand why people sometimes “remember” things that never happened.

False memories can cause big problems in court. Sometimes people are found guilty because a witness says they saw it. Later, DNA or other strong evidence can show the witness was wrong.

Susumu Tonagawa, a scientist at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), and his team studied how memories form. A memory has parts, like what you saw, where you were, and when it happened. The brain stores these parts in changes in brain cells and their connections. Tonagawa says real memories and false memories seem to use the same brain systems.

The team used a method called optogenetics. This lets scientists control certain brain cells. They changed brain cells in the mouse hippocampus, a brain area used for making memories. They made these cells produce a protein called channelrhodopsin. When blue light shines on cells with channelrhodopsin, the cells turn on. They also set it up so the protein would be made in the cells the mouse was using to store a memory.

First, the team put mice in a chamber and let them explore it. While the mice explored, the memory-making brain cells made channelrhodopsin. The next day, the mice were put in a different chamber and got a small electric shock, so they learned fear. At the same time, the scientists shone light into the mice’s brains to turn on the memory of the first chamber. This made the mice link the fear to the first chamber.

At the end, the mice were put back in the first chamber. The mice froze in fear, even though they had never been shocked there. Tonagawa said this was like putting a false memory into the mouse brain.

Tonagawa said something like this may happen in humans too. He said humans have many thoughts inside their brains, even when nothing outside is happening. People might connect a thought in their mind with a bad or good event, even if the event did not really happen that way.

He also said real and false memories are made in very similar ways in the brain. Because of this, it can be hard for a person to tell if a memory is real or false. He hopes this work will warn lawmakers and legal experts that memory can be unreliable.

Chris French at Goldsmiths, University of London, studies false memories in people. He said the results are an important first step to understanding what happens in the brain. He said memory does not work like a video camera. Instead, the brain rebuilds memories from pieces of the real event and sometimes from other information.

French warned that the false memories made in mice were much simpler than some false memories in people. He gave examples like false memories of childhood sexual abuse, bizarre satanic ritual abuse, abduction by aliens, or “past lives.” He said these rich false memories would involve many brain systems, and scientists still do not understand how they form at the level of neurons.

Mark Stokes at Oxford University said the experiments were very impressive, but people should not take them too far. He said it was more like adding a fear link to a place, so someone feels uneasy there without knowing why. He said this probably would not create the many details found in normal memories. But over time, the fear link might join with other memories and create a more detailed false story.

The mouse work will help scientists ask harder questions about human memory. Steve Ramirez at MIT said that now scientists can reactivate and change memories in the brain, they can test questions that used to be only philosophy. He asked if there are different ways false memories can form, if they can be made on purpose, and if false memories could be made about objects, food, or other mice.

French said scientists must think carefully about how this technology is used. He said there are serious ethical issues, and the risk of misuse is very great.

text:
Sir Alex Ferguson will retire as Manchester United manager at the end of the season. This is his 27th year in the job. He has become the most successful manager in British football. He will become a United director and ambassador. The club now must find someone to replace him.

Ferguson has won 13 English Premier League titles, two Champions Leagues, the Cup Winners’ Cup, five FA Cups, and four League Cups.

Ferguson said he has thought about retiring for a long time. He said it is the right time to stop. He wanted to leave the club in the best possible shape. He said the team that won the league is strong, with a good mix of ages. He also said the youth set-up is strong, which is good for the club’s future.

He said the training facilities are among the best in the world. He said Old Trafford is one of the leading stadiums in the world. He said he is happy to become a director and ambassador for the club. He said he is looking forward to the future.

He thanked his family for their love and support. He said his wife, Cathy, was very important in his career and gave him stability and encouragement.

He thanked his players and staff, past and present. He said their hard work and good behavior helped the club win many trophies. He said the board supported him in his early years, and Sir Bobby Charlton in particular helped by giving him confidence and time to build the club.

He said that over the past decade, the Glazer family helped by giving him the chance to manage Manchester United as well as he could. He also thanked David Gill, the chief executive, for working with him. He thanked the fans and said it was an honour to lead the club.

The news came suddenly, like he had said he would do before. He had mentioned a first retirement during the 2001/2 season, but then changed his mind. It is understood he called the players into the first-team changing room after they arrived for training on Wednesday morning. In an emotional speech, he said he was stepping down. He then spoke to his backroom staff, and then to the rest of the staff in the canteen.

Joel Glazer, joint owner of Manchester United, said Alex is a fantastic manager and a wonderful person. He praised his drive and loyalty to the club. He said he will always remember moments like the night in Moscow.

Avie Glazer said Alex has agreed to stay as a director. He said Ferguson’s work in the last 27 years has been extraordinary, and he wants him to be part of the club’s future.

David Gill, the outgoing chief executive, said he worked closely with Alex for 16 years. He mentioned the treble, the double, many trophies, and many signings. He said they knew retirement would come one day, and they planned for it by keeping the squad and the club’s systems in first-class condition. He said Ferguson built strong teams on and off the pitch, and the next manager can depend on them.

Gill said he admires how Ferguson cares for the club, the staff, and football in general. He said this side of him is not always seen by the public. He said what Ferguson did for the club and the game will not be forgotten. He said working with Ferguson was the greatest experience of his working life, and he is honoured to call him a friend.

First-team coach René Meulensteen said Ferguson told the backroom staff on Wednesday morning. He said Ferguson called them into his office and told them his decision. Meulensteen said people guess about retirement every season, but Ferguson did not speak much about it. He said Ferguson thought hard and decided the time was right. He wished him well. He said Ferguson has been fantastic for the club, and he hopes the fans support the next manager in the same way.

text:
Most exercise classes do not stop for tea in the middle. But Margaret Allen’s class does. After a gentle warm-up and some faster exercises, the 93-year-old great-grandmother lets the group drink tea and sit down for a short rest.

There are eight people in the class. Some look like they need the break more than others. Allen wears a thick turquoise shirt, a navy knitted waistcoat, black slacks, and sensible shoes. She is not sweating. She has a very painful trapped nerve in one leg and a knee that needs to be replaced, but she still looks like she could keep going for a long time.

Usually it is not a good idea to eat right before sport, or during it. But when I visit her class in a church hall in Saltburn-by-the-Sea near Middlesbrough, people are passing around slices of fruitcake during the break. The cake was baked for Allen’s recent birthday by her sister Joan, who is 89. People in the town call Joan the “scone queen of Saltburn”.

Soon after the last crumbs are eaten, Allen stands up again. She leads the group in a lively Scottish dance with toe pointing and leg kicking. Forty-five minutes later, the class ends.

Allen used to volunteer with the Red Cross. She has led classes in this seaside town for 45 years. She was not very sporty at school. During the second world war, she began playing the piano for a keep-fit class, mainly to have something to do during the blackouts. Later, when she was in her 40s, she took over the class when the old instructor retired.

At its busiest time, the class had more than 18 regular members, and each person paid £1 each time. Now the group is getting smaller fast. During the tea break, the women talk about a funeral they mostly went to that week. It was for one of the younger members, who died at 68 from motor neurone disease.

Allen is the oldest member, and her sister is next. The youngest person is Jean Cunion, who is 60. She says the group helped her through a hard time when her mother died. She feels a bit shy to say she may be the least fit. She remembers the first time she came, Allen said, “Who’s that huffing and panting?” and she had to say it was her. Ruth Steere, who is 76, is surprised that Allen always knows what is happening, even when her back is to the class. She says Allen shouts at them if they do it wrong, and she is very good at knowing what they are doing.

Allen loves dancing. She has never had formal training as a fitness instructor. She makes up her own moves using five tapes from the BBC’s first fitness guru, Eileen Fowler. Fowler died in 2000 at age 93, which is Allen’s age now. Allen thinks her good health mostly comes from staying busy, especially after her husband Joe died in 1997. When she was 80, she started writing poetry.

She says she writes poems about everything and cannot stop. A few days after the interview, she phones me to read a short poem she wrote about the joy of exercise. Winnie Robertson, an 84-year-old former teacher in the class, thinks the secret to staying fit is not letting yourself go. She says, “Use it or lose it.”

Allen still plays the piano and gives speeches. She is president of the Women’s Fellowship at the local methodist church. She is also one of three people over 90 at the scrabble club of the University of the Third Age. She did a computer course when she was 88 and tried to get online, but it did not work out.

Allen says getting older is not fun. She reads me a few lines from her poem called “That Beast Called Age”. She also remembers a doctor who saw her for the first time a few years ago and said she could not be older than 78. She told him, “Thank you, doctor. You can go now.”

She is also very direct about weight gain. She says people should not eat too much. When someone says, “Oh, I can’t lose weight,” she says, “Sellotape.” She acts like she is taping her mouth shut. She says she said this the other day to a very fat man. Her motto is: everything in moderation.

Earlier in 2013, Allen watched the news and saw a woman getting the British Empire Medal. She seems to mean Margaret Chartwood from Horley, who got the honour in January at age 77. Allen says the woman said, “I’m 80 and I’m the oldest fitness instructor in the country!” Allen thought, “No, you’re not.” But she says she will not write to Buckingham Palace.

text:
Top water scientists gave a very serious warning about the world’s food supply. They say that by 2050, people may need to eat almost completely vegetarian food to stop very large food shortages.

Today, humans get about 20% of their protein from animal products. The scientists say this may need to drop to only 5% to feed the extra two billion people expected by 2050.

A report by Malik Falkenmark and coworkers at the Stockholm International Water Institute (SIWI) says there will not be enough water on today’s croplands to feed about nine billion people in 2050 if diets keep moving toward western-style eating. The report says there will be just enough water if animal-based foods are limited to 5% of total calories and if food trade can reliably move food from places with enough water to places with water shortages.

These warnings come as Oxfam and the UN prepare for a possible second global food crisis in five years. Prices for basic foods like corn and wheat have risen almost 50% on world markets since June. This was caused by severe droughts in the US and Russia, and weak monsoon rains in Asia. More than 18 million people already face serious food shortages in the Sahel.

Oxfam says the higher prices will badly hurt developing countries that depend a lot on imported food. This includes parts of Latin America, North Africa, and the Middle East. Food shortages in 2008 led to civil unrest in 28 countries.

The scientists say eating vegetarian is one way to use water better in a world with more unpredictable climate. Foods high in animal protein can use five to ten times more water than a vegetarian diet. One third of the world’s farm land is used to grow crops to feed animals. Other ways to feed people include cutting food waste and increasing trade between countries with extra food and countries that do not have enough.

The scientists also say that 900 million people already go hungry, and two billion people are malnourished, even though food production per person keeps rising. They say 70% of all available water is used in agriculture. Growing enough food for two billion more people by 2050 will put more pressure on water and land.

The report is being released at the start of the yearly world water conference in Stockholm, Sweden. At the meeting, 2,500 politicians, UN groups, non-governmental groups, and researchers from 120 countries come together to talk about world water supply problems.

The scientists say competition for water between growing food and other uses will increase. The report says the UN predicts we must increase food production by 70% by mid-century. This will add pressure to already stressed water resources. At the same time, the world also needs more water for energy needs. Global energy demand is expected to rise by 60% over the next 30 years. The report also says we need to produce electricity for the 1.3 billion people who do not have it now.

The report says overeating, poor nutrition, and waste are all increasing, and water shortages may limit future food production.

“We will need a new recipe to feed the world in the future,” said the report’s editor, Anders Jägerskog.

A different report from the International Water Management Institute (IWMI) says the best way to protect millions of farmers in sub-Saharan Africa and south Asia from food insecurity is to help them buy small pumps and simple technology, instead of building expensive, large irrigation projects.

Dr Colin Chartres, the Director General, said the world’s poor often depend on farming for their income and already suffer from water shortages. He said farmers in developing countries are using small, local water solutions more and more. He said these methods could raise crop yields up to 300% and add tens of billions of US dollars to household incomes in sub-Saharan Africa and south Asia.

text:
Junior Smart knows a lot about gangs. He is 36 now. He says his life has two clear parts.

When he was in his late teens, his mother died. After that, he joined a gang in south London. He says the gang filled an empty space in his life.

He says, “They became my new support group. At first it was just a bit of fun. Then it became more serious and more about making money. They got involved in crime.”

At school, Smart failed his GCSEs. He retook them at college and passed them all. He got a full-time job in administration and also worked as a DJ. But he was also making money illegally with the gang.

Later, he was arrested for serious drug-related offences. He was sent to prison for 12 years. He says this shocked him. He says the first night after his arrest was a big wake-up call. He says he was living two lives: one with his friends and another with their enemies. He says he spent a long time trying to change himself.

Today, Junior Smart runs a team with 12 full-time workers and six volunteers. Their goal is to help young criminals and gang members leave crime. Many workers are ex-offenders. A few are still serving their sentences, but they can go out in the day to help. The team works with the police, the probation service, and other voluntary organizations. They help people who feel trapped and scared in violent gangs across London.

Smart says his change from gang member to helper started in prison. He saw a prison system that was failing prisoners. He remembers a drug addict he became friends with who kept coming back to prison. Smart says many people knew what they needed to change, but the prison system only dealt with the main offence.

He gives an example of a man with a £300-a-week cocaine habit. The man paid for it by burglary. He would even go into houses when he knew people were there. Smart says the drug addiction was not dealt with. He says it was like a “revolving door,” with people going in and out of prison.

Because of cases like this, Smart became a prison “listener.” This is a prisoner who helps new prisoners feel calmer and get through their first days. He then made a plan to start his own program after prison, using ex-offenders to help others stay out of prison. He was released early, after five years. He says he then got the chance to use his plan.

Smart is asked if things are getting better after the riots of last August and the government promise to crack down on gangs. He gives some praise to the police and says some of their work has been good. But he criticizes the government approach. He says the government often thinks that arresting a gang leader ends the problem. He agrees with a report that says arresting leaders can even make things worse. He says removing the leader can upset the gang.

He explains it like this: when the top person is arrested, people fight for power. They ask who was most loyal and who had the most respect. He says it is like a family. He says this can lead to more violence. He says arresting leaders may not have a long-term effect, and it may not even give the community a break.

He says the streets can become more dangerous. He says when a leader is gone, gangs may recruit more young people. Other gangs may think the gang is weak, and fights can start between areas. He also says the leader can make alliances in prison, or try to take control again after release, and violence can happen.

Smart says gangs are now recruiting many more children in primary schools. The youngest members are called “tinies.” He says they can be as young as eight to eleven. He says young children are used to protect older gang members from risk. He says the young ones may be the ones who do street dealing or even stabbing.

Smart says the work is very hard because there is not enough government funding and there is not enough long-term support. He says it is hard to help a young person who has been earning £300 a week illegally. He says it is even harder now because unemployment is high and benefits have been cut.

Still, his project has well over 1,000 clients and is getting results. Fewer than 20% of the people who get help offend again. Smart says he will not give up on anyone. He says, “I don’t think that about anybody.”

text:
Virunga National Park is in DR Congo. It is home to rare mountain gorillas. A WWF report says the park could bring DR Congo about $400m a year from tourism, hydropower, and carbon credits.

But WWF warns that if oil is taken from the park, it could cause serious pollution and long-lasting conflict in a region that is already unstable. Virunga is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and lies on the equator.

SOCO International, a British company, is the only company trying to explore for oil inside Virunga. SOCO says its work would stay in one area called Block V and would not harm the gorillas.

SOCO Chairman Rui de Sousa said SOCO understands how important the park is for nature. He said oil companies still play a central role in the world’s energy supply. He also said a successful oil project could improve the economy and social well-being of a whole country. He added that the park has been getting worse for many years and has fallen below the standards for a World Heritage Site, and that development could help reverse this.

Raymond Lumbuenamo, WWF’s country director in DR Congo, said security in and around the park would get worse if SOCO continues. He said the security situation is already bad. The UN is involved with fighting units, and the M23 rebel force is inside the park. He said oil would increase conflict and attract sabotage. He warned the park could become like the Niger Delta.

Lumbuenamo also said the population there is very dense, with over 350 people per square kilometre. He said if land is taken for oil, it puts more pressure on the rest of the land. He said oil would not create many jobs, but many people would still move in looking for work.

WWF also fears the area has earthquakes, and a volcano in the park could erupt again. This could damage oil equipment and cause oil spills in the lakes. Lumbuenamo said Virunga’s natural resources should benefit the Congolese people, not foreign oil prospectors. He said the country’s future depends on sustainable development. He said, "Once you have started drilling for oil, there’s no turning back."

Lumbuenamo said the gorillas are safe right now, but he also said it is unlikely the park can earn the full $400m a year at present. He said Virunga used to be peaceful and could be again, but security is bad now and the UN is involved with fighting units.

The WWF report says the park’s ecosystems could support hydropower, fishing, and ecotourism. It also says the park helps provide safe water, regulate climate, and prevent soil erosion.

Virunga is Africa’s oldest and most diverse park. It has over 3,000 kinds of animals. But many very poor people now live there, including people who fled after the Rwanda massacre in 1994.

The report says the park could support about 45,000 permanent jobs. It also says people around the world gain value from knowing the park is well managed and safe for future generations. The report says plans to explore for oil put Virunga’s value at risk.

Lasse Gustavsson, executive director of WWF International, said WWF will not stop until Virunga is safe from what he called a possible environmental disaster. He said, "Virunga has snow fields and lava fields, but it should not have oil fields."

The UNESCO World Heritage Committee called for all Virunga oil permits to be cancelled. It asked Total SA and SOCO International plc not to explore in World Heritage Sites. Total has agreed to respect Virunga’s current boundary. This leaves UK-based SOCO as the only oil company planning to explore inside the park.

text:
Nobody knows what came first in Greece: the economic crisis or shisha, a drug now called the “cocaine of the poor”. But people agree on one thing: shisha can kill. It costs €2 or less for one use, and it has spread in Greece, a country badly hit by Europe’s financial crisis.

“As drugs go, it is the worst. It burns your insides, it makes you aggressive and ensures that you go totally mad,” said Maria, a former heroin addict. “But it is cheap and it is easy to get, and it is what everyone is doing.”

This drug problem has put extra pressure on Athens’s health services, which were already struggling because of very severe budget cuts.

Shisha is used by many homeless Greeks who ended up on the streets because of poverty and despair. Addicts and officials say shisha is a type of crystal meth. They say it can make users very violent. It is often mixed with dangerous things like battery acid, engine oil, and even shampoo.

It is easy to find and easy to make, which fits a society that feels there is little hope.

“It is a killer, but it also makes you want to kill,” said Konstantinos, a drug addict. “You can kill without understanding that you have done it. And it is spreading faster than death. A lot of users have died.”

Charalampos Poulopoulos, head of Kethea, a leading anti-drug centre in Greece, said shisha shows how bad the crisis has become. The crisis has brought record poverty and unemployment. He called shisha an “austerity drug”. He said drug dealers make cheap synthetic drugs for people who cannot afford more expensive drugs like heroin and cocaine.

“The crisis has given dealers the possibility to promote a new, cheap drug, a cocaine for the poor,” said Poulopoulos at a centre for addicts in Exarcheia in Athens. He said shisha can be sniffed or injected. It can be made in home labs, without special knowledge. He said it is extremely dangerous.

Across Greece, six years of recession have caused serious harm. Depression has risen a lot, and so have drug and alcohol abuse. Crime has increased as austerity measures cut ordinary people’s income by 40%. Prostitution has also risen, because it is an easy way to get money to buy drugs.

“Desperation is such that many women agree to engage in unprotected sex because that way they’ll make more money,” said Eleni Marini, a psychologist with Kethea. She said shisha has been linked to a very strong sexual drive, but it also harms clear thinking. She said they are seeing more pregnancies among drug addicts who do prostitution. In 2012, two sex workers gave birth on the streets of Athens.

At the same time, suicides have increased, and HIV infections have spread so much that it has become like an epidemic. Drug addicts, a group believed to be about 25,000 people, have become more self-destructive. Experts say young Greeks are leading this trend. Youth unemployment is at 64%, the highest in the EU.

“The crisis has created a widespread sense of pessimism,” said Poulopoulos. He said people who might have stopped using drugs now have no reason to stop. He said there is misery, and people who think they will not find work are becoming more self-destructive. In Athens, where the crisis hit hardest, shisha is part of this.

Greece’s coalition government, led mainly by conservatives, has tried to deal with the problem by pushing drug users and other homeless people out of the city centre. Police operations cleared central streets, crowded doorways, and malls.

“But with such actions, authorities are only sweeping the problem under the carpet,” said Poulopoulos. He said this pushes people even further to the edge of society and into the hands of drug dealers who offer them protection.

Even though more people need help than ever, state-funded groups like Kethea have had their budgets cut by one third. This was requested by the “troika” (the EC, ECB, and IMF), which has been supporting Greece’s debt-hit economy.

Since the crisis began in 2009, Kethea has lost 70 of its 500 staff. The cuts happened even though studies show that for every euro spent on programmes like Kethea, the state saves about €6 in criminal justice and healthcare costs. “The cuts we have witnessed are a false economy, a huge mistake,” said Poulopoulos.

On the streets of Athens, where shisha has spread, people are more afraid. They fear that austerity does not only fail to help—it kills.

text:
During an important day at Liverpool’s Anglican Cathedral, the families of the 96 people who died at Hillsborough talked most about one thing: the truth. Years ago, The Sun newspaper used the words “The Truth” as a headline, but the stories under it were lies. We now know these lies were given to the paper by South Yorkshire Police to move blame away from the police and on to the victims.

Margaret Aspinall’s son James was 18 when he died at the FA Cup semi-final between Liverpool and Nottingham Forest. She said the families had to fight for 23 years to get the truth. Aspinall, Chair of the Hillsborough Family Support Group, said the families’ grief would never go away, but she was “delighted” by the clear and “profound” apology from David Cameron.

The Hillsborough Independent Panel looked at 450,000 documents from the police, Sheffield Wednesday, and other groups. It then wrote a 395-page report. The report showed many official failures and cleared the victims and the football supporters.

Some facts had been shown before. But the report still shocked people because it showed how deep the cover-up was. The families said the police ran a plan to avoid responsibility and to falsely blame the supporters.

The panel said this plan was led by Chief Constable Peter Wright. South Yorkshire Police spread a story that drunk supporters, or supporters without tickets, caused the disaster. The police tested the victims’ blood for alcohol. The panel said this was “an exceptional decision” and found “no rationale” for it. If alcohol was found, the police then checked whether the victims had criminal records.

The report, mainly written by Professor Phil Scraton of Queen’s University, Belfast, and agreed by all eight panel members, found “no evidence” to support the serious claims of unusual drunkenness, having no tickets, or violence by Liverpool fans.

The report said that even as families began suffering, Wright met with the police federation in a Sheffield restaurant to prepare “a defence” and “a rock-solid story”. The Secretary of the South Yorkshire Police Federation branch, Constable Paul Middup, said at the meeting that the Chief Constable had said the truth could not come from him, but that Middup had a free hand and support from many senior officers.

This meeting happened just four days after the disaster. It was the same day The Sun printed “The Truth” on its front page, using lies from four senior South Yorkshire police officers. Middup was encouraged to keep blaming Liverpool supporters for supposed drunkenness and bad behaviour, and to spread “togetherness” across the force.

The report supported the claim made in parliament that senior officers changed junior officers’ statements like a “black propaganda unit”. These statements were used as official police accounts for the later inquiry. They were changed to remove criticism of the police and to mostly add claims about bad behaviour by supporters. The panel found that 116 of 164 statements were changed “to remove or alter comments unfavourable to South Yorkshire police”.

The police said the changes were only to remove “conjecture” and “opinion”. But the panel said it was more than that. Scraton said: “It was done to remove criticism of the police.”

This propaganda did not fool the first inquiry. By August 1989, the inquiry said the claims about drunkenness and misbehaviour were false, and it criticized the police for making them. The report also showed that Sheffield Wednesday’s ground was unsafe in important ways. It said the Football Association chose it for the match without even checking for a valid safety certificate. Hillsborough did not have one.

In this unsafe situation, the report said the main cause of the disaster was poor crowd control by the police, led by an inexperienced Chief Superintendent David Duckenfield. The police lost control outside the ground. About 24,000 Liverpool fans had to go through only 23 turnstiles. Duckenfield then ordered a large exit gate to be opened, letting many people in. The inquiry called his biggest mistake the failure to close the tunnel that led to the already overcrowded central section of the Leppings Lane terrace.

The inquiry showed this, but the police repeated their claims at the later inquest. The inquest was affected by the coroner’s decision not to hear evidence about what happened after 3.15pm. This left out what the panel said was a chaotic emergency response. The panel found that 41 of the 96 people who died might have been saved if the police and ambulance service had done their jobs properly. Aspinall said this was very hard for the families to think about.

After the panel’s report, the Attorney General will consider asking the High Court to cancel the inquest verdict of accidental death and hold a new inquest. There may also be prosecutions, even after all these years, of Sheffield Wednesday, South Yorkshire Police, and Sheffield City Council, which did not properly oversee safety at the ground. Trevor Hicks, President of the HFSG, lost both his teenage daughters, Sarah and Victoria, in the crush. He said: “The truth is out today. Tomorrow is for justice.”

text:
Insects are crunchy and full of protein. People often forget them as food, except in a few places. But in spring 2013, more people will try to make insects seem less “yucky.” Insects may appear not only on special restaurant menus but also in supermarkets.

In April, London will have a festival called Pestival 2013. It is supported by the Wellcome Trust. The event is about learning to like insects, and eating them is an important part. There will be a two-day “pop-up” restaurant by the Nordic Food Lab. This group is linked to the Danish restaurant Noma. Noma served ants at Claridge’s hotel in Mayfair in 2012 for ten days, and it sold out.

Restaurant magazine named Noma the world’s best restaurant for three years in a row. Its chef, René Redzepi, says ants taste like lemon. He says a purée made from fermented grasshoppers and moth larvae tastes like strong fish sauce. Bee larvae can make a sweet mayonnaise used instead of eggs. Scientists keep finding new ways to use small creatures as food.

In March, a BBC documentary will show food writer Stefan Gates looking for and eating deep-fried locusts and barbecued tarantulas. Some people may laugh about this, like jokes about flies in soup. But there is also a serious message. Many experts think eating insects can help the environment.

The UN’s Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) has funded projects since 2011 to support eating and farming insects in south-east Asia and Africa. About two billion people already eat insects and caterpillar larvae regularly. In 2012, the FAO published a list of 1,909 edible insect species. With support from the Dutch government, it planned a major international conference on “this valuable food source” in 2013.

Insects are very common. Around the world, for every human there are 40 tonnes of insects. So they are not likely to become endangered, and they are unlikely to have been given chemicals. Redzepi said it is taboo to eat bugs in the western world, but asked why not. He said that in south-east Asia it is common, and that people eat bugs in many parts of the world. He also said that if you like mushrooms, you have eaten many worms without knowing it. He added that we eat honey, and honey is the vomit of a bee, and said to think of that next time you put it in tea.

Redzepi said the basic idea behind Nordic Food Lab was: “Nothing is not edible.”

Insects are important for life on Earth. There are more than a million species, and insects are the most varied group of creatures on the planet. But people often misunderstand them, hate them, and kill them just because they are there.

In the next 30 years, the human population will grow to nine billion. Already one billion people do not have enough food. More people will put more pressure on farmland, water, forests, fisheries, biodiversity, nutrients, and energy.

Meat is becoming more costly. This is not only about money. Rainforest is also destroyed to make land for grazing or to grow food for cattle. There is also methane released by cows. Livestock farming adds a lot to greenhouse gas emissions: 35% of the planet’s methane, 65% of its nitrous oxide, and 9% of its carbon dioxide.

Edible insects produce fewer gases. They have high-quality protein, vitamins, and amino acids. They also turn food into body mass efficiently. To produce the same amount of protein, they need about a quarter of the food that sheep need, and half of what pigs and chickens need. They create fewer greenhouse gases and less ammonia than cows, and they can be raised on organic waste.

China is setting up large maggot farms. Zimbabwe has a strong mapone caterpillar industry. Laos received nearly $500,000 from the FAO for an insect-harvesting project. In the UK, it is already big business, though not always official. A man was detained by Gatwick customs after a flight from Burkina Faso with 94 kilos of mapone in his luggage, worth nearly £40,000.

A study by FoodServiceWarehouse.com said that replacing pork and beef with crickets and locusts could cut greenhouse gas emissions by up to 95%. The text ends with a dark joke: when we die, worms and insects will get a chance to eat us back.

text:
Takahito Iguchi says the problem with Google Glass is that it is not cool. Many people online share photos of people wearing it and looking silly or smug. Google first gave 10,000 pairs to software developers. Some people think it would have looked better on supermodels or Scarlett Johansson. If you search online, you can even find a photo of a large naked man wearing it in the shower. Iguchi, a Japanese business owner, hopes this hurts Google. He is making a competitor that looks more stylish, like something from a science-fiction movie.

Iguchi’s product is called Telepathy One. It is not really a pair of glasses. It is one piece of metal with a camera and a tiny projector. He showed it at the South by Southwest festival in Austin, Texas. After that, it got $5 million in venture capital. Like Google Glass, Telepathy One is planned to launch in 2014.

Iguchi says Telepathy One is a simpler version of Google Glass. He calls Glass “an egotistical device” because it can do many things, like use the internet, read emails, take photos, and use apps. He says Telepathy One will be more for communication. It connects to your phone by Bluetooth. It will focus on sharing what you see and hear right away. You can post photos and videos from your view on Facebook or send them by email. You can also see and talk to a floating video image of a friend.

Iguchi says it will help people feel closer to friends and family. He has worked in Japanese technology for 20 years. His most recent work was a location-based phone app called Sekai Camera.

Some people may not want to get closer to a person wearing a futuristic headset. Iguchi says he is a visionary. He says when he goes to London, he is a stranger and sometimes feels afraid. He believes everyone wants to understand others and be understood. He says this device can help you learn information about people before you even speak to them.

Compared to Google, Telepathy is very small. But Iguchi is not worried. In his shared office in San Francisco, a converted warehouse full of young startup workers, he quotes Sun Tzu’s The Art of War. He says even small armies can sometimes beat big ones. When he was young, Japanese technology was very strong, like how the Sony Walkman was very popular. Now, to compete, he has left Tokyo for Silicon Valley.

Iguchi says Tokyo has a lot of fashion and culture, but it is still like an island and feels isolated. He says Silicon Valley has people from everywhere and helps you connect with the world. The hardware will be made in Japan. He is building a team of software engineers in the US to make the apps. When the writer meets him, a Japanese news crew is following him. They are interested in Japanese business owners who feel they must leave Japan.

The reporter, Takashi Yanagisawa, says Japan is losing confidence and needs a way to get power back. He hopes Iguchi could be part of a new answer. Iguchi says building the Telepathy One prototype was easy because Tokyo has many kinds of technology. The hard part is showing it to the world. He says big manufacturers want to work with him because they have good technology but find it hard to sell. He says a product needs a strong story. He gives Apple as an example, like “1,000 songs in your pocket” for the iPod. He also mentions the story of David and Goliath, and says Steve Jobs was inspired by Akio Morita of Sony, and Morita inspired him too.

Iguchi may have strong confidence, like Jobs. But because of his thick Japanese accent, he sometimes has trouble being understood. This may have helped give him the idea for Telepathy One. He tells a story about London, where he stayed in an Airbnb. He talked with the house owner for three hours, and then they became friends. He says that is how long it can take to understand each other and share feelings, background, and career. He hopes Telepathy can make that faster. He says if you get information from the cloud and social networks, it will be easier. He says the house owner works on getting investment from the UK to Africa. The man was excited about Telepathy and thought it could help educate people about Africa by showing other people’s point of view.

This is Iguchi’s biggest hope: that seeing what someone else sees will help you understand how they think and feel. As a student, he studied philosophy in the day and taught himself coding at night. He says one day he opened his apartment door and realized that everything is code. He says everything can be shared between humans, and everything can be encoded and decoded. He believes that if people can exchange code, it will end all war against each other.

text:
The problem started with a fake “scallop” dish. Now many famous hotels and department stores in Japan are in trouble for lying on their menus. This could hurt Japan’s good name for great food around the world.

One luxury hotel chain admitted that it lied about where some ingredients came from. After that, Japanese news reported new cases almost every day.

The Hankyu-Hanshin hotel chain in Osaka said it gave false menu descriptions for many items at some restaurants from 2006 to October 2013. About 78,000 diners were affected.

One main example was a dish called red salmon “caviar.” It was not salmon eggs. It was flying fish eggs, which are cheaper.

The company president, Hiroshi Desaki, went on TV and said he would take a 20% pay cut, and other executives would take 10% cuts. This did not calm angry customers.

A few days later, Desaki resigned. He said the group had “betrayed our customers,” but he also said, “We never had the intention to deceive them.” Later, one head chef refused a medal of honour he was supposed to get from the government.

The company has refunded more than 10,000 customers, paying about 20m yen so far. The total cost may reach 110m yen.

The scandal spread to other well-known food businesses, like Japan’s version of the UK horsemeat scandal. No one has become sick from the wrongly labelled food, but people are still very angry.

For example, some customers thought they were eating expensive kuruma shrimps. They were actually eating cheaper black tiger shrimp.

The first case was almost ignored. The Prince Hotel in Tokyo admitted a problem after a diner wrote a blog post. He said a “scallop” dish he ordered used a similar but cheaper shellfish.

The hotel investigated and corrected more than 50 menu items at many restaurants. This report pushed Hankyu-Hanshin and other hotels to admit they also tricked diners who thought they were paying for top ingredients.

The Hotel Okura chain, which has hosted guests including Barack Obama, admitted many problems. It said it injected beef with fat to make it juicier, and it called tomatoes organic when they were not.

It said, “We deeply apologize for betraying the expectations and confidence of our clients.”

More examples of false menu items were found: carton orange juice sold as fresh juice; Mont Blanc desserts topped with Korean chestnuts instead of French chestnuts; bought chocolate cream sold as home-made; imported beef sold as high-end wagyu.

Japan’s top government spokesman, Yoshihide Suga, commented. He said these cases appeared one after another, and the wrong labels caused people to lose trust. He said these were “clearly cover-ups.”

The scandal came at a bad time. Japan is trying to get South Korea and other countries to remove bans on food imports after the Fukushima nuclear accident. Also, UNESCO is thinking about adding Japanese cuisine to its intangible cultural heritage list.

One local newspaper wrote the headline, “Japan’s proud food culture in tears.” The newspaper Yomiuri Shimbun said it was “astonished by the industry’s lack of morals.”

The newspaper warned that the scandal could harm “brand Japan,” including products and services that foreign countries and tourists praise for safety and security.

Experts said the 2008 global financial crisis forced luxury hotels to cut costs, but they still tried to attract diners with detailed menu descriptions.

Hiroshi Tomozawa, a hotel and restaurant consultant, told Kyodo News that menu descriptions were made because consumers like brand products. When hotels could not get the ingredients listed, they used food from different places instead.

Even so, the hotels and restaurants may not face legal action. Menus are not covered by the agricultural standards law or a new food labelling law planned for 2015. The government’s only legal tool is a law that bans misleading claims about goods and services.

The biggest problem for the industry may be Japan’s careful and demanding customers. In a 2009 poll by an online restaurant guide, 72% said where food comes from was the most important thing when choosing menu dishes. Next were calorie and nutrition details.

text:
In 2010, the old city in Srinagar was very dangerous. Police only went there wearing body armour. Violent separatists wanted an independent Kashmir. Uprisings happened there, and more than 100 people died.

Now things look very different. Tourists walk in the same streets and line up for food like mutton tikka and rogan josh. The Nowhatta mosque, where young people once met after Friday prayers to throw stones at security forces, will be part of an official walking tour about history, crafts, and markets. Near Dal Lake, houseboats are booked months ahead. In the gardens by the lake, visitors take photos. Until the snow melted, nearby ski resorts were also full of rich Russians.

In 2002, only a little over 27,000 tourists visited the Kashmir Valley. Many were scared because of an anti-Indian insurgency that has killed up to 70,000 people. In 2012 so far, almost one million holidaymakers have visited, including more than 23,000 from outside India. But fewer than 150 Britons came. One reason is that the UK’s Foreign Office still gives strong warnings about travel there, and it lists recent security incidents.

Omar Abdullah, the Chief Minister of Jammu and Kashmir, was born in the UK. He asked the British High Commission in Delhi to relax the travel advice, but it did not change. Abdullah said this makes people’s insurance invalid when they visit. The last well-known case of foreign tourists being murdered in J&K was in 1995. Then, six westerners, including two Britons, were kidnapped by Al-Faran, a Kashmiri militant Islamist group. Only one person escaped alive.

Abdullah said British citizens have been killed in other places too, but people still travel there. He mentioned 9/11 in New York, and also Spain and Bali. He also said Indians have died in London, but Indians still travel to London. He said there is still a chance al-Qaida could do something, but that is not a reason to single out Jammu and Kashmir, or Srinagar, as unsafe.

Germany changed its travel advice in 2011. It said foreigners are usually not direct targets in clashes. Some people thought this was brave, because it happened less than a year after the 2010 troubles ended. On 15 August, India celebrated 65 years of independence. In the past, this day could be dangerous in a state where many people do not feel part of India. This time, the celebrations had no trouble.

Abdullah says tourists are safe if they take normal precautions. He says they should not go trekking near the border that separates the Indian- and Pakistani-controlled parts of J&K. Syed Ali Shah Geelani, a leader who supports Kashmiri independence, disagrees with Abdullah about many things. But they agree on tourism. At the start of summer, Geelani wrote to tourists and pilgrims. He said that no matter their faith, language, or region, they are connected by humanity. He said visitors are honoured guests, and protecting guests is a duty.

Some visitors may feel unsure about enjoying a place where many people have high anxiety and mental health problems. But locals in Srinagar said they support tourism. Amjid Gulzar, 26, said Abdullah should look for truth and reconciliation and also bring foreign visitors. He said without tourism, the economy would be in chaos. He welcomed the million tourists this year, but said Kashmir should do more to attract big-spending visitors, especially foreigners.

Gulzar said Kashmir needs better infrastructure, better roads, and reliable electricity. He said tourists also need more to do in the evening. He said there is not even one cinema in the city, and not enough to do after dark.

Some tourists might wonder if they will feel welcome. In June, a local Islamic group suggested a “dress code” for foreign tourists. Abdullah said tourists are not expected to cover their faces. He said the issue was probably short shorts and sleeveless vests. He said the main idea was to respect the local culture and dress appropriately.

Abdullah said he is pushing tourism because he needs to help the economy. Jammu and Kashmir’s finances are in very bad shape after more than 20 years of trouble. The state gets only £72m a year in taxes, but it pays £155m in salaries to 500,000 public employees. He needs more money quickly. For now, he is careful but happy to see tourists return. He said one million tourists does not mean everything is normal, but he feels satisfied that people can come, enjoy themselves, and go home.

text:
On Tuesday, the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge won the first part of their fight for privacy. A French magazine was told it could not sell or reuse photos of the couple taken at a private chateau in Provence.

But the case is not finished. French prosecutors must now decide if there will be a criminal case against the magazine editor and the photographer or photographers. The photos showed the duchess sunbathing topless while on holiday in the south of France.

A court in Nanterre, near Paris, ordered the gossip magazine Closer to give the digital photo files within 24 hours. The court also ordered the magazine not to share the photos again, including on its website and tablet app.

The ruling only affects Mondadori Magazines France, the company that publishes Closer. It must pay €2,000 in legal costs. If it does not follow the order, it can be fined €10,000 for each day it fails. The couple did not ask for damages.

The judges said the photos were very intrusive. They showed a couple partly naked on the terrace of a private home, in a park hundreds of metres from a public road. The couple could reasonably think they were protected from people passing by.

The judges also ruled that any future photo published in France by Mondadori would bring a €10,000 fine for each breach.

But the ruling only talks about the 14 pictures already published. Closer’s editor has suggested she has other, more private pictures.

St James’s Palace said the couple welcomed the ruling. A source said they believed the law was broken and they had a right to privacy. Maud Sobel, a lawyer for the couple in Paris, called it a wonderful decision and said they had been proven right.

Even though they won this civil case, the couple also want a criminal case for breach of privacy against the magazine and the photographer or photographers.

The prosecutor must decide who would be charged. The complaint names “persons unknown.” But it is understood the couple want a case against Closer’s editor, and whoever took the photos at the chateau. The chateau belongs to Lord Linley, the son of the late Princess Margaret.

Paris police started a preliminary investigation on Tuesday. Under French law, breach of privacy can lead to up to one year in prison and a €45,000 fine.

The couple hope this criminal action will send a strong message and stop paparazzi in the future.

Their lawyers did not ask for copies of Closer to be taken off shop shelves.

On Saturday, the Irish Daily Star printed the photos. Its editor was suspended on Monday night while an internal investigation happens. Also on Monday, the Italian celebrity magazine Chi, owned by Mondadori, published a special issue with 26 pages of the photos.

The couple’s lawyer, Aurélien Hamelle, told the court it was necessary to stop the “highly intimate” photos of the duchess without her bikini top. He said she is a young woman, not an object.

Delphine Pando, defending Closer, said the legal action was not fair compared to what happened. She also said the magazine could not control people reselling the photos because it did not own the original images.

Copies of Closer were selling quickly on the auction site eBay. One sold for £31.01. eBay later removed all listings after strong feedback from its community.

text:
The British government expects thousands of legal claims. This is after the High Court in London said three elderly Kenyans can sue Britain for money. They say they were jailed and badly treated during the Mau Mau rebellion in the 1950s, near the end of the British Empire.

On Friday, the court rejected the government’s argument that too much time had passed and a fair trial was not possible. In 2011, the same judge, Mr Justice McCombe, also rejected the claim that the three people should sue the Kenyan government instead. Kenya became independent in 1963.

Human rights activists in Kenya say more than 5,000 of the 70,000-plus people held by British colonial authorities are still alive. Many of them may also sue the British government. The ruling may also help victims in other former colonies sue.

The Foreign Office said the ruling could have big legal effects and it plans to appeal. A spokesman said the usual time limit for a civil case is three to six years, but this case is more than 50 years later. He said key decision makers have died.

The three claimants are Paulo Muoka Nzili, 85, Wambugu Wa Nyingi, 84, and Jane Muthoni Mara, 73. They won after a three-year court fight. Their lawyers say they suffered very cruel acts, including castration, beatings, and severe sexual assaults. A fourth person left the case, and a fifth, Susan Ciong’ombe Ngondi, died two years ago at age 71.

In Nairobi, Nyingi and Mara heard the news by mobile phone while sitting with supporters in a hot garden. They were very happy. They hugged, danced, and then raised their hands to the sky to pray.

Nyingi said he was held for about nine years, beaten until he lost consciousness, and still has marks from leg shackles, whipping, and caning. He said he wanted the truth to be known, even by his grandchildren’s children. He said people should be paid so they can begin to forgive the British government. Mara said she was very happy and felt her heart was clean. She said she would tell her four children: “I will tell them I won.”

In 2011, McCombe said there was plenty of evidence that detainees may have been tortured in an organized way. On Friday, he said a fair trial was possible. He noted that thousands of documents appeared in 2011 after the Foreign Office admitted it had a secret archive of colonial-era files.

During the government’s efforts to end the case, the claimants’ lawyer, Martyn Day, said those efforts were “morally repugnant.” The British government’s lawyers accepted that all three Kenyans were tortured by the colonial authorities.

Day said the British government admitted the three were brutally tortured, but used legal arguments for three years to avoid responsibility. He said victims of colonial torture in places from Malaya to the Yemen, and from Cyprus to Palestine, would read the judgment carefully.

Some people watching closely include veterans of the Eoka insurgency in Cyprus in the 1950s. One has already met the Mau Mau lawyers. Cypriot claimants could use British documents and the archives of the International Committee of the Red Cross in Geneva. These files are kept secret for 40 years and then opened. The Red Cross recorded hundreds of torture cases in Cyprus. Reporters there called British interrogators HMTs, meaning Her Majesty’s Torturers.

There may also be claims from Malaysia, where many people were detained during a 12-year war that began in 1948. It was against communist fighters and their supporters. Relatives of 24 unarmed rubber plantation workers killed by British troops are now in British courts, asking for a public inquiry. Former prisoners in Aden may also have claims. But because Aden is now part of Yemen, British lawyers may find it hard to contact possible clients there.
