text:
Since Moses King, 48, found out he was HIV positive, he has had one big problem: food. He could handle the shame some people in Liberia put on HIV-positive people. He could also get antiretroviral medicine. The Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria paid for the medicine, and the Liberian government gave it out. But King and his family, with six children, could not get the right food.

King is a farmer who grows food mainly for his family. He grew vegetables and bought rice. But meat and fish cost a lot in Liberian markets. They are important sources of protein, but his family could not afford them.

King said, “Farming helped us survive, but we had many problems. We could not get any protein, and we were not getting the nutrients we needed.”

Pate K Chon is a counsellor who works with people with HIV in Liberia. She had an idea to help. Years earlier, she watched a film about a fish farm in Thailand. She wanted to start a similar fish farm in Liberia. This could give people with HIV work and a steady source of protein.

Chon said she saw fish being raised in a cement pool and thought it was a good idea. Chon was diagnosed with HIV in 1992. She said many people she works with cannot eat a balanced diet with enough protein. She said fish is a clean source of protein and can be farmed.

Chon started building a pool to raise fish. In June 2012, she met John Sheehy. He is a “strategic philanthropist” and the founder of the consultancy Emerging Business Lab. He raised money for a non-profit fish farm in the northeast of Monrovia, Liberia’s capital. He also learned about fish farming by taking an online course through Cornell University and talking to other fish farmers in Africa.

Sheehy said he raised the money, built the farm, and learned how to set up the tanks and the water flow. He said he learned a lot on his own, and he would like to write a manual to share with other people.

The project has grown into the Grow2Feed Liberia Fish Farm. It has 12 tanks. When the tanks are full, each tank will have 5,000 fish. The farm can provide up to 200,000 fish per year. It serves a community of 1,200 people, mostly HIV positive, including King and his family.

The farm also uses waste from the tanks. It is collected and used to water crops. This also gives food and income to the community. The community has 503 women, 206 men, and 346 children.

Sheehy said the community members live near the farm and agreed to join the co-operative. Many people work on the fish farm or take care of the crops. When the fish are fully grown after 90 days, people receive fish. They can eat the fish and also sell fish in the market to get money for other basic items.

Sheehy said the fish farm helps people with HIV join society again. Now they trade with people in the market every week.

Liberia’s demographic health survey says 1.5% of Liberia’s 3.5 million people are HIV positive. Of those, 60% are women or girls. There is still stigma and discrimination, and this has led to about half of cases in Liberia going untreated.

Good nutrition is very important for people with HIV. Research shows they need much more protein to keep their health from getting worse and to support healthy growth. Chon said nutrition is key when taking antiretroviral drugs. She said the drugs are toxic, and if you do not have food, they can make you very sick. She also said food in Liberia is very expensive. People buy expensive imported rice, even though Liberia should be growing rice, and fish is too costly for many people.

Sheehy said Grow2Feed is the first co-operative fish farm in Liberia that works to help an HIV-positive community. The project has interested the Liberian government and the UN Food and Agriculture Organization. The organization has worked with Grow2Feed to provide training. It will also support teaching at the University of Liberia.

Fish farming experts say fish farming could grow a lot in Africa. Paul White owns the HydroFish fish farm in Ivory Coast, which produces 3,000 tonnes of fish each year. He said fish farming can work in Africa. He said a lot of fish in markets is frozen fish imported from China. He said its quality would not be allowed in Europe or America. He said there has not been enough money spent and not enough knowledge about fish production, but this is changing.

Some critics do not trust farmed fish. They worry about inbred fish and high levels of toxins. Sheehy said good practices can reduce these problems. He said some farmed fish is inbred, but Grow2Feed uses local fish from Liberia, not fish from another place. This helps them keep bringing in new broodstock using local fish. He also said they do not use closed-off parts of lakes, where fish can take in toxins. He said the tanks let them control the environment, test the water, and monitor it all the time.

Sheehy hopes to copy this model in more places in Liberia and nearby. He said a rice-growing co-op in Sierra Leone asked if Grow2Feed could do this on their land to feed workers. He also said there has been interest from Nigeria and Central America. But he said Grow2Feed is 100% non-profit and will keep its social justice focus.

text:
For almost 100 years, police and intelligence agencies around the world have used lie detectors to help catch criminals and find spies.

Many movies show a polygraph test, with the guilty person sweating while connected to the machine.

But the polygraph may soon be replaced. Researchers in Britain and the Netherlands have made a new method. In tests, it worked more than 70% of the time. It could be used in police stations around the world within 10 years. Instead of looking for face twitches, talking too much, or waving arms, this method watches full-body movement. It looks for signs of guilty feelings.

The polygraph is used a lot in the US in criminal cases and for security checks for the FBI and CIA. It is less popular in Europe. Many scientists and lawyers doubt how reliable it is. The new method has done well in experiments.

The idea is simple: people who lie move around more. The researchers used an all-body motion suit, like the kind used in movies to make computer characters. The suit has 17 sensors. They measure movement 120 times each second in three directions, for 23 joints.

Ross Anderson, a professor at Cambridge University, said that after many years of research, interviewers can tell truth from lies only a little better than guessing: about 55 out of 100.

He said the polygraph has existed since the 1920s. It measures stress from anxiety, and this can raise success to about 60 out of 100. But it can be misused to scare people, and many people feel anxious during a polygraph because their job or freedom may depend on it.

Anderson said the new method reached over 70% reliability, and he believes it can get even better. In some tests, the team has already reached more than 80%.

He said the main message is that guilty people fidget more, and this can be measured in a strong way.

Anderson also said this research matters now because of the US Senate report about torture by the CIA. He said torture is wrong, and it is also a very unreliable way to get true information. He said, “We have known for a long time that torture does not work.” The new method offers a practical, science-based choice for interviews.

The research paper was written by Dr Sophie van der Zee of Cambridge University, Professor Ronald Poppe of Utrecht University, Professor Paul Taylor of Lancaster University, and Anderson.

The polygraph was created in 1921 by policeman John Larson, based on research by psychologist William Marston. It records changes in pulse, blood pressure, sweating, and breathing to decide if a person is lying.

Movies often make the polygraph seem almost perfect, but it is not. In 1998, the US Supreme Court said there was no agreement that the polygraph was reliable. The US National Academy of Scientists supported this in 2003.

Anderson and his team did an experiment with 180 students and employees at Lancaster University. Half were told to tell the truth and half to lie. Each person was paid £7.50 for a 70-minute experiment with two tests.

In one test, some people were interviewed about a computer game called Never End, which they played for seven minutes. Others lied about playing it and had only seen notes about it.

In the second test, there was a lost wallet with £5 inside. Some people took the wallet to a lost-and-found box. Others hid it and lied about it.

The report said: “Overall, we correctly classified 82.2% (truths: 88.9%; lies: 75.6%) of the interviewees as either being truthful or deceptive,” using the combined movement of their limbs.

Anderson said their first try looked at which body parts showed lying. They found that liars wave their arms more, but this was only about 60%, like a normal polygraph.

He said the big success came when they looked at total body motion. That could tell truth from lies more than 70% of the time. He said it might work even better when combined with the best ways of asking questions.

Anderson said total body motion is less affected by culture, anxiety, and cognitive load (how much you are thinking). These things can confuse other lie-detection methods.

All-body suits are expensive, costing about £30,000, and they can be uncomfortable. So the team is looking for cheaper options. One idea is to use motion-sensing technology from video games, such as Kinect devices made by Microsoft for the Xbox.

Anderson said agencies like the CIA could teach agents to fight the method by keeping their bodies very still, but he said that could also be a clear sign.

text:
A girl born today in the UK will live to almost 82 years old on average. Her brother will live to about 78. In Andorra, they would live longer (85 and 79). In the US, they would live a little less (81 and 76). In the Central African Republic, they would live much less (49 and 44).

In most places in the world, people are living longer. A few countries, such as Lesotho, are different because HIV and violence have hurt people’s lives. The best news is that young children are much less likely to die than they were 40 years ago. Deaths of children under five dropped by almost 60%, from 16.4 million in 1970 to 6.8 million in 2010.

This is why a large project led by the Institute of Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME) in Seattle is important. Over five years, nearly 500 researchers worked to measure the world’s “burden of disease.” If we know how many children die and why, the world can better use money and effort to keep them alive. The database can help world groups and governments make better health plans, like focusing more on diet, dealing with alcohol problems, and continuing work against HIV in Africa.

The Lancet published seven papers from this project. The work is big and some people disagree with it. IHME used bold methods. In places without death records or medical records, they used “verbal autopsies,” which means they interview the family to decide the cause of death.

One surprising result was about malaria. In 2012, IHME said 1.2 million people die from malaria each year. This was about twice as many as people thought before. The biggest increase was in adult deaths. Many people have believed malaria mostly kills children under five.

Dr Christopher Murray, the IHME Director, said doctors are usually taught that adults in malaria areas become partly immune. He said they first agreed with that view, but later changed as they used more real-world evidence. African doctors often write in hospital records that many adults die of malaria. But Murray said a fever could be caused by something else. The results have led to more studies.

Margaret Chan, the Director General of the World Health Organization (WHO), officially welcomed the IHME study. But some WHO staff were careful. Colin Mathers, a senior scientist, said they must be very careful about whether the numbers are correct and should wait for strong evidence. Dr Ties Boerma said the WHO cannot quickly accept every new academic paper that gives different numbers. Mathers also said that IHME’s new kinds of analysis are stimulating. Murray said one main message was that the top causes of death are changing very fast, faster than expected.

Because people are having fewer children and living longer, the average age of the world’s population rose in one decade, from 26 to almost 30. This change has been very strong in Latin America. Murray said that in places like Brazil, the change is so fast that many institutions are not ready for it.

Another theme is that, outside Africa, many countries are moving away from infectious diseases and common causes of mother and baby deaths. More people now die from “lifestyle” diseases like heart disease, stroke, diabetes, and cancer. Some of these diseases can be linked to genes. Murray said this change is clear in Latin America, the Middle East, and south-east and south Asia.

A third big finding surprised the researchers. Many people are living longer but not living healthier, and disability is very common. Murray said the main causes of disability are not the same as the main causes of death. Common causes of disability include anxiety and depression, arthritis and lower back pain, anaemia, sight and hearing loss, and skin disease. There is also substance abuse. Murray said these problems are not decreasing over time, and there has been no progress in reducing them.

text:
A few months before he died, Carl Sagan recorded a hopeful message for people who might go to Mars. He told them: “Whatever the reason you’re on Mars is, I’m glad you’re there. And I wish I was with you.”

Seventeen years after Sagan shared his ideas in 1996, a company from the Netherlands said it wants to help make a Mars trip real. The company is called Mars One. It plans to send four astronauts to Mars in 2023 to start a human colony. But there are serious problems.

First, on Mars the astronauts would live with gravity that is only 38% of Earth’s. People think this could change their bodies so much—bones, muscles, and blood flow—that they might not be able to live on Earth again. Second, they would have to leave their family and friends, because the plan does not include a ticket back.

The Mars One website says a return “cannot be anticipated nor expected.” To come back, they would need a complete rocket with fuel to leave Mars, life support for a trip of up to seven months, and a way to connect to a space station near Earth or land safely back on Earth.

The website says none of this is easy, and it would take a lot of technology, weight, and money.

Still, the company’s Medical Director, Norbert Kraft, said 10,000 people had applied. He told The Guardian that the applicants were from age 18 to at least 62. Some were women, but most were men.

Kraft said people had many reasons for wanting to go. A 32-year-old American woman named Cynthia told the company she had dreamed of going to Mars since she was a child. She remembered a trip her mother took her on in the early 1990s to a talk at the University of Wisconsin.

Cynthia said the speaker was Carl Sagan. She asked him if humans would land on Mars during her lifetime. She said he asked her if she would want to be stuck in a “tin can spacecraft” for the two years it would take to get there. She said yes. He smiled and told her he truly believed humans would reach Mars in her lifetime.

Cynthia told the project that when she first heard about Mars One, she thought it was her chance. Her childhood dream could come true. She could be a pioneer, help build the first settlement on Mars, and show people back home that humans can still reach new places.

Kraft said Mars One is looking for people who are strong in hard times, able to adjust to change, curious, able to trust others, and good at finding solutions. They must also be over 18.

Mars One was founded in 2010 by Bas Lansdorp, an engineer. The company says it has a realistic plan and a way to pay for it using existing technology, and that the mission can be done. The website says Mars already has basic things needed for life. For example, water can be taken from ice in the soil. Mars also has nitrogen, which is the main part of the air we breathe. The company says the colony would use specially adapted solar panels for power.

Mars One said it signed a contract with the American company Paragon Space Development Corporation to begin work on life support systems and spacesuits for the mission.

The project is reported to cost $6bn. Lansdorp said he hopes to pay for part of it by selling broadcasting rights. He said the money from broadcasting the London Olympics was almost enough to pay for a Mars mission.

Another ambassador for the project is Paul Römer, who helped create Big Brother, an early and very successful reality TV show.

On the website, Römer said this Mars mission could be the biggest media event in the world. He said it would be like a reality show and a talent show with no ending, with the whole world watching.

The company’s goal is to build a permanent human colony. The first team would land on Mars in 2023 to start building it. After that, a team of four astronauts would arrive every two years.

Not everyone believes the plan will work. Some people worry about how astronauts would get to Mars and build a colony with all the life support and other things they would need.

Professor Gerard ‘t Hooft, who won the Nobel Prize for theoretical physics in 1999, is also an ambassador for the project. He says there are unknown health risks. He said the radiation is very different from anything tested on Earth.

The mission also hopes to inspire people for many years to believe that anything can be achieved, like the Apollo Moon landings.

Mars One says it believes it is not only possible, but necessary, to build a permanent settlement on Mars. It says this would help humans learn more about how the solar system formed, how life began, and our place in the universe.

text:
When the lottery balls fall and all six numbers match, many winners start spending. They may buy an Audi, book a holiday in the US, and call an estate agent. A study says this is common for lottery millionaires.

The UK National Lottery started in 1994. Since then, it has made 3,000 millionaires. Together they have won more than £8.5bn, about £2.8m each on average. The study by Oxford Economics says their money also helped make 3,780 more millionaires among their children, family, and friends.

Most winners (59%) stop working right away. But 19% keep their normal job, and 31% do unpaid volunteer work. The study says 98% of winners’ spending stayed in the UK. It estimates that each winner’s spending keeps six people in full-time jobs for one year.

Winners added almost £750m to UK GDP and created more than £500m in tax money for the Exchequer. Most money was spent on homes. Winners spent £2.72bn on their main homes, and £170m to pay off debts and mortgages.

Winners also wanted steady income, so they spent £2.125bn on investments. They gave £1.17bn as gifts to family and friends. They spent £680m on cars and holidays.

Camelot, which runs the UK National Lottery, paid for the study to mark 3,000 winners. The study used research from 100 winners who won £1m or more. It found that the 3,000 winners bought 7,958 houses or flats in the UK, about 2.7 each, and spent £3.3bn. Most winners (82%) moved to a new main home and spent about £900,000 on average.

Some winners added special things to their homes. Almost a third (29%) bought a hot tub. A walk-in wardrobe was important for 28%. Nearly a quarter (24%) chose a home behind electric gates. About 22% had a games room, and 7% put in a snooker table.

Bigger homes need help to look after them. About 30% hired a cleaner, and 24% hired a gardener. A small number (5%) hired a beautician.

Audi was the favourite car for 16% of winners. Range Rovers and BMWs were also popular (11% each). Mercedes was 10%, and Land Rovers were 5%. Winners spent £463m on 17,190 cars. The average price of their favourite car was £46,116.

Holidays were important too. Most winners (68%) stayed in five-star hotels overseas. The US was the top place to visit for 27%, and the Caribbean was next at 9%. In the UK, caravan sales also grew. In the past 18 years, 10% of millionaires bought a caravan, creating about £7.4m in sales.

Some winners used their money to start businesses. About 15% started their own business, 9% helped others start one, and 6% invested in or bought other people’s businesses. The study says these businesses employ 3,195 people.

Andy Logan, who wrote the report, said a win affects more people than they expected. It helps friends and family, and it also affects the UK economy because so much is spent in the UK. He said each win creates a ripple effect in this generation and often the next.

text:
In October 2012, the Taliban sent a gunman to shoot Malala Yousafzai. She was riding home on a school bus. They wanted to stop her from speaking up for girls’ education.

Nine months later, after many surgeries, she spoke at the United Nations on her 16th birthday. She said, “They thought that the bullet would silence us. But they failed.”

Her birthday was not like most birthdays. Instead of a cake, she sat in a big UN meeting room, in a seat often used by world leaders.

She listened as Ban Ki-moon, the UN secretary-general, called her “our hero, our champion.” Gordon Brown, a former British prime minister and a UN education envoy, told her, “happy 16th birthday, Malala.”

The day was called Malala Day. It came after four very difficult years for Malala, a girl from Mingora in Pakistan’s Swat valley. People learned about her when she wrote a BBC Urdu blog about trying to get an education while the Taliban were gaining power.

When she was 11, she showed strong determination. She called Richard Holbrooke, the US special representative to Pakistan, and asked him to use his influence to fight the Taliban’s attack on girls’ education. When she was 14, Archbishop Desmond Tutu supported her for the International Children’s Peace Prize. When she was 15, she became the youngest Nobel Peace Prize nominee in history.

But being famous also brought danger. She received death threats. On 9 October 2012, after a meeting of Pakistani Taliban leaders, the gunman was sent to kill what they called the “symbol of infidels and obscenity.”

After the attack, she had many operations in Pakistan and the UK. Doctors put a titanium plate on her left forehead and a cochlear implant to help her hear again. Now she lives with her family in Birmingham. She does what the Taliban tried to stop her from doing: she goes to school every day.

At the UN she said she was not against anyone. She said she was not speaking for personal revenge against the Taliban or any other terrorist group.

She answered violence with words. She said she does not even hate the Talib who shot her. She said that even if she had a gun in her hand and he stood in front of her, she would not shoot him.

She spoke clearly and confidently. Only her injured eye and the drooping left side of her face showed what she had been through. She wore a white shawl that belonged to Benazir Bhutto, a former prime minister of Pakistan who was also targeted by extremists and did not survive.

Malala said extremists are afraid of books and pens. She said they fear education and the voices of women.

She talked about an attack in June on a hospital in Quetta, the capital of Baluchistan, and about female teachers being killed in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa. She said extremists blast schools every day because they are afraid of change and afraid of equality.

She also spoke about Islam. She said the Taliban are wrong when they say God would send girls to hell for going to school. She said terrorists misuse the name of Islam and Pashtun society for their own benefits. She said Islam is a religion of peace, humanity, and brotherhood. She said Islam says education is not only a right for each child, but also a duty and responsibility.

Many people responded to her message. A petition called “Stand with Malala” asks for education for the 57 million children who do not go to school. It has more than four million signatures, and more than a million were added soon after her speech.

At the start of her speech, Malala said she did not know where to begin or what people expected her to say. But she did very well.

text:
Galina Zaglumyonova woke up in her apartment in central Chelyabinsk because of a huge explosion. The blast blew in her balcony windows and broke clay pots with her few houseplants. When she jumped out of bed, she saw a long white trail in the morning sky. She also heard car alarms in the street.

“I didn’t understand what was going on,” Zaglumyonova said. “There was a big explosion and then many smaller ones. I first thought a plane had crashed.”

But she had really seen a ten-tonne meteorite breaking apart as it fell to Earth. It made several fireballs just after sunrise.

Officials said almost 1,200 people were hurt, and more than 40 were taken to hospital. Most were hurt by flying glass. The glass broke because of a loud sonic boom made by the meteorite as it came down. No deaths were reported.

The Russian Academy of Sciences said the meteorite entered the atmosphere at at least 33,000mph. It broke into pieces 18 to 32 miles above the ground.

People in Chelyabinsk panicked. The city has more than one million people and is south of Russia’s Ural mountains. Mobile phone networks quickly became jammed because so many people were calling. Videos from the area showed the glowing pieces getting brighter as they came closer. The trail in the sky could be seen for hundreds of miles, even in nearby Kazakhstan.

Tatyana Bets was working at the reception area of a hospital clinic in the city centre when it happened. “First we noticed the wind,” she said. “Then the room filled with a very bright light, and we saw a cloud of strange smoke in the sky.” A few minutes later, the explosions came.

The Ministry of the Interior said at least three craters were found and the military was watching them. One crater was more than six metres wide. Another piece of meteorite was said to have crashed through thick ice on a nearby lake. Local military officials said radiation levels at the impact sites were normal.

In Chelyabinsk, schools and universities were closed, and many workers were told to go home early. About 200 children were among the injured.

Many people with small injuries came to the clinic where Bets works, mostly with cuts from broken glass. Bets said a nearby college dormitory was hit especially badly, and many students were brought in because they were very scared. “Many girls were in shock,” she said. “Some were very pale and many fainted.”

Early estimates said more than 100,000 square metres of glass was broken and 3,000 buildings were hit. The total damage in the city was estimated to be over one billion roubles (£20m).

The meteorite came less than a day before asteroid 2012 DA14 was expected to pass close to Earth, about 17,510 miles away. Experts said the two events were only a coincidence.

In the first hours after the event, rumours and conspiracy theories spread. Russian state TV and local media suggested local air defence units shot at the meteorite and broke it apart more than 15 miles up.

Vladimir Zhirinovsky, the ultranationalist leader of Russia’s Liberal Democrat party, said it was not a meteorite. He said it was military action by the United States. “It’s not a meteorite falling – it’s a test of new American weapons,” he said.

Some people tried to make money from the confusion. Within hours, people were offering pieces of meteorite for sale on internet sites.

President Vladimir Putin and Prime Minister Dmitry Medvedev were told about the event. Putin held a meeting with the head of the Emergency Situations Ministry. Medvedev said at an economic forum in Siberia, “It’s proof that not only are economies vulnerable but the whole planet.”

Dmitry Rogozin, Russia’s Deputy Prime Minister and former Ambassador to NATO, wrote on Twitter that countries should work together to make a warning system for “objects of an alien origin”. He added that neither the US nor Russia could bring down such objects.

text:
Sometimes, in a man’s life, he feels sadder. He thinks more about death. He may feel like the only smart thing to do is to spend all his saved money on a sports car.

Big, and often unwise, life changes are often linked to a “midlife crisis.” Some people think it is real. If it is real, humans may not be the only animals that have it.

A team of scientists from several countries says they found a drop in happiness in middle-aged chimpanzees and orangutans. They say this drop is like a midlife crisis for apes.

The study was published in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. Some scientists were surprised. But the authors say the results may mean the midlife crisis could come from biology that humans share with apes.

Alex Weiss, a psychologist at Edinburgh University, told the Guardian that many people think well-being goes down in middle age.

He said the team asked if the midlife crisis might not be only human, and not only caused by social life. It might be something that has evolved, so middle-aged individuals feel less well.

The team from the US, Japan, Germany, and the UK asked zookeepers and carers to fill out questionnaires about apes of different ages.

The questions were about each ape’s mood, how much they enjoyed being with others, and how well they reached certain goals. The last question asked how the carer would feel about being that ape for a week. Answers were scored from one to seven.

More than 500 apes were in the study, in three groups. Two groups were chimpanzees. The third group was orangutans from Sumatra or Borneo. The animals came from zoos, sanctuaries, and research centres in the US, Australia, Japan, Canada, and Singapore.

After studying the answers, the researchers found that ape well-being went down in middle age and went up again in old age. In captivity, great apes often live to 50 or more. The lowest point happened, on average, at 28.3 and 27.2 years old for chimpanzees, and 35.4 years old for orangutans.

Weiss said that in all three groups, well-being was lowest at an age that is like midlife in humans. On average, scores were lowest when animals were around 30 years old.

The team says this temporary drop could happen because apes may see depressed apes die younger, or because of brain changes with age that also happen in humans.

Weiss agreed that apes, unlike men, are not known to make big and often bad life changes in middle age.

Robin Dunbar, Professor of Evolutionary Psychology at Oxford University, doubted the results. He said it is hard to see what in an ape’s life would create this pattern. He also said apes are not very good at thinking far into the future, and that is a big difference between them and humans.

Alexandra Freund, Professor of Psychology at the University of Zurich, also doubted it. She said the idea of a midlife crisis is weak even in humans. She said many studies show no real evidence, or only a very small drop in well-being.

But Weiss thinks the results could help people understand the emotional crisis some men may have. He said that to understand the midlife crisis, we should look at what is similar in middle-aged humans, chimps, and orangutans.

text:
The inventor of a new computer system for autopsies says it could one day make it very hard to commit a “perfect murder.”

The method is called “Virtopsy.” It is being used in some forensic medical institutes in Europe. Scientists at the University of Zurich first developed it. In a normal autopsy, doctors use a scalpel and make a Y-shaped cut in the chest. With Virtopsy, doctors can look at the body in 3-D on computer screens.

Michael Thali, Director of Zurich’s Institute for Forensic Medicine, helped invent Virtopsy. He said it could change criminal investigations. He said a virtual autopsy could help find every piece of evidence.

Virtopsy uses images from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), and surface scans of dead bodies. Together, these tools are called a “Virtobot.” The method can find injuries like lesions and blows that are sometimes missed in a normal autopsy. It can also show air pockets, heart attacks, and even cancer.

Richard Dirndorfer, a pioneer of DNA analysis in criminology and a developer of Virtopsy, said Virtopsy might replace autopsies one day. He said it may happen slowly, like how DNA analysis slowly replaced blood group analysis.

These computer images let doctors see deep inside the body. The method has already found bleeding and broken bones that regular autopsies did not find. Right now, the main goal is to use Virtopsy together with the traditional autopsy.

Dominic Wichmann, a doctor at Hamburg’s University Hospital, said it could help forensic scientists plan autopsies more efficiently. Criminologists from many countries have traveled to Switzerland in the past few years to see the new method.

The US TV drama CSI has shown Virtopsy twice. In one story, it proved that a murder victim was killed by a bullet through the cheek. In another, a Virtopsy meant a murdered man’s wish to be cryogenically frozen could still happen because his body was not cut.

Scientists have worked on this method for decades. At first they worked in an unheated lab and many people laughed at the idea. Later, a rich ophthalmologist donated money, and the project grew. Even then, some people rejected it because they thought it could harm the traditional skills of forensic scientists and pathologists. But younger scientists often see it as helpful. They think it can support normal methods and maybe replace some of them, though probably not completely.

Lars Oesterhelweg, Deputy Director of the Institute of Forensic Medicine at the Charité Hospital in Berlin, said doctors still need the usual cuts to study things like the color of the blood, the thickness of body fluids, and smells.

He also said Virtopsy is very useful when the cause of death is unclear. It can help get third opinions, look at investigations again, and reopen cases.

Scientists also said families are often more willing to accept Virtopsy, because it does not cut or disfigure the body like a traditional autopsy can.

text:
The Senate Intelligence Committee approved a bill about the National Security Agency (NSA) collecting many US phone records. The bill would make the program more open to the public, but it would still let the program continue. The bill was sponsored by Chairwoman Dianne Feinstein. It would let the NSA keep collecting phone “metadata” (information about calls, like numbers and times) from millions of Americans for renewable 90-day periods, and let the government keep the data. Some lawmakers suggested that phone companies should hold the metadata instead. The bill passed the committee by a vote of 11-4, and it can now go to a full Senate vote.

The bill says analysts can search the data if they have a “reasonable suspicion” that a person is connected to international terrorism. It also says the NSA can keep watching a foreign person who was being watched outside the US if that person comes into the US for a short time, up to 72 hours.

The bill challenges a different bill by Senator Patrick Leahy that would stop collecting US phone records inside the country. Mark Udall, a leading member of the committee, opposed Feinstein’s bill. He said it does not change enough. Udall said the NSA’s surveillance of Americans’ private information does not respect constitutional values and needs major reform, not small changes.

Another committee Democrat, Ron Wyden, said the bill keeps “business as usual” and is not meaningful reform. Feinstein defended the bulk collection program, but said public trust needs to be rebuilt. She said the program is legal and watched by Congress and courts, and she believes it helps national security. But she also said more should be done to increase transparency and public support for privacy protections.

Feinstein said the bill would improve transparency and oversight in several ways. It would require a public report each year with the total number of searches of the phone metadata database and how often the program leads to an FBI investigation or a probable cause order. It would require the foreign intelligence surveillance court to limit how many people at the NSA can approve or search the call-records database. It would create criminal penalties of up to ten years in prison for intentionally and illegally accessing data collected under the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA). It would require the FISA court to limit how many contacts an analyst can get back from a search of bulk communication records.

After the hearing, Feinstein strongly supported the NSA’s main domestic program. She said there is a big misunderstanding about the program and that it is vital for protecting the country.

Independent legal experts also raised concerns. They said this could lead to a fight with the USA Freedom Act, a bill by Leahy and Jim Sensenbrenner that would ban bulk collection of Americans’ phone records. Elizabeth Goitein of the Brennan Center for Justice said the two bills show two different ideas about the relationship between law-abiding Americans and the national security state. She said the key question is whether the government should have a reason to suspect wrongdoing before collecting very personal information. She said Leahy and Sensenbrenner say yes, and Feinstein says no.

Wyden said recent news about the NSA spying on foreign leaders took attention away from the bigger issue of mass domestic surveillance in the US. He said collecting information about foreign leaders has long been understood and affects foreign policy. But he said his top priority is ending mass digital surveillance of millions and millions of law-abiding Americans.

Feinstein unexpectedly said she was “totally opposed” to spying on foreign leaders like German Chancellor Angela Merkel. But she has strongly supported the NSA’s bulk collection of Americans’ phone records.

Wyden said Americans are making it clear that they never agreed to give up constitutional liberties just for the appearance of security. He said the fight will continue and it will take a long time.

In a separate issue, Feinstein said James Clapper, the Director of National Intelligence, agreed to give her a written statement about a Washington Post story. The story said the NSA intercepted data while it was being sent between Google and Yahoo data centers. Feinstein said she would not decide what she thinks about the story until she sees Clapper’s response. Her strong support for collecting domestic phone records suggests she is not ready to expand her criticism of the NSA beyond opposing spying on allied foreign leaders.

Wyden did not comment on the Washington Post report about Google and Yahoo. But the senators suggested it could affect the privacy of Americans’ communications. Wyden said that in the past, countries had separate communication systems, but now communication is global. He said this makes spying on foreigners harder and can also affect US citizens.

text:
A top-secret document says the National Security Agency (NSA) got direct access to the computer systems of Google, Facebook, Apple, and other big US internet companies. The document says this is part of a secret program called PRISM. PRISM lets officials collect things like search history, email content, file transfers, and live chats.

The Guardian says it checked and confirmed the document is real. The document is a 41-slide PowerPoint and was marked “top secret.” It was not meant to be shared with foreign allies. It seems it was used to train intelligence workers. The document says the NSA can collect information “directly from the servers” of major US service providers. The presentation suggests the companies help with the program, but the companies that answered questions said they did not know about any such program.

Google said it cares about keeping users’ data safe. Google said it shares user data with the government only when the law requires it, and it carefully reviews requests. Google also said it does not have a government “back door” to private user data.

Several senior tech leaders said they did not know about PRISM or anything like it. They said they would not take part in such a program. One said that if the NSA is doing this, it is doing it without their knowledge. An Apple spokesman said he had “never heard” of PRISM.

The document says the NSA access was made possible by changes to US surveillance law. These changes started under President Bush and were renewed under Obama in December 2012. The program allows deep surveillance of live communications and stored information. The law allows targeting of customers of the companies who live outside the US. It also allows targeting of Americans when their communications include people outside the US. It could also allow communications made only inside the US to be collected without warrants.

The PRISM news came after another leak to the Guardian. That earlier leak was a top-secret court order that required the phone company Verizon to give phone records of millions of US customers. PRISM adds to the debate about how much surveillance intelligence services do. Unlike collecting call records, this kind of surveillance can include the content of messages, not only the metadata.

The document claims some of the biggest internet brands have been part of PRISM since 2007. It says Microsoft was first, starting in December 2007. Then Yahoo joined in 2008. Google, Facebook, and PalTalk joined in 2009. YouTube joined in 2010. Skype and AOL joined in 2011. Apple joined in 2012. The document says the program is still growing and more providers may join. Together, these companies cover most online email, search, video, and communication services. The document says the amount and type of data collected from each company is different.

Under US law, companies must follow legal requests for users’ communications. But the PRISM program, the document says, lets intelligence services get direct access to the companies’ servers. The NSA document says the operations have the “assistance of communications providers in the US.”

The news also supports worries raised by several US senators when the FISA Amendments Act (FAA) was renewed in December 2012. They warned the law could allow very large-scale surveillance and that the protections might not be strong enough. When the FAA first became law, people who supported it said a key protection was that the NSA could not get electronic communications without the agreement of the phone and internet companies that control the data. But PRISM, the document says, makes that agreement unnecessary, because it lets the NSA take communications directly from the companies’ servers.

A chart in the document lists many types of data the NSA can get: email, video and voice chat, videos, photos, voice-over-IP chats (like Skype), file transfers, social networking details, and more. The document is dated April 2013. Leaks like this are very rare for the NSA, which tries to keep things secret.

The document says PRISM lets the NSA get targeted communications without asking the service providers for them and without getting separate court orders for each person. It says the NSA can reach into the companies’ servers to get stored messages and also collect information in real time about targeted users.

A senior administration official said the Guardian and Washington Post articles describe collection under Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA). The official said the law does not allow targeting any US citizen or anyone inside the United States. The official said the program is overseen by the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Court, the Executive Branch, and Congress. The official said there are court-approved rules to make sure only non-US people outside the US are targeted, and to reduce how much information about US people is collected, kept, and shared.

The official also said Congress recently approved the program again after hearings and debate. The official said information collected under this program is very important and is used to protect the nation from many kinds of threats.

text:
Nelson Mandela, a famous leader in Africa’s fight for freedom, has died at age 95.

South Africa’s first black president died at home in Johannesburg. His family was with him. His health had been getting worse for years, so he stayed out of public life.

The current president, Jacob Zuma, told the country on television. He said Mandela died around 8.50pm local time and was at peace.

Zuma said the nation was very sad. He said Mandela was a great person because he was also human, and people saw in him what they wanted to be.

Zuma said Mandela helped bring South Africans together. Zuma announced a state funeral. He also ordered flags to fly at half-mast.

Archbishop Desmond Tutu led a memorial service in Cape Town. He said South Africa should try to be like Mandela.

Many world leaders spoke about Mandela. Barack Obama called him by his clan name, Madiba. Obama said Mandela gave up his own freedom to help others be free. He said Mandela changed South Africa and inspired people everywhere. Obama also said Mandela showed that people and countries can change for the better. He said Mandela set an example by handing over power and making peace with people who had jailed him.

UK prime minister David Cameron said, “A great light has gone out in the world,” and called Mandela “a hero of our time.”

FW de Klerk, the South African president who freed Mandela and shared the Nobel Peace Prize with him in 1993, said the news was very sad. He said Mandela believed in reconciliation and was a great unifier.

In South Africa, people went into the streets to remember Mandela’s life. In Soweto, people sang and danced near a house where he once lived. They stood in a circle on Vilakazi Street and sang songs from the anti-apartheid struggle. Some people wore South African flags and the green, yellow, and black colours of Mandela’s party, the African National Congress (ANC).

Mandela’s death made South Africa mourn and think deeply, almost 20 years after he helped lead the country from racial apartheid to inclusive democracy.

People around the world also felt his death strongly. Many saw him as a great statesman and a moral leader, like Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi and Martin Luther King.

After spending 27 years in prison, including 18 years on Robben Island, Mandela chose forgiveness. South Africa faced possible civil war, so he tried to make peace with the white minority and build a new democracy.

He led the ANC to win the country’s first multiracial election in 1994. After one term, he chose to step down.

Mandela was born Rolihlahla Dalibhunga in a small village in the Eastern Cape on 18 July, 1918. A teacher at his school gave him the English name Nelson.

He joined the ANC in 1943 and helped start its youth league. In 1952, he started South Africa’s first black law firm with Oliver Tambo.

Mandela was charming and liked boxing. He also joked about women noticing him.

When the ANC was banned in 1960, Mandela went underground. After the Sharpeville massacre, when police shot dead 69 black protesters, he decided to start an armed struggle. He was arrested and later charged with sabotage and trying to overthrow the government.

At the Rivonia trial in 1964, he defended himself. He said he believed in a democratic and free society where everyone could live together in harmony with equal opportunities. He said it was an ideal he hoped to achieve, and if needed, he was prepared to die for it.

He was not given the death penalty, but he was sentenced to life in prison. This was a huge blow to the ANC, but the struggle continued. Unrest grew in townships, and pressure from other countries against apartheid slowly increased.

In 1990, FW de Klerk lifted the ban on the ANC, and Mandela was released from prison. People around the world celebrated.

As president, Mandela had strong support from around the world, but there were also difficulties. After leaving top politics in 1999, he said he should have acted sooner against the spread of HIV and Aids in South Africa.

Desmond Tutu, who led the truth and reconciliation committee after apartheid ended, said Mandela rose above race and class. He said Mandela was warm, listened to others, and understood them. He said Mandela helped people believe in Africa and Africans.

Mandela sometimes appeared at ANC events and went to Jacob Zuma’s inauguration. His 91st birthday began the first yearly “Mandela Day” in his honour.

Mandela was married three times. He had six children, 17 grandchildren, and 14 great-grandchildren.

text:
A new count shows there are more tigers in Nepal now than at any time since the 1970s. This gives conservation workers hope. Tiger numbers have been falling across South Asia for 100 years, but people think tigers can still be saved.

Nepal now has 198 wild royal bengal tigers. This is a 63.6% rise in five years, the government survey said. “This is very encouraging,” said Maheshwar Dhakal, an ecologist with Nepal’s Department of National Parks and Wildlife Conservation.

The count used photos from more than 500 cameras. The cameras were placed in five protected areas and three wildlife corridors. More than 250 conservationists and wildlife experts worked on the survey. It cost about £250,000. Dhakal said India did a similar survey. Results from both countries will be published later in 2013. India has 1,300 big cats in several large protected areas and needs a few more months to finish its data. Nepal has promised to double its tiger population by 2022, from 121 in 2009, when the last full tiger count happened.

As Asia has become richer, prices for tiger skins and tiger body parts have gone up. Some body parts are used in traditional Chinese medicines. International gangs pay poor local people in Nepal a lot of money to kill tigers. The skin and bones go to middlemen. The middlemen can cross the loose border to India easily, where the main dealers are.

One big problem is that some senior officials work with mafia networks in this trade. Experts say the tiger increase comes from better policing in national parks, stronger efforts to stop poaching, and better care of tiger habitats. In Nepal, forests cover 29% of the land. But experts say Nepal must do more to protect tiger habitat and the animals that tigers hunt, so tigers have enough space and enough food.

As tiger numbers have grown, conflicts with villagers have also grown. Park officials said seven people were killed by tiger attacks near national parks in 2012, compared to four in 2011. Villagers want better protection. “The government is making conservation plans for tigers. But it should also come up with plans to protect people from tigers,” said Krishna Bhurtel, a village headman in Chitwan, to the Nepali newspaper Nagarik. Wildlife authorities caught a tiger in Chitwan after it killed two people, including a villager pulled from his bed in May.

Long ago, thousands of tigers lived in the forests of Bangladesh, India, and Nepal. Now their total number is about 3,000. This is a 95% drop over a century. Chitwan National Park in central Nepal has the most adult tigers, with 120. Bardiya National Park has 50, and Shukla Phanta Wildlife Reserve has 17.

Diwakar Chapagain, who leads a World Wildlife Fund Nepal unit that watches wildlife trade, said tiger skins are wanted in Tibet. Rich people there use them as festival clothes.

In Nepal, kings used to stand on tiger skins in front of stuffed tigers at special events. Some rich Nepali people have put tiger heads on the walls of their living rooms. Tiger bones are also wanted for traditional Chinese medicines. Chapagain said selling tiger parts can bring thousands of dollars in illegal markets, and this is a serious threat to tigers.

text:
In the market square in Rjukan, there is a statue of the town’s founder, Sam Eyde. He was a famous Norwegian engineer and businessman. The statue looks north across the square at a very steep mountain.

Behind the statue, to the south, is another steep mountain called Gaustatoppen. It is 1,800 metres high. Between the mountains, in the narrow Vestfjord valley, is the small town of Rjukan. Long ago it was a strong, busy town. Eyde built it in the early years of the last century so workers could live near his factories.

Eyde used the 100-metre Rjukanfossen waterfall to make hydroelectric power. At that time, it was the biggest power plant in the world.

But Eyde could not change where the sun shines. Rjukan is deep in a valley with high mountains around it. So the town and its 3,400 people are in shadow for half the year. From late September to mid-March, it is not fully dark in the day, but it is not bright.

Now there is something new. On the mountain across from the town, 450 metres above the square, there are three big mirrors. They are solar-powered and controlled by computers. They follow the sun in the sky and reflect sunlight down to the square.

“It’s the sun!” says Ingrid Sparbo, smiling. She lifts her face to the light and closes her eyes. She is a retired secretary and has lived in Rjukan all her life. She says people get used to the shade and stop thinking about it. But the sunlight feels warm, not only on the body, but also in the mind.

Two young mothers bring their children into the square and stand in the sun. On a freezing day, an older couple sit on a new bench and smile as the warmth hits their faces. Children smile. Many people take photos. A shop assistant, Silje Johansen, says it is “awesome. Just awesome.”

Eivind Toreid, an electrical engineer, pushes his child in a buggy and is not so sure. “It’s a funny thing,” he says. “Not real sunlight, but very like it. Like a spotlight.”

Heidi Fieldheim says she heard about it on the radio. “But it’s far more than I expected,” she says. “This will bring much happiness.”

In the Nye Tider café across the road sits the person behind this idea. Martin Andersen is a 40-year-old artist who moved to Rjukan in the summer of 2001.

Andersen says he got the idea for an artwork called the Solspeil, or Sun mirror, as September went on. “Every day, we would take our young child for a walk in the buggy,” he says. “And every day, I saw we had to go a little further down the valley to find the sun.” By 28 September, the sun disappears from the market square. It does not come back until 12 March.

During the long months in between, Andersen says they could see blue sky above and the sun high on the mountain sides. But to feel it, they had to leave town. He says it is sad when people must leave a town just to feel the sun.

Twelve years after he first dreamed of the Solspeil, a German company that works with CSP, or concentrated solar power, brought in the mirrors by helicopter. There were three glass mirrors, each 17 square metres. Andersen says it took longer than they thought.

The mirrors really work. At first, many local people were against the plan. There were petitions, letters, and a Facebook page. Many people thought it was a vanity project and a waste of money. But now most of them seem to accept it.

“I was strongly against it,” says Nils Eggerud. Like others, he thought the money could be used in better ways, like more carers for old people, better schools, cycle paths, or fixing country roads.

“And I still have doubts about the maintenance costs,” he says. “What will they be, and who will pay? But it does feel nice standing here. And you just have to look at people’s faces.”

In his office looking over the square, Rjukan’s young mayor, Steinar Bergsland, thinks about the benefits. He is less focused on the cost.

Bergsland says more visitors are coming at this time of year. Shopkeepers say they are earning more money too. A hi-tech company is thinking about moving to Rjukan because of the new technology on the mountain and the attention it has brought.

“This is a powerful symbol for Rjukan,” Bergsland says. With some government grants and a donation from a local business, the town only had to pay 1m krone, or £100,000, out of the total 5m-krone cost.

Bergsland says that compared with the town’s 650m krone yearly budget, 1m krone was not much. He says the mirrors can help the town raise money for better schools and more nursing care. He tells people to look out of the window at the happy faces. Now that the mirrors are here, he says, people love them.

text:
Barack Obama flew back to Washington on Wednesday. He went back to work in the Oval Office. Earlier that day, he gave a victory speech in Chicago after winning the election. He asked the country to unite.

Obama said, “You voted for action, not politics as usual.” But it did not look like things would get easier. The House of Representatives was led by Republicans, and Obama would have to work with them for at least two more years. There was also a coming fight about government spending and debt, called the “fiscal cliff.”

After his 2008 election, Obama got a short “honeymoon” time. This time, he likely would not.

House Speaker John Boehner and Senate Democratic Leader Harry Reid both said they needed to work together. Still, the fight between the White House and Congress could become very big.

Obama beat his Republican opponent, Mitt Romney. He won many swing states. But the election also showed that America is still very divided.

Obama’s inauguration would not be until January, but he began his second term in practice on Wednesday. Many supporters felt disappointed by his first term. Now he wanted to build a strong legacy.

Obama hoped the economy would keep getting better. He also hoped to work on immigration reform, education, climate change, and foreign policy issues like Iran and Israel-Palestine.

Obama won more than the 270 electoral college votes he needed. He also won a higher share of the popular vote.

Boehner spoke in a friendly way. He said both parties should find common ground to help the economy grow, create jobs, and solve the debt problem. Reports said Obama called Boehner to start talks.

Reid also spoke in a friendly way. He said he wanted to work with Republicans in the Senate and the House to solve the issues.

During the campaign, Obama said he was hopeful about a “grand bargain” with Republicans. He could not reach that kind of deal in 2011. Problems would come when they talked about details. Republicans wanted to protect military spending, while Democrats wanted cuts. Obama called for tax increases on households earning more than $250,000. Boehner rejected any tax increases.

Stock prices fell because people expected continued gridlock. By lunchtime, major US markets were down over 300 points.

The new House in January would look similar to the current one, with a large Republican majority. The Senate also stayed similar. Democrats kept their slim majority, gaining three seats and losing one.

In the presidential race, Romney won only one swing state: North Carolina. Obama won New Hampshire, Virginia, Ohio, Wisconsin, Nevada, Iowa, and Colorado.

In his Chicago victory speech, Obama talked about long lines of people waiting to vote. He said the country needed electoral reform.

He used the kind of inspiring language he was known for in 2008. He used less of that in 2012 because many people were unhappy with his presidency and the economy was tough. But after winning, he returned to famous lines and his 2008 slogan about “hope.”

As Stevie Wonder’s “Signed, Sealed, Delivered, I’m Yours” played, Obama spoke to cheering supporters. He said the road had been hard and the journey long, but Americans had fought their way back, and that the best was still to come. His speech lasted more than 25 minutes. He thanked his wife, Michelle, his daughters, Malia and Sasha, and his Vice-President, Joe Biden.

Obama said, “We are not as divided as our politics suggests.” He said Americans were more than red states and blue states. He said they were the United States of America.

Obama said he had plans for his second term. He mentioned changes in the tax code, immigration reform, and an America “that isn’t threatened by the destructive power of a warming planet.”

Before that, Romney called Obama to admit he lost. In a concession speech in Boston, Romney said the nation was at a critical point. He said leaders should stop partisan fighting and work together. He also said he prayed Obama would be successful in guiding the nation.

For much of the campaign, the election was a referendum on Obama. Many people were unhappy about the slow economic recovery and high unemployment. But Americans chose to keep him as president.

For African Americans and many white liberals, it would have been a major disappointment if the first black presidency had ended in failure and stopped early.

text:
Sweden is the best country for older people, and Afghanistan is the worst, says the first global index on ageing. But being a rich country does not always mean life is better for people over 60. Sweden is number 1, followed by Norway, Germany, the Netherlands, and Canada. The Global AgeWatch index also has some surprising results.

The US, the world’s richest country, is only in 8th place. The UK is not in the top ten and is 13th. Sri Lanka is 36th, much higher than Pakistan at 89th, even though their gross domestic product (GDP) levels are similar. Bolivia and Mauritius do better than their economy size might suggest. Brazil, Russia, India, and China have mixed results. Brazil and China rank fairly high, while India and Russia rank much lower.

“This survey shows that history counts,” said Mark Gorman, director of the HelpAge International advocacy group. He said the top countries are what people might expect, but Scandinavian countries were not wealthy when they started universal pensions. Older people in Sri Lanka today benefit from good basic education and health care because of past policy choices. He said resources are limited, but countries should also think about old age when they decide how to invest.

The index was developed with the UN Fund for Population and Development. It covers 91 countries and 89% of the world’s older people. The report comes during a big change in population: by 2050, there are expected to be two billion people aged 60 and over. That will be more than one fifth of the world’s population.

Population ageing means older people make up a bigger share of the population. This is happening fastest in developing countries. More than two-thirds of older people live in poor countries. By 2050, this is expected to be about four-fifths. In France, it took 115 years for the older population to double from 7% to 14% (from 1865 to 1980). In Brazil, the same change is likely to happen from 2011 to 2032, in only 21 years.

The index shows that the fastest ageing countries—Jordan, Laos, Mongolia, Nicaragua, and Vietnam—are in the lower half of the ranking. In these countries, the number of older people is predicted to more than triple by 2050. This suggests policymakers need to deal with ageing directly so they can support their people.

There are also differences between men and women. Women usually live longer than men. In 2012, for every 84 men aged 60 and over, there were 100 women. Many women are at a disadvantage in old age because they have less paid work (so less savings), less power in family decisions, and more risk of violence.

The report says that if the right steps are taken, an ageing population does not always mean much higher health care spending. It says long-term investment in education and health care is important. Bolivia is ranked 46, even though it is one of the poorest countries. Bolivia has a national plan on ageing, free health care, and a non-contributory universal pension. Nepal is ranked 77. Nepal started a basic pension in 1995 for people over 70 who have no other pension income. The pension is limited in value and eligibility, and coverage is uneven, but it shows how a poor country can begin to address poverty in old age.

Good basic health care, introduced decades ago in Chile and Costa Rica, has helped their ageing populations. A good education system also helps later in life. Basic literacy is important for older people when they deal with pensions bureaucracy. In the Philippines, older people benefited from education reforms after independence in 1946, which made elementary and high school education compulsory. The same is true for Armenia, which, like other former Soviet Union countries, benefited from a strong education system. South Korea is a surprisingly low 67 on the ageing index. It did worse than similar countries by GDP per head, partly because it started a pension only recently.

The ageing index uses 13 indicators in four areas: income security, health care, employment and education, and an enabling environment. All indicators have equal weight, except pension income coverage, life expectancy at 60, healthy life expectancy at 60, and psychological well-being. These were given more weight because the data quality was better. Countries were included only when there was enough data.

Professor Sir Richard Jolly, who created the human development index, said this new index helps people understand the needs and opportunities of older people in a wider way. He said it challenges countries around the world to aim higher and see what is possible.

text:
Wales will be the first country in the UK to use a new organ donation rule. People will be treated as if they agree to donate their organs when they die, unless they choose to opt out.

The Welsh Assembly voted for this opt-out policy. This means hospitals can assume a person wanted to donate, unless the person had officially registered an objection.

The final vote on the bill passed by 43 votes to eight, with two people not voting. Some religious groups objected for moral reasons. Others worried it could upset families who are grieving.

Welsh Health Minister Mark Drakeford said, “This is a huge day for Wales, for devolution and, most importantly, for the 226 people in Wales waiting for an organ transplant.”

He said he is proud Wales will be the first nation in the UK to do this. He said it could increase organ donation and give hope to people waiting for transplants.

He said families often refuse donation, and the main reason is that they do not know what their loved one wanted.

He said the family of a possible donor has an important role. The bill aims to respect the wishes of the person who died. Relatives or friends may object if they believe the person would not have wanted to donate.

He said when families know the person wanted to donate, they usually agree. He said the new law should make people’s wishes clearer and increase agreement to donate.

He said this is a landmark day for Wales, and he expects the rest of the UK to watch closely when the law starts in 2015.

The issue is controversial. Opponents worry that the need for more kidneys and hearts could lead to the wishes of the dead person and their family being ignored. Ministers say there will be safeguards.

Some people may not get around to registering that they do not want to donate. Because of these concerns, the government said families would have a bigger role.

Relatives will have a “clear right of objection.” This gives them a chance to show that the person who died would not have wanted to be an organ donor.

Wales is doing this because there are not enough organs. Drakeford said there is a long-term problem of too few organs for people who need them. He said about one person every week dies in Wales while waiting.

He said about one third of people in Wales are on the organ donor register. But surveys show well over two thirds say they are happy to be donors. He said the other third often do not get around to signing up.

The new law would apply to anyone over 18 who lived in Wales for at least the year before their death. Donated organs would go not only to people in Wales, but to anyone in the UK.

Doctors are pleased. The British Medical Association has campaigned for an opt-out system for a long time. It is worried about the growing number of people needing transplants, which is linked to progress in transplantation.

The number of young donors fell a lot when seatbelt laws started. In recent years, people have worked hard to increase the number who carry an organ donation card, with good success. Hospitals also improved how they organize transplants, including important talks with relatives when the dead person’s wishes are not known. But there are still not enough organs.

Some religious groups strongly oppose the plan. They say it could cause more distress to families. Members of the Muslim Council of Wales and the South Wales Jewish Representative Council have concerns. The Archbishop of Wales, Barry Morgan, said donation should be “a gift of love, of generosity.” He said that if organs can be taken unless someone clearly registered an objection, it is not an expression of love, but more a medical use of a body.

text:
In 2005, Pope Benedict XVI said he came as “a simple, humble worker in God’s vineyard”. On a grey, cold, windy Monday in February, he ended his time as Pope in a similar way. He was like an old worker who could not ignore his back pain and could not rely on his strength anymore.

Because he was a very traditional pope, he gave his message in Latin. He was the first German pope in modern times, and he gave the exact time he would leave. He told the cardinals in the Vatican: “From 28 February 2013, at 20.00 hours”, the seat of Rome, the seat of Saint Peter, would be empty, and the people in charge would need to call a conclave to choose a new pope.

A Mexican church leader, Monsignor Oscar Sanchéz Barba from Guadalajara, was there. He was in Rome to hear the date for a canonization that he helped with. He said they were all in the Sala del Concistoro on the third loggia of the Apostolic Palace. After the Pope gave the canonization date, 12 May, he took a sheet of paper and read from it.

Sanchéz Barba said everyone was left speechless. The cardinals looked at each other. They could not believe the man they saw as God’s representative on earth was giving up the job.

Angelo Sodano, the Dean of the College of Cardinals, spoke next, and he seemed to have known before. He said they had listened with confusion and could hardly believe it. He then promised the Pope the cardinals’ loyalty. At the end, the Pope blessed them and left. Sanchéz Barba said it was very simple. Then everyone left in silence. There was complete silence and sadness.

John Thavis, who reported on the Holy See for 30 years, said he had a feeling the Pope might resign. He planned his return to Rome from the US because of this, and another Vatican watcher confirmed it. Thavis said that in a long interview from 2010, published as Light of the World, Benedict had said it would be right to leave if he felt he could not do the job anymore. Thavis tried to guess when Benedict would resign. He thought 22 February, the Feast of the Chair of St Peter, was most likely, but he was wrong.

Soon after the announcement, the Vatican said the Pope’s choice was brave. They said Benedict was facing reality: modern medicine can keep people alive long after they are strong enough to lead a huge worldwide organization. Thavis agreed. He said it was courageous because Benedict was leaving even though he was not sick, and because he was tired, not because he was seriously ill. But some people wondered if there was more to the story, and if the Pope knew more about his health than the Vatican had said.

Benedict explained that he thought about both his body and his mind. He said that to lead the “bark of Saint Peter” and preach the Gospel, a person needs strength of mind and body, and his strength had gotten worse in recent months. He said he had to admit he could not do the job well.

More ideas and rumors were expected around the Vatican in the coming days and weeks, like after the death of Pope John Paul I in 1978, only 33 days after he was chosen. Some people were already guessing that something might soon be revealed about Benedict’s past. The Vatican would likely reject these ideas. Still, the writer says it is understandable, because what Benedict did was extremely important.

Outside St Peter’s Basilica, Julia Rochester from London, who called herself a lapsed Catholic, was still thinking about what the resignation meant. She asked, “If you’re God’s chosen one, how do you choose not be chosen?” Many practicing Catholics would ask their priests the same question. In his first speech as Pope, Benedict had said he was not fully fit for the task, but that God could work even with “insufficient tools”. Over the last eight years, it seems he stopped believing that.

text:
Prince Harry has left Afghanistan after a four-month tour. During this time, he said he had killed insurgents while flying an Apache helicopter. He also talked openly about how hard it is to be a royal who wants a more private life.

He said he does not like or trust some parts of the media. He also said his father often told him to act more like a member of the royal family.

Prince Harry was a commander of an attack helicopter. He said he fired at the Taliban during missions to help troops on the ground and to rescue injured Afghan and NATO people. He said he was doing his job. He also said the helicopter often worked as a warning to stop attacks.

While he was based at Camp Bastion in Helmand Province, he gave interviews about having different roles in life. In the army he was known as Captain Wales. He said he had “three mes”: one in the army, one in his private social life, and one with his family. He said he can switch between them when needed.

He said he sometimes disappointed himself with “laddish” behaviour. He said this was “probably being too much army and not enough prince.” But he also said he should have privacy.

He strongly criticized some newspapers, especially the Sun, the Daily Mail, and the Daily Telegraph. He said he was annoyed by stories that compared his job as an Apache co-pilot gunner to Spitfire crews waiting to fly in the second world war. He said it was not like that.

He said he became suspicious of the media because of how his family was treated when he was very small. He said he still reads stories about himself, but they upset and anger him. He said his father tells him not to read them, and others say the stories are “always rubbish.”

Prince Harry was sent to Afghanistan last September to command a £45m Apache helicopter. During his tour, Apaches supported NATO troops fighting the Taliban. They also flew with British Chinook and US Black Hawk medical helicopters during casualty evacuations.

Four years ago, he had to leave Afghanistan during his first tour after a media embargo was broken by mistake by an Australian magazine. This time, the Ministry of Defence told the public about his deployment, with the understanding that the media would not report on his daily life there. Two-man crews from the BBC, Sky, and ITN each visited once. A photographer and a reporter from the Press Association were embedded on all three visits.

When asked if he felt more comfortable as Captain Wales than Prince Harry, he said yes. He said his father often reminds him who he is, but in the army it is easy to forget because everyone wears the same uniform and does the same work. He said he gets on well with the other soldiers and enjoys his job.

Before he went to Afghanistan, there was another media storm after pictures appeared of him naked at a private party in Las Vegas. Harry said he let himself, his family, and others down. But he said he was in a private area and should have expected some privacy. He said it was another example of being “too much army, and not enough prince.” He also said he did not think the way the papers treated him was acceptable, because they already knew he was going to Afghanistan. He said some people remind him to “Remember who you are” and not to drop his guard.

When asked why he and his brother like helicopters, he said it might be because only a few people can fit in one, so no one can follow them “like you guys.”
