text:
Scientists have connected the brains of two animals so they could share sensory information. The researchers say this is a big step toward what they call the world’s first “organic computer.”

A team in the US put devices called brain-to-brain interfaces on two rats. These devices let the rats work together on simple tasks to earn rewards, like a drink of water. In one unusual test, the scientists used the internet to connect two rats that were thousands of miles apart. One rat was at Duke University in Durham, North Carolina. The other rat was in Natal, Brazil.

The work was led by Miguel Nicolelis. He is known for devices that help paralyzed people control computers and robotic arms with their thoughts. The researchers say their new work may let many brains be connected to share information. Nicolelis said, “These experiments showed that we have established a sophisticated, direct communication linkage between brains.” He also said, “Basically, we are creating what I call an organic computer.”

The scientists showed that rats can share and use each other’s sensory information. They connected the rats’ brains with tiny grids of electrodes that reached into the motor cortex, the part of the brain that controls movement.

The rats learned to press a lever when a light turned on above it. If they did it right, they got a drink of water. To test brain sharing, the rats were put into two separate compartments. Only one compartment had a light above the lever. When that rat pressed the lever, an electronic version of its brain activity was sent straight to the other rat’s brain. In the tests, the second rat pressed the lever correctly 70% of the time.

The communication was two-way. If the second rat failed, the first rat did not get water. The first rat seemed to change its behavior to help its partner do better.

In another task, the rats worked together on a job where they had to tell the difference between narrow and wide openings using their whiskers.

In the final test, the scientists connected rats on different continents and sent their brain activity back and forth over the internet. Miguel Pais-Vieira, the first author of the study, said that even with noisy signals and delays, the rats could still communicate. He said this suggests it may be possible to make a network of animal brains in many different places.

Nicolelis said the team is now working on ways to connect several animals’ brains at the same time to solve harder tasks. He said they cannot predict what new effects might appear when animals interact as part of a “brain-net.” He said that in theory, a group of brains could find solutions that one brain alone cannot.

The research is published in the journal Scientific Reports.

Anders Sandberg studies the ethics of neurotechnologies at the Future of Humanity Institute at Oxford University. He said the work was “very important” for understanding how brains store information. But he said the possible future uses of the technology could be much wider.

Sandberg said humans do well because we are good at communicating and coordinating. Without that, even though we are smart animals, we would not dominate the planet.

He also said he does not think this will create supersmart rats. He said there is a big difference between sharing sensory information and being able to plan. He said he is not worried about an invasion of “rat multiborgs.”

Very little is known about how thoughts are stored in the brain and how they could be sent into another person’s brain. So this is not realistic any time soon. Sandberg said much of what is in our minds is a “draft” of what we might do. He said we often do not want to share these drafts because it would be embarrassing and confusing, and we often change them before we act. He said most of the time we would be glad not to be inside someone else’s head.

text:
At a special press conference at Leicester University, experts shared new details about Richard III. They did not only talk about finding his body in a car park. They also described his terrible last moments and the humiliating way his naked body was treated after he died.

People cheered when Richard Buckley, the lead archaeologist, said the team was sure “beyond reasonable doubt” that they had found Richard III, the last Plantagenet king. His spine was badly bent by scoliosis, and his body had been pushed to fit into a quickly dug grave in Grey Friars church. The hole was a little too small.

Many kinds of experts explained their evidence. Scientists did DNA tests. Others used computer images to look closely at the bones. Genealogists found a far-off descendant with matching DNA. Academics read old writings about the king’s death and burial.

Philippa Langley from the Richard III Society said, “What a morning. What a story.” She had pushed the project for years, even when many people did not believe it. She also helped find money from around the world when funds were almost gone.

Work has begun on a new tomb in Leicester Cathedral, only 100 yards from where the body was found. A ceremony will place him in the new grave there, probably next year. Leicester’s Museums’ Service is planning a new visitor centre in an old school building looking over the site.

Richard died at Bosworth on 22 August 1485. He was the last English king to die in battle. The researchers showed a picture of the base of his skull cut by one powerful blow. They think it was from a halberd, a medieval weapon with a sharp iron axe blade weighing about two kilos on a wooden pole. It was swung very close to him. The blade likely went several centimetres into his brain. Bone expert Jo Appleby said he would have passed out at once and died soon after.

This injury supports old reports that he died fighting closely in the middle of the battle and after being knocked off his horse. Shakespeare later wrote the line: “A horse! A horse! My kingdom for a horse!” Another sword cut also went through the bone into the brain and could have killed him. But many other wounds happened after he died. This suggests people carried out a cruel ritual. His body was stripped, mocked, and cut as it was brought back to Leicester.

One very bad injury was a stab through the right buttock into the pelvis. It happened after death, because armour would have protected him in battle. This supports the story that his naked body was carried back over a horse and abused. Bob Savage, a medieval arms expert from the Royal Armouries, said this wound was likely made by a sharp knife or dagger like a workman might carry, not a war weapon.

Michael Ibsen, a furniture maker born in Canada, was shown to be a descendant of Richard’s sister. He heard the confirmation on Sunday and listened quietly. He said, “My head is no clearer now than when I first heard the news.” He also said many hundreds of people died on that field. Richard was a king, but one of the dead. Times were very violent, and deaths would not have been pretty or quick.

Mathew Morris first found the body in the first hour of the first day of digging. He did not think it was the king. A machine was still removing the tarmac from the council car park. Local historians and the Richard III Society had studied for years and believed this was the place where Grey Friars church once stood. The priests there had bravely taken the king’s body and buried him in a quickly dug grave, probably still naked, but in a place of honour near the high altar. The leg bones showed through the soil, and they were covered again.

Ten days later, on 5 September, more digging showed Morris had found the right spot. He returned with Lin Foxhall, head of the archaeology department, to dig out the body. Morris said they lifted the arms, legs, and skull first, and then moved slowly to the body. Only when they saw the twisted spine did he think, “My word, I think we’ve got him.”

Langley believes Richard was the true king, the last king of the north, and a brave leader. She thinks he was hurt by strong Tudor propaganda, helped by Shakespeare. There is still the dark story of the princes in the tower. In Richard’s time, people said the children Edward V and his brother Richard were called illegitimate when Richard III took the throne. They were put in the Tower of London and were never seen alive again. It is not certain that bones found at the tower centuries later are theirs, but there may be more DNA work to do.

text:
The Duchess of Cambridge had a baby boy on Monday, 22 July. The baby is third in line to the throne. He is expected to be the 43rd monarch since William the Conqueror took the English crown in 1066.

Kensington Palace announced at 8.30pm that the baby was born at 4.24pm in the Lindo Wing at St Mary’s Hospital in Paddington, West London. The Duke of Cambridge said, “We could not be happier.”

Kensington Palace said the duchess gave birth safely at 4.24pm. The baby weighs 8lbs 6oz. The Duke of Cambridge was there for the birth. The palace said the labour lasted at least ten and a half hours and “progressed as normal.”

The Queen, the Duke of Edinburgh, the Prince of Wales, the Duchess of Cornwall, Prince Harry, and other family members were told. They were happy to hear that the duchess and the baby were doing well.

The couple waited before they made the news public so they could have some private time with the new baby. William called his family, including the Queen, his father Charles, and Prince Harry.

Because of this birth, the monarchy has three generations of heirs to the throne for the first time since 1894.

The baby is the first Prince of Cambridge born in more than 190 years, since Prince George of Cambridge, a grandson of George III and the only son of Prince Adolphus Frederick, the 1st Duke of Cambridge.

Prince Charles said he and his wife were very happy about their first grandchild. He said it was a special moment for William and Catherine, and they looked forward to seeing the baby soon.

The new royal will be called HRH Prince George of Cambridge. When Kate was three months pregnant, the Queen signed a decree that said the children of the eldest son of the Prince of Wales should have the title Royal Highness, and be called Prince or Princess.

A formal notice was put on an ornate rococo-style easel in front of Buckingham Palace shortly before 9pm. It was the same easel used to announce Prince William’s birth in 1982. Soon, the crowd outside grew from hundreds to thousands of local people and tourists.

A New Yorker, Sharon Surloff, was happy with the photo she took of the notice. She said the police told people they had 20 seconds each. She said she arrived at nine in the morning and was glad to be there.

The palace also announced the birth in a press release. Soon after, the medical notice was taken from the hospital to a car by Ed Perkins, Prince William’s press secretary. He gave it to a soldier, who took it to Buckingham Palace with police escort. The notice was signed by Marcus Setchell, the Queen’s gynaecologist.

Prime minister David Cameron congratulated the couple outside 10 Downing Street. He said people in the country and the Commonwealth would celebrate and wish them well. He said it was an important moment for the nation, and also a happy moment for the couple.

Barack Obama and his wife also sent congratulations from the White House. Obama said he and Michelle were happy for the Duke and Duchess of Cambridge and wished them happiness.

The Archbishop of Canterbury, Justin Welby, wrote that he was delighted for them and wished them love, health, and happiness.

Labour leader Ed Miliband also congratulated them and wished them and their son happiness and good health.

The campaign group Republic said the royal birth raised questions about Britain and democratic values. Chief Executive Graham Smith said the baby should be able to grow up without “constant interference and intrusion”. He said the baby’s life, including career, religion, and personal relationships, was already planned, and he said people should think about whether this is the best way to run things.

text:
Felix Baumgartner stood near the edge of space above the deserts of New Mexico. He paused for a moment. He was just one small step from the capsule, but he would fall 24 miles back to Earth. Mission control said, “Our guardian angel will take care of you,” and Felix jumped.

About ten minutes later, the Austrian landed safely. He reached speeds up to 725 mph. He broke three world records. One record was becoming the first person to skydive faster than the speed of sound. He broke the sound barrier at Mach 1.24. People in the control room cheered, “We love you Felix,” and his mother, Ava Baumgartner, cried. Felix raised his arms to thank his team.

He wore a special survival suit. It protected him from big changes in air pressure during the fall. Without the suit, his blood would have boiled and his lungs might have exploded. Later he said, “When I was standing there on top of the world, you become so humble, you don’t think about breaking records.” He said he mostly thought about getting back alive. He also said, “Sometimes you have to go up really high to see how small you are.”

The week before, two attempts were stopped. On Sunday morning, the weather was good, so the mission went ahead. A huge balloon carried him up into clear skies. The balloon was 30 million cubic feet. Its skin was one-tenth the thickness of a sandwich bag. A capsule hung under the balloon, and Felix sat inside it in his suit.

When he reached the right height, he went through a 40-item checklist with his mentor, Joe Kittinger. Kittinger was the previous record holder for the highest altitude manned balloon flight.

There was a problem with a heater for Felix’s visor. The visor could fog up. He told Kittinger, “This is very serious, Joe. Sometimes it’s getting foggy when I exhale. ... I do not feel heat.” But they decided to continue. About 8 million people watched the jump live on YouTube.

The ride up took two and a half hours. During the ride, Felix could see the curve of the Earth, and the sky slowly turned black. Coming down was much faster.

Three cameras on his suit recorded his free fall. He fell for a little over four minutes, but he did not beat the record for the longest free fall. Then his parachute opened.

The mission and the suit may help astronauts in the future. It suggests astronauts might survive a high-altitude disaster by leaving their spacecraft. The space shuttle Columbia had such a disaster in 2003. The top medical doctor for Felix’s jump was Dr Jonathan Clark. His wife, Laurel Clark, died in the Columbia accident. He now works to improve astronauts’ chances in a high-altitude disaster.

Felix is known for dangerous stunts. He used to be a paratrooper. He has jumped off buildings and mountains. He once jumped into a cave that was 600 feet deep. He did two practice free falls for this event: one from 71,000 feet in March and one from 97,000 feet in July 2012. His big jump was above Roswell, a town known for UFO stories.

Felix tried to break five records: first person to break the sound barrier in free fall, highest free-fall jump, highest manned balloon flight, longest free fall, and a jump platform that is believed to be the largest manned balloon in history. The stunt took seven years to plan. Red Bull sponsored it. Felix beat two of Joe Kittinger’s old records. Kittinger, a retired US air force colonel, had jumped from a balloon 19 miles above Earth in 1960. During Felix’s climb, the only voice guiding him on the radio was Kittinger, who was 84.

After the jump, Felix said, “I want to inspire a generation.” He said he hoped that in the next four years he could be like Joe Kittinger, giving advice to a young person who wants to break Felix’s record. He said his most exciting moment was standing outside the capsule “on top of the world.” He also said the best moment was after he landed, when Mike Todd, the life support engineer who helped him put on the suit, came over smiling like a little kid.

Felix said he felt like Todd’s son, and that Todd was very happy he was alive. Todd said, “The world needs a hero right now, and they got one in Felix Baumgartner.” Kittinger joked that he wanted to give a “one-fingered salute” to people who said Felix would come apart when he went supersonic.

Felix said this would be his last jump. He said he will settle down with his girlfriend, Nicole Oetl. He will fly helicopters on rescue missions in the US and Austria.

text:
Valdevaqueros is one of the last clean, natural beaches in southern Spain. The sand is golden, and the sky is full of kites that pull surfers over the waves. There is only a small road to the beach. Many camper vans from Germany, France, Italy, and Britain park there. Windsurfers and kitesurfers come because the wind is strong.

For many years, this beach was very different from the built-up beaches in places like Torremolinos and Marbella. But on 29 May, the local council in Tarifa approved a plan to build a tourist complex next to the beach. It would have 1,400 hotel rooms and 350 flats. Environmental groups say it will hurt the homes of protected animals. Many councillors say the main reason to support it is jobs. Tarifa has 18,000 people, and 2,600 do not have work. Spain is in a very bad economic crisis, and it has raised questions about the future of the euro.

Sebastián Galindo, a councillor from the Socialist Party, said fishing is no longer a good way to earn money because fishing fleets are being taken apart and there are fewer fish. He said tourism is the only way out, as long as it is sustainable. His party is in opposition in Tarifa, but it voted with the governing People’s Party to approve the plan.

Tarifa’s Mayor, Juan Andrés Gil, did not comment. Galindo said the plan follows environmental rules. The buildings would be 800 metres from the coast. The law says buildings must be at least 200 metres from the coast. This law was made to stop more ugly building like the building that damaged much of Spain’s coast in the 1960s and 1970s.

People against the project say Spain already has a million empty homes, so it does not need more flats. Last week the central government suggested a plan to sell homes by giving non-Spaniards residency permits if they buy property worth at least €160,000.

The Socialist opposition in Madrid attacked this idea. Galindo said it is unfair to migrant workers who came to Spain during the boom years, many from Morocco. Morocco is only 14km away and you can see it from Tarifa. He said the plan helps rich people more than the people who came to work.

Surfers worry that new buildings in Valdevaqueros could make the local Levant wind weaker. They also think the place may not attract normal package tourists. Henning Mayer, from Augsburg in Germany, has visited for 20 years. He said it is not really a place for families, and the Levant wind can be very strong. He also said that 10 years ago people talked about building a new highway there, but it did not happen, so he thinks it will be impossible to build new hotels.

Tarifa is at the southernmost tip of Spain. It is an important place between Africa and Europe, where the Mediterranean Sea meets the Atlantic. Campaigners say it is also important for animals that migrate.

A campaign to save the beach started a few hours after the council vote. It has a Facebook page. Groups supporting it include Greenpeace, the World Wide Fund for Nature, and the Spanish branch of Birdlife. Noelia Jurado, who works with multimedia, said the project is like putting a shopping centre in the middle of the Alhambra. She also said the resort would be near the ancient Roman town of Baelo Claudia, and it might be built on top of more Roman ruins.

The Andalusian College of Geographers also opposes the plan. In an early study on its website, it said “free areas,” including car parks, may overlap part of Alcornocales National Park. The geographers also say the site goes into two wildlife conservation areas. One area in Valdevaqueros is home to two bat species that are in danger.

Raúl Romeva, a member of the European Parliament and Vice-President of the Greens group, said money is being put before planning laws and European environmental rules. He also said the area does not have enough water. Tarifa already has water shortages in summer in Andalusía. A lack of water recently made the Andalusía Supreme Court support an appeal against building a different complex in the region, which would have had golf courses, hotels, and luxury homes.

Many local people ask why the resort should be built 10km away, instead of on empty land near Tarifa’s old centre. The old centre has white walls, small winding streets, and a 10th-century Moorish castle. Cristóbal Lobato, who has worked at the same beach bar for 30 years, said he agrees with the complex if it creates jobs for the town, but he is against it only helping a few people. He said if it was built in the centre of Tarifa, visitors could go to shops, tapas bars, and restaurants.

Biologist Aitor Galán, who does environmental impact studies, pointed to one of only two seaside breeding places for vultures in Europe. He said that in other parts of Europe, this place would have very strong protection, but here people want to cover it with buildings. He said they want to turn it into Benidorm, but people come for the wildlife and the wind. He said builders and mayors use the current crisis and unemployment to justify a lot of destruction.

text:
On a normal day, Starbucks shops are busy places. People meet friends, hold business meetings, and join reading groups. Many people go there for a morning coffee. But Starbucks may need to be careful.

A direct action group called UK Uncut plans to take over dozens of Starbucks branches in the UK. They want to turn them into crèches, refuges, and homeless shelters. They want to show what they say are Starbucks’ tax avoidance tactics.

UK Uncut announced this plan on the same day a Starbucks executive was questioned by the House of Commons Public Accounts Committee. The questions were about why Starbucks paid no corporation tax in the UK in the past three years. This happened even though senior US managers said the company’s business in Britain was profitable.

Starbucks’ Chief Financial Officer, Troy Alstead, appeared before the committee. He tried to improve the company’s reputation, which has been hurt by this controversy.

MPs also blamed officials at HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC). They said HMRC had cosy relationships with big businesses. Conservative MP Richard Bacon said about the Starbucks arrangements: “It smells. And it doesn’t smell of coffee – it smells bad.”

UK Uncut says government cuts, especially cuts that affect women, are linked to tax avoidance by big international companies.

UK Uncut activist Sarah Greene said refuges and rape crisis centres could face cuts unless companies paid their fair share of tax. HMRC estimates about £32bn was lost to tax avoidance in 2011.

Greene said the government could get billions to fund important services by clamping down on tax avoidance.

UK Uncut became well known after sit-ins at Vodafone stores, Topshop, and Fortnum & Mason. The group focused on Starbucks last month after a Reuters investigation. Reuters said Starbucks had paid only £8.6m in corporation tax since it started in the UK in 1998, even though total sales were £3bn.

UK Uncut campaigner Anna Walker said: “We’ve chosen to really highlight the impact of the cuts on women. So there is going to be a real focus on transforming Starbucks into those services that are being cut by the government … [such as] refuges and crèches.”

She said Starbucks is a good target because it is on many high streets across the country. She said this matters to UK Uncut because people can take action in their local areas.

Other international organizations have also been criticized for their UK accounts. Amazon, eBay, Facebook, Google, and Ikea have paid little or no corporation tax, even though they have big businesses in Britain.

But pollsters at YouGov’s BrandIndex say Starbucks’ image has been hurt the most.

Starbucks says it pays the correct amount of tax. Chief Executive Howard Schultz said in a statement: “Starbucks has always paid taxes in the UK despite recent suggestions to the contrary.

“Over the last three years alone, our company has paid more than £160m in various taxes, including National Insurance contributions, VAT and business rates.”

Margaret Hodge, who leads the Public Accounts Committee, told parliament last month that Apple, eBay, Facebook, Google, and Starbucks had avoided nearly £900m of tax. Prime Minister David Cameron replied: “I’m not happy with the current situation. I think [HMRC] needs to look at it very carefully. We do need to make sure we are encouraging these businesses to invest in our country as they are, but they should be paying fair taxes as well.”

A Starbucks spokeswoman said tax law can be very complex. She said Starbucks respects and follows tax laws and accounting rules in each of the 61 countries where it does business, including the UK. She said Starbucks is committed to the UK for the long term, and that the company has posted facts about its UK tax practices on its website.

The spokeswoman also said Starbucks helps the UK economy in other ways. She said Starbucks spends hundreds of millions of pounds with local suppliers on milk, cakes, sandwiches, and on store design and renovations. She said that when indirect employment is included, Starbucks’ extended economic impact on the UK economy is more than £80m each year.

She said Starbucks hopes UK Uncut will respect the wellbeing of its employees and customers. She also said Starbucks creates jobs and apprenticeships, and pays its fair share of taxes in the UK.

text:
Dame Sally Davies, the Chief Medical Officer for England, says antibiotic resistance is as dangerous as international terrorism. This may sound too strong, but the danger may actually be even bigger. Every year, many more people die because of bacteria that resist antibiotics than die in terrorist attacks.

It is hard to measure the worldwide harm across all kinds of bacteria. But the World Health Organization says that for tuberculosis alone, multi-drug resistance causes more than 150,000 deaths each year. Antibiotic resistance is not just a future worry. It is happening now.

In the last 100 years, life and health have changed a lot. At the start of the 1900s, life expectancy in the UK was about 47 years for a man and 50 for a woman. This was strongly affected by many babies dying. About one third of all deaths were in children under five, mostly because of infectious disease.

Today, a child born in Britain has a better than one in four chance of reaching 100 years old. We can thank public health systems, vaccination, and antibiotics. Preventing and treating illnesses caused by microorganisms is how we mainly win the fight against disease.

We also keep very sick people alive in intensive care. This is the area where I trained. It is also where antibiotic resistant organisms are most common. In intensive care, doctors often use strong antibiotics to treat life-threatening illness. These drugs kill normal bacteria, but they can leave behind tougher bacteria that learn ways to avoid antibiotics.

When I was a new doctor in the late 1990s, I learned about Methicillin Resistant Staphylococcus Aureus (MRSA). This bacterium resists methicillin and all other penicillins. To fight it, we used a few drugs like vancomycin and teicoplanin. These were meant to be our last lines of defence. But resistant bacteria became more common, and new kinds of resistance appeared. Drugs we hardly knew before became normal. New last-line drugs replaced the old ones. We got used to this rising "arms race" between people and bacteria.

But the situation has been changing. In hospitals and GP surgeries, we have used antibiotics too often and without enough care. Some of the worst misuse has happened outside healthcare too. Antibiotics have entered the food chain through agriculture, such as adding anti-bacterial drugs to livestock feed. We acted as if antibiotics would work forever. We also trusted that the pharmaceutical industry would always stay ahead.

Now that is not happening. New, more resistant bacteria have been found. Vancomycin, which we used to treat MRSA, stopped working in some cases. Vancomycin Resistant Staphylococcus Aureus (VRSA) appeared in hospitals. Other bacteria learned similar tricks. Enterobacteria, usually found in the gut, also became resistant to vancomycin. Today, infections with very resistant organisms are common, and the pharmaceutical industry is not keeping up. Fewer new anti-microbial drugs are being made. It is getting harder to develop drugs that work against resistant strains.

When drug companies invent a new way to attack, bacteria quickly evolve a defence. The simple ideas have already been used up. Antibiotics are expensive to develop, used only for short courses, and can stop working as resistance grows. Because of this, drug companies have less reason to make new antibiotics.

Soon after antibiotics became widely used in the 1940s, the first signs of resistance appeared. At first, this seemed like a small and unusual issue. The numbers were too small to seem important. But today, resistance is a normal part of medicine. Less than a century after penicillin was discovered, we are starting to lose the fight.

After the first MRSA deaths in otherwise healthy children in the US in 1998, yearly deaths from MRSA infection in the US rose to tens of thousands. This became more than the number of deaths caused by AIDS. Resistance in hospitals is everywhere. This fight is like no other.

We need a big change in how we prescribe antibiotics. We also need to use antibiotics more carefully in farming and agriculture. And drug companies must somehow be encouraged to develop these drugs, even though they make less profit.

During my working life, antibiotic resistance in healthcare has changed from rare to a very large problem. If we want to avoid going back to the time before antibiotics, when many more people died, we must act boldly. Losing the advantage we have only recently gained against microorganisms would be unthinkable.

text:
Swedish prisons have been known around the world for being modern and fair. But some people ask: are Sweden’s prisons too easy?

In November 2013, Nils Oberg, the head of Sweden’s prison and probation service, said four prisons would close. This was because the number of prisoners had dropped in an “out of the ordinary” way.

Crime rates had not fallen. But from 2011 to 2012, Sweden’s prisoner number fell by 6%. It was now a little over 4,500 people. A similar drop was expected in 2013 and 2014. Oberg said he was surprised, but he hoped it was because prisons were helping people change. He said they hoped their work in rehabilitation and stopping people from committing crime again had helped.

Kenneth Gustafsson runs Kumla Prison, Sweden’s most secure prison. It is about 130 miles west of Stockholm. He said Sweden’s prison service is very different from when he started as a young officer in 1978. But he does not think it has become soft. He said prisoners were treated well, maybe too well, some people said. After some famous escapes in 2004, Sweden put more focus on security.

Even with more focus on security, Sweden still tries to be humane. Prison sentences rarely go over ten years. People with life sentences can ask the courts after ten years to change the sentence to a fixed time, usually about 18 to 25 years. Sweden was the first country in Europe to use electronic tags for convicted criminals. Sweden also tries to avoid short prison sentences when possible. It uses community measures instead, because they have been shown to reduce reoffending better.

In Sweden, about 30 to 40% of people offend again within three years. This is about half the UK rate. One possible reason is the age of criminal responsibility in Sweden is 15. In the UK, children aged 10 to 17 and young people under 21 have the highest reoffending rates. Many of them later go to adult prisons. In Sweden, no one under 21 can get a life sentence. Sweden also tries hard to keep as many young offenders as possible out of prison.

Another reason prisoner numbers may be dropping is strong support after prison. A government probation service supervises people on probation. It also must provide treatment programmes for people with drug, alcohol, or violence problems. About 4,500 volunteer supervisors from the public also help by supporting offenders.

Gustafsson said that in 2013 and 2014, the main focus would be young offenders and men convicted of violent behaviour. Sweden has had programmes for drug addiction for many years. Now it is also developing programmes to deal with aggression and violence. He said this matters for society when people are released.

The writer spoke to a former prisoner, Peter Soderlund, who runs a social enterprise called X-Cons Sweden. Soderlund served almost three years of a four-year sentence for drug and weapons offences, and was released in 1998. He got help from a new group called Kris (Criminals’ Return Into Society), which was run by former prisoners.

Soderlund said a big difference between Kris and X-Cons is that X-Cons lets people join even if they are still taking addiction medicines. Both groups want to help prisoners return to society after release. Soderlund said he was treated well at Osteraker Prison, where he served his sentence, because the governor was “enlightened.” But he said not all prisons were like that. He met many people who needed help. X-Cons meets people when they leave prison, helps them find accommodation, and gives them a support network.

Gustafsson said Sweden believes strongly in rehabilitation, but not in a naive way. He said some people will not or cannot change. But he believes most prisoners want to change, and prisons should help them. He also said change does not always happen in one prison sentence.

He said rehabilitation is not only done in prison. It is often a process that includes probation and wider society. Prisons can give education and training, but when people leave prison they also need housing and jobs.

text:
It is hard to know exactly where the noise comes from, but everyone in Damascus can hear it. Day and night there are dull booms from artillery, rockets, or planes hitting rebel areas. The war is coming closer to Syria’s capital. More than two years into the Syrian crisis, many people in Damascus have gotten used to hearing death and destruction nearby.

Over the weekend, men smoked water pipes in a park with palm trees. Children played near flowerbeds, and couples talked on benches. Loud explosions could be heard a few miles away. Smoke rose near the minarets of an old Ottoman-era mosque. No one seemed to notice.

“Actually you do get used to it after a while,” said George, an IT technician from a village on the coast. “But you never know exactly what they are hitting.” People often learn later from videos posted by opposition media outlets on YouTube.

The sounds are even more upsetting because the government tries to act like life is normal. An army officer said, “As you can see, everything here is fine but we have to hit the terrorists, these extremists.” An official, whose way home has been attacked by rebels in Daraya, said, “If I was afraid, I would just shut my door and stay inside. I have to work and I am not afraid. If I don’t defend my country, who will?”

In private, ordinary people speak differently. A shopkeeper in the center of town said sadly that his baby daughter cries when she hears shelling. Zeina, a student in her twenties, worries she is getting used to suffering, and maybe to danger too. “In the beginning, when there started to be explosions, I used to have nightmares,” she said. “Now I can sleep through anything.”

The danger is also getting closer to home. In Sabaa Bahrat Square, in what was supposed to be the safest part of Damascus, a car bomb exploded. It left blackened concrete, broken windows, twisted metal, and damage to the Syrian Central Bank building next door.

Notices of mourning for two of the 15 victims, Muhammad al-Sufi and Manal al-Tahan, are stuck to a wall nearby. Untidy militiamen with machine guns stand around the square. The square is often used for televised pro-regime rallies, where civil servants are brought in large groups to chant slogans under huge banners of President Bashar al-Assad.

That bombing was not the worst Damascus has seen as things have gotten worse. In February, 80 people, including schoolchildren, reportedly died near the ruling Ba’ath Party headquarters in Mazraa. The crater can still be seen, marked by a large patch of new asphalt on the main road going north. “I live nearby but luckily I wasn’t there,” said Munir, a university lecturer.

Mortar bombs, fired from rebel-held areas now close enough to reach the city easily, have become a frightening new danger. These bombs killed 15 students in a university cafeteria on 28 March. The target was thought to be a government building.

Security has become stronger since the bombing of the national security crisis cell in July 2012, when four of Assad’s most senior aides were killed. Concrete blast barriers, often painted black, red, and white like the Syrian flag, now protect many official places, not only military sites. The Iranian Embassy in Mezze, with a turquoise mosaic front, now looks like a fortress. A foreign diplomat said, “The regime did manage to set up a ring of steel round Damascus. But for whatever reason the perimeter is starting to be punctured and that brings home the reality of the war.”

Because of this, moving around the city is hard, uncertain, and slow. Checkpoints on main roads make traffic stop for ID checks and bag searches. Soldiers use handheld explosive detectors to try to stop future bombers. Only drivers with official security clearance can use special fast lanes to avoid waiting.

Many people are thinking about one big question: will there be a battle for Damascus, like the one that has badly damaged Aleppo? Parts of Damascus already feel like a war zone. The most expensive hotel is strangely empty, even though international agencies use many rooms as offices because of the growing crisis. Blue helmets and flak jackets are piled on Persian carpets in a decorated reception room. White UN vehicles are parked behind blast barriers outside. The streets become empty soon after 9pm.

Some people think the fight for the capital is coming, but not yet, maybe in the summer, after rebels strengthen their gains in the south. Others think neither side will win completely and hope for a political solution pushed from abroad. But few people in Damascus expect things to get better soon.

text:
At Addis Ababa airport, visitors see pictures of golden grain, tiny red-brown seeds, and men around a very large pancake. Signs say: “Teff: the ultimate gluten-free crop!”

Ethiopia is one of the poorest countries in the world, and many people worry about having enough food. But Ethiopia is also the home of teff. Teff is a very healthy old grain. More and more, it is being sold in health-food shops and supermarkets in Europe and America.

Teff seeds are tiny, about the size of poppy seeds. They have a lot of calcium, iron, and protein, and many amino acids. Teff is naturally gluten free. People can use it instead of wheat flour to make bread, pasta, waffles, and pizza bases. Like quinoa, teff’s strong nutrition could bring new markets in the west.

In Ethiopia, teff is very important. About 6.3 million farmers grow it. Teff fields cover more than 20% of all farm land. People grind it into flour and make injera, a soft, fermented flatbread that is a basic food in Ethiopia. Teff is also used in many religious and cultural events. In Ethiopia and nearby Eritrea, people eat together around large injera. They use it to pick up stews, and they sometimes feed each other to show friendship or loyalty. This tradition is called gursha.

For many years, outside diaspora communities in the west, most people did not notice teff. Now, more people want traditional crops, and health-food and gluten-free markets are growing. Some people call teff Ethiopia’s “second gift to the world”, after coffee.

Sophie Kebede is a London entrepreneur with a UK company that focuses on teff. She says she was very surprised when she learned how nutritious it is. She says she is Ethiopian and has eaten injera her whole life.

But higher demand for “ancient grains” does not always help poor communities. In Bolivia and Peru, the global quinoa trade has raised incomes for some people. But there have also been reports of malnutrition and fights over land, because farmers sell all their crop to meet western demand.

In Ethiopia, the growing middle class also wants more teff. Prices inside the country have gone up, and the poorest people cannot always afford it. Today, many small farmers sell most of what they grow to city buyers.

This may raise income in some rural areas, but it can also hurt nutrition, the government says. Teff is the most nutritious grain in the country. Estimates say people in cities eat up to 61kg of teff each year, but people in rural areas eat about 20kg. Rich people mostly eat the more expensive magna and white teff. People with less money often eat red and mixed teff, which are less valuable. More than half also mix teff with cheaper grains like sorghum and maize.

The Ethiopian government wants to double teff production by 2015. A 2013 plan says teff could help school meals and emergency aid, and could reduce malnutrition, especially for children and teenagers.

Ethiopia’s economy is growing fast, but the country is still on the UN list of least-developed countries. About 20% of children under five are malnourished or have stunted growth.

The government’s Agricultural Transformation Agency wants higher harvests. It plans to create better types of teff, use new planting methods, and use tools to reduce losses after harvest.

Since 2006, the government has blocked exports of raw teff grain. Only injera and other processed teff products can be shipped out. But this could change. The government’s plan says Ethiopia wants enough teff for people at home and also a strong export market.

Mama Fresh is a family company that has sold injera to top restaurants and hotels in Addis Ababa for years. It also ships injera to Finland, Germany, Sweden, and the US, mainly for diaspora communities. Now the company is also looking at the gluten-free market. It plans to double exports to America in 2014 and soon will make teff pizzas, bread, and cookies.

David Hallam, a director at the UN Food and Agriculture Organization, says these products pass through many hands before they reach stores like Sainsbury’s. Each step takes some profit, and small farmers may not be able to bargain well with big traders. He says quinoa is a warning that exports can be good and bad for poor countries.

Regassa Feyissa, an Ethiopian agricultural scientist and a former head of the National Institute for Biodiversity, warns that if teff exports grow without careful planning, farmers may stop growing other important crops. He also warns that attempts to raise production could help businesses more than small farmers.

Because there is little Ethiopian teff on the world market, farmers in the US have started growing it. Farmers in Europe, Israel, and Australia have also tried it.

Kebede says she buys teff from farms in southern Europe, but she would rather buy it from Ethiopia. She says teff is natural for Ethiopians, so Ethiopian farmers should be the best suppliers. She asks why Ethiopian farmers cannot benefit from this popular grain.

text:
Margaret Thatcher has died. She was one of the strongest British prime ministers since Winston Churchill in 1940. She supported free market economics. The British government said she would have a ceremonial funeral with military honours at St Paul’s Cathedral.

Prime minister David Cameron said he was very sad. He said Britain had lost a great leader and that she was the first woman prime minister. He said she led the country and that many people would remember her as a great peacetime prime minister.

President Barack Obama said many Americans would remember her working closely with President Ronald Reagan. He said she showed that people can shape history with strong beliefs and courage.

Lady Thatcher was the first woman elected to lead a major western state. She served 11 years in a row at No 10 Downing Street, the longest time since 1827.

Thatcher was 87. For some years she had poor health and dementia. After several small strokes in 2002, she stopped going to public events.

Many people from different political parties paid tribute to her.

The Labour Party said it would stop campaigning in local elections for a short time as a sign of respect. Labour leader Ed Miliband said she was a special person who changed politics for a whole generation. He said Labour disagreed with many of her actions and she was controversial, but they could still respect her achievements and personal strength.

Deputy prime minister Nick Clegg said she was one of the most important people in modern British politics. He said she made a lasting mark on the country.

Former Conservative prime minister Sir John Major said she showed courage and determination in politics, and kindness in private life.

Thatcher was called the “Iron Lady.” She was a key ally of US president Ronald Reagan during the cold war. The Soviet Union later broke up during changes led by Mikhail Gorbachev. Thatcher said she could “do business” with Gorbachev. Many people in former Soviet bloc countries saw her as a strong supporter of freedom. Many people in the US also shared this view, but not everyone in Britain or among key EU partners agreed.

Thatcher was a middle-level Conservative politician until she became party leader in 1975. Within 10 years, people around the world knew her. Some admired her and some hated her. She supported market reforms at home and took a hard line in foreign policy. She also fought the IRA. The IRA almost killed her in 1984 with a bomb at the Grand Hotel in Brighton.

In Britain, “Thatcherism” meant selling state-owned industries to private owners. The word “privatization” became widely used in many countries. It also meant defeating powerful trade unions, especially the National Union of Miners. The miners’ strike in 1984–85 lasted a year and was very bitter.

Money from Britain’s North Sea oil fields gave Thatcher more freedom to change the economy. She used this chance to fight her opponents, including some moderate people in her own party.

She also made strong verbal attacks in the European Union to get a British rebate, which she called “my money.” She was less successful in stopping the European Commission from gaining more power. She called it the “Belgian Empire.” This was especially true when the Commission was led by Jacques Delors.

Some tabloid newspapers supported her strongly. Later, the British economy improved after a severe recession. Her strict economic policy was meant to control unions and reduce inflation. For a short time, she seemed impossible to beat.

But when she had more power and fewer allies to restrain her, she made mistakes and became less popular. Sir Geoffrey Howe, her deputy in name, resigned after a major disagreement, mainly about Europe. His resignation speech led to a challenge for her leadership. Thatcher stepped aside for John Major instead of risking losing in a vote.

After she retired, she wrote very successful memoirs in two volumes. She also worked hard for the Thatcher Foundation, which tried to spread her values around the world.

text:
The International Monetary Fund (IMF) asked Washington to fix its money problems. This came after the United States stepped back from a possible debt default. It also came after hundreds of thousands of federal workers went back to work after a 16-day government shutdown.

US President Barack Obama said, “We’ve got to get out of the habit of governing by crisis.” The IMF’s leader, Christine Lagarde, also asked for more stability.

Lagarde said it is important to lower uncertainty about government spending and taxes. She said the US should raise the debt limit in a longer-lasting way.

She also said the US should pass a budget for 2014. She said the US should replace the sequester with slower, step-by-step changes that would not hurt the economic recovery. She also said the US should make a balanced, complete plan for the next several years.

A deal written in the Senate was passed by the House of Representatives just hours before the deadline to extend the debt limit. The deal gave almost no concessions to conservatives who had pushed the country close to a new financial crisis.

The World Bank said it was relieved that the world economy had “dodged a potential catastrophe.” Its president, Jim Yong Kim, asked leaders in all countries to keep making and using policies that help economic growth and create jobs and opportunity for everyone.

Stock markets in Japan, China, Hong Kong, and South Korea first showed relief after Republicans gave up on their failed effort to weaken Obama’s healthcare reforms. But overall, markets in Asia and Europe reacted only a little. Traders seemed to expect another fight in Washington early in 2014.

Standard & Poor’s estimated the shutdown cost the US $24bn. China’s official Xinhua News Agency said Washington was putting other countries’ dollar assets at risk. China is the US government’s largest creditor.

Obama signed the needed law shortly after midnight on Thursday. This happened after Republicans were split in the House. The bill passed easily in the Senate with support from both parties, after Democratic and Republican leaders made the agreement. The bill was only a temporary fix. It funded the government until 15 January and raised the debt ceiling until 7 February.

But Obama said he did not expect another harsh budget fight and shutdown in 2014.

Before the House vote, Obama said he hoped the deal would “lift the cloud of uncertainty” over the country. He said, “Once this agreement arrives on my desk, I will sign it immediately.” He also said he hoped next time would not be at the last minute, and repeated, “We’ve got to get out of the habit of governing by crisis.”

After a Wednesday night press briefing, a journalist asked Obama if the crisis would happen again in a few months. Obama answered, “No.”

Earlier, Republican senator Mike Lee spoke in a strong, challenging way, which may mean more trouble later. He said the media asked, “Was it worth it?” He answered that it is always worth it to do the right thing. He also said, “This is not over.”

Still, the deal was one of the worst possible results for Republicans. They did not reach any of their goals, and polls showed voters mostly blamed them for the crisis.

text:
A new World Health Organization (WHO) study says that more than one third of women worldwide (35.6%) will face physical or sexual violence during their lives. It is usually done by a male partner. The report says 30% of women are attacked by a partner. It also says that 38% of murders of women are done by an intimate partner.

Dr Margaret Chan, director general of the WHO, said this shows that violence against women is a global health problem like an epidemic. She said health systems can and must do more to help women who experience violence.

The highest levels of violence against women are in Africa. There, nearly half of women (45.6%) will face physical or sexual violence. In low- and middle-income Europe, the number is 27.2%. In high-income countries, about one third of women (32.7%) will experience violence at some time in their lives.

Among women who suffer violence, 42% get injuries. This may bring them to doctors and nurses. The report says this is often the first time the violence at home is noticed and the woman can be offered help. Violence can badly harm women’s health. Some women come to hospital with broken bones. Others have problems during pregnancy and mental illness.

The WHO made two reports: one about how common violence is, and another with guidelines for healthcare staff on how to help women. The work was done by Dr Claudia Garcia-Moreno at WHO and Professor Charlotte Watts from the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine.

Garcia-Moreno said that, for the first time, they compared data from all over the world about partner violence and sexual violence by non-partners, and how this violence affects health. The health effects include HIV and other sexually transmitted infections, depression, women turning to alcohol, unwanted pregnancies, and low-birthweight babies.

Rates of violence were different in different regions, but Garcia-Moreno said the level was unacceptably high everywhere. Data from 81 countries shows that even in high-income countries, 23.2% of women will face physical and/or sexual violence from a partner in their lives. The global figure for women attacked by partners was 30%.

The report says that in high-income countries, more sexual assaults and rapes by acquaintances or strangers are reported than in other places. It says 12.6% of women in wealthy countries will be sexually attacked by a non-partner in their lives. This is higher than the African rate of 11.9%. But the report also says that data on these crimes is not collected well in all regions.

The authors say earlier research shows that women with more education are less likely to suffer violence. Women who have jobs are also less likely to suffer violence, but not in all regions. Watts said societies need to change social norms. She asked what society thinks is acceptable, because in some societies, some kinds of violence against women are seen as OK.

Garcia-Moreno said the numbers should be a wake-up call. She said people have paid more attention to this problem in the past decade, but it is complex. She said there is no vaccine or pill to fix it.

The new WHO clinical and policy guidelines say healthcare staff should be trained to recognize signs of domestic violence and sexual assault. But the guidelines do not support general screening, which means asking every woman at a clinic if she has faced violence.

Garcia-Moreno said that if a woman comes back many times with injuries that are not explained, healthcare staff should ask about domestic violence. She said that when she was in medical school, this was not something students learned. Later, she sometimes felt something else was going on with a woman she was talking to, but did not think about domestic violence. Now she says she would handle the interview very differently.

text:
Angela Erdmann never met her grandfather. He died in 1946, six years before she was born.

On Tuesday 8th April, 2014, she told people about an unusual moment. She received a message in a bottle, 101 years after her grandfather threw it into the Baltic Sea. People think it may be the world’s oldest message in a bottle. A museum in Germany, which is showing it, gave it to her.

Erdmann, 62, said it was a big surprise. She said a man came to her door and said he had mail from her grandfather. He explained that someone had found a message in a bottle, and the name on the card was her grandfather’s name.

The visitor was a genealogical researcher. He found Erdmann in Berlin after the letter was given to the International Maritime Museum in Hamburg.

The bottle was a brown beer bottle. It had been in the water for 101 years. A fisherman named Konrad Fischer found it in his catch in the Baltic Sea near the city of Kiel. Holger von Neuhoff, a curator at the museum, said this was the oldest bottled message he had seen. He said the museum has older documents, but not with the bottle. With both the bottle and the document, this is the oldest right now. He said it is in very good condition.

Researchers think Erdmann’s grandfather, Richard Platz, threw the bottle into the sea in 1913. He was hiking with a nature appreciation group. He was 20 years old then.

Most of the postcard could not be read. But people could read the address in Berlin on the front. They could also read his polite request: he asked the person who found it to send the note to his home address.

Erdmann said he also put two stamps from that time into the bottle, so the finder would not have to pay. She said he did not think it would take 101 years.

She said the message made her feel emotional. She did not know her grandfather because he died at age 54, six years before she was born.

She said she knew very little about him. Later she learned that he was a writer. She said he was open-minded, believed in freedom, and thought everyone should respect each other. She said he did a lot for young people. Later he travelled with his wife and two daughters. She said it was wonderful because she could see where her family roots came from.

Erdmann said she also likes culture and travelling around the world. She said she is open-minded too. She said what her grandfather taught his two daughters, her mother taught her, and she then taught her sons.

Even though she was happy to get the message, she said she hoped other people would not throw bottles with messages into the sea today. She said the sea is already full of bottles and rubbish.

The message and the bottle will be shown at Hamburg’s Maritime Museum until the beginning of May 2014. After that, experts will try to read the rest of the text. It is not clear what will happen to the bottle later, but Erdmann hopes it will stay at the museum.

She said they want to add some photos with the bottle. This way visitors can see the young man who threw the bottle into the water.

text:
In the past, not sleeping much was seen as a good thing. It was a sign you were busy and important. People said that sleep was for weak people. Now things have changed. Arianna Huffington’s book, The Sleep Revolution, is a New York Times best-seller. It is a “call to bed” that says it can change your life “one night at a time”. Huffington tells people to “sleep their way to the top”.

At the same time, the sleep business has grown a lot. Many start-ups are changing where, when, and how we sleep. They also want to change how much money we will pay for sleep. For rich customers, luxury hotels offer “sleep retreats”. For more than $1,000, you get dinner and a movie about sleep. If you stay at home, you can buy new things for your bedroom. You can buy a mattress cover with a sensor that tracks your sleep ($249). You can also buy a sleeping mask that monitors brainwaves and says it helps you nap better ($299).

Sleep is not only a business now. It is also part of work life. Some companies already have nap pods. Huffington says nap rooms in offices will become “as common as conference rooms” in the next two years. So why did this happen? Why did sleep suddenly become fashionable?

For some people, getting enough sleep fits with a lifestyle where paying $10 for green juice and $34 for a SoulCycle class is normal. Also, wearable technology has become popular. People track their bodies with devices and try to improve them. Sleep has become another set of numbers to track. Huffington says sleep does not just rest you; it restores you. She says sleep is the best performance enhancer. Getting eight hours of sleep has become a status symbol.

How does Arianna Huffington get her eight hours? A recent New York Times profile says she has “nine or so” assistants. She calls them her “A-Team”. They run errands, plan travel, and load The Huffington Post on her computer in the morning. The Times says most of them can only do the job for about 12 months because it is so hard. The low pay also means many take second jobs. Basically, they do not sleep so that Huffington can, and can sell books about it.

Getting enough sleep is not only about choosing to go to bed on time. It also depends on where you live and what life is like for you. Many studies show that poor people are more likely to sleep badly. It is hard to sleep if you worry about safety or do not have enough to eat. It is hard to sleep if you are one of the 15 million American shift workers with irregular hours. Research has also found a black/white sleep gap. One study shows white people sleep an average of 6.85 hours, while African Americans sleep an average of 6.05 hours. African Americans also have lower quality sleep. Researchers say this is partly because of the stress of discrimination.

Who gets the most sleep and the best quality sleep in America? Wealthy white women. That is probably the same group Huffington is aiming her book at. She calls her sleep message a “revolution”, but it is really a rebranding. The real sleep crisis is not because a few rich people think sleep is a waste of time. It is because the 99% cannot afford to spend time sleeping.

Sleep is popular right now, but it may not last long. Sleep can improve performance, but it is not efficient. The bigger goal is to find a way to stop the harm from sleep deprivation, so people can work well with less sleep. The military is leading this research. In 2008, the Pentagon published a report called “Human Performance”. It looked at a future where soldiers could do their best with only a couple of hours of sleep. It said: “Suppose a human could be engineered who slept for the same amount of time as a giraffe (1.9 hours per night). This would lead to an approximately twofold decrease in the casualty rate. An adversary would need an approximately 40% increase in the troop level to compensate for this advantage.” The report also looked at the effects of ampakines, a type of drug that changes neurotransmitters in the brain, to remove the effects of sleep deprivation.

The writer says that one day humans will find a way to get rid of sleep. Then spending a third of your life unconscious will not be seen as a luxury. It will be something only poor people are forced to do. At that point, we may need a very different kind of sleep revolution.

text:
Apple’s brand and logo have been named the most valuable in the world. It is worth nearly $119bn. That is more than the whole gross domestic product of Morocco, Ecuador, or Oman.

Apple is already the world’s biggest company. Its stock market value is $591bn. Interbrand’s Best Global Brands report says Apple’s brand value grew by 21% in 12 months.

Apple is known around the world for its simple logo: an apple with a bite missing. In the 2014 report, many technology companies moved up. Older famous brands like Coca-Cola, McDonald’s, and Gillette moved down.

Google’s brand value rose by 15% to $107bn, taking second place. Coca-Cola was next, up 3% to $81.5bn. IBM was worth $72.2bn, and Microsoft was worth $45.5bn.

Facebook had the biggest rise. Its brand value grew by 86% to $14.3bn. It reached 29th place, ahead of Volkswagen, Kellogg’s, and Ford.

Jez Frampton, the chief executive of Interbrand (part of Omnicom), said digital and mobile technology helped brands like Apple grow stronger. He said Google, Amazon, and Facebook have changed what customers expect and have raised the standard for brand experiences.

Apple was founded by former Chief Executive Steve Jobs in his Los Altos garage in 1976. Apple only entered the top ten of Interbrand’s yearly study in 2011.

The Apple logo was created by advertising executive Rob Janoff in 1977. It was designed with a bite taken out so it would not be confused with a cherry. Janoff also described the logo as a symbol with ideas like lust, knowledge, hope, and anarchy.

Graham Hayles, Interbrand’s chief marketing officer, said it makes sense that Apple’s brand could be about one fifth of the company’s total market value. He said Apple makes a lot of money because it has a very strong brand, and that branding and profit are closely linked.

Hayles said Interbrand has done this yearly study since 2000. It calculates brand value by looking at company financial results, how loyal customers are to the brand, and an analysis of brand strength.

Some technology companies fell a lot. Nokia dropped 41 places to 98th, with a value of $4.1bn. Nintendo was 100th, down 33 places. Hayles said this shows innovation is important, because falling behind can hurt a company.

A Chinese company entered the top 100 for the first time. Huawei, a mobile-phone and broadband company, came in at 94th with a brand value of $4.3bn. Huawei has been partly banned by the US and Australian governments because of fears its equipment could be used for cyber-espionage by the Chinese.

Most brands in the top 100 are owned by US companies. The highest non-US brands were South Korea’s Samsung (6th), Japan’s Toyota (8th), and Germany’s Mercedes-Benz (10th). The highest British brands were HSBC (33rd), Shell (65th), and Burberry (73rd).

Other fashion brands in the top 100 include Boss, Prada, and Ralph Lauren. Louis Vuitton was the highest fashion brand, at 19th, worth $23bn. It was just ahead of H&M at 21st, worth $21bn.

Nike was 22nd, worth nearly $20bn. It was far ahead of Adidas, which was 59th, worth $7bn.

Frampton said social media like Twitter lets people talk to brands and criticize them. He said companies must respond faster to protect and improve their reputations. He said customers now have more control, and brand-building is now about two-way conversations, support from customers, influence, and engagement. He also said customers expect smooth service, quick replies, 24/7 access, choices for customization, and personal service. He said customers increasingly expect brands to know them.

text:
In Canada’s Arctic, summer days are very long. Bright sunlight covers the treeless land for more than 20 hours each day. Many people like this after the dark winter. But for Muslims in Iqaluit, Nunavut, it can be hard during Ramadan. During Ramadan, Muslims usually do not eat or drink from sunrise to sunset.

Abdul Karim is 29 years old. He moved from Ottawa to Iqaluit in 2011. He follows the local sun to fast. This year, he eats at about 1:30 a.m. before sunrise. He eats again at about 11 p.m. when the sun sets. He said he has not fainted. He said he would stop only if fasting hurt his health. He also joked that he does not think fasting will hurt him.

As Ramadan nears its end, Muslims around the world focus on community work, prayer, and thinking quietly. In the Arctic, the long days have changed how some people fast.

Most Muslims in Iqaluit follow the Ramadan times used in Ottawa, about 1,300 miles south. Muslim scholars have said that Muslims in the far north can follow the timetable of Mecca or the nearest Muslim city. Atif Jilani moved to Iqaluit from Toronto in 2015. He said this still means fasting for about 18 hours each day, but it is easier to manage.

Many people in the community, about 100 people, break their fast together. They meet at the city’s new mosque, finished in February. At that time, the temperature with wind chill dropped as low as -50C. They eat nightly suppers. They eat foods like dates and goat or lamb curry, while the sun still shines through the windows.

This also happens in other northern mosques in Canada. In Yellowknife, in the Northwest Territories, about 300 Muslims have a few choices for fasting, said Nazim Awan, president of the Yellowknife Islamic Centre. There are exceptions for people who are pregnant or ill. He said some people might want to fast for 23 hours. Others can follow nearby cities or follow the times of Mecca and Medina.

In recent years, many people there have followed Edmonton’s timetable, in Alberta. Awan follows Mecca’s timing. He has two young children, including a 12-year-old who recently started fasting. He wants to help his son by using a more manageable fast of about 15 hours, compared with about 18 hours in Edmonton. He said if he followed Yellowknife or Edmonton times, his son might say, “Papa, you are really insane. What are you doing?”

In Inuvik, a town 125 miles north of the Arctic Circle, it is impossible to follow the local sun at some times. About 100 Muslims there follow Edmonton’s timetable too. Ahmad Alkhalaf said they currently have 24 hours of sunlight, with no sunrise or sunset.

Alkhalaf said this plan was already used in 2001 when he moved from Toronto to Inuvik, a community of 3,500 people. His first Ramadan there was in December. At that time there was no sun, and it was dark day and night. So they used Edmonton time.

Alkhalaf said it can feel strange to follow the clock instead of the sky. He said people should break the fast at dusk, but there they sometimes eat when the sun is still up. He said it is not usual to have iftar, the meal that breaks the fast, when the sun is up.

In Inuvik, many people are Inuit. The Muslim community tries to respect Ramadan and local culture. The iftar meal includes dates and rich curries, and also local meat such as reindeer. The reindeer is prepared according to Islamic law. Alkhalaf said they make soup or curry, but use reindeer instead of beef.

In Iqaluit, as Muslims prepare to end Ramadan, some say the timing in 2016 was hard because it happened during some of the longest days of the year. This is especially true for people like Karim who follow the local sunrise and sunset. But Karim said it will be easier in future years because Ramadan follows a lunar calendar. One day it will happen in winter. In Iqaluit in winter, the sun rises and sets within a few hours each day. Karim said he will follow those hours too, and he laughed and said, “Oh yes, definitely.”

text:
“Some people are MOOC junkies,” says Dr Ben Brabon from Edgehill University with a grin. They take MOOCs only because they are free and they enjoy being part of the online group. Brabon is not insulting them. He is just saying that people join for different reasons. Because MOOCs have no entry rules and no fees, people on them often act differently from students at a normal university.

MOOCs are a new and popular idea to help more people get higher education. A lot of venture capital money is going into online platforms that can teach hundreds or even tens of thousands of people. Investors want to find a way to make money from MOOCs. So far, universities have paid for famous teachers to plan the course and teach online. One possible way to make money is to use the data about how people join, use the course, turn in work, talk to each other, and then leave.

A big problem is that nobody is sure who MOOCs are really for. Are they for universities to attract international students who will pay fees? Are they for students in developing countries who want access to top universities? Are they for workers who want to improve their skills? Are they for people without qualifications who want a step toward higher education? Or are they for hobby learners who just want to learn about a topic they like?

Brabon says MOOCs often start with many students, but very few finish. In his vampire fiction course, 1,000 people enrolled, and only 31 finished. He says almost all who finished already had a first degree or education at degree level. So MOOCs may not be reaching new groups of people.

David Kernohan from Jisc says online learning is different and needs strong study skills. In MOOCs there is little support. Students usually do not get personal attention. Instead, they may get help from other students in online discussion forums. This can make MOOCs hard for people without high-level qualifications. But he says MOOCs could be very useful for continuing education.

As degree costs rise and part-time student numbers drop, some people wonder if free open courses could give a new path to university-level learning. Could a whole degree be taught through MOOCs? Brabon says he does not think that is how MOOCs work. He suggests a blended approach, mixing time on campus with a MOOC. He also says it might be possible in the future to have a global degree, where students take courses from universities around the world.

For now, Brabon says employers will not care much about a MOOC completion certificate, because there may be no quality checks on the course or its marking. So accreditation is a main challenge for MOOCs. Brabon is in a Quality Assurance Agency working group that is trying to agree on standards and marking.

Some people like the idea that MOOCs can bring great teaching to students in less developed countries. But others are cynical and say universities might use MOOCs to advertise their on-campus courses to more students from outside the EU.

Mike Sharples, chair of Educational Technology, does not agree with that cynical view. He says MOOCs are mainly a way to share universities’ best teaching and to get interaction and feedback from students around the world. He thinks recruiting international students is a second goal, though MOOCs can still be smart marketing. He says if 20,000 people join a MOOC, you only need 20 of them to enrol later to run a master’s.

Matthew Poyiadgi, managing director at Pearson VUE, says these debates may not matter in the future. He thinks universities may have no choice. British universities may worry that if they do not have a presence in China, they will fall behind.

Sharples adds that in South America, China, and countries in Africa, many people want to learn, and some of the world’s best courses are now online. He says that if people love learning, that is a good thing. The real challenge is to help those countries not only use MOOCs, but also create them.

text:
A world agreement to make Bangladesh clothing factories safer may face a court case. Some factory owners want money back for the cost of closing factories and fixing buildings. They say repair work can take months. During that time, they cannot pay workers while the factory is closed, and they also have to pay for big repairs.

These repairs began after the Rana Plaza building in Dhaka, Bangladesh, collapsed in 2013. In that disaster, 1,138 people died.

Now, hundreds of factories each month are checked for fire safety and building problems under the Accord on Fire and Building Safety in Bangladesh. More than 170 international brands support it, including Primark and Marks & Spencer. International trade unions also support it, including IndustriALL.

One factory owner in Dhaka, from Softex Cotton, has threatened legal action after his factory was closed because of building problems. He is asking for a reported $100m in compensation.

Another factory owner, who did not give his name, said a factory cannot close “just for a short time.” He said that even closing for a few months could make a factory lose orders and shut down forever. He said the Accord was unclear about who should pay the costs when factories close.

Jenny Holdcroft, a policy director at IndustriALL, said brands promised to keep placing orders with suppliers for two years, so factories would not lose orders during closures. She said 12 factories have been found so far that need major work. But she said many only need to partly close, so work can continue on other floors.

She also said the Accord legally requires brands to make sure workers are paid during factory closures. She said the Accord left open the details of who pays, so factory owners who can afford repairs and worker payments will pay their share.

Holdcroft said payment was always going to be negotiated. She said brands do not want to pay if rich factory owners have kept profits and did not spend money on their factories for years. She said there would be disruption, and that without disruption, there would be no change. An Accord spokesman said talks about payments, and even legal action, would not stop the work to improve factory safety.

Pressure on the Accord has increased because another safety group, the Alliance for Bangladesh Worker Safety, has offered money to help pay workers. The Alliance is backed by US retailers including Walmart and Gap. It set aside $5m to help pay workers for up to two months while building work is done. It has found five factories in four buildings where work needed to stop.

Shaidullah Azim, a director of the Bangladeshi Garment Manufacturers and Exporters Association, said the Alliance is paying part of workers’ salaries with factory owners, and this has caused confusion. He said they met with the Accord and told them they need to help, or workers will not get support.
