text:
The small room is made to look like a classroom. The walls are corrugated iron. There are learning posters on the walls, with pictures of alphabet letters and a map of Bangladesh.

But it is hard to study there. There is always loud hammering. There are also chemicals in the air that make your throat hurt and your eyes sting. Even so, the children in this three-square-metre room are lucky. They do not have to work in the factories across the street.

For 14 years, SOHAY, a grassroots nongovernmental organization (NGO), has worked in slums in Dhaka to help child workers go to school. It is funded by the Global Fund for Children and Comic Relief. SOHAY helps children who work in dangerous places, like aluminium and plastic factories, and tanneries.

This classroom is one of 23 urban development centres that SOHAY has set up in the city. The centres teach children and help them get ready for primary school. They help children catch up on lessons they missed. After the children start primary school, they can come to the centres to do homework and get help from other children.

Alamin is ten. He used to work in a plastic factory, but now he goes to one of the centres. His father sells things on the street. His mother works part time in other people’s homes. His family is happy he is in school now and not doing dangerous work. His friend Rabi says he wants to forget his factory past. “I like school,” he says.

SOHAY’s programme manager, Mohammed Abdullah al-Mamun, says the centres try to make the community support education and change how people think about children. SOHAY also holds sessions for parents and employers to discourage child labour. It also offers skills training to help families earn more money.

Mamun says it is very hard to move working children into formal school. Their bodies and minds are not like other children’s, because of the work they did. After they stop working, they may find it hard to make friends and get used to school. It is also hard to make sure they stay in school, because many drop out. Mamun says schools need to be more sensitive and caring toward these children.

Zhorna Akter Sumayya is seven. She has two older brothers who work. One works at a restaurant, and one works at a local club. After learning at a SOHAY centre, she now goes to a state primary school. Her family lives in a slum. Her parents need the money her sons bring home to survive. Her father works in a rickshaw garage, and her mother is a domestic worker. But they really wanted their daughter to go to school.

In 2015, SOHAY helped 1,540 children leave dangerous work and helped 2,125 vulnerable children go to school. These vulnerable children were at risk of starting work. About 780 more children were preparing to start school in 2017. SOHAY also helped 635 children working in dangerous conditions learn about their rights under Bangladesh’s 2010 child labour elimination code of conduct. That policy wanted to end all child labour by 2015, but the goal was not reached.

Bangladesh’s Labour Law of 2006 bans children under 14 from working. But UNICEF says 4.7 million children under 14 work in the informal sector. UNICEF also says 1.3 million children aged five to 17 work in hazardous industries.

Sadia Nasrin runs Sonjag, another grassroots NGO in Dhaka. She says it was hard to get children into school because families did not get money to replace the children’s work time. To solve this, Sonjag worked closely with slum communities where the children live.

Sonjag talked with community members about why school is important. It chose community volunteers who wanted to change children’s lives. It formed groups with social workers, community leaders, mothers, young volunteers, and local government.

Nasrin says these groups are very important. They encourage employers to let children leave work for two or three hours each day to go to school. They also work to make the workplace safer for children. She says the community people are the real change-makers.

Nasrin also says people in slums face eviction, police raids, and being forced to move. She says the national plan of action for children does not recognize the needs of street children. She also says the laws are limited.

When children miss starting school at age five, there is little time to help them. If they get too old, it becomes very hard for schools to accept them. Mamun says many children then pass their time without education and wait until they start dangerous work. He says SOHAY is working to stop the flow of children into child labour.

text:
Barack Obama told young people not to be hopeless. He said they should talk with people who have different beliefs if they want to change the world.

On the last day of his final trip to Britain as US president, Obama spoke to 500 youth leaders at a town hall meeting in London. He said they should not believe that some forces cannot be controlled. He quoted JFK, saying that human-made problems can be solved by people.

He told A-level students and UK–US exchange students that they have better tools than ever to make a difference. He said to reject pessimism and cynicism, and to believe that progress is possible. He also said progress is not guaranteed, and it takes struggle, discipline, and faith.

Obama said he knew young people face hard times, with big changes like 9/11 and 7/7, and with constant bad news on the internet and Twitter.

People cheered when he was introduced. He spoke about his policies, including healthcare and education.

He encouraged the audience to talk with people who have different political beliefs. He said this can help people learn to compromise.

Obama said social attitudes are changing. He said it is not happening fast enough, but young people should keep pushing, and that brave young people have helped cause change.

When asked what he is most proud of, Obama said he was proud of healthcare reforms, and the US response to the 2008 financial crisis. He said it helped save the world from a great depression. He also named diplomatic deals with Iran and the response to the Ebola crisis as important parts of his presidency. He said he felt he had stayed true to himself.

He told young people not to give up if poverty is not ended after five years. He quoted Dr Martin Luther King, who said the moral universe is long but bends toward justice.

When asked about the Transatlantic Trade and Investment Partnership (TTIP), Obama said the answer to globalization is not to shut off from the world. He also said it was important to pay attention to workers’ rights.

Before Obama arrived, Tanya Williams, a community engagement officer, told the Guardian she liked Obama and was excited to hear him speak.

Oliver Sidorczuk, 26, said people were very excited. He said he wanted to ask Obama about electoral rights and about joining a campaign to ask David Cameron for automatic registration.

Obama ended the session by answering a question from a young Sikh Londoner. The young man asked about racial profiling at airports and being mistaken for a Muslim.

Obama said that even though some people have “crazy ideology,” pluralism is important. He said he had visited a mosque a few months earlier and said America’s greatest allies are American Muslims who are well integrated and doing well economically.

Furqan Naeem, a campaigner from Manchester, said he visited the States on a US embassy community leaders programme. He said he saw important work Obama did to celebrate America’s diversity and bring communities together.

Kenny Imafidon, the managing director of the youth organization ClearView Research, said the event was a great opportunity. He said he would remember Obama’s advice about meeting people with different politics and making compromises. He also mentioned what Obama said about being a good leader and finding great talent.

Later, Obama met Jeremy Corbyn. Corbyn said they had an “excellent” 90-minute discussion. Corbyn said they talked about challenges in postindustrial societies, the power of global corporations, the growing use of technology, and its effects around the world. Corbyn said Obama’s comments about Britain’s membership of the EU were also discussed briefly.

After the meeting, Obama joined Cameron to play golf at the Grove in Chandlers Cross, Hertfordshire. Obama ended the day with a dinner with the prime minister and the US ambassador, Matthew Barzun, at the ambassador’s home, before travelling on to Germany.

text:
US researchers say that the government’s large collection of phone records is a clear danger to the privacy of ordinary people. They showed that even simple phone logs can help identify people and reveal private details about their lives.

Two scientists at Stanford University used anonymous “metadata” about calls and texts. This metadata did not include what people said or wrote. It only showed things like which numbers people contacted and when. Even so, the scientists figured out people’s names, where they lived, and the names of their partners.

The same data also helped them find very personal information. They found that one man owned a rifle. They found that another person had recently been told they had an irregular heartbeat. Other data suggested a new pregnancy and a person with multiple sclerosis.

The study shows how powerful phone metadata can be, especially when it is combined with public information from Google, Yelp, and Facebook. Phone metadata does not have the same legal protections as the actual content of messages and calls. Security services have known for a long time that this data is valuable. After Edward Snowden’s disclosures, Stewart Baker, former general counsel at the US National Security Agency (NSA), said: “Metadata absolutely tells you everything about somebody’s life.”

Patrick Mutchler, a computer security researcher at Stanford, said the public does not understand how powerful metadata is because few studies have shown how much it can reveal. He said this makes it harder for people who strongly oppose these programmes to fight them. He said the new study gives strong evidence that did not exist before.

For the study, the researchers recruited 823 people. The people agreed to let the researchers collect metadata from their phones using an Android app. The app also collected information from their Facebook accounts, which the scientists used to check their results. In total, the researchers collected metadata for more than 250,000 calls and over 1.2m texts.

In the NSA’s metadata system, analysts were first allowed to look up to three “hops” away from a person. A hop means one step in a chain of calls: a call from the target person to another number is one hop; from that number to another is two hops; and so on. Analysts could also look at records going back five years. Later, the rules were changed to only two hops and a maximum of 18 months.

Writing in the journal Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, Mutchler explained how he and another graduate student, Jonathan Mayer, found a lot of personal information using little money. With computer searches and manual searches, they identified 82% of people’s names. They also found the names of businesses people called. When they put these business locations on a map, they saw groups of nearby businesses. The scientists guessed these groups were near the person’s home. Using this method, they named the city where people lived 57% of the time, and they were almost 90% accurate in placing people within 50 miles of home. Mutchler said some mistakes may have happened because some people did not update Facebook after moving, such as moving out of their parents’ home to go to college.

Next, the scientists looked at people’s personal relationships. They used a simple computer program to study call patterns and guess who was in a relationship. After they knew someone had a partner, they said it was easy to identify who that partner was.

In the last part of the study, the researchers tried to see what sensitive information they could get from metadata. They looked at calls to and from a list of organizations, including hospitals, pharmacies, religious groups, legal services, firearms retailers, and repair firms. From this, they put together detailed stories about people’s lives.

For one person, the metadata showed a long call from a cardiology center, a short call with a medical laboratory, many short calls from a local pharmacy, and calls to a hotline for abnormal heart-rate monitoring devices. For another person, the metadata showed frequent calls to a local gun supplier that sold semiautomatic rifles, and later several long calls to a customer support hotline for a major gun manufacturer that produced the rifles. For two other people, the metadata suggested one had multiple sclerosis and the other had just become pregnant.

Mutchler said this shows what two graduate students can do with limited resources. He said the results should make policymakers think carefully before approving mass surveillance programmes. The scientists wrote that large metadata surveillance programmes, like the NSA’s, will expose highly confidential information about ordinary citizens. They added that future policymaking should be guided by relevant sciences to balance national security and civil liberties.

Ross Anderson, a professor of security engineering at Cambridge University, said the study gives numbers that people can use in discussions. He said that with the right analysis on nation-scale communications data, it is possible to guess huge amounts of sensitive information about everyone. He said people had suspected this, but now there is data to show it.

text:
A thick layer of bird droppings covers a shiny railing at one of Britain’s most expensive homes. Pigeon bones lie near broken mirrors, and water runs through broken parts of the ceiling. This home is called The Towers. It is a £30m house on “Billionaires’ Row” in north London. Its serious damage was kept secret until now.

The Towers is one of ten big houses in the middle of The Bishops Avenue. This street is a key part of London’s rising property market. These houses have been almost empty since they were bought about 25 years ago. People believe they were bought for members of the Saudi Arabian royal family. The Greek-style columns are breaking. Swimming pools with mosaic tiles are full of broken pieces. Nature has taken over, and owls have moved in.

This sad picture is seen all along the street. Lloyds Bank says The Bishops Avenue is the second most expensive street in Britain. While more people struggle to buy a home in London, house prices rise 11.2% each year. On the most expensive part of the avenue, 16 big houses are empty. Many sit behind locked gates. Windows are covered with steel bars, and the gardens are overgrown. Guard dogs patrol the grounds.

Across the street is another empty house worth £18m, with broken windows and walls covered in anti-climb paint. Another house has metal bars on the windows and was sold for £20m.

Still, prices keep rising. Dryades, once owned by a Pakistani politician, sold for £12m in 2007 and is believed to be worth about £30m today. Heath Lodge, where fashion tycoon Aristos Constantinou was murdered in 1984 by a silver bullet, is worth £13m today. It was sold in the late 1970s for £400,000.

For people who struggle to pay for housing in one of the world’s most expensive cities, these empty, falling-apart houses can feel upsetting. A security guard on the avenue said it was frustrating to see so much space—enough for dozens of people—being wasted and breaking down.

In the past, royals with oil wealth from Nigeria and Saudi Arabia were among the first to come to this curving road near Hampstead Heath. Iranians moved here after the fall of the shah. Now, Chinese buyers are coming after Russians and Kazakhs. Estate agents tell them the address is as famous as the Champs Elysées and Rodeo Drive. Recently, two houses were for sale for £65m and £38m, offering Italian marble, lifts with leather padding, and luxury panic rooms.

But on the land of the empty houses, stone fountains are falling apart and lawns have turned into muddy bogs. Inside one house, water drips through a large crystal chandelier onto a thick carpet that is rotting under parts of a collapsed ceiling. Moss grows through broken bricks. Broken mirror tiles are scattered in a bathroom. A swimming pool has about a foot of dirty, salty water and flowers growing through the tiles. Wooden pieces on the sauna are pushing outward.

The most dramatic ruin is The Towers. It is a grand house among many trees, including hornbeams, oaks, and limes. Pigeons fill its large halls. Water pours down through three floors, and the walls have turned bright green with algae. The water drops into a huge, empty basement swimming pool. Wooden crates marked “bullet-proof glass” show the security fears of the past owners.

Today, very few people live on The Bishops Avenue all the time. A security guard outside one house said the owners were not there. Another guard, outside Royal Mansion, would not say if anyone was home. A staff member at another house warned the Guardian about the guard dogs. Magdy Adib Ishak-Hannah, an Egyptian-born private healthcare mogul with personal wealth of £45m, said he is one of the few full-time residents.

He said, “It’s not a neighbourly place, where you can chat over the fence. To be honest, I have never seen what my neighbours look like. Next door, a Saudi princess spent £35m on a new house and I’ve never seen her. There are about three houses that are lived in 24/7 and half of the properties are occupied three to six months a year. The other half, who knows if they come or not?”

These broken, multi-million-pound houses show a property culture where the world’s richest people treat British homes as investments. One Hyde Park, an apartment block in Knightsbridge, is another example. More than half of its flats are registered with the council as empty or second homes.

Even so, people on the avenue talk about building £5m apartments instead of £50m mansions to bring residents back.

Anil Varma, a local property developer, plans to rebuild one valuable site on the avenue into 20 apartments. They would have a concierge, maid service, a 25-metre pool, a spa, and a cinema.

He said, “If you build a big house and try and sell for £30m to £40m, it won’t sell. Locals won’t buy and so you have to bring in overseas buyers.”

But using the empty homes to help solve the housing crisis still seems far away. Andrew Harper, a local Conservative councillor, laughed when asked if some of the run-down homes could become affordable housing. He said the land price would be too high.

He said, “Very wealthy people own property there. Sometimes they live in them and sometimes they don’t.”

text:
“I got a Dyson vacuum cleaner, but I don’t even know if I want it. I just picked it up,” said Louise Haggerty. She is 56 and works as a hairdresser and waitress. She went to the Black Friday sales at 1am. “It was mental in there. It was crazy. It was absolutely disgusting,” she said.

Haggerty went with a friend to the 24-hour Sainsbury’s supermarket in Harringay, north-east London. They hoped to get a cheap flat-screen TV. “But so many people pushed in the queue we didn’t have a chance,” she said. “The poor woman who was second in the queue was pushed out by a crowd of youths. She didn’t get anything. People were behaving like animals. It was horrible. I only saw two security guards.”

Haggerty could not buy a Blaupunkt 40" TV that was reduced from £299.99 to £149.99. So she rushed to pick up a Dyson Animal Vac, down from £319.99 to £159.99. “I don’t even know how much it costs. I don’t even know if I’m going to buy it. I just wanted something,” she said. “There are lads in there with three, four, five tellies. It’s not fair.”

One of those lads was Andy Blackett, 30, an estate agent. He had two trolleys full of bargains. “I got two coffee makers, two tablets, two TVs and a stereo,” he said. “I couldn’t tell you the prices but I know they’re bargains.” But his friend Henry Fischer, a 19-year-old student, did not do as well. “Someone snatched my telly from me. It’s cos I’m the smaller one.”

Blackett, Fischer, and some friends drove to Sainsbury’s at 12.45am. They left Tesco’s 24-hour Lea Valley supermarket because it was “bedlam.” The Black Friday sale there started at midnight. “Tesco was scary so we came here instead,” Blackett said.

More than a dozen police officers went to the Tesco store on Glover Drive, Upper Edmonton. Fights broke out between shoppers. People were seen tearing down cardboard barriers that were meant to hold back sale items until midnight. Tesco delayed selling its most popular sale items, TVs, for almost an hour until police got control. One police officer was heard criticizing the manager for not having enough security. The officer suggested stopping the sale. This Tesco is across the road from an IKEA store where there were riots when it opened with a midnight sale in 2005.

Police also stepped in at other stores, including Tesco in Willesden and Surrey Quays, just before doors opened at midnight. Greater Manchester Police said at least two people were arrested at Black Friday sales that morning. On Twitter they said: “Keep calm, people!” South Wales Police said staff at Tesco stores made calls because they were worried about how many people came to the sales.

When TV sales started just before 1am, one of the first buyers was James Alled, 30, a businessman. He bought two flat-screen TVs and started trying to sell one to someone farther down the queue. “I bought them for £250. I’ll sell it to you for £350, £300 cash,” he said. Farther back, Christine Ball, 62, was angry. “I got here at 10.15pm and I’m further back now than when I got here,” she said. “None of that lot know what a queue is.”

Ball, like most shoppers, had not heard of the US-inspired Black Friday sales before now. She said she came to buy her grandson a TV for Christmas. “Not one of those massive ones; just a normal one at £100 or so,” she said. In her basket she had a pint of semi-skimmed milk and a loaf of bread. “Telly, milk and bread – the necessities,” she said.

Mel Mehmet, 23, had been to Black Friday sales in 2013 and expected queues. But she said the feeling in Tesco scared her this time. “It’s crazy, really, having it at midnight. The police must have more important things to do at night than be called to sales. We’re going to PC World first thing. Their sale starts at 8am.”

text:
A group of experts and charities says some allergy tests are fake or wrong. These tests make thousands of people get treatments they do not need. They can also make people, or their children, stop eating many foods. This can lead to poor diets and sometimes malnutrition.

Allergies and food intolerances are increasing. But many people mix them up. Many people are also wrongly told they have an allergy. Sense About Science made a guide with allergy specialists and charities to help.

Tracey Brown from Sense About Science said there is a big problem with myths, wrong ideas from studies, and fake medicine. She said this costs a lot. Some people do things they do not need to do, and other people do not get the help they truly need.

Experts worry about restaurants and caterers. They see many people say they have allergies. But some of these people really have food intolerance, which is not dangerous. Then staff may stop being as careful. This can be very dangerous for a person with a real allergy.

Moira Austin from the Anaphylaxis Campaign said this is very important. If a caterer thinks someone is only avoiding a food because it makes them feel bloated, they may be less careful. There have been deaths when people told a restaurant about an allergy, but the meal still contained that food.

The guide says most allergy tests bought online or in shops do not have scientific proof. One home test checks for IgG (immunoglobulin G) antibodies in blood. These antibodies are part of the immune system’s response to infections. But the best medical evidence shows high IgG levels do not mean a person has an allergy. Many people without an allergy or food intolerance get a positive result.

The guide also says another test is not valid. It mixes acupuncture and homeopathy. It tries to measure electrical resistance on the skin while a person holds the food. Hair follicle testing is also useless. Hair is not involved in allergic reactions, so hair tests cannot show if someone has an allergy. People should also not believe that allergies are caused by an “energy blockage” found by muscle testing and cured by acupuncture.

Paul Seddon, a consultant paediatric allergist, said he often sees children on diets that are too limited. Parents believe the children are allergic to many foods because of these tests. He said this must stop.

Adam Fox from Guys and St Thomas’ Hospital in London said he also sees patients who sent hair for testing or removed many foods from their child’s diet. He said it is hard to fix this later. Children need proper diets. Avoiding foods that are not harmful can also hurt quality of life. A child who cannot eat wheat or drink milk may not be able to go to parties.

Some parents believe a child’s long-lasting tiredness, headaches, or eczema is caused by an allergy. It can take time and many tests to find out if this is true or not. It can be tempting to go to an alternative therapist who gives one quick test and a fast answer, but the answer can be wrong.

Allergies are rising in developed countries. In the last 30 years, the percentages of children diagnosed with allergic rhinitis and eczema have tripled. Better diagnosis is part of the reason, but the rise is real. More people now think allergies may be causing their own health problems or their children’s. The guide lists myths about what causes allergies, like E numbers in food colourings, “toxic overload”, and fast food.

text:
More and more people are looking for dark places to see the night sky. On Black Fell in Northumberland, near the Kielder Water reservoir, people wait in a car park beside a strange wooden building. This is Kielder Observatory. It is part of Britain’s new “astrotourism” industry, where people travel to look at stars. Only a small number of people can come each night, so many people who applied did not get in.

Inside the observatory, the lights are low and there is a woodburner. The founder and lead astronomer, Gary Fildes, talks to staff and volunteers. They hope to see the northern lights, but Fildes does not think they will. Instead, they plan to use strong telescopes to look at Jupiter and Venus. Later they will look for stars like Capella and Betelgeuse. They also hope to see the International Space Station.

Fildes tells his team that the night should be interactive, entertaining, and inspiring. He plays music by Pink Floyd, the Jam, and the Pogues. He says that by 9.30 the sky will look amazing.

Fildes, 49, is an important person in the UK’s growing astrotourism industry. A key moment for Northumberland came in 2013. The national park that includes Hadrian’s Wall, plus Kielder Water and Forest Park, about 1,500 sq km, was given Dark Sky Park status. It was the only one in England. Dark Sky Parks are rare. A 2013 Star Count showed that only 5% of people in the UK can see more than 31 stars on a good night.

The International Dark-Sky Association (IDA), based in Tucson, Arizona, gives this status only to places that work hard to reduce light pollution. They must also show that their skies are dark enough. In Northumberland Dark Sky Park, it is so dark that Venus can cast a shadow on the Earth.

Duncan Wise, a visitor development officer for the Northumberland National Park Authority, helped lead the work to get dark-sky status. The Council for the Protection of Rural England said it was one of Britain’s most peaceful places. Wise said people often look only at the land up to the horizon, but they should also think about the sky above it. Wise and others spent years preparing their application to the IDA. They took many light readings and worked with local councils, park groups, and community groups. They created an outdoor lighting plan that affects how new buildings are made in the area.

Their work has paid off. Many of the 1.5 million people who visit Northumberland each year now know about its Dark Sky status. A man at a car-hire firm in Newcastle says many people now come to see the sky, especially in autumn and winter when nights are darkest. This helps B&Bs because they can get business all year. Some hotels give guests night-vision torches and put deckchairs outside at night. Hotels can earn a badge to show they are “Dark Sky Friendly.”

Wise says Northumberland should do more to use this special resource. He thinks the area needs a couple more observatories so visitors are more likely to see what they came for. A £14m national landscape discovery centre, which he calls the north’s answer to the Eden Project, will include an observatory when it is finished in a couple of years.

Fildes has big plans too. He wants to build Britain’s first “astrovillage.” It would include the largest public observatory in the world, a 100-seat auditorium, a 100-seat planetarium, a one-metre aperture telescope, and radiomagnetic and solar telescopes. The project would also have a hotel. It would bring in 100,000 people each year, four times the number who can use the observatory now. Fildes does not say much about who is paying for it, but he believes it will be real by 2018.

Northumberland also has competition. Galloway Forest Park in Scotland has Dark Sky Park status too. Exmoor became Europe’s first International Dark Sky Reserve in 2011, and local businesses there now offer stargazing breaks and safaris. The UK also competes with northern Chile, which has more than a dozen tourist observatories and some of the clearest skies in the world. Teide National Park in Tenerife is also becoming a major place for astrotourism.

Why do more people want to look up at the sky? The media has helped. TV presenters like Brian Cox have brought in a new group of stargazers. Wise says Brian Cox has made astronomy easier for everyone, not just professors with old brass telescopes. Technology has helped too. Apps like Stellarium can turn smartphones into small planetariums. In the end, Fildes thinks people are starting to value what is above them. He says that if you had to build a visitor attraction from nothing, nothing could be better than the universe.

text:
A new study says Europe’s forests, and even its suburbs, are again home to big wild predators. The study says brown bears, wolves, and lynx are doing well. Even though many people worry that these animals will die out as the human population grows, the study in the journal Science found that their numbers in Europe are stable or growing.

Brown bears, wolves, Eurasian lynx, and wolverines live in almost one-third of mainland Europe (not counting Belarus, Ukraine, and Russia). Most of these animals live outside nature reserves. This suggests that people’s attitudes are changing and that large conservation efforts are helping animals that humans have hunted and harmed for a very long time.

Bears are the most common large predator in Europe. There are about 17,000 bears, about 12,000 wolves, about 9,000 Eurasian lynx, and about 1,250 wolverines. Wolverines live only in the northern parts of Scandinavia and Finland. In mainland Europe, only Belgium, Denmark, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg, like Britain, have no breeding populations of at least one large predator species. The lead author and other conservationists said this wide spread, even in crowded places, suggests the British countryside could also support big predators.

Guillaume Chapron from Sweden’s University of Agricultural Sciences and other researchers found that some wolves live in suburban areas with up to 3,050 people per square kilometre. This is higher than the population density of Cambridge or Newcastle. On average in Europe, wolves live where there are 37 people per square kilometre, lynx where there are 21 people per square kilometre, and bears where there are 19 people per square kilometre. The Scottish Highlands have nine people per square kilometre.

Chapron said that to have wolves, people do not need to leave the land. The researchers said the return of big predators shows that a “land-sharing” conservation model can work. This means people and predators share the same areas. This is different from fencing off “wilderness” areas to keep people and predators apart, as happens in North America and Africa.

Chapron said it is not a perfect story, because living together can bring conflict. He said it is important to manage conflict, keep it low, and solve problems. He also said wolves can be hard neighbours. He said it is not only people versus predators; often it is conflict between people about predators. He said these animals represent hard questions about how people should use land.

The researchers said this “land-sharing” approach could work in other parts of the world too. They said it works in Europe because of political stability, more prey animals such as wild deer, and money to help farmers protect animals without killing predators, such as using electric fences. Chapron said the most important reason has been the EU Habitats Directive, which requires member states to protect and bring back rare species.

Chapron said that without the Habitats Directive, this recovery might not have happened. He said the recovery shows that if people want to protect nature and make strong laws like the Habitats Directive, wildlife protection can succeed.

The return of these animals was welcomed by author and commentator George Monbiot, who is starting Rewilding Britain. This is a new charity that encourages the return of wild landscapes and extinct species.

Monbiot said it is good to see numbers rising, but that Britain is unusual. He said Britain has lost more large mammals than any country except Ireland. He said that apart from the accidental return of boar, Britain has done very little. In much of Europe, bears, lynx, and wolves are coming back. He said this is a big change after centuries of persecution.

The survey found Eurasian lynx living permanently in 11 population groups across 23 European countries. Only five of these were native populations. This shows that reintroduction has worked. Monbiot said support is growing to reintroduce lynx to the Cairngorms in Scotland.

Monbiot said that if it works in the rest of Europe, there is no reason it cannot work in the UK. He pointed out that bears and wolves live within an hour of Rome. He said there is no population reason why wildlife cannot return in a similar way in the UK.

text:
The roof of the school is made of plastic and held up by a crooked tree trunk. The desks are mixed old chairs. But inside the Chemin des Dunes school, the students study very hard. They hope for a new life in France.

“The French language is very difficult but we try hard. If we come every day, maybe we can touch our dreams,” says Kamal, a refugee from Sudan’s Darfur district, which has been hurt by war. He comes to class three or four hours every day. “It’s a good thing to keep your brain active.”

Kamal is 29 years old and an electrical engineer. He is one of many refugees living in the “jungle” camp outside Calais. They have applied for asylum in France and want to learn French for what they hope will be their new home.

Kamal is upset because the media often talks only about people who try at night to sneak onto lorries or trains to cross the Channel.

“I need to tell people in the UK, they think that everyone wants to go there. But there are a lot of people here who want to stay in France,” Kamal said.

France already has more than a quarter of a million refugees, according to United Nations data. France has taken in more than twice as many as the UK, even though the countries have similar populations.

There are also 56,000 asylum seekers waiting for their case to be processed in France, the second highest number in Europe. In the UK, there are 36,000.

While people wait, France does not give them money support and does not let them work. The process can take many months. The jungle camp gives one free meal a day and a plastic roof. So many people stay in the hard conditions for a few more months, instead of working illegally and possibly hurting their asylum request.

Some people had the idea for the school at the start of the summer. They were bored from waiting and worried about starting life in France without being able to talk to people. The school became real within weeks and opened on 11 July.

“We did it to reunite the ‘brothers’ and, at the same time, they can learn French,” said Zimarco Jones, the school’s founder from Nigeria. He arrived in Calais in 2013 and is still waiting for his asylum claim to be processed. “Now, we need to build another one,” he says with a grin.

When it is busiest, the small classroom has 30 pupils. They sit in five rows in front of a big green chalkboard. There are pictures of cartoon animals for each letter of the French alphabet. There are also classes in English, art, and t’ai chi. But the most popular classes are French lessons, taught by volunteers from Calais and other places.

“French is not as easy as English but, two weeks ago, I decided there was no way to get to the UK,” says George, another refugee from Darfur and a serious student. He wanted to cross the Channel because he speaks English very well. But with language classes, he says he is happy to live in France.

“Anywhere there is peace, I can stay, no problem,” he says. He is already waiting at the classroom more than half an hour before his teachers arrive.

He says he does not know much about France. But the classes are slowly helping him understand the country and the language.

Many volunteers at the jungle school are local teachers who are giving up their summer holidays.

Jenny Flahaut, 33, works at a children’s home. She decided to volunteer after seeing an ad on Facebook. “I saw these people in Calais every day and I wanted to do something for them,” she said.

She is also upset about how the media shows migrants. She is upset with politicians who have never visited the camp, including David Cameron, the British prime minister. In a speech that many people criticized, he talked about “swarms” of people trying to reach the UK.

“They don’t know them and have a bad vision but they are not like that,” Flahaut said as she got ready for an afternoon lesson. “Most of them are very good people. They are welcoming and friendly. They want to improve their life and make it better, and learning is part of that.”

Now the teachers and Zimarco are working to set up a separate classroom for about 200 women and two dozen children. Volunteers say there are about ten men for every one woman in the Calais camp. Many women feel uncomfortable going to classes with male students they do not know.

After that is done, Zimarco has more dreams to make the camp a place to live, not only to survive. People in the camp call it “Zimarco’s school.” He wants to start a football team for migrants. He also dreams of changing the camp’s name. He hates “the jungle” because he says it makes it sound like the people there are not human.

“We have a discotheque, a house, a mosque, a school, shops,” he says. “We are not animals.”

text:
About 20 years ago, Larry Pizzi worked in the bicycle business. When he first heard about electric bikes, he asked, “Why would anyone ruin a bike by adding a motor and batteries?”

Many people who love regular bikes still wonder the same thing. Bicycle shops in the US were slow to sell e-bikes, even though e-bikes have existed since the late 1990s. Sales in the US have been small.

Now Pizzi is the CEO of Currie Technologies, the top seller of e-bikes in the US. He thinks this is going to change soon, and other people in the industry agree. Brands like Trek, Raleigh, and Specialized now sell electric bikes too. They think the market will grow fast.

Pizzi said e-bikes are close to becoming common. More companies are joining the e-bike business all the time.

The US is different from many other places. In 2014, almost 32m e-bikes were sold, mostly in China, where people use them to get around. E-bikes are also popular in much of Europe. They are common in the Netherlands and Switzerland. German postal workers use them, and BMW sells one for about $3,000.

E-bikes are not the same as motorcycles or mopeds. A motorcycle or moped depends on the motor. An e-bike is still a bicycle you can pedal, with or without help from an electric motor. Riding one feels like riding a regular bike with a strong wind pushing you from behind. The motor helps you go faster or climb hills, but it usually is not the main power. Unlike mopeds, e-bikes are usually allowed on bike paths, and they cannot go faster than 20mph.

No one knows the exact number of e-bikes sold in the US. There is no official count. Estimates say about 50,000 to 175,000 are sold each year. This is similar to electric car sales in the US, which were 118,000 in 2014. But while many people know cars like the Nissan Leaf, Chevy Volt, and Tesla, few know e-bike companies like Currie, Pedego, or ElectroBike.

To do well in the US, e-bike companies must deal with legal, cultural, and money problems. Some states ban e-bikes, including New York, even though the law is not enforced strongly. Many bike shop owners do not like adding motors to bicycles. One reason is weight. Some e-bikes are close to 30kg.

E-bikes can also cost a lot. Some low-end models cost as little as $700. But Court Rye, who runs ElectricBikeReview.com, says a good e-bike with a good battery usually costs at least $1,500. The most expensive models cost more than twice that.

E-bike companies say they can solve these problems. The technology is getting better, especially the batteries. Don DiCostanza, the founder and CEO of Pedego, says batteries are getting smaller, lighter, more reliable, and they last longer.

Big companies like Bosch and Shimano are entering the e-bike business. This may help bike shops feel better about selling e-bikes. Pizzi says bicycle dealers are paying more attention. Pedego and the startup ElectroBike are not waiting for bike shops. They are opening their own stores. At the same time, people are working to change laws so e-bikes are allowed everywhere.

Another big reason e-bikes may grow is that more cities are building bike lanes and other cycling paths. More people are biking to work. The US Census Bureau said that bike commuters grew from about 488,000 in 2000 to 786,000 in 2012. The report said this was a bigger percentage increase than any other way of getting to work. E-bikes can make commuting easier and more fun, because they help with hills, wind, tiredness, and sweating.

DiCostanza says many Pedego customers are “ageing baby boomers” who want to feel like they did when they were kids. He says many stopped riding because of hills. Pedego has opened nearly 60 stores in the US. It has also sold bikes to tour companies in San Francisco and Washington, DC.

ElectroBike runs 30 stores in Mexico. It opened its first US store in Venice Beach, California in the autumn of 2014. It hopes to grow to 25 US stores in a year. CEO Craig Anderson says the company wants to help reduce traffic, reduce the carbon footprint, and support a healthy lifestyle. He tells customers, “Ride this once and try not to smile.”

Startups like Pedego and ElectroBike must compete with big companies like Trek and Currie. In 2012, Currie was bought by the Accell Group, a public company in the Netherlands. Accell is Europe’s market leader in e-bikes. Accell owns the Raleigh brand and also Haibike, a German electric bike that has won awards.

Pizzi says Accell expects e-bikes to grow in North America. He says baby boomers are still important customers, but more younger people are also using e-bikes for transportation instead of cars.

Accell’s Yuba brand even sells a cargo bike with a stronger motor and a rear rack. Pizzi says you can carry two children or 45kg of shopping. He says it is an alternative to a minivan.

text:
A Canadian man named Jordan Axani became famous after he offered a free trip around the world to a woman with the same name as his ex-girlfriend. He has now returned to Canada from the trip with Elizabeth Quinn Gallagher. People hoped they would fall in love, but they did not. Axani said they became like brother and sister.

Axani, who is 28 and from Toronto, made news in 2014. He had booked a three-week vacation with his ex-girlfriend, but they broke up. He could not change the name on the plane tickets, so he offered the ticket to any Canadian named Elizabeth Gallagher.

Elizabeth Gallagher, a 23-year-old student from Cole Harbour, Nova Scotia, answered his Reddit post. She goes by the name Quinn. Axani chose her from other people with the same name. Before the trip, Quinn said she had a “pretty serious” boyfriend.

After they got back to Toronto, Axani said the trip was never meant to be romantic. He said he did not see Quinn in a romantic way and there was no romantic future for them. He said she was a good friend, like a little sister, and she felt the same.

Axani said it took time for them to feel comfortable traveling together. It took about a week to understand each other. At first, they had to learn the dos and don’ts of traveling together. Later, they had a good rhythm. They joked together and also knew when the other person needed space.

They visited Milan, Venice, Vienna, Prague, Khao Lak (in Thailand), and Hong Kong. Axani said the trip was “fantastic.” He said Prague was a favorite place. In about two and a half days there, they met about two dozen people.

Axani said many people followed them on Twitter and Instagram. In Hong Kong, people even recognized them on the street. He said it was a real adventure and that they learned a lot about themselves and each other.

Axani returned to Toronto at 3am on Monday, 12 January. The trip ended quickly, and he went straight to a board meeting for his charity, A Ticket Forward. He started the non-profit after his Reddit post became very popular. He wants to offer round-the-world trips to survivors of abuse, cancer, and war.

Axani also said he is talking with companies about turning the story into a television show or film, but he did not say what it would be. He said many production companies were interested. He also said he was not looking for his next Elizabeth Gallagher yet. He said, “life happens and we’ll see.”

text:
Two islanders saw a small fibreglass boat on a far-away island in the Pacific Ocean. They went to look. Inside they found a very thin man with long hair and a beard. He said he had been drifting at sea for 16 months. He said he started his trip in Mexico, more than 12,500km away.

The man was wearing only torn underpants. He said he left Mexico for El Salvador in September 2012 in a 7.3-metre fibreglass boat. The boat’s engines had no propellers. He said another man who was with him died at sea months earlier.

Ola Fjeldstad, a Norwegian student doing research on Ebon Atoll in the Marshall Islands, said the man was not in good condition but was getting better. The man said his name was José Ivan. He said he stayed alive by catching turtles and birds with his bare hands. He spoke only Spanish, so the details were not clear. There was no fishing gear on the boat, but there was a turtle inside when the boat arrived. Fjeldstad said the boat was very scratched and looked like it had been in the water a long time.

The islanders took the man to the main island of the atoll to meet the mayor, Ione de Brum. The atoll is very remote. It has only one phone line and no internet. The mayor called the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Majuro, the capital of the Marshall Islands. Officials said they were waiting for more information and expected the man to be taken to the capital.

The government airline’s only plane that can land at Ebon is being repaired and will not fly for several days. Officials may send a boat to pick up the man. Fjeldstad said the man was staying at the local council house and a family was feeding him. He had a basic health check and had low blood pressure, but he did not seem to have any life-threatening problems. He could walk with help from men on the island. Fjeldstad said they were giving him lots of water and he was getting stronger.

Fraser Christian, who teaches sea survival in Dorset, said the story, if true, would be amazing but not the only one like it. He said it is possible to catch turtles or small fish by hand, because they may come close to a small boat to hide under it. He told how some people eat turtles for survival.

Christian said the biggest dangers would be too much sun and not enough water. He said water is more important than food. If there is no fresh water and it does not rain for a few days, a person may not survive. He also said a person’s body and mind matter, and some people can handle hard times better than others.

Survival stories in the Pacific Ocean are not rare. In 2006, three Mexicans were found drifting in a small fibreglass boat near the Marshall Islands. They said they survived for nine months by drinking rainwater and eating raw fish and seabirds, and by reading the Bible for hope. But Cliff Downing, who teaches sea survival to sailors, said he did not fully believe this new story. He said many dangers would make survival for so long very unlikely, and people would need to know more.

text:
Every morning, before it gets hot in India’s capital, a small group of old friends meet. On the dry grass near the India Gate monument in the centre of Delhi, they stretch, breathe, and meditate.

“It is the only healthy way to start the day. Much better than an egg or a sandwich or a cup of tea,” said Arvind Singh at 6.15am as he did breathing exercises on a bench.

Singh is a 42-year-old salesman. He and his friends are not the only ones. Across India, in crowded cities, people do the same thing in the green spaces that are left.

On 21 June, the new International Day of Yoga, Narendra Modi, India’s prime minister, hopes the world will join. The grass near India Gate will become the place for what they hope will be the biggest single yoga session ever. Up to 45,000 people may do a 35-minute routine.

The group will include 64-year-old Modi, most of his government, and, they hope, some celebrities. Officials have been sent to find volunteers from many countries to show it is an international event.

Getting Indians, and other people, to do yoga has become important for Modi. He led his Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) to a big election win in 2014. In May 2015, schools were told to make sure students went to yoga events planned for the big demonstration in Delhi, even though it is on a Sunday.

Officials have said yoga may become required for India’s police officers, who are known for being out of shape. They also said free daily yoga lessons would be offered to three million civil servants and their families. Air India, the national airline, said it will add yoga for trainee pilots. Some senior politicians also said that doing more yoga could reduce high levels of sexual violence in the country.

Modi is an ascetic. He is a committed vegetarian and he also practises yoga. He suggested an international yoga day when he spoke to the United Nations in New York in 2014.

He said, “Yoga is an invaluable gift of India’s ancient tradition. It brings together mind and body, thought and action, restraint and fulfilment, and harmony between people and nature. It is not only exercise. It is about feeling oneness with yourself, the world and nature.” He also said yoga could help in tackling climate change.

Yoga is between 3,000 and 6,000 years old. It began somewhere on the Indian subcontinent, possibly among religious ascetics. Its meditation and physical exercises have long been linked to local religions, including Buddhism and Jainism, and also Hinduism, which is practised by 80% of Indians.

Modi once worked as an organizer for a hardline Hindu nationalist organization. He has been criticized before for supporting a view of Indian culture that leaves little room for other traditions. One commentator called the 21 June event “a mix of cultural nationalism, commercialization and subtle coercion”.

Novelist Ajaz Ashraf wrote on India’s Scroll website that there is hope of bringing “modern-day gurus and their many followers” into the BJP, and that these followers are mostly the urban middle classes.

Other people point to a court ruling in the United States that said yoga is not always linked to religion. A court in California said that yoga may be religious in some situations, but yoga classes in the San Diego district were “devoid of any religious, mystical or spiritual trappings”. This happened after two parents said yoga in schools promoted Hinduism and held back Christianity.

Amish Tripathi, a writer of best-selling novels set 4,000 years ago in India that retell stories from Hindu mythology, said people in his books practise yoga.

He said that in ancient India, yoga was part of daily life, including both the body and the mind. He also said every culture gives something to the world, and yoga is India’s gift.

Suneel Singh, a guru in south Delhi, agreed that yoga does not belong to one religion. He said, “Is t’ai chi just Chinese? Is football just English? Same with yoga. It is a complete package for everybody’s body and a cheap way to keep you healthy.”

text:
The top-selling book on Amazon in the US is not Harper Lee’s second novel, Go Set a Watchman. It is not George RR Martin’s Song of Ice and Fire series. It is not Zoella’s Girl Online. Instead, a Scottish illustrator named Johanna Basford is number one with colouring books for adults. Her books are taking top places on Amazon.com’s bestseller lists.

Basford’s book Secret Garden has very detailed drawings of plants and animals. It has sold 1.4 million copies worldwide so far. Her new book, Enchanted Forest, has already sold just under 226,000 copies. Famous people also like the books. Zooey Deschanel shared a link about the book on Facebook. The South Korean pop star Kim Ki-Bum posted a picture on Instagram for his 1.6 million followers.

“It’s been crazy. The last few weeks since Enchanted Forest came out have been utter madness, but fantastic madness,” said Eleanor Blatherwick. She is head of sales and marketing at the publisher, a small British press called Laurence King. “We knew the books would be beautiful but we didn’t realize it would be such a phenomenal success.”

Basford is not the only person doing well from this. Many adults want something to colour. In the UK, Richard Merritt’s Art Therapy Colouring Book is fourth on Amazon’s bestseller lists. Millie Marotta’s Animal Kingdom is seventh. A mindfulness colouring book is ninth. Basford’s books are second and eighth. That means 5 of Amazon.co.uk’s top ten are colouring books for adults.

At the UK publisher Michael O’Mara, they have sold almost 340,000 adult colouring books so far. Ana McLaughlin, Head of Publicity, Marketing and Online, says the craze is happening because people now see these books as a way to relax. “The first one we did was in 2012, Creative Colouring for Grown-Ups. It sold strongly and reprinted but it was in 2014 that it all really mushroomed with Art Therapy. It really took off for us – selling the anti-stress angle gave people permission to enjoy something they might have felt was quite childish,” she said.

The Mindfulness Colouring Book pushes this idea strongly. Its publisher tells readers it is “filled with templates for exquisite scenes and intricate, sophisticated patterns,” and says it helps you meditate while you colour. It also tells people to “take a few minutes out of your day, wherever you are, and colour your way to peace and calm”.

“I think it is really relaxing to do something analogue, to unplug,” said Basford. “And it’s creative. For many people, a blank sheet is very daunting; with a colouring book, you just need to bring the colour. Also, there’s a bit of nostalgia there. So many people have said to me that they used to do secret colouring in when their kids were in bed. Now, it is socially acceptable; it’s a category of its own. These are books for adults. The art in my books is super intricate.”

Basford lives in Aberdeenshire. She has been surprised by the reaction since Secret Garden came out in 2013. “I had a kids’ book commissioned and I told them I would like to do one for grown-ups. It really wasn’t a trend then. I drew up the first story and they thought, ‘Let’s go for it’. I was thinking simply that people like me would like to do it. My intention was just to make a book I would like to have. So it’s been a real surprise to see the category bloom.”

Basford is now working on a third book. Michael O’Mara already has 17 adult colouring books for sale, and will increase this to 22 by May. New titles will include The Classic Comic Colouring Book and The Typography Colouring Book. “It’s just an enormous trend and shows no signs of slowing down,” said McLaughlin. She said buyers also like to show other people that they can colour neatly inside the lines.

“The pictures are all over Twitter and Instagram. People are really proud of them – they are so intricate,” she said. “You don’t have to have any artistic talent but what you create is unique. People send us pictures of them, framed and laminated. The appetite is simply enormous. I reckon people are taking their kids’ pictures off the fridge and replacing them with their own.”

text:
He liked the things many millionaires like. He owned a gold and silver Rolex and many expensive cars. He also liked modern art. But this Chinese businessman, who had several companies and a very large villa near Madrid, had almost no money in the bank. This made Spanish officials interested.

Gao Ping had lived most of his adult life in Spain. He controlled supplies for 4,000 Chinese bazaars in Spain. But officials thought he was not paying taxes on clothes, furniture, and other goods he brought in from China.

In 2012, police raided his warehouses. They found piles of cash. Bundles of €100, €200, and €500 notes were tied with rubber bands. About €12m was taken away in trolleys. It was the biggest cash seizure ever by Spanish police. The gang, with Gao as the suspected leader, is accused of washing up to €300m a year. They are also accused of selling fake goods and toys with false safety marks. A government prosecutor said Gao’s illegal business was so big it was hurting Spain’s ability to compete. Gao is out on bail. The case has not yet gone to trial.

Police have long worried about €500 notes. They are small and easy to carry for their value. People who avoid taxes, wash money, and drug bosses often use them. €1m in €500 notes can fit in a small laptop bag. The same amount in €50 notes would need a small suitcase. Some cash carriers have folded the notes into plastic pellets and swallowed them. A safer way is to hide the notes inside a car.

The UK stopped giving out €500 notes in 2010. It said people wanted them “almost entirely for criminal purposes”. In 2009, Italy’s central bank said mafia money washers and terrorists used the notes a lot. Other countries have limited large notes because of links to organized crime. Canada stopped its $1,000 note in 2000 after advice from law enforcement.

Today many people use electronic payments and contactless cards. More people now ask if printing these big notes makes sense. Peter Sands, the former head of Standard Chartered Bank, has asked to end high-value notes. He included the €500, the $100, the 1,000 Swiss Franc note, and the £50. In a report for the Harvard Kennedy School of Government, Sands said it was time to get rid of high-value notes that help “bad guys” with tax cheating, financial crime, terrorist funding, and corruption. He said criminals might switch to smaller bills, or to gold or diamonds. But those choices are bigger and easier to track, so criminals are more likely to be caught.

At a conference in London about terrorist funding, the Head of Europol, Rob Wainwright, asked the European Central Bank (ECB) to review if it “should continue to produce and circulate these notes” that make it easier for criminals and terrorists to hide their work and pay for illegal acts. Europol says the purple €500 note is 30% of the total value of all euro notes in use, even though most people have never seen one.

The €500 note started in 2002, when the euro began. It replaced the 1,000 Deutschmark, the 10,000 Belgian franc, and the 500,000 Italian lira. Some European countries liked big banknotes. Pia Hüttl, an affiliate fellow at the Bruegel thinktank, says this preference has existed for a long time. She says it comes from the idea that cash costs less and is accepted everywhere.

Cash is still used a lot in Germany and Austria. More than half of all payments there are made with notes and coins. Hans-Jürgen Papier, former president of Germany’s constitutional court, said limits on cash go against personal freedom. The tabloid newspaper Bild started a petition to defend cash, including the €500 note, saying “hands off my cash”.

Police are less sure about keeping these notes, because there are many reports of suspicious cash. In one case, two men went into a bank and tried to deposit €200,000 in torn and muddy €500 notes. In the same week, €1.3m in €500 notes was found in the false bottom of a suitcase. But suspicion alone is not enough to keep people in custody. Jennifer MacLeod, a specialist in Europol’s financial intelligence group, says it is often impossible to give judges enough proof that suspicious cash is linked to a crime. She says it is even harder because time is short and cooperation and information sharing are broken up.

Europol wants central banks to take more responsibility for strange patterns in €500 note use. For example, Luxembourg issued more than twice its yearly GDP in banknotes in 2013, even though it is one of Europe’s countries that uses cash the least. Europol asked Luxembourg’s central bank to explain. MacLeod says Luxembourg replied that it only gives out the notes people ask for and has no explanation for the demand. She says she is surprised the bank does not think it has responsibility here.

This may be changing. Mario Draghi, head of the ECB, said he does not want the money the bank earns from issuing notes to be “a comfort for criminals”. Other ECB leaders, like Yves Mersch, say there is no evidence about criminal use of the €500 note. But because fears about terrorism are stronger, this view may not convince people now. EU finance ministers asked policymakers to look for “appropriate restrictions” on high-value notes and report back by 1 May 2016.

text:
Young people in Cuba are rushing to use the first known free internet service that anyone can access. It is in Cuba, a communist island nation. A small cultural center in Havana has become a rare place with free wi-fi. The service is offered by a famous Cuban artist named Kcho. His spokesman said the state phone company, Etecsa, approved it.

The internet is very slow, especially when many people are there. But in Cuba, only about 5% of people are thought to have internet with no limits. So a place that is free and has no restrictions is seen as something very special.

People are excited because they can read world news, talk to family and friends in other countries, and use Facebook and Twitter. Adonis Ortiz, who is 20, said, “I come as often as I can,” while he video-chatted with his father in the US. He has not seen his father in nine years.

The US and Cuba are slowly easing a long freeze in talks and trade. People expect that American tech companies like Google and Apple may enter the Cuban market when they are allowed. Cuba has also put a high-speed fiber-optic cable under the sea from Venezuela. Internet users also have some access to Chinese equipment.

Another estimate says about one quarter of Cubans have internet access. But this includes people who use a restricted Cuban intranet with only certain websites and limited email.

Kcho has let the public use his own personal internet connection. Some people think he may be an approved figure for the government as Cuba slowly accepts that people want the internet.

Kcho has close ties to the Cuban government. He said the Ministry of Culture approved what he is doing. He said he wants Cubans to learn to use the internet. He told the Associated Press, “It is expensive but the benefit is tremendous. I have something that is great and powerful. I can share it and I am doing so.”

Kcho’s real name is Alexis Leiva Machado. He became famous around the world for his paintings, sculptures, and drawings after winning the grand prize at an important art biennial in South Korea. He is now getting ready for the Havana biennial in May. He was born on one of Cuba’s outlying islands. He is known for modern art with rustic, seaside, and patriotic themes.

In the center’s courtyard, many young people sit in wicker chairs under a white canopy, or outside when it is full. They use laptops, tablets, and smartphones.

Cuba has some of the lowest internet connection rates in the world. Dial-up accounts are tightly limited. Home broadband is almost never available, except for foreigners who pay hundreds of dollars each month. In Cuba, the average salary is about $17 to $20 a month. Kcho is estimated to pay $900 a month to provide the free wi-fi.

Since 2013, the Cuban government has opened hundreds of internet salons. One hour online costs $4.50, and the speed is much slower than at Kcho’s studio, which is around 2mbps. A 2014 report by Akamai Technologies said average internet speeds were about 10.5mbps in the US and 23.6mbps in South Korea. The world average was about 3.9mbps.

Because dozens of people use Kcho’s wi-fi at the same time, the signal gets weaker. One user said he sometimes goes in the middle of the night, when nobody else is there, and then it feels unbelievably fast.

text:
David Bowie died from cancer. Until the end, he could still surprise people. His last album, Blackstar, came out on his 69th birthday, 8 January 2016. It showed he could still make strong messages and unusual, sometimes upsetting music.

In the 1970s, Bowie led new music and fashion trends. In the late 1960s he did mime and cabaret shows. Then he became a singer-songwriter. He helped start glam-rock. Later he made a style he called “plastic soul”. After that he moved to Berlin and made new electronic music. He often changed his sound and his look, and he was very curious and thoughtful.

Bowie was born David Robert Jones in south London. In 1953, his family moved to Kent. David was good at singing and playing the recorder. Later he studied art, music, and design. In 1961, his mother bought him a plastic saxophone. This instrument appeared many times in his music.

When he was 15, David formed his first band, the Kon-rads. Soon it was clear he should work alone. He chose the name Bowie so people would not confuse him with Davy Jones of the Monkees.

His first album came out in June 1967 and was called David Bowie. In July 1969 he released the song Space Oddity. This was his first big success. It came out at the time of the Apollo 11 moon landing and reached the top five in the UK.

In March 1970, Bowie married an art student, Angela Barnett. His music career kept growing. The Man Who Sold the World came out in the US in late 1970 and in the UK the next year. It had bold songs and a dark hard-rock sound. It talked about immortality, insanity, murder, and mysticism, which were not usual pop topics.

In 1972 he released Hunky Dory, with detailed and wordy songs. It was very good but only had moderate success. Later that year, The Rise and Fall of Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars changed everything.

With this album, Bowie became a science-fiction character: a glam-rock star from space visiting a doomed Earth. The song Starman made the album a big hit. His stage clothes and daring shows made fans very excited, like the excitement seen during Beatlemania.

Bowie kept having success. His album Aladdin Sane (1973) was his first UK number 1 album. It included the hit songs The Jean Genie and Drive-in Saturday. But he was already planning new changes.

His interest in funk and soul was clear on Young Americans (1975). It gave him a US number 1 song, Fame, with John Lennon as a guest singer.

Station to Station (1976) introduced a new character, the Thin White Duke. Bowie also used this character in Nicolas Roeg’s film The Man Who Fell to Earth, where he played a sad space traveller.

Bowie’s marriage was falling apart, and he and Angela divorced in 1980. That year he had more success with the album Scary Monsters (and Super Creeps) and the number 1 song Ashes to Ashes. He also performed on Broadway as John Merrick in The Elephant Man.

In 1981 he worked with Queen on Under Pressure, which reached number 1. He also worked more across music and film. He appeared in the German movie Christiane F (1981) and wrote music for the soundtrack. He had another hit with Cat People (Putting Out Fire) from Paul Schrader’s movie Cat People (1982).

Bowie acted in more films, including The Hunger and the war drama Merry Christmas, Mr Lawrence, both in 1983. In music, 1983 was the year of his biggest push for mainstream success with the album Let’s Dance and concerts. Let’s Dance made him a worldwide rock star. The album and its songs Let’s Dance, China Girl, and Modern Love became huge international hits.

MTV was very popular then, and Bowie’s eye-catching videos helped. His six-month Serious Moonlight tour drew very large crowds. This was the most commercially successful time in his career.

In 1985 his fame grew again when he performed at the Live Aid concert at Wembley Stadium. He was one of the best performers there. He also recorded Dancing in the Street with Mick Jagger, and it quickly reached number 1.

In April 1992, a few days after he appeared at the Freddie Mercury tribute concert at Wembley Stadium, Bowie married the Somalian model Iman. They bought a home in New York. This new start in his private life came with a search for new musical ideas.

On Black Tie White Noise (1993), he mixed in soul, electronica, and hip hop. The album reached number 1 in the UK and had a top 10 single, Jump They Say.

New media and technology also affected his music. His 1999 album Hours… used music he wrote for the computer game Omikron. In the game, Bowie and Iman appeared as characters. In August 2000, Bowie and Iman’s daughter, Alexandria, was born.

Living in New York, Bowie opened the Concert for New York City in October 2001. He performed with Paul McCartney, Jon Bon Jovi, Billy Joel, the Who, and Elton John. This benefit show happened six weeks after the 9/11 attacks.

Bowie made the album Reality the next year. During the Reality tour in 2004, he had chest pains while performing in Germany. He had emergency surgery in Hamburg to clear a blocked artery.

He took this as a warning and slowed down. In 2006, he said he would take a year off from touring and recording. In February 2006,

text:
David Mitchell is a British writer. He is often in the running for the Man Booker Prize. Critics often study his novels closely. Mitchell says it is a relief that his newest work will not be seen by anyone until the year 2114.

Mitchell is the second writer to join the Future Library project, made by Scottish artist Katie Paterson. In 2014, 1,000 trees were planted in the Nordmarka forest near Oslo, Norway. The first writer was Margaret Atwood. In 2015 she gave a manuscript called Scribbler Moon. Each year for 100 years, one writer will give a piece of writing. No one will read these texts until 2114. In 2114 the trees will be cut down to make paper, and the 100 texts will be printed.

Each year the next writer’s name will be announced. A group of experts and Paterson choose the writers while she is alive. The writers travel to a place in the forest above Oslo and hand over their manuscripts in a short ceremony.

Mitchell said the project gives him hope during a time of very sad news. He said it suggests civilization might still be here in 100 years. He said many things make people feel doomed, but the Future Library shows a possible future. It brings hope that humans are stronger than we think, and that there will still be trees, books, readers, and civilization.

Mitchell said writing the book felt freeing because he will not be alive to see what people think of it. But he said he is between Margaret Atwood and other great writers, so his work should be good. He said he would look like a historic fool if people opened it in 2114 and it was not good.

Mitchell was shortlisted for the Man Booker Prize for his novels number9dream and Cloud Atlas. He said he usually polishes his writing again and again, and even too much. But this time was different. He wrote until the last minute. The first two-thirds were polished, but he did not have time to polish the final third. He said that was also freeing.

Paterson asked the writers to write about imagination and time, in any way they wanted. At the ceremony in the Norwegian woods, Mitchell shared only the title of his manuscript: From Me Flows What You Call Time. The title comes from a piece of music by Japanese composer Toru Takemitsu. Mitchell would not say more, except that the work was more substantial than he expected.

In the forest, Mitchell gave his text while it rained on and off and he used an umbrella. Around him were small pine trees growing. He read the audience, including children and adults, a short story and the poem A slumber did my spirit seal by William Wordsworth. The poem ends with the lines “Rolled round in earth’s diurnal course / With rocks, and stones, and trees”. This felt right in that small part of the forest, with blueberry bushes on the ground. The forest will be cared for during the next 98 years, before it is used to make the Future Library manuscripts.

In a piece he wrote for the Future Library, Mitchell said it seems foolish to think his writing will matter to future generations. But he also said it is quiet and modest to work so hard on a manuscript when nobody will ever praise you and say, “Nice one,” or “I loved the bit where she did that and he did this”.

Mitchell has now delivered his manuscript. It will be sealed and stored with Atwood’s in a wood-lined room in Oslo’s new public library, which will open in 2019. A trust of experts will watch over it until it is printed. Mitchell said it is now as far from him as “a coin dropped in a river”.
tier2_proxy_low_frequency_types: above; expected; ground; group; shared; study; substantial

text:
Some cities have pigeons. Lima has black vultures, also called gallinazos. They fly in circles above the city in groups. They sit on famous buildings, like old churches and old buildings in the city centre. With their wrinkled heads and small eyes, they remind many people of things they do not want to see: neglect, poverty, and dirt.

But now these birds are being used to help the city. Environmental authorities are trying to improve the birds’ image. They put GoPro cameras and GPS trackers on them. The birds get a new job: helping fight illegal rubbish dumping.

Samuel is one of ten certified disease-free black vultures (Coragyps atratus) in the project. He does what vultures do well: find rubbish. With his tracker, he is released above the city. He finds secret rubbish dumps and records their GPS locations on a live map.

Samuel’s trainer at Lima’s Huachipa Zoo is Alfredo Correa. He says vultures can eat dead animals because their bodies protect them from viruses and bacteria. He says they have some of the strongest gut flora in nature.

This work is a project between USAID and the Peruvian Environment Ministry to deal with Lima’s rubbish problem. Other vultures have names like Capitan Huggin, Capitan Fenix (named after a mythological creature that rises from ashes), and Capitana Aella (“Whirlwind”). A playful video uses a dramatic voice and says the vultures are fighting sickness and disease while “humanity is calmly ignoring the danger.”

The project has a serious message. Lima has nearly ten million people but only four landfills. Because of this, there are many illegal dumps. The Environment Ministry says one fifth of the rubbish ends up in these illegal places. Water from the waste pollutes Lima’s main water source, the Rimac river. It also pollutes the Chillon and Lurin rivers, which flow into the Bay of Lima.

The environmental supervision agency, OEFA, says three poorer neighbourhoods have the most fly-tipped rubbish, even though they have only 12% of Lima’s population. These areas are Villa Maria del Triunfo (39.4%), Villa El Salvador (25.3%), and El Agustino (18.3%).

Part of the problem is unpaid taxes. Many residents, especially in the barrios, do not pay. This means some of the 43 district municipalities do not have enough money for basic services like rubbish collection.

It also means that even when vultures find illegal rubbish, it may not get cleaned up. “We share the vulture’s GPS coordinates with the municipalities,” says Javier Hernandez, the project director. “It’s their job to collect the rubbish and to try and change the habits of their residents.”

The project also wants citizens to be “vultures on the ground.” It asks people to report illegal dumping, make less waste, and recycle. Some residents are helping by posting photos of illegal dumps on the project’s Twitter feed and Facebook page.

The idea began at the 2014 United Nations Climate Change Conference in Lima. Hernandez says they wanted ways to involve not only authorities but also citizens, to build environmental awareness. Artist Cristina Planas put huge vulture heads on top of 25 dead palm trees in a wetland reserve in the south of the city. Rich residents disliked it and thought it was ugly, but Planas asked citizens to “adopt a vulture” to support conservation and recycling.

Planas said people are a little scared of how vultures look. But she said the vulture is really helping people. She said it is “recycling” in the last place where people threw their rubbish.
