text:
Europe will be the first place in the world to make car makers do “real-world” emissions tests. These new rules will check how much pollution cars make when they drive on real roads and in traffic. This is different from today’s tests, which happen in lab-like conditions.

The European Commission Vice President, Frans Timmermans, approved the plan. The tests aim to enforce a limit of 80mg of nitrogen oxide per kilometre. Researchers say only one car out of 16 meets this level. Other countries, like China and Korea, are also thinking about real-world tests, and they will watch what Europe does.

Pollution from diesel engines, such as nitrogen oxide, carbon monoxide, and tiny particles, is believed to cause at least one quarter of the 29,000 pollution-related deaths each year in the UK. This number may rise when the committee on the medical effects of air pollutants publishes “strengthening evidence” later in 2015 about harm to public health from nitrogen oxide.

The current lab test is called the “New European Drive Cycle.” It is 25 years old and has not kept up with new car technology. Studies say lab tests can be made easier in unfair ways. For example, people can tape up doors and windows to reduce air resistance, drive on very smooth roads, and test at unusually high temperatures. Campaigners also say car makers program some vehicles to go into a low-emissions mode during lab tests, when the front wheels spin but the back wheels do not.

Lucia Caudet, a Commission spokesperson, said the Commission is finishing a proposal for a new testing method that will properly check cars in real driving. The plan still needs approval from other commissioners and a technical committee, but an EU source said they do not expect major problems.

Greg Archer from Transport and Environment said a key reason why air pollution kills 400,000 citizens each year is that car makers cheat diesel tests, so cars pollute much more on the road. He said real-world tests are an important step to reduce city air pollution, and EU states should support the proposals.

Research from the International Council on Clean Transportation in 2014 said real nitrogen oxide emissions from cars are seven times higher than the 80mg/km standard. Some models are 22 times above the recommended limit. Only one car out of 16 met the target. About one third of all nitrogen oxide pollution comes from road transport, mostly diesel. In cities, this can rise as high as 64%, according to the European Environment Agency.

Campaigners say the car industry has tried to delay changes to test cycles. Industry groups deny this and say they need five years for technical and economic reasons. Cara McLaughlin from the European Automobile Manufacturers Association (ACEA) said Real Driving Emissions (RDE) is a new rule that could require big changes to emission control hardware during a car model’s production lifetime. She said ACEA accepts that RDE will apply to new types of cars from September 2017.

ACEA sent Timmermans its own draft regulation to consider. This happened after EU representatives agreed a regulation to enforce nitrogen oxide limits with stronger road trials and strict monitoring of exhaust fumes. ACEA’s draft would have covered fewer pollutants and delayed the step-by-step start until 2020. It would also have made tests shorter, from 1,300m to under 700m, raised the minimum temperature from -7C to -3C, and used more rural roads. Archer said lobbyists pushed hard, and he called it an attempt to weaken the lawmaking process.

After Timmermans seemed to reject ACEA’s proposal, the regulation will go to commissioners for a rubber stamp and then to member states for final changes, before a planned start in September. By 2017, the first real-world car emissions tests are expected to begin seriously. Because there will also be debate about CO2 testing at the end of 2015, and other countries have similar debates, many people will watch the EU’s new emissions tests closely.

text:
It was a nice summer evening. I decided to swim near Doolin Pier in County Clare, Ireland. I moved there in 2012. A woman was in the water with Dusty, a dolphin. Dusty often swims with a group of people.

Dusty came to Doolin around 2008. Hundreds of people have swum with her, so she seemed very tame. She was even in an Irish tourist ad where a girl says she wants to touch a dolphin.

That evening, the woman was tickling Dusty’s belly. About 20 tourists and local people watched from the pier. After I got into the water, Dusty left the woman and suddenly went wild. Later I learned she can be very protective of her space when she is with someone. Her tail was flapping hard. At first I thought she was just showing off, but then I thought she might be angry.

I tried to swim back to the pier to get out. But very fast, Dusty hit me with her nose. It was very strong and it hurt a lot. I was thrown forward.

People on the pier stared at me. Dusty stayed close, still flapping her tail. That scared me the most. I thought if she hit me with her tail, I could go under the water.

I reached the pier, but I could not climb out because I was hurt. I was very scared. I shouted for help. A man reached down and pulled me out onto the steps. Then another man came. He said he was an orthopaedic surgeon who specialized in marine trauma. He had been driving into Doolin and saw what was happening.

I was very cold and worried. I did not know how bad my injuries were. I was most afraid of internal bleeding. The surgeon checked me and said he could not feel signs of internal bleeding, but he thought I might have broken bones. Later I learned I had six spinal fractures, three broken ribs, and a damaged lung.

I stayed in the hospital for five days. I wore a back brace for weeks. I could not work for five months. I had trouble moving, and I had stiffness and pain. Then doctors said I had post-traumatic stress. I felt anxious about everything and overreacted. I thought people were looking at me the wrong way. Loud noises were hard for me. I also had memory loss.

Three months before the accident, I opened a health-food shop. But I had to give it up because I could not work. It was the hardest year. Now it is behind me. I had craniosacral therapy, osteopathy, and massage. Now I am building my own osteopathy practice. I understand patients more because I have been a patient.

I am thankful I am healthy. I want to stop other people from getting hurt. Many people trust dolphins and think they are always friendly. Who would think a dolphin could attack a person? If you see a dangerous animal with its teeth showing, you feel scared. But dolphins have a wide smile.

I am not angry at Dusty. I respect her. But I was in her area, and she is a wild and unpredictable animal. People need to know that. Many people come to swim with her and do not understand the danger. My injuries were said to be one of several injuries that summer.

After the man pulled me out, Dusty swam away. Then she came back and floated upright next to me, looking at me. We looked into each other’s eyes. I felt she was sorry. She seemed like a different dolphin. The anger was gone. The people on the pier were amazed. After that moment, the terror ended for me. I made peace with her.

text:
Many people know about animals in danger, like tigers in India, orangutans in Indonesia, and pandas. People do not want these animals to disappear. But scientists now worry that the biggest danger may be closer to home. Even small animals like ladybirds could be very important for nature.

Climate change, fewer animals, more humans, and many species dying out are making scientists say we are in the Anthropocene. This means humans strongly control Earth’s natural systems.

WWF’s Living Planet Index (LPI) 2014 showed a very serious problem. It said the number of vertebrates (animals with backbones) fell by 52% over four decades. Biodiversity loss is now at “critical levels”. Some groups have lost even more. Freshwater species fell by 76% over the same time.

Scientists are especially worried about animals that help nature work. Three quarters of the world’s food production is thought to depend on bees and other pollinators, like hoverflies. Worms help by breaking down waste and turning it into nutrients in the soil. Bats catch mosquitoes and help keep malaria rates down. A North America study said the loss of pest control from declining bats could cost more than $22bn in lost agricultural productivity.

Dr Nick Isaac, a macroecologist at the Centre for Ecology & Hydrology in Oxfordshire, said losing common species will affect people more than losing rare ones. He said Britain’s insects and other invertebrates are declining as fast as vertebrates, and this could have “serious consequences for humanity”. He also said it is not only about how many species go extinct, but also about how many animals are left in each population.

He said that 23 to 36% of all birds, mammals, and amphibians used for food or medicine are now threatened with extinction. In many places, wild animals are an important food source, especially for poor people.

Many people agree the main cause is unsustainable human consumption. This damages ecosystems, creates climate change, and destroys habitats faster than people thought. It is not only big animals we should worry about, but also smaller creatures we depend on, like insects and worms. They may not die out soon, but if their numbers drop, it will affect everyone. Isaac said the problem is not simple.

Sir David Attenborough said in 2013 that humans are a “plague on earth”. But WWF says there is still time to stop the damage. WWF UK Chief Executive David Nussbaum said this report should be a wake-up call. He said politicians, businesses, and all people must act to protect a healthy future for people and nature.

Nussbaum said humans are cutting down trees faster than they can regrow. We are catching more fish than the oceans can replace. We are taking water from rivers and aquifers faster than rain can refill them. We are also sending out more carbon than the oceans and forests can absorb.

text:
Canadian tennis player Frank Dancevic criticized Australian Open organizers. He said they made players play in “inhumane” heat. He collapsed on court when the temperature reached 41C.

Dancevic collapsed in the second set of his first-round match against France’s Benoît Paire. It happened on uncovered court six at Melbourne Park. He passed out for about a minute. He said the conditions were dangerous. He also said the heat made him hallucinate: “I was dizzy from the middle of the first set and then I saw Snoopy.”

Dancevic said it was not fair to players, fans, or the sport when people were pulling out of matches or passing out. He said he had played many long matches, and passing out after about a set and a half was not normal. He said players complained it was too hot, but the tournament still kept matches going. He said he and many other players did not think it was fair.

Other players agreed. Britain’s number one, Andy Murray, said it should be looked at. He said one bad event could happen, and it looks terrible when players, ball kids, and people in the stands collapse. He said the heat was very tough when he practiced before his match around 2.30–3pm. He said he did not know if it was safe, and he mentioned problems in other sports like heart attacks.

Caroline Wozniacki said she put a water bottle on the court and it started melting underneath the plastic. John Isner said it felt like opening an oven. Victoria Azarenka said it felt very hot, like “dancing in a frying pan.”

The tournament used its “extreme heat” plan for women’s matches on Tuesday. This allowed an extra ten-minute break between the second and third sets. But under a 2014 rule change, the tournament director, Wayne McKewen, decides whether to stop matches.

Organizers did not only use the Celsius temperature. They preferred the Wet Bulb Globe Temperature, which also includes humidity and wind. Organizers said temperatures peaked at 42.2C early Tuesday evening. They said the conditions never reached the level where play would be stopped.

McKewen said they must reach a minimum level and have a forecast that the heat will last long enough. He said that did not happen. He said it was hot and uncomfortable, but low humidity meant play would continue.

After medical help, Dancevic continued the match but lost 7–6, 6–3, 6–4. He said he was close to stopping. He said he was not tired, but his body temperature felt too high.

Earlier, a ball boy needed medical help after collapsing during Milos Raonic’s match on uncovered court eight. Raonic beat Daniel Gimeno-Traver 7–6, 6–1, 4–6, 6–2. After that, the tournament shortened ball boy rotations to 45-minute shifts. China’s Peng Shuai said the heat made her cramp and vomit. After losing 7–5, 4–6, 6–3 to Japan’s Kurumi Nara, she had to be helped off the court.

Officials said the health risks were not as serious as they seemed. They said most matches finished without medical calls. Tim Wood, the tournament’s chief medical officer, said a few players had heat illness or discomfort, but none needed major medical treatment after finishing.

Many players followed Roger Federer’s view that the conditions were hard but the same for both players. He said it is “a mental thing,” and if you cannot handle it, you quit.

Dancevic disagreed. He said it is not only about fitness. He said some players’ bodies handle heat better than others. He said it was dangerous. He also said that an hour and a half after his match, he still could not pee.

text:
Early in the day, police carried out new dawn raids at the Baur Au Lac hotel in Zurich. Later that day, 16 football officials were charged in the United States with corruption crimes. This included five current or former members of FIFA’s executive committee. They included the former Brazilian federation chief Ricardo Teixeira and his replacement, Marco Polo Del Nero, who recently stepped down from the FIFA executive committee.

The 16 people were accused by the US Department of Justice of fraud and other crimes. The new charges were written in a new 240-page document that replaced the earlier one from May 2015. The total number of defendants charged by the US became 27. The document also mentioned 24 unnamed “co-conspirators,” including former FIFA executive committee members. US Attorney General Loretta Lynch said the betrayal of trust was outrageous and the corruption described was completely unacceptable.

That same day, FIFA’s executive committee had hoped to show new reforms during an ongoing corruption crisis. Instead, Swiss police led away Juan Ángel Napout, president of the South American football confederation, and Alfredo Hawit, head of the North and Central American and Caribbean body. Hawit replaced Jeffrey Webb in May 2015 after Webb was arrested in the US operation. That operation pushed FIFA into crisis and led to the downfall of Sepp Blatter. Webb’s predecessor, Jack Warner, was also seized in May.

The Swiss Federal Office of Justice said the arrested men were being held while waiting for extradition. It said the US suspects them of taking bribes worth millions of dollars. Webb and Luis Bedoya, a former FIFA executive committee member from Colombia, were among those who pleaded guilty in the US. Lynch said that eight people had pleaded guilty since May, and five of them were not named in the original charges.

So far, 11 current and former members of FIFA’s executive committee have been charged in the investigation. The investigation says there were $200m in bribes. Most were kickbacks from TV and marketing contracts, and some were linked to FIFA development programmes. The last three presidents of the regional bodies CONCACAF and Conmebol have all been charged.

Lynch warned that people still hiding should not think they can avoid the investigation. Teixeira, who was once the son-in-law of former FIFA president João Havelange, was charged along with Del Nero and José Maria Marin, who was charged in May.

In May 2015, 14 men were charged, and four more guilty pleas were entered. A few days later, Blatter won a fifth term as president, but later agreed to step down as the crisis grew. He was then suspended, along with UEFA President Michel Platini, over an alleged £1.3m “disloyal payment” to Platini. Both men could face life bans when their case is heard by the FIFA ethics committee in December if they are found guilty.

Others charged on Thursday included Rafael Salguero from Guatemala, who left the executive committee in May; Eduardo Deluca, a former Secretary General of the South American Confederation; Manuel Burga, Peru’s former football federation president; and Carlos Chaves, Bolivia’s football president, who was already in jail in his own country.

Lynch said the Department of Justice is committed to ending the corruption described in international football leadership. She said this is because of the size of the schemes, how wide the operation was, and because this behaviour goes against international principles.

Acting FIFA President Issa Hayatou did not comment on the details of the arrests. But he said neither he nor the organization was corrupt. Speaking to the media for the first time since becoming acting president in September 2015, when Blatter was suspended, Hayatou said the crisis was caused by only a few people. “FIFA is not corrupt. We have individuals that have shown negative behaviour. Do not generalize the situation,” said Hayatou, who has been president of the Confederation of African Football for more than 25 years. He said many people have been in FIFA for 20 or 30 years and have not been accused of anything.

text:
Some people call it “the hotel of mum and dad”. This means living with your parents as an adult. Because of the housing crisis, many young adults are staying at home until they are at least 26. About one in five do not pay any money to live there.

A survey showed that the number of adults living at home is different in different places. It was just under 9% in the East Midlands. It was more than double that in London, where house prices and rents are the highest. The survey also found that 20% of adults living at home paid nothing at all.

Many young adults have low wages, and rents have gone up to record highs. People who want to buy a home often cannot save enough because renting costs so much each month. Research by the homeless charity Shelter showed that half of tenants could not save any money for a deposit. A quarter could only save £100 or less each month. Interest rates are very low, so mortgages can be cheaper than before, but the best mortgage deals are for people with large deposits.

Because of this, more young adults are moving back home to save money. Parents who cannot give their children a large amount of money still often want to help. The survey found that 28% of adults lived at home to save for a deposit. But it also found that 30% were not saving any money.

A spokesman for the company that did the survey said the “hotel of mum and dad” is staying open longer than many people expected. High rent costs and the need to save for a deposit mean some young adults have to wait longer before moving out. For some, moving out may never be possible.

Michael Day, 30, lives with his parents in Bristol. He says he feels stuck between paying high rent and saving for a deposit. Rents for a one-bedroom home in Bristol are between £500 and £800 a month. Buying a similar home would cost about £130,000. He said he does not want to rent because rent can cost more than a mortgage. But it is hard to get a mortgage because you need a big deposit.

Day does not want to live with strangers, so he has fewer choices. At home, he pays a small rent to help with bills. He keeps the rest of his pay from his job at a candle shop. He plays golf at county level. He says he spends extra money on golf and holidays instead of saving. He said he will need to save for a long time. Because saving has been so hard, he has been going on holiday and enjoying it.

Sue Green, from Saga, a company that sells insurance and products to people over 50, said many parents did not plan to have their children living at home into their 20s or 30s. She said many parents are happy to let them live at home without rent because it could help them buy a home sooner. She said some children who do not pay rent still help in other ways, like buying groceries, buying family takeaways, or doing small jobs around the home.

Angus Hanton, co-founder of a thinktank called the Intergenerational Foundation, said older generations were “the architects of the housing crisis” and young people should not be blamed for living at home. He said average incomes for under-30s have fallen about 20% since the 2008 downturn. He said rents and car insurance have never been so high. He also said mortgage lending rules have become stricter for young people, but not for older buy-to-let investors, who push young people out of the market. He said student-fee debt is rising quickly, and many jobs are zero-hour or short-term contracts. He said we should support young people so they can afford to build their own lives.

Jenna Gavin, 29, lives in Southport, Merseyside, in the home where she grew up. She moved out for one year to go to university, but she has lived with her parents since then. She works nearby as a medical receptionist and wants to stay in the area. Renting a one-bedroom flat would cost more than £420 a month before bills, which would take a large part of her pay. She said she does not want to rent because she would spend a lot of money and have nothing at the end. She has looked at buying and talked to mortgage advisers, but she cannot borrow enough.

Gavin is trying to save, but it is hard to save enough. She said even a 5% deposit is a lot of money, and she would like to pay more than that. Her parents are happy not to charge her rent. She said they want her to save, and she helps in other ways by bringing food home and doing things around the house.

Gavin gets along with her parents and has her own space in a room she moved into when she was 14. She said she always thought she would have her own home by the time she was 30. She said she does not think that will happen because she will be 30 next year. But she hopes she will move out in a couple of years.

text:
Women were often kept out of important roles in football. This changed when France chose its first female professional team manager. It did not matter that the club was in the second division. Some men said it was only a trick to get attention for a small team, Clermont Foot 63, which was 14th out of 20. But the key point was that Helena Costa got the top job.

Her appointment made football history. She became the first woman to be hired as a manager in the top two divisions of any professional European league. Véronique Soulier, who leads the club’s supporters’ group, said she was happy. She said she was surprised at first, but then most people agreed it was good news. She said a woman leading a group of men can be a good thing.

Clermont Foot 63 plays at Clermont-Ferrand in the Auvergne region of south-central France. The average home crowd is about 3,800. Costa used to work as a talent spotter for Celtic in the Scottish Premiership. Costa is 36. She was born in Alhandra, on the River Tagus in southeast Portugal. She has a master’s degree in sports science and is a UEFA-licensed coach. She coached Benfica’s male youth teams. She also coached the Qatar women’s team and led them to their first international win in 2012. More recently, she coached the Iranian women’s national team, which she left in September 2013.

Costa got a two-year contract from the club president, Claude Michy, who often gets attention for his club. In 2013, he said the team had signed Messi. They did sign someone named Messi, but it was not Lionel Messi. It was Junior Messi Enguene, a 20-year-old midfielder from Cameroon.

France’s women’s minister, Najat Vallaud-Belkacem, tweeted: “Bravo to Clermont Foot for understanding that giving women a place is the future of professional football.”

Carolina Morace, an Italian, was the only woman before Costa to coach a men’s professional team. Morace said she did not know Costa, but if a team hired her, it means she can do the job. She said she hopes one day this will seem normal. Morace was also a great player. She was the top scorer for 12 seasons in Italy’s Serie A and played 153 international games for Italy. In 1999, she became coach of Viterbese in the men’s Serie C1. After two games, she quit after a conflict with the club owner, Luciano Gaucci, who also owned Perugia at the time.

Morace said she refused Gaucci’s demand that she fire her assistant and the team’s trainer. Gaucci said she could keep working with who she wanted, but Morace said trust was gone and she did not want to work in that uncertain situation. Today, Morace works as a lawyer in Rome and as a football commentator on TV and for the daily Gazzetta dello Sport.

Morace said there are still too many men working in football, even in the women’s game, even when they do not have the same skill as women who are not working. Raymond Domenech, former manager of the French national team, said women know how to play football and how to manage teams. He asked why women should not manage men’s teams. He said the reverse happens and no one complains. He said it is a natural choice and shows that women are equal to men. He praised President Michy and said that if he managed a club again, he would hire a woman as his number two, but Michy did it first.

Clermont Foot 63’s website said Costa’s appointment would bring “a new era.” On the supporters’ website, people reacted with surprise and some doubt. One fan said it was only a publicity trick and she would not last the season. The fan said it was hard to believe she would earn the players’ respect, especially since she is the same age as the oldest players. Another fan said her experience was not bad, but asked if she would be good enough. A third fan wished her well but said it might be hard for a woman to succeed in such a macho business, and asked if the president had made a media move.

Soulier had hope. She said the club might find the motivation it is missing. She said the boys on the team can be hard to manage, and with a woman in charge, maybe they will be less demanding.

But Costa may be the one who makes demands. She once did work experience at Chelsea during José Mourinho’s first time as manager there, from 2004 to 2007. Someone reportedly called her “Mourinho in a skirt.” Costa rejected that sexist comment. She said that like Mourinho, she always wants to win. She said she was happy to be compared with him in that way.

tier2_proxy_matched_types: central; conflict; degree; demand; equal; european; experience; female; future; group; history; important; international; male; natural; professional; region; rejected; science; understanding; working

text:
For 85 years, Pluto looked like a small grey blur on school maps. But on 15 July, Pluto was seen clearly for the first time. The new pictures showed big mountain ranges made of solid water ice, as large as the Alps or the Rockies.

These special pictures of Pluto and its big moon, Charon, were sent about 4bn miles back to Earth from the New Horizons spacecraft. This was the high point of a mission that has been running quietly for almost ten years.

Alan Stern, the mission’s main scientist, said the pictures were a “home run” for the team. He said the results were amazing and that Pluto and Charon were “mind-blowing.”

One big surprise was that there are mountains in the Kuiper belt. The Kuiper belt is the solar system’s “third zone,” where Pluto sits with about 100,000 smaller icy objects. John Spencer, a mission scientist, said the mountains seem to be about 11,000ft high and several hundred miles wide.

A close picture of one edge of Pluto showed no craters at all. This suggests the surface was covered over not long ago by geological activity. This activity could include geysers that shoot ice into the air, or cryo-volcanoes that erupt with ice instead of melted rock.

At a NASA press conference, people talked about Pluto’s old status as the ninth planet. Pluto was changed to a dwarf planet in 2006. NASA spokesman Dwayne Brown said they had brought a blurry point of light into focus.

Stern said these pictures were only the start of what scientists will learn in the next year. Scientists think the mountains are made from water ice, with only a thin top layer of “exotic” ices: methane and nitrogen. Spencer said you cannot make mountains from methane and nitrogen.

Spencer said water ice is strong enough to hold up large mountains. The methane and nitrogen are just a coating. The mountains on Pluto may have formed no more than 100m years ago, which is very recent compared to the 4.56bn-year-old solar system. The area seen up close is about 1% of Pluto’s surface, and it may still be geologically active. These are the first pictures to show ice mountains outside the moons of giant planets. Scientists now wonder what process made this mountain land.

The mountain shapes, and the smooth surface, suggest that activity is filling in holes made by asteroid hits. Scientists think this “paving” could come from heat inside Pluto that softens rock and ice, or from snowfalls that cover the ground. The pictures are so clear that, if the spacecraft were flying over London, we could see the runways at Heathrow airport.

Pluto is very far away, about 5bn km. Because of this distance, New Horizons needs hours to send just one picture back to Earth. It will take 16 months to send all the data from the fly-by. The team also said the heart-shaped area on Pluto will be called the Tombaugh Regio, to honour Clyde Tombaugh, who discovered Pluto in 1930.

The new view of Charon shows many different kinds of land. An area of cliffs and long dips that stretches about 1,000km suggests big cracks in Charon’s surface. This could also be caused by geological activity. The picture also shows a huge canyon that may be 7 to 9km deep.

Cathy Olkin, a mission scientist, said the new Charon image greatly excited the team. She said there is a lot of science in just this one picture. Scientists think Pluto is about two thirds rock, covered in a lot of ice, with surface temperatures near minus 230C. The mission cost £460m. As New Horizons continues into the Kuiper belt, scientists hope it will help them learn about the ancient solar system and how planets formed, and it may help explain how Earth formed.

Andrew Coates, head of planetary science at the Mullard Space Science Laboratory, said Kuiper belt objects are the building blocks of the outer solar system. He said they are very cold, like a deep freeze, which helps keep solar system history.

In August 2015, mission scientists will pick which of two objects to visit next. NASA says the spacecraft may keep recording and sending data until the mid-2030s. After that, its plutonium power will run out. It will shut down and drift toward the edge of the solar system and into deep space.

New Horizons also watched smaller parts of the Pluto system. Pluto has four other moons: Nix, Hydra, Styx and Kerberos. A new early picture of Hydra showed its uneven shape and its size, about 43 by 33km. John Grunsfeld, from NASA’s Science Mission Directorate, said New Horizons is a real mission of exploration and shows why basic scientific research matters.

He said the mission had nine years to build hopes about what it would see near Pluto and Charon. Now scientists have the first sample of the scientific “treasure” collected at the closest time, and it is even better than they expected.

The observations also suggest Hydra’s surface is probably covered with water ice. Future pictures will give more clues about how this moon, and the other moon, formed billions of years ago.

text:
At the start of the year, many people feel unhappy about work. The next holiday feels far away, and it is dark in the morning and when you get home. If you are in a job you do not like, you might want to update your CV and look for a new job. But before you do that, try to think about things in a different way. What other jobs could you be doing instead? We asked five people with unusual jobs how much they are paid, what the worst parts are, and why they enjoy their work.

1. Dog-food taster  
The job: Tasting dog food to check it meets a premium brand’s quality standards.

What it involves: Opening sample tins from each new batch of dog (or cat) food, smelling it, and eating it. Philip Wells, the chief taster for Lily’s Kitchen pet food, says taste is an important check to make sure the ingredients are balanced in the right way. He says tasting also helps him notice small differences in how the food is cooked, especially the dry food.

Typical salary: £20,000 for an entry-level job in the quality department. Wells says £50,000 or more is possible for an experienced technical director. This person tastes products, develops new recipes, and advises the business on technical and regulatory matters.

Worst part of the job: The deadlines. Wells says he actually likes the food. Under the Animal Feed Regulations 2010, the meat used in pet food must come from animals passed as fit for human consumption. He says the firm uses “human-grade freshly prepared raw food” in its recipes. He adds that some pet foods in the market are very unpleasant, and the smell can make him feel sick when he does market research.

Job satisfaction: “No two days are ever the same.” Wells says it feels good to work on projects that help pets become happier and healthier. He also says another important member of the tasting team is Lily, the border terrier.

2. Hygiene technician  
The job: Disinfecting places that might have been exposed to bio-hazardous situations.

What it involves: Cleaning up crime scenes, road accidents, and suicides. Cleaning hoarders’ houses full of rubbish, rats, and excrement, and other things. Richard Lewis, a hygiene technician for Rentokil, says the job is about keeping people safe. He says they work in very dirty places.

Typical salary: Around £14,500 for entry level, and up to £22,000 at the top.

Worst part of the job: Cleaning up after suicides. Lewis says you get used to the job being disgusting, but the emotional side is still hard. He says you learn not to take the work home with you, and you need a sense of humour because some days are tough.

Job satisfaction: Lewis likes that the work is different every day. One day he might clean up after a dead body, and another day he might work in a prison cell or be 100 feet in the air being lowered into a silo to clean it. He also feels proud to make a dangerous place safe again, and he says it helps society.

3. Biogas engineer  
The job: Setting up biogas plants in developing countries.

What it involves: Connecting digesters to toilets to make biogas for cooking and lighting. The digesters can be filled with human excrement, animal dung, and other waste. Baburam Paudel, chief technical officer in Nepal for the charity Renewable World, says you must know the right size and shape for the mixing pit, how to create the best temperature, and where to put the biogas plant. He says you also must convince poor communities that poo can be useful, because many people do not like the idea of connecting toilets to kitchens.

Typical salary: Around £10,000 for entry level. Around £30,000 for a chief technical officer.

Worst part of the job: Paudel says it is hard to see people trying to live on very little money. He also says the job is difficult if you do not like dirty work. You must be willing to get your hands dirty during building and inspections. Anaerobic digestion, when bacteria break down waste and produce methane, smells like rotten eggs. He says it can be disgusting and mistakes are not allowed.

Job satisfaction: Paudel says it feels very good to help people earn more money and to help girls attend school by replacing the need to collect firewood. He says his work improves health and hygiene for whole communities.

4. Eel ecologist  
The job: Protecting the critically endangered European eel.

What it involves: Ecologists go into the Thames and other London rivers and marshes to check the number of eels. Sometimes they wade in up to their armpits and reach into a net with up to 20 adult eels to grab one with bare hands. Stephen Mowat, an eel conservationist and ecologist for the Zoological Society of London, says adult eels can be a metre long or more and can weigh up to 2kg. He says they are not dangerous, but they are very strong and can be slimy. They have to weigh and measure them, and the eels wriggle a lot. He says it can be hard to look professional while chasing an eel across the grass.

Worst part of the job: Mowat says eels are hard to work with, and it can be embarrassing when an eel outsmarts you. He says you must also be ready to move from one project to another. Once he had to dissect a tub of dead eels to look for parasites, and then quickly go to a meeting with government officials. He says he remembered to wash his hands. text:
Being a chemist today can be surprising: you wait a long time for a new element, and then several appear at once.

Researchers in Japan, Russia, and the United States found four new chemical elements. These were the first new elements added to the periodic table since 2011, when elements 114 and 116 were added. The four new elements live for a very short time and are highly radioactive. They finish the seventh row of the periodic table, so many science textbooks now need updates.

The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) is a world group based in the US that checks and approves element names and measurements. IUPAC confirmed the four elements on 30 December 2015, after studying research going back to 2004.

For now, the elements have temporary Latin-style names: ununtrium, ununpentium, ununseptium, and ununoctium. These names match their atomic numbers: 113, 115, 117, and 118. An atomic number is the number of protons in the center (nucleus) of an atom.

IUPAC said a Russian-American team at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna and at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in California provided enough evidence for elements 115, 117, and 118. IUPAC gave credit for element 113 to a team from the RIKEN Institute in Japan, even though Russian and American scientists also claimed it. This means Japan is the first Asian country that will get to name an element. IUPAC rules say new elements can be named after a myth idea, a mineral, a place or country, or a scientist.

When elements 114 and 116 were officially named in 2012, they were called flerovium and livermorium. The names came from the Flerov Lab in Dubna and the Lawrence Livermore Lab in the US.

Kosuke Morita, who led the RIKEN research, said his team plans to look for element 119 and beyond. Jan Reedijk of IUPAC said chemists are excited to see the periodic table completed down to the seventh row.

The Japanese team is thought to be choosing between three possible names for ununtrium: japonium, rikenium, and nishinarium. The last name comes from the Nishina Center for Accelerator-Based Science, where the element was found. Polly Arnold, a chemistry professor at Edinburgh University, said this kind of work takes great care. She said learning about radioactive decay helps scientists improve their models, and may help with problems like nuclear waste. It can also lead to new technology for building the equipment needed to make these measurements.

The scientists must also suggest two-letter symbols for the elements. After IUPAC gets the suggestions, they will be open for public review for five months so people can object. In 1996, scientists suggested the symbol Cp for copernicium (element 112), but it was changed to Cn because other scientists said Cp already referred to another substance.

To make the new elements, researchers at the three labs smashed lighter atomic nuclei into each other. Then they looked for special patterns of radioactive decay that should happen if a new element was made. Ununtrium and ununpentium are thought to be metals. Ununseptium might be a metalloid, which means it has some metal-like properties. Ununoctium may be a noble gas, like helium, neon, and argon in group 18. But it is hard to be sure because only a few atoms of each element have ever been made.

Paul Karol, who led the IUPAC group that checked the elements, said the results will mostly help scientists who study very heavy nuclei. These scientists are looking for an “island of stability” near element 120, or maybe 126. Some elements there might live long enough for detailed chemistry studies. He also said any practical uses for these new elements are far away, because they are very hard to make.

text:
Glastonbury Festival wants to fight the problem of plastic water bottles. This is part of a long-term plan to become the world’s most environmentally friendly outdoor music festival.

Organizers are focusing on single-use bottles. They are a clear sign of “throwaway” culture. Each year, the 900-acre site in Somerset is left covered with plastic. About one million plastic bottles are used during the festival.

The festival will give stainless-steel reusable bottles to 2,000 road crew and band members. Thousands more will be sold to people at the festival. The goal is to help people stop using plastic bottles. The 140,000 ticket-holders are also being asked to bring reusable bottles. They can fill them at 400 drinking-water taps around the site.

Lucy Smith, the festival’s organizer for green issues, said: “We have amazing water quality in the UK but everyone is obsessed with drinking bottled water.”

She said this action comes before a plan for Glastonbury 2015. The festival wants to replace all plastic beer glasses and cutlery with reusable items. This is to remove plastic waste left on the large rural site.

Environmentalists say there are about 150 million tonnes of plastic waste on Earth and in the oceans. It harms ecosystems and kills wildlife.

In the end, organizers hope to make Glastonbury the world’s greenest greenfield festival. They want to copy America’s Burning Man festival in the Black Rock Desert of Nevada. Burning Man is a “leave-no-trace” event, where people must take away everything they bring.

People going to Glastonbury are also being asked to use public transport or share cars with friends. Smith said, “We want to be as sustainable as we can. We do everything we can, but coping with the litter of 140,000 people is a challenge. We can’t put bins everywhere.”

Campaigners say plastic water bottles can take hundreds or even thousands of years to fully biodegrade. Making them also makes the problem worse. Millions of barrels of oil are used to make plastic bottles. Moving mineral water around the world creates even more carbon emissions.

About 13 billion plastic water bottles are sold in the UK each year, but only one in five is said to be recycled.

Smith said that instead of buying bottled water, people should use the tap water at the festival. It comes from huge underground reservoirs, not old water tanks that gave heavily chlorinated water. The charity WaterAid will also set up water kiosks around the site. They will sell reusable bottles and cups and give free refills. In 2015, these kiosks, based on ones found in Africa, will also be DJ booths at night.

Organizers say almost half of the rubbish left on site was recycled in 2013. They also say there will be 15,000 recycling bins across the festival grounds in 2014.

Even with its greener image, some critics still attack Glastonbury. They say it is becoming more corporate. One critic, Iron Maiden’s Bruce Dickinson, said he will never bring his band to Glastonbury. He called it “the most bourgeois thing on the planet”.

The weather forecast was good. The festival was expected to be sunny and dry. Experts said it would not repeat 1985, the windiest year; 1997, the muddiest; or 2005, known as the “year of thunder”.

text:
In homes and cafes in the UK, tea, baked beans on toast, and fish and chips have been important foods for a long time. But a survey shows that British tastes have changed over time. The National Food Survey began in 1940, after the government worried about health and having enough food.

Even though people think the British love tea, people drink much less tea than before. Since the 1970s, tea use has fallen from 68g per person each week to 25g. A teabag or small amount of loose tea weighs about 3g. This means people now drink about eight cups of tea a week, down from 23 cups in 1974. Tea is still the most common hot drink in the UK, but households now spend more money on coffee.

The data was published by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. It comes from 150,000 households in the survey from 1974 to 2000, plus information from 2000 to 2014. The data shows some healthier changes, like more low-calorie soft drinks, more skimmed milk instead of whole milk, and more fresh fruit. But people now eat a lot more chips, pizza, crisps, and ready meals each week.

People have also changed from white bread to brown and wholemeal bread. But they eat less bread overall: about 25 slices a week in the past, and about 15 slices a week now, using a 40g slice. People also eat fewer baked beans in sauce, down by a fifth. At the same time, other easy foods have become more common, especially Italian foods. Adults now eat about 75g of pizza each week, compared with none in 1974. Pasta eating has almost tripled.

Fresh potatoes are eaten much less. Since 1974, they have dropped by 67%. In 1974, adults ate about 188g of fresh potatoes each day. Other vegetables, like cucumbers, courgettes, aubergines, and mushrooms, are eaten more now. Takeaway food has almost doubled since 1974, from 80g per person each week to 150g. About 33g of this is chips and 56g is meat. Popular meats include kebabs (10g), chicken (7g), burgers (5g), and “meat-based meals” (32g).

Some changes look healthier. Fruit eating, fresh and processed, has gone up by 50% since 1974. In 2014, adults ate about 157g of fruit each day. That is almost two portions out of the government’s five-a-day advice. Bananas have been the most popular fruit since 1996. In 2014, adults ate 221g of bananas each week, more than apples (131g) and oranges (48g). In 2014, low-calorie soft drinks were half of all soft drinks for the first time.

The survey also shows social changes. Questions about owning chickens and getting your own eggs ended in 1991. In the same year, they also stopped asking the “housewife” to fill out the form. People in the UK now spend a smaller part of their pay on food: 11% today, compared with 24% in 1974.

The UK Environment Secretary, Elizabeth Truss, said this data shows what people ate 40 years ago and how culture changes led to big changes in food. She said shoppers now care more about where food comes from, and the internet can bring good food to homes quickly. She also said pop-up restaurants show new trends, and foods from around the world are now common, like fish and chips.

She also said that sharing this data helps people see when habits changed. She said there is still much more to learn from the National Food Survey. She looks forward to seeing the data used for things like local food maps, school projects, and predicting new food trends, and to help the UK food and farming industry in the future.

text:
Google vice-president Vint Cerf warned that many digital things could be lost forever. These include blogs, tweets, photos, videos, court rulings, and emails. The problem is that the computer programs needed to open them may stop working in the future. He said this could create a “forgotten generation or even a forgotten century.” He called the problem “bit rot,” when old computer files turn into useless junk.

Cerf wants people to create something like “digital vellum.” This would keep old software and hardware, so very old files could still be opened. He said we save a lot of daily life in digital form, like email, tweets, and the world wide web. If we want to keep this history, we must make sure digital files can still be read far in the future.

Some experts say the problem may not be as big as Cerf suggests. But there is an irony: people digitize music, photos, letters, and documents to save them for a long time. Yet the programs and machines needed to read the files keep changing and going out of use.

Cerf said we are throwing data into something like an “information black hole” without noticing. We think digitizing will save things, but without extra steps the digital versions might be no better, and might even be worse, than the original objects. He said if you really care about photos, you should print them out.

Ancient people did not have this problem. Writing on clay tablets or papyrus could be read with just eyes. But future scholars studying our time may find PDFs, Word documents, and many other file types. These can only be read with special software, and sometimes special hardware too.

The problem already exists. In the 1980s, people saved files on floppy disks. They loaded Jet Set Willy from cassette to the ZX spectrum, used a Quickfire II joystick, and stored Atari game cartridges in the attic. Even if the disks and cassettes still work, the equipment to use them is now mostly found in museums.

Cerf also warned that important political and historical documents may be lost. He talked about the 2005 book Team of Rivals by American historian Doris Kearns Goodwin. She used real letters she found in libraries in the US to rebuild conversations from Abraham Lincoln’s time. Cerf said that today those letters would be emails, and it would be very hard to find them 100 years from now.

Cerf agreed that historians will try to save things that people today think are important. But he said we often do not know what will be important until hundreds of years later. He gave an example: people learned new things after the Archimedes palimpsest was found. It was hidden under the words of a Byzantine prayer book from the thirteenth century. He said we often learn important things from objects that were saved only by chance.

Researchers at Carnegie Mellon University in Pittsburgh are working on a possible solution. Mahadev Satyanarayanan takes digital “snapshots” of computer hard drives while they run software. Later, these snapshots can be uploaded to a computer that imitates the old one. This can let a computer read files that would otherwise be unreadable. In a project called Olive, they saved Mystery House (1982, Apple II), an early version of WordPerfect, and Doom (1993).

But new technology is only part of the problem. It may be even harder to deal with legal permission to copy and store old software. When IT companies go out of business or stop supporting products, they may sell the rights to someone else. Then it can be very hard to get approval. Cerf said we may need to think about preservation rights in copyright, patents, and licensing, because we want to preserve things for hundreds to thousands of years.

text:
A car that can only go 25 mph, has two seats, and has no pedals or steering wheel might not seem exciting at a car show. But Google in the US surprised the car and taxi industries when it showed a new driverless car.

The electric car is being tested near Google’s headquarters in Mountain View, California. It has no normal controls, not even foot pedals. Instead, people use a smartphone app to call the car and choose where to go. There is one STOP button between the two front seats if the people inside need to stop the computer.

The car does all the driving jobs by itself. It handles navigation, steering, speeding up, and braking.

Google is building about 100 test cars for a two-year test. Google co-founder Sergey Brin said the car is still a prototype. He said the project is meant to help people who do not have good transportation today.

Brin said sitting in the car was relaxing because there was no steering wheel and no pedals. He said that after about ten seconds, he forgot about it. He said it felt like riding a chairlift alone and enjoying the quiet.

Google says the main goal is to make travel safer. The car has foam on the front to absorb impacts and a plastic windscreen. Google says this should make it much safer for pedestrians than other cars.

The cars were specially built by a company in Detroit, but its name has not been shared yet. Google will use the cars in a pilot program to learn how to make driverless cars work better. The cars are not for sale yet.

One problem is that the cars need very detailed maps of roads and nearby areas before they can drive there. They cannot collect and understand enough information fast enough while driving.

Right now, there are detailed maps of about 2,000 miles of roads in California. But California has more than 170,000 miles of public roads.

Google says it may license the technology to regular car makers after it improves it enough. Some team members worked on this idea for more than ten years, even before joining Google.

Some people worry that driverless cars could replace taxi drivers. Dennis Conyon, southeast director of the UK National Taxi Association, said that if you remove the driver, the driver will not have a job. He said it would strongly affect workers.

London has about 22,000 licensed black cabs. Conyon thinks about 100,000 people drive taxis for hire in the UK.

Steve McNamara, general secretary of the London Taxi Drivers’ Association with 10,500 members, said he does not expect driverless cars in London for 20 or 25 years. He also said they might need a charge point, because he said there is not a single one in London now.

Other car makers, like Volvo, Ford, and Mercedes, are working on cars that assist drivers. Unlike Google’s car, these still have driver controls.

Chris Urmson, director of Google’s self-driving car project, said the prototypes have no steering wheel and no brakes because a person inside might not be able to take over during an emergency. He said it is simpler to have an emergency stop button.

Urmson said the vehicles will be very basic so the team can learn quickly and improve them. He said they will take you where you want to go with the push of a button. He said this is an important step toward safer roads and better travel for millions of people.

So far, Google’s self-driving cars have driven 700,000 miles with no accident caused by the computer. Google says thousands of people die on roads each year, and about 80% of crashes are caused by human mistakes.

But Conyon says he has been driving a taxi for 50 years and has never had an accident. He is 79 years old.

text:
The last series of Downton Abbey starts with a sad feeling and a sense that times are changing. It is 1925. People can already feel that hard economic times are coming later in the decade. Neighbours are selling their big house. Lord Grantham wants fewer servants because he says under-butlers cost too much.

But at the real “Downton Abbey”, Highclere Castle, the money situation is much better. Highclere is owned by George “Geordie” Herbert, the 8th Earl of Carnarvon. Lady Fiona Carnarvon says the world-wide success of Downton Abbey has paid for many building repairs, to protect Highclere for the next generation.

Lady Carnarvon said it has been like a “magic carpet ride”. She said the show gave them great marketing and made them well known around the world. She said she is very grateful. She said she and her husband love the house and the people there, and now millions of other people love it too.

Right now, only the ground floor and first floor of Highclere, near Hampshire, are used. A project has started to fix old tower rooms. Later, visitors will be able to climb up to an exhibition about Sir Charles Barry. He was the architect of the Houses of Parliament, and he rebuilt the house between 1839 and 1842.

When the Downton Abbey producers first contacted Highclere in 2009, the family had a repair bill close to £12m. Urgent work cost £1.8m. By 2012, the “Downton effect” was helping. Lord Carnarvon said it was just after the banking crisis and things felt gloomy. They had done corporate events, but there were fewer after that. Then Downton came, and Highclere became a major tourist attraction.

The number of visitors doubled to 1,200 a day. Downton Abbey, written by Julian Fellowes, became a hit in the UK in 2010 and then in the US. Now it is shown in 250 countries.

Highclere’s ticket system used to be simple. Now it is a computer system where people book in advance, which helps make sure foreign visitors can get in. Accounts from Highclere Enterprises for 2014-15 show that current assets have almost tripled since 2012, to about £1m. Gareth Neame, the executive producer, said Downton Abbey helped secure Highclere’s future.

Peter Fincham, ITV’s director of television, remembers when they booked Highclere. At first he thought it did not matter because he had never heard of Highclere Castle. He thought one stately home looked like another. But he was wrong. He said the castle became a huge “character” in the show too.

Downton tourists are part of a growing trend. VisitBritain says nearly 30% of foreign visitors, almost nine million people, include castles and historic houses in their plans. Almost half of people who may visit Britain now say they want to do “set jetting”, which means visiting places they saw in films or on TV.

Each year, more than a million people take tours of historic buildings and spend more than £1bn. From new growing tourist markets, 51% of Brazilians, 42% of Russians and Chinese, and 35% of Indian visitors are likely to visit an interesting site.

VisitBritain’s director, Patricia Yates, said the connection between tourism and films and TV is very strong. She said period dramas have also made places outside London more popular.

Neame is now an ambassador for the GREAT Britain campaign. It is supported by government departments and the British Council, and it promotes the UK around the world. Some events are special Downton-themed receptions at British embassies.

Neame said they asked him because the show reaches so many people. He said some people in the UK think it is just “soapy” entertainment. But in other countries, people greatly respect British actors, writing, and production skills. He said he believes in “soft power”, and he thinks the UK should be prouder of what it does well.

He said Downton Abbey is famous for showing “Britishness”. He said it is really a fantasy world in a certain time in history. He said it is the first TV period drama that has jumped off the screen and become part of popular culture.

Lady Carnarvon still says Highclere’s long-term future is not fully safe. She said there is not much extra money. She said the show has let them spend faster on the buildings and restore the follies.

They are planning a Tutankhamun centenary event in 2022. It is 100 years after the 5th Earl of Carnarvon and Howard Carter discovered the tomb, which changed what people knew about Egyptology. Another chance to keep Highclere famous is the 300th anniversary of the birth of Lancelot “Capability” Brown, who designed the grounds.

Lady Carnarvon said you should never relax and think the work is finished. She said you must not take anything for granted. She said great houses need constant investment. She said there has been a deficit since the 1930s. In the past, the estate and house supported the family’s lifestyle. Today it is the opposite: she and Geordie must find ways to support and take care of Highclere.

She said she tries to show people that visiting can be fun, with special events, not only walking around an old dusty house. She said they also have to compete with attractions like the London Dungeon.

text:
My Spanish degree helped me get my first job as a journalist. I worked for an international news agency in Mexico City. But I still made big mistakes as a new reporter.

I arrived in Mexico City after a long Greyhound bus trip from New York. The job interview tested my Spanish. In the daytime, I went out to cover political rallies. At night, I stayed alone in the office and helped organize news from El Salvador, Nicaragua, Guatemala, and other parts of Central America. I also had to report on disasters like fires, floods, and explosions at firework factories.

Soon I learned something about myself: I was bad at understanding numbers in Spanish. I also was not great at math in English. When I tried to call the police, I sometimes dialed the wrong number. I woke up Mexican grandmothers at 2 a.m. because I mixed up a digit.

Even worse, I got numbers wrong in my news stories. A report of almost 83 people dead in a fire at 6 p.m. became only 38 by 7 p.m. A report of 12 people hurt in a bus crash soon became two. This kept happening. Finally, an editor from the main office in Washington called me and shouted, “I don’t know what training you have had, but has no one ever told you a death toll can’t go down?!”

Why are numbers so hard in another language? One reason may be that languages use numbers in different ways. For example, in German, 2:30 p.m. is halb drei (“half of three”), and 21 is einundzwanzig (“one and twenty”). These differences can confuse people. Some indigenous languages have very rare number systems, and some are close to dying out.

Some experts think there may be a link between dyscalculia (trouble understanding arithmetic) and trouble learning foreign languages, especially when students learn by repeating things from memory. This is because it uses step-by-step processes that people with dyscalculia may find hard. But some students who do badly with a grammar textbook may do well in a real foreign-language setting, where learning feels more natural. For me, languages were usually easy, except for numbers.

Another reason may be that people often hear numbers alone, without other clues. If a listener has stopped paying attention to the foreign language, it can be hard to suddenly focus again. I asked some friends who speak many languages. Many said they can speak French or Italian well when ordering food, but they freeze when they must say numbers, especially on the phone. Numbers feel difficult, but no one could explain exactly why.

My trouble with numbers in other languages followed me from Mexico to other countries, and from Spanish to German and Portuguese. Still, in that first journalism job, getting numbers wrong did not always mean I failed.

One night, a Mexican co-worker found out that the American consul in the port city of Veracruz was being held hostage at gunpoint in his office. No senior reporter who spoke English was there, so I had to call the consulate. I got the number wrong, and the call went to another extension in the building. The voice was clear: I had reached the gunman. I talked with him for 15 minutes. I did not get him to put away his gun, but my reputation as a new reporter rose overnight.

text:
Some famous people refused to do the Ice Bucket Challenge, like David Cameron, Barack Obama, and Pamela Anderson. Others did it, like George W Bush, Benedict Cumberbatch, and Stephen Hawking.

The Ice Bucket Challenge raised $100 million for a US charity for a motor-neurone disease, and £4.5 million for a British one. It also raised thousands more for charities in Hong Kong and Australia. But soon, many people began to criticize it.

Some people said celebrities were showing off their bodies. Some said people were having fun but not donating money. Others complained it wasted water. Commentators, animal-rights groups, and environmentalists made these complaints. The US ALS Association (not the same as Britain’s MND Association) was also criticized because its top staff were paid high salaries.

The challenge kept growing. Here is how it works: a person talks on camera about the charity, then pours a bucket of ice water over their head (or a friend does it). Then they name three people who must do the same or donate.

It started in the US in July, but people disagree about where it began. It first appeared on mainstream American TV on 15 July. The ALS Association tried to trademark the phrase “Ice Bucket Challenge,” but stopped after criticism. A spokeswoman, Carrie Munk, said they understood people’s concern and withdrew the trademark applications. The ALS Association had not yet answered the criticism about its high costs and wage bills.

At the same time, 31 August to 5 September was World Water Week. People met in Stockholm to discuss the world’s water crisis. The charity WaterAid asked people to use recycled bath water, water from garden butts, or sea water.

Douglas Graham, fundraising director of the MND Association, said criticism was expected. He said the donations were a wonderful surprise and they were very grateful. The money helps a small charity that supports people with a serious disease. The disease has no cure and kills five people a day in the UK.

Pamela Anderson, a longtime animal-rights activist, wrote an open letter to the ALS Association. She said she could not support it because of animal experiments. Some US stars rejected the challenge because of California’s drought. Actor Matt Damon used water from his toilets and said much of the world has less clean drinking water. Actor Verne Troyer used milk for environmental reasons. On the Scottish island of Colonsay, people blamed the challenge for a water shortage after many of the island’s 135 people joined in.

In Australia, TV anchorman Lincoln Humphries apologized for his strong “no, thanks” reply after being nominated. He said people should not waste water and ice. He shared a list of charities that help communities in great need. He said people who have more than they need should donate what they can to those who need it most.

Another criticism was that small charities might not be able to handle so much extra money. The MND Association disagreed. Graham said they could cope. He said they fund world-class research to find causes, and to find a treatment or cure. They also provide care and support for 3,500 people. He said the disease gets worse very fast and is costly to manage. More than 50% die within two years of diagnosis. He said it is heartbreaking to see people decline in just a few months.

For many people connected to the disease, the awareness from the challenge is as valuable as the money. Graham said it is priceless. Normally, the MND Association gets about 300,000 website hits in a year. On one recent day, it got 330,000.

Graham said they could not have created this attention on their own. He said all charities are worthwhile, and some people may want to give to a different one. In 2013, British people gave £62 billion to charity, and he said people should be proud. He said the extra money is wonderful for them, and they will plan how to use it in the best way. He said every penny will be used for good.

text:
In Iceland, a girl can have many names, like Aagot, Arney, Ásfríður, Baldey, Bebba, Brá, Dögg, Dimmblá, Etna, Eybjört, Frigg, Glódís, Hörn, Ingunn, Jórlaug, Obba, Sigurfljóð, Úranía, or Vagna. But a girl in Iceland cannot be called Harriet.

Tristan Cardew said the situation is silly. He and his Icelandic wife, Kristin, are fighting a decision by the National Registry in Reykjavik. The office will not renew their 10-year-old daughter Harriet’s passport because it does not accept her first name.

The office also does not accept the name of Harriet’s 12-year-old brother, Duncan. Until now, the children traveled with passports that called them Stúlka and Drengur Cardew. These words mean Girl and Boy Cardew. Cardew said the authorities are now following the law closely. The law says no official document will be given to people who do not have an approved Icelandic name.

Because of this problem, the family from Kópavogur almost missed their holiday in France. They asked the British embassy for an emergency UK passport. This should now let them leave.

Names are very important in Iceland. The country has about 320,000 people. The phone book lists people by first name. This is because most Icelandic last names show who your father (or mother) is. For example, Jón Einarsson’s children might be Ólafur Jónsson and Sigríður Jónsdóttir.

The law says the names of children born in Iceland must be sent to the National Registry within six months. This is required unless both parents are foreign. If a name is not on a list of 1,853 female names and 1,712 male names, the parents must ask the Icelandic Naming Committee to approve it.

About 5,000 children are born in Iceland each year. The committee gets about 100 requests and rejects about half. A 1996 law tries to protect the Icelandic language. The rules say names must work with Icelandic grammar endings, must not hurt the language structure, and must follow normal Icelandic spelling rules.

In practice, this means some names are not allowed. Names with letters not in Iceland’s 32-letter alphabet, like “c”, are not accepted. Also, names must be able to change form for Icelandic grammar cases. Cardew said this was the problem with Harriet.

Many people have criticized these naming laws. In 2013, a person named Blær (“Light Breeze”) Bjarkardóttir Rúnarsdottir won the right to use that name officially instead of “Girl.” A court said it was against the Icelandic constitution to deny her name. The former mayor of Reykjavik, Jón Gnarr, also said the naming law is unfair, stupid, and against creativity.

The Cardew family could fix Harriet’s problem by giving her an Icelandic middle name. But Cardew said it is too late and too silly. He asked, “Are they saying they don’t want us here?”

text:
Passing clouds
One pleasure of flying is seeing clouds close up. Clouds look light, but they hold a lot of water. A small cumulus cloud can hold about 500 tonnes of water. Water is heavier than air. So why don’t clouds fall like rain? They do fall, but the tiny drops fall very slowly. A normal cloud would take about a year to fall one metre.

On cloud nine
Many people just say clouds are “fluffy” or “black”. But weather scientists (meteorologists) name more than 50 cloud types by their shape and height. They group them into types numbered one to nine. Cloud nine is the huge, tall cumulonimbus cloud. So “on cloud nine” means feeling on top of the world.

Around the rainbow
A plane is a great place to see a rainbow. Rainbows happen when sunlight hits raindrops. From the ground we see a bow because the Earth blocks part of it. From a plane, a rainbow can be a full circle. When a plane passes over clouds, the plane’s shadow can appear in the middle.

Mr blue sky
Sunlight is white and has all colours in it. When it goes through air, some light is scattered by gas molecules. Blue light scatters more than the other colours, so the sky looks blue.

There’s life out there
From a plane window, we expect to see clouds and other planes. But the air is also full of bacteria. Over cities, scientists have found as many as 1,800 different types. They can reach twice the normal cruising height of a plane.

Turbulence terror
Turbulence can make even experienced flyers feel sick. It can be light bumps or sudden drops. The good news is that no modern airliner has ever crashed because of turbulence. People can be hurt, and sometimes killed, if they are not strapped in or if loose luggage hits them. But turbulence will not knock a plane out of the sky.

In-flight radiation
When body scanners arrived at airports, people worried about radiation. But the scanner level is the same as what passengers get in one minute of flight. The Earth is always hit by cosmic rays, which are natural radiation from space. This radiation is stronger at high altitude.

You can’t cure jet lag
The world has time zones. Long flights can make local time different from your body clock, and this causes jet lag. You can reduce it by eating bland food for 24 hours before travel, drinking lots of fluids, and living on your destination time as soon as you get on the aircraft.

Supersonic 747s
Many people have travelled faster than sound. High in the sky there are fast winds called jet streams. On trips from the US to Europe, a temperature inversion can make a band of air move at up to 250mph. If a plane flies at 550mph and enters this wind, its speed over the ground can be 800mph. That is faster than the speed of sound, which is 740mph.

Flying through time
Time zones feel like travelling through time, but special relativity says flying also causes real time travel. The effect is tiny. If you crossed the Atlantic every week for 40 years, you would only move 1/1,000th of a second into the future.

Terrible tea
If your tea tastes bad, it is not the cabin attendant’s fault. Tea needs water just under 100°C. But on a plane the water cannot get hotter than 90°C during flight. So it may be better to choose coffee.

I can’t hear my food
Airline food is known for being bland. Some of the problem may not be the cooking. A plane is very noisy, and there is evidence that loud noise makes food taste less savoury.

Needle in a haystack
With today’s technology, it seems strange that Malaysian flight MH370 could disappear. But finding a missing aircraft is very hard. The plane knows where it is using GPS and inertial tracking, but it does not send this information elsewhere in real time. It could do so. Ships have had tracking since the 1980s. The problem is not technology, but missing laws that require it.

Volcanic fallout
Flights can be cancelled because of volcanoes. Ash has tiny glass-like pieces. They can melt in the hot engine and then harden on the rotors. Avoiding ash clouds can be annoying, but flying through ash is dangerous.

The wing myth
For many years, people taught the wrong reason for why wings lift planes. In fact, almost all lift comes from Newton’s Third Law of Motion. The wing shape pushes air downward. When air is pushed down, the wing gets an equal push upward, and the plane rises.

Forget electric planes
Small, light electric planes exist, so it is easy to think big electric airliners will come soon. But not any time soon. Plane fuel holds a huge amount of energy. Batteries hold much less. To match the energy in one tonne of fuel, you would need 100 tonnes of batteries. A 747 uses 150 to 200 tonnes of fuel. Unless batteries become much better, electric airliners will not be able to fly.

Beware the vortex
Pilots often wait before take-off. This is to let the air calm down after another plane leaves. A plane’s wingtips make spinning air called vortices. These can take two or three minutes to disappear. If the next plane takes off right away, the fast spinning air can make it hard to control. Waiting lets the air recover from the small whirlwinds.

The doors aren’t locked
Plane doors do not need to be locked. When a plane door opens, it moves in a special way. It must first move inward, then be moved out of the way. After take-off, the air pressure inside the plane becomes much higher

text:
People call it the “Richie Rich Club,” and it may get richer soon. A report says India’s richest people will make their total wealth about four times bigger by 2018. Many new business owners and people who get money from family will become multimillionaires. The report is based on interviews with 150 very rich people. It also says business confidence is coming back in India.

In recent years, India had slow growth, higher food prices, and a weaker currency. But the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP) won a big election in May 2014 and promised to improve the economy. Even with the slowdown, there are almost one-sixth more Indians worth more than $3.75m than in 2013, says the Kotak Mahindra Bank report. Murali Balaraman, a co-author, said more cities are growing, the middle class is growing, and there are more chances to make money. He also said politics has improved in recent months.

India’s rich people together own assets worth about $1 trillion. That is about one-fifth of all the wealth in India. By 2018, the report says total wealth could reach $4tn, and there could be three times as many multimillionaires.

A luxury market is growing to sell to new rich people and old rich families. Balaraman said rich people want to show their wealth in a quiet way, and buying luxury goods helps them do that. Abhay Gupta, the CEO of Luxury Connect, said the market for expensive goods and experiences will get bigger. He said many people want to be like the very rich and copy what they do.

Cars are one of the most popular luxury items, the report says. In 2009, rich people showed off SUVs made in India. Now, only foreign cars get attention. Mercedes had a 47% jump in sales in India in 2013. BMW launched a new $200,000 model in Delhi.

But India’s bad roads and traffic can reduce demand for some cars. In 2013, Lamborghini’s Chief Executive, Stephan Winkelmann, said India’s roads and traffic “are not so suitable” for $450,000 sports cars. Lamborghini sells two models in India: the Gallardo and the Aventador. The Aventador can reach 217mph. Winkelmann said Lamborghini buyers in India are younger than in Europe, usually in their 30s. Still, the most popular investments are real estate (mostly in India) and jewellery.

India’s super-rich are known around the world for very big spending. Mukesh Ambani, India’s richest man, built a very expensive home in Mumbai. The 27-storey tower has helicopter pads, indoor cinemas, and more than 600 staff. It is estimated to be worth $1bn.

A three-day wedding for the niece of Lakshmi Mittal, a UK-based steel tycoon worth an estimated $16bn, was reported to cost $80m. Hundreds of guests were flown to Barcelona. The ceremony and party were held in a museum.

The report says luxury buyers now want even more special items. One person ordered nine cases of Japanese whisky that cost over $750 a bottle for a wedding reception. The report says the whisky was attractive because guests would not know how to buy the same drink in India. Another big spender bought pairs of Louis Vuitton bags, then cut up half of them to make clothes that matched her accessories.

Traditional weddings are also changing fast. Gifts like silver plates, dried fruit, or sweets, once sent with wedding invitations, are being replaced by gifts from top western designer brands. Gupta says, “These days, it’s Rolex watches and Louis Vuitton bags.”

Almost half of new ultra-high-net-worth individuals live in smaller provincial cities. Many give large amounts to charity, but the report says charity spending has not grown as fast as their wealth.

Co-author Balaraman says having more rich people will not cause social tension because a big gap between rich and poor is accepted in India. He said people know some are rich and some are poor, and they continue with their lives.
