text:
You can no longer legally smoke a cigarette inside a bar in New Orleans, Louisiana. Many cities have banned indoor smoking, but New Orleans is different. The city attracts tourists by advertising a “let the good times roll” attitude. So a smoking ban in New Orleans may have special effects.

The Wall Street Journal reported that since Hurricane Katrina in 2005, the New Orleans city government has tried to calm things down. With help from neighbourhood groups, the city has policed bars and nightclubs more strictly. At the same time, the city has tried to create a new “noise ordinance” (a rule about music and noise).

Bar owner William Walker dislikes the anti-smoking law. “This is just the wrong time for them to have pursued something like this,” he says. He thinks making people go outside to smoke will make tensions worse.

Many of New Orleans’s best bars and some live music places are in quiet residential neighbourhoods. Living side by side like this is part of what makes the city charming. But lately, some people do not accept this arrangement, and people worry about the future of New Orleans’s music culture.

Martha Wood lives next to a loud bar with live music. She says the bar was one reason she bought her house, so she will not complain about noise. She also manages a live-music bar. After noise problems in 2013 (including complaints about loud smokers outside), her bar became one of the first in New Orleans that was legally not allowed to serve drinks to go. Wood says, “Now, any small infraction could get us shut down.” She says they installed a “smoke eater” to deal with smoke because they cannot open the doors at night because of the “noise.”

The Maple Leaf club chose to go smoke-free in 2014. Another club did the same after artists asked for smoke-free nights. Zalia BeVille, manager of the All Ways Lounge, says many performance places were already doing this for performers. She wishes the city had let it happen naturally instead of forcing the ban on every corner bar, even bars without music. The All Ways Lounge went smoke-free in 2013 because, she says, “The horn players and the singers, they asked us for it.”

All Ways has an outdoor patio. Lost Love Lounge does not. Its owner, Geoff Douville, likes the ban. Before, he felt he had to allow smoking so his bar could make enough money. He says he could not ban smoking unless the whole city did it too. If he banned it alone, smokers would go to another bar nearby. He wanted the rule to be the same for everyone.

Many small business owners fear they will lose money without smokers. Smoker Neil Timms owns an English pub. He saw a smoking ban before in England. He remembers that in Coventry, every pub where he was a regular closed within a year of the UK ban that began in 2007. To avoid the same problem, he is spending money to build a patio.

Douville thinks the ban could help business. He says many people would like to come for the bar and food, but did not want to smell like smoke for the next seven days. Now those people may come. Douville also is not worried about noise complaints the way Walker is. He says a court will not call a bar a “nuisance” after the city has approved a smoking ban that makes people go outside. He says, “I want to see a judge reconcile ‘no loitering’ with ‘no smoking inside.’”

Councilwoman LaToya Cantrell introduced and pushed the ban. She disagrees with Douville. She says bar owners must keep their customers respectful outside too. Owners and bartenders should tell people they can smoke outside but must respect the community. She says it is silly to say the city cannot have clean air because of noise. She believes it can be a win-win, with better communication and partnerships between communities and businesses.

Cantrell says New Orleans is unique, but still needs the ban. She says New Orleans is known as “the City that Care Forgot,” but the city should say, “We care about our people.” She says the most vulnerable people are workers in smoky places. These workers are important to the hospitality industry, which drives Louisiana’s economy. She adds that in New Orleans, people can take their drink outside when they go out to smoke.

Many people worried that the New Orleans Police Department, already under strain, should not have to police smokers. Instead, the health department will handle warnings and fines. Customers are encouraged to fill out a form or call 311, and to include date-and-time-stamped photos of illegal smoking. Neil Timms says he will follow the ban for this reason. He does not want someone to take a photo of a person smoking, and then the smoker gets beaten up.

Geoff Douville says he is used to noise complaints, so he is not worried about the ban. He says the neighbours who complain about noise now may be the same ones who wanted the smoking ban. In the end, he agrees with Cantrell’s win-win hope. He says people will complain, but that does not mean they will win.

text:
When we talk about climate change, we often focus only on the problem and forget that there are many solutions. Solutions can help us recycle faster, reduce emissions, and find greener choices instead of plastic, air conditioning, smartphones, and fast fashion.

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change met in Copenhagen to share its newest report. Climate change is now measured on all continents. We must work harder to lower emissions so the problem does not get out of control. The meeting also talked about solutions, not only risks.

Sustainia Award, led by Arnold Schwarzenegger, honored ten strong sustainability solutions used in 84 countries. The solutions covered many areas, like food, fashion, energy, transportation, education, and health. The awards showed that a better future is possible and helped innovators, investors, consumers, and policy makers.

From California, people showed a way to make plastic from greenhouse gases. This plastic can match oil-based plastic in price and quality. From Switzerland, people showed how to recycle and reuse old clothes and shoes better, using a system that is in over 60 countries. From Canada, people showed how smartphones can make bike sharing easier.

All ten projects had different ideas, but the Nigerian project Wecyclers won the Sustainia Award 2014.

Wecyclers helps low-income communities earn money from waste in their streets. In Lagos, it uses cargo bicycles to collect and recycle waste. Families can trade their garbage for consumer goods using an SMS point system.

Recycling companies buy Wecyclers’ sorted waste and turn it into new products, like mattresses, pillows, and trash bags. Wecyclers answers a local problem, because only about 40% of the city’s rubbish is collected. The World Bank says only 46% of municipal solid waste in Africa is collected. More than 5,000 households have joined so far, and there are plans to expand to other cities in Nigeria.

People often think climate change solutions must be high-tech and focused only on cutting emissions, building infrastructure, or improving efficiency. But we need many kinds of solutions. Sustainability is not only about lowering emissions. It is also about using natural resources wisely and creating healthier lives. Some ideas may be low-tech but can still make a big difference for whole communities.

Because there are many challenges and many solutions, we should focus more on the part of the story that gives hope, builds energy, and leads to action for needed change.

text:
A senior US scientist warns that today’s children may not hear many sounds from nature. People often block out the sounds around them.

Kurt Fristrup, a senior scientist at the US National Park Service, says some places are getting louder. This can make people stop noticing nice sounds like birds singing, water running, and leaves moving in the wind. These sounds can even be heard in cities.

He says the problem is worse because many people listen to music on earphones. Then they do not listen to birds and other nature sounds. Nature sounds can be covered up by traffic, music, and other noise.

Fristrup says people can learn to ignore sounds. He says we may lose a special ability we are born with: to hear many things from far away. He also says we may lose the habit of listening to the world around us.

For ten years, the US National Park Service recorded sound levels at more than 600 places in the US. These places included Yosemite in California, Yellowstone, and Denali in Alaska. Every place had some noise from people, such as airplanes flying over, motorbikes, motorboats, or tour buses.

Fristrup’s team used park data and city data to model noise across the US. They predict noise pollution is growing faster than the US population. They say it more than doubles every 30 years.

Fristrup says it is not surprising that people use earphones or noise-cancelling headphones to try to make a quieter place for themselves.

He says loud background noise affects hearing like fog affects sight. When it is loud, you only notice sounds close to you instead of many sounds all around you. He says cities still have birds and other things to enjoy, but people are losing this.

Fristrup says people get used to louder places over time. He fears we will accept much worse conditions and forget that the world could be much quieter. He says many children may grow up without peace and quiet.

Other scientists at the same meeting reported that nature sounds may help health. Derrick Taff, a social scientist at Pennsylvania State University, described early experiments. He said listening to recordings from national parks, like waterfalls, birds, and wind, helped people recover from stressful events.

In one experiment, people had to give a short talk that researchers judged from behind a one-way mirror. Researchers measured heart rate and the stress hormone cortisol before and after the talk. People calmed down faster when they listened to nature recordings than when the same recordings also included sounds from road traffic, airplanes, or normal conversation.

Taff said natural sounds are important to people. They are one reason people visit protected areas. People want to hear quiet, birdsong, and wind and water. He says people may be losing this because they listen to iPods all the time, but he believes the public still likes these sounds. He advises people to visit protected areas and experience these sounds.

Scientists are not sure why nature sounds are calming. Fristrup suggests that over millions of years of evolution, humans may connect calm nature sounds with safety. He thinks these soundscapes may feel safe because they do not suggest a predator is nearby.

text:
Many governments in Europe want a “magic” answer to rising unemployment. But in the parts of the EU that are doing the worst, unemployment keeps going up. Strong words do not help people get jobs faster.

In one struggling part of Italy, a mayor thinks he has an answer for his town’s long problem of not having enough work. Some people say it is not really a solution, but more like giving up. Valter Piscedda is the centre-left mayor of Elmas, a small town near Cagliari, the capital of Sardinia. He wants to pay residents to leave. The town council will pay for ten unemployed local people to take intensive English lessons, buy a cheap flight, and look for jobs elsewhere in Europe.

“This is, above all, an idea born of common sense and experience,” he told the Guardian. He said that for the past year and a half, and especially in recent months, many young people have come to him almost every day. They feel hopeless about finding work. Some ask for help to find work in Elmas. Others have tried everything and feel so discouraged that they do not want to stay and wait. They want to go abroad to get work experience and life experience.

He said his idea was to do everything the council can do, so that people who want to get experience abroad can do it.

Italy’s national economy is still weak. Sardinia, like much of southern and central Italy, has very high unemployment. The overall jobless rate was 17.7% in the second quarter of 2014, according to Italy’s National Institute of Statistics, Istat. More than 54% of people under 25 are out of work.

For the Adesso Parto (Now I’m leaving) programme, Elmas’s council has set aside Û12,000. Money is given on a first-come, first-served basis. Applicants must be between 18 and 50. They must be out of work and must have lived in the town for three years. They do not need a university degree. Their yearly income must be no more than Û15,000.

Encouraging people to leave is a sensitive idea now, because many Italians leave their country every year, including many smart young graduates. But Piscedda, who is in the Democratic Party of Prime Minister Matteo Renzi, says he is not causing a “brain drain.” He believes the people he sends away may return “and give me back 100 times what they were given.” Most of all, he wants the scheme to help the people who need it the most.

He said it is for people who have no other help and it is their last chance. He said it lets them keep their dignity, so they do not have to ask friends for money or put pressure on families who cannot afford it.

He added that earlier in 2014, the council started another plan. Businesses were given financial rewards to hire young workers from Elmas. He said they advertised 20 jobs and received 120 applications.

People in Elmas have different opinions about the new plan. Alessandro Macis said there is little work in the town. He said going abroad to learn about work and other cultures can be very valuable. He gave an example: the son of a friend did not study much, but he went to London and is doing well. He started as a waiter. Now he is a cook and is learning English.

Other people were confused. Consuelo Melis, who works behind the bar in a local cafe, said she heard about it and thought it was strange. She asked why the town does not use the money to help people stay and work in Elmas. On Twitter, many people reacted with shock. Marco Patavino called it “the state’s admission of defeat.” Carlo Mazzaggio said, “Institutions are raising the white flag.”

Piscedda said he is not stopping. He said his online critics are probably people who are not in need. He said he deals with people’s problems every day and must try to solve them. He said if people had another choice, they would not be asking for help.

He said the work he can create as mayor is only temporary. He can pay for a piazza to be cleaned, and cleaned again, and for streets to be cleaned. But he said these are short-term jobs that only give a little money for a few months. He said he wants to do more than that.

text:
Robert Mys_ajek stops and looks closely. On a muddy mountain path, between two paw prints, he finds what he wants. “Droppings!” he says happily. People rarely see wolves, so finding their droppings is a good day, even for an expert.

But wolves are becoming easier to find in Poland. There are now about 1,500 wolves there. The number has doubled in 15 years. Wolves, along with the brown bear, the lynx, and the wolverine, are Europe’s last large meat-eating predators. Conservationists from Britain, Germany, and the Netherlands come to Poland to learn how the country has saved this protected animal, which has a bad name even in fairy tales.

The black droppings have bits of bone and hair. “It ate a red deer,” says the University of Warsaw biologist. “In my lab, I can learn a lot about this wolf: what it ate, if it is male or female, its age, its health, and its family.”

DNA tests show that Polish wolves travel far. “One wolf reached the Netherlands, but it was hit by a car,” says Mys_ajek. “They need a lot of space. The average area for a Polish wolf pack is 250 square kilometres.”

“Could we ever bring wolves back to Scotland?” asks student Alex Entwisle, 23. He is on a field trip from a UK college to southern Poland. The students spend the day looking for droppings and paw prints in the spruce forests of the Beskidy mountains in the Polish Carpathians.

They talk a lot about bringing wolves back to the British Isles, for the first time since the 18th century.

Mys_ajek visited the Scottish Highlands in 2015 as a guest of the British charity Wolves and Humans Foundation. People there asked him about Poland. “The big difference is that in Poland we eat pork. We do not have many sheep here,” he says.

“The similarity is that we have many wild animals: 300,000 red deer and more than 800,000 roe deer. We also have too many wild boar, about 200,000. They damage farmers’ cereal crops. Here, wolves help with this problem.”

Mys_ajek, who is often on Polish television, says wolves can travel up to 30 km during one hunt. “The Beskidy pack is strong, with eight or nine wolves. This year we recorded five cubs, two yearlings, and two adults.”

“We track them with cameras that turn on when something moves, and by following prints in mud and snow. In each family group, only one pair has pups, once a year. All pack members help care for the young.”

Mys_ajek, whose father was a shepherd, does not understand why wolves have such a bad reputation. “Why do people say ‘lone-wolf gunman’? Why did we need Little Red Riding Hood?”

He is interested in wolves because they stayed wild 33,000 years ago, while others became domestic dogs.

Wolves are not pet dogs. Mys_ajek says only scientific reasons can save Europe’s wild predators, especially the unpopular wolf. Wolves help forests grow again and control the number of wild animals. “Natural predators balance nature. They stop plant-eating animals from eating too much, so trees can grow tall and birds can nest in them.”

What about shooting deer instead? “It is only a partial solution,” he says. “In a varied natural area, animals also change where they go because they fear predators. They do not stay all day eating soft grass near rivers. They move away to avoid being trapped. This gives plants a chance to grow.”

Poland banned wolf hunting in the western Carpathians in 1995 and across the whole country in 1998. Now there are wolf packs in almost all major forests. Wolves live near people instead of being kept behind fences, like in African safari parks.

The Polish government pays farmers when wolves kill livestock. Mys_ajek advises farmers on using electric fences. He has also helped bring back two old ways to scare wolves: red bunting hung around sheep pens, and the barking of the fluffy white Tatra Mountain Sheepdog.

Poland’s wolves have also been helped by the country’s slower building of roads and motorways. In 1989, when the communists lost power, Poland had only one motorway. Big road projects started after Poland joined the European Union in 2004, and they required studies about wildlife. Poland now has one of the highest numbers in the world of wildlife bridges and underpasses.

People’s attitudes have changed too. “For many years, hunting was part of culture. In 1975, there were fewer than 100 wolves in Poland. From the 1950s, the authorities encouraged people to kill wolves. They paid a reward worth a month’s salary. It was slaughter,” says Mys_ajek.

Mys_ajek says wolves are doing much better now, but their situation is still fragile. Packs cross borders, and wolves are still hunted in nearby Russia, Lithuania, Belarus, Ukraine, and Slovakia.

He says Poland’s government, elected in October 2015, is against wolves. “The Environment Minister, Jan Szyszko, is openly a hunter. There are 120,000 licensed hunters in Poland, and they have influence in parliament.”

“Hunters say wolves are pests and claim there are 4,000 wolves in Poland. He says this number is false and comes from an unscientific count. He says the government could undo the progress.”

Supporting wolves is not easy. “You cannot tell politicians that wolves bring many tourists,” he says. “Most tourists want to see them, but wolves avoid people. Their sense of smell is very strong.”

text:
A big scientific study says organic food has more antioxidant compounds than regular food. It also has lower levels of toxic metals and pesticides. The researchers say that eating organic fruit and vegetables could give a similar benefit to adding one or two extra portions in the daily “five a day” advice.

The team was led by Professor Carlo Leifert at Newcastle University. They said the differences were “statistically significant” and “meaningful.” They found many antioxidants were higher in organic food, by about 19% to 69%. They said this is the first study to show clear differences across many kinds of fruits, vegetables, and cereals.

The researchers said the extra antioxidants could be like eating one or two of the five daily portions of fruit and vegetables. They said this would matter for nutrition if it is confirmed that these compounds really cause the health benefits linked to eating more fruit, vegetables, and wholegrains.

Not everyone agrees. Tom Sanders, a nutrition professor at King’s College London, said the study does show some differences. But he questioned if the differences are just normal natural changes, and if they really matter for health. He also said Leifert’s work has caused arguments before. Sanders added that the research showed organic cereals have less protein than conventional cereals.

The results came from looking at 343 peer-reviewed studies from around the world. Helen Browning, the chief executive of the Soil Association, said the study shows that the way we farm can affect the quality of food.

Plants can make antioxidants to protect themselves from pests. So organic crops may have more antioxidants because they are not protected by chemical sprays. The scientists also said other reasons might matter, like organic plants being bred to be tough and not being given too much artificial fertilizer.

Leifert and his team said many antioxidants have been linked before to a lower risk of long-term diseases, including cardiovascular diseases, neurodegenerative diseases, and some cancers. But they also said there have not been long-term studies that show health benefits from eating a wide organic diet. They found much higher levels of cadmium, a toxic metal, in conventional crops. They also found pesticide residues on conventional crops four times more often than on organic crops. The EU and an organic farming charity paid for the research.

The study will likely be criticized. Using so many studies could let some poor-quality studies affect the results. The team did extra checks and said removing weaker studies did not change the results much. Also, the cadmium and pesticide levels in conventional food were still below legal limits. But the researchers said cadmium builds up in the body over time, and some people may want to avoid it. They also said pesticide limits are set for each chemical one by one, not for mixtures of chemicals.

Another criticism is that the differences might come from different climates, soil, and crop types, not from organic farming. The researchers said using many studies should reduce these effects. The biggest argument is about possible health benefits. A major study in 2012 that used 223 studies found little evidence. It said there was not strong evidence that organic food is much more nutritious than conventional food.

Smaller studies in 2009, including one by the UK Food Standards Agency, also said about the same thing. The 2012 study did say organic food might help people avoid pesticide residues. Sanders said he was not convinced by the new study. He said people will not be better nourished just because food is organic. He said what matters most is eating fruit and vegetables, whether they are organic or not. He said some people also buy organic because they want food without chemicals and not from big business.

Opinion polls show people buy organic food mainly because they want healthy eating (55%) and to avoid chemical residues (53%). Other reasons include caring for the environment (44%), animal welfare (31%), and taste (35%). Browning said this research supports what many people already believe about organic food. She said that in other countries there has long been more support for the benefits of organic food and farming, and she hopes the UK will become more like the rest of Europe.

text:
Tua Pittman is a master traditional navigator from Raratonga in the Cook Islands. He says a canoe is more than transport. “The canoe is our island, the crew members are the community and the navigator is the leader,” he says. He also says it can be the other way around. “An island is our canoe, the community are the crew members and the politicians and leaders are the navigators.” He explains that navigation is not only using the stars, the moon, the sun, and the birds to travel. It is also about leadership and showing people the right way to live.

A group of four sailing canoes arrived in Sydney for the start of the World Parks Congress. Tua’s trip began in the Cook Islands on 25 September. The first part of the trip went to Samoa, then Fiji, Vanuatu, and the Gold Coast, and then south to Sydney. About 100 crew members joined at different times. They wanted to travel using only traditional navigation, but sometimes they had to use modern equipment to reach Australia in time for the Congress.

The trip is called the Mua Voyage. It is a partnership between the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Oceania Regional Office and five Pacific Island countries: Samoa, Tonga, New Zealand, the Cook Islands, and Fiji. The main goal of the 6,000-nautical-mile (11,000km) journey was to bring a special message to the World Parks Congress.

Part of the message said the ocean is being used too much. The islanders said they do not see fish and other sea animals in the same size, variety, or number as before. They said foreign fishing fleets are taking resources quickly. They said coral reefs, mangroves, and wetlands where sea life begins are disappearing. They said the ocean is huge, but it is not endless. They also said more people in the world and unsustainable development are making the ocean less able to support life.

During the Congress, people spent a lot of time discussing how much of the ocean should be protected in marine sanctuaries. The IUCN said that in 2013, less than three per cent of the world’s oceans were in marine protected areas, and less than one per cent was “no take.” This was even though the last World Parks Congress in South Africa in 2003 had set a target of 20–30% no-take areas.

Professor Callum Roberts from the University of York helped set the 20–30% target in 2003. He said it was not enough. He said the IUCN should lift the target from 30 to 33%. He said new research supports raising the target, and that reducing efforts now would be disastrous for the oceans.

After difficult talks, the Congress delegates passed a motion that changed the goals for managing the ocean. The IUCN’s new official position is to urgently increase the ocean area that is managed well and fairly in connected systems of MPAs (marine protected areas) or other conservation measures by 2030. These should include strictly protected areas of at least 30% of each marine habitat and should protect both biodiversity and ecosystem services.

Tua Pittman was very happy that a strong decision about the oceans was passed. He said it felt like a huge reward for the hard work to come and be heard, and that it was a step in the right direction. He said the sun, moon, and stars do not change, but damage to the environment was becoming clearer during the Pacific voyage.

Tua said he is 55, and in his lifetime it has become much harder to catch fish in the open ocean. He also said pollution was getting worse, especially near big cities like Sydney. He said climate change was already seriously affecting Pacific Islanders. He said decisions made by big countries can have two, three, or four times the impact on small countries. He said many people do not even know where the islands are, but decisions made far away affect their homes.

Tua said the Mua Voyage was a very large planning job. It took years of preparation and navigation planning. The voyagers needed the world to listen and act. He said leaders of wealthy countries should think more like traditional navigators, who know their vessels are tiny in a huge sea. He said politicians must find a different route to avoid the ecological crises that are beginning to affect small island nations. “The world needs to find a different path.”

text:
Angry waiters want the public to help them keep their tips. Protesters plan to target PizzaExpress restaurants. They want PizzaExpress to stop taking part of the tips that customers pay by credit or debit card. Campaigners also started an online petition, hoping customers will support them.

Some workers are upset because PizzaExpress keeps 8p out of every £1 of card tips as an “admin fee.” Unite, a union, says the company makes about £1m a year this way. PizzaExpress has 430 branches in the UK and is popular with families.

Chantal Chegrinec from Unite said the 8% fee is unfair. She said staff should get all the tips customers give. She also said PizzaExpress is not the only company doing this, and the union may look at other companies later.

Local Unite groups are organizing the protests. The first protest will be at the PizzaExpress at the British Museum in London. The union also wrote to PizzaExpress CEO Richard Hodgson. Unite started the campaign after a survey of PizzaExpress union members. The chain was sold to a Chinese private equity firm, Hony Capital, in 2014. One big issue was the 8% taken from tips.

One PizzaExpress employee, who did not want her name used, said the fee costs her £3 each night. She said she has worked there for 15 years but is still paid the national minimum wage of £6.50 an hour. She said she and her co-workers need tips because their wages are low. She said it upsets her when PizzaExpress takes part of tips paid by card.

PizzaExpress is not the only chain that takes 8% from card tips. Ask and Zizzi do the same. Some chains take even more: Cafe Rouge, Bella Italia, and Belgo take 10%. Strada and Giraffe also take 10%, and Giraffe is owned by Tesco.

A PizzaExpress spokesperson said the admin charge pays for a “tronc.” This is a system used to share tips among staff. She said it is run by employees, with a troncmaster and a committee of waiters and pizzaiolos. They decide how card tips are shared between front-of-house and back-of-house teams.

PizzaExpress sells 29m pizzas a year in the UK. The company said it does not profit from the admin fee. Some other restaurant groups do not take any fee from tips. Wagamama, Pizza Hut, and TGI Friday take nothing. The Restaurant Group, which owns Frankie & Benny’s, Chiquitos, and Garfunkels, used to take 10% but stopped several years ago.

Unite recently went to ten PizzaExpress restaurants in south London and gave leaflets to customers. Unite said customers were “shocked and disgusted.” PizzaExpress said the charge is written in small print at the bottom of menus. But the employee said customers were always surprised when she told them. Many then paid the tip in cash. Almost 6,000 people have signed the online petition.

A waiter who does not work for PizzaExpress, but has worked for another chain for 11 years, said tips are at least a third of his income. He did not want his name used because he is afraid of punishment. He said he works in a busy London branch. On an average night, he serves 150 people and takes home £40 to £50 in tips. He said this money is very important because his basic pay is only £6.50 an hour.

Conservative MP Andrew Percy said he wants the law to change so restaurant staff have more control over tips. He said he plans to bring the issue up in parliament after the summer recess.

text:
A new science study says that if climate change is not controlled, Earth’s temperature could rise to the most severe levels scientists have predicted.

The lead scientist said that if we do not cut greenhouse gas emissions, the planet will warm by at least 4C by the year 2100. This is twice the level that many governments say is dangerous.

The study says that as the planet warms, fewer clouds form. With fewer clouds, less sunlight is reflected back into space. This makes the planet warm even more. How clouds affect global warming has been a big mystery.

Professor Steven Sherwood from the University of New South Wales in Australia led the work. He said the study helps explain what controls cloud changes. He also said it makes the lowest warming estimates less likely, and supports higher and more harmful estimates.

Sherwood said that 4C of warming would probably be catastrophic, not just dangerous. He said it could make life very hard, or even impossible, in much of the tropics. He also said it would guarantee that the Greenland ice sheet and some of the Antarctic ice sheet would eventually melt. That would raise sea levels by many metres.

Other scientists said the research is a big advance. They said it cuts the uncertainty about warming from carbon emissions in half. The study was published in the journal Nature. Hideo Shiogama and Tomoo Ogura from Japan’s National Institute for Environmental Studies said the explanation for fewer clouds was convincing. They agreed it points to more future climate change than expected. But they said it will still be hard to make temperature predictions even more exact.

Scientists often measure how strongly greenhouse gases warm Earth by asking what would happen if CO2 in the air doubled compared with pre-industrial levels. On current trends, this could happen within 50 years. For 20 years, estimates for this warming have ranged from 1.5C to 5C. The new research narrowed the range to 3C to 5C by closely studying clouds.

The researchers tried to make sure computer climate models show cloud formation more accurately. Climate models are the main tool scientists use to predict future temperatures. When water evaporates from the oceans, the vapour can rise over nine miles and form rain clouds that reflect sunlight. Or it may rise only a few miles and then sink back down without forming clouds. In real life, both happen. Models that include both processes predicted much higher future temperatures than models that include only the nine-mile-high clouds.

Sherwood said climate sceptics often say climate models are wrong, and he agreed they are not perfect. But he said the models that make the biggest mistakes are the ones that predict less warming, not more. He also said sceptics point to a “hiatus” in warming since the end of the 20th century, but evidence suggests this “hiatus” is not seen in other parts of the climate system and is almost certainly temporary.

Global average air temperatures have risen more slowly since a high point in 1998, which was caused by El Nino. But scientists see that greenhouse gases are still trapping more heat, and over 90% of that heat is going into the oceans. A study in November 2013 also suggested the “pause” may be mostly an illusion because there are not enough temperature readings from polar regions, where warming is greatest.

Sherwood said his team’s work cannot fully prove that lower temperature rises are impossible. But he said that for low rises to happen, there would need to be a major missing piece that we currently have no evidence for. He said this is possible but would take a lot of faith. He added that if global temperatures rise by at least 4C by 2100, it will strongly affect the world and the economies of many countries unless we urgently reduce emissions.

text:
A big report warns that up to 1 billion people could still live in extreme poverty in 2030. This may happen unless countries work on inequality and on social, economic, and cultural problems that stop people from escaping poverty or push them back into it.

The report is by the Chronic Poverty Advisory Network (CPAN). It says some people can rise above the poverty line of $1.25 a day, but then fall back into poverty. This can happen when they face shocks like drought, illness, or insecurity or conflict.

Using household surveys, the report found that in parts of rural Kenya and in South Africa, 30% to 40% of people who escaped poverty later fell back. In some areas of Ethiopia, the number was 60% between 1999 and 2009. Even in countries doing well, like Indonesia and Vietnam, about 20% fell back.

Some stories show how easily this can happen. Amin, 61, from rural Bangladesh, has seen his work and income slowly get worse. This happened because he and his wife were ill, his son’s marriage cost money, his father died, and he lost goods like fishing nets. Lovemore, 74, from Zimbabwe, is now one of the poorest people in his village. He lost his job as a car-park attendant because of poor health. He also had to take in his five grandchildren after his daughters died.

Andrew Shepherd, a research fellow at the Overseas Development Institute and lead author of the report, said governments must help people stay above the poverty line permanently. He said too many families make progress and then lose it. He said governments should not think the job is done just because someone’s income reaches $1.25.

A UN high-level panel said ending extreme poverty by 2030 is possible. But this report says that doing “more of the same” will not bring poverty down to zero.

Extreme poverty fell from 1.9 billion people in 1990 to 1.2 billion in 2010. But the report says the next 15 years will be harder. Big progress like China’s may not happen in many other places. Climate-related shocks and very deep poverty in parts of sub-Saharan Africa may slow progress.

The report says we should focus more on the chronically poor. These are people who are poor for many years or for their whole lives. The report also says it is important to stop impoverishment, which means stopping people from falling into poverty. Shepherd said governments often help people just cross the poverty line because that is easier, but they avoid the harder work of ending chronic poverty.

The report says past progress has helped people who were already close to the poverty line more than it has helped the chronically poor. It says poverty will not reach zero unless policies put the chronically poor first.

The report gives three connected goals: tackle chronic poverty, stop impoverishment, and help people stay out of poverty. It also suggests three main policies, which need very large global investment.

First, it calls for social assistance, or a safety net, to help the poorest people reach a decent standard of living. One example is Ethiopia’s productive safety net programme. It helped many vulnerable households survive drought without cutting spending on education.

Second, it calls for education from early childhood until people start work. Education can help people escape poverty and stay out of it. The report says education is also a “portable” asset, because people can carry what they learn anywhere.

Third, it calls for economic growth policies that help the very poorest people the most, so they get a share of the country’s increasing prosperity.

Shepherd said economic growth is important, but it must create decent jobs, not only low-paying and insecure jobs. He gave India’s rural employment guarantees scheme as an example, saying it has raised wages in urban areas as well as rural areas. The report also points to South Africa extending the minimum wage to groups like domestic workers.

The report says all of this costs money. It says countries will need stronger tax systems and higher tax revenues. Aid will also be needed to pay start-up costs for social assistance, universal health coverage, and education, including scholarships for the poorest children. Shepherd said aid will still matter in the next 20 years because many developing countries spend less than $500 per person each year. He said Nigeria, even with oil wealth, spends only $650 per person.

With crises in Syria, South Sudan, and the Central African Republic, the report says conflict and environmental disasters must be dealt with. It says governments need to reduce the risk of conflict and create inclusive peace settlements.

The report says making “reducing (income) inequality” a goal after 2015 would help. It says lowering or controlling inequality makes economic growth reduce poverty faster. It also says that if we fix inequalities that hurt the poorest people, such as access to land, labour markets, and power between men and women, we can reduce chronic poverty and inequality at the same time.

The authors ask governments to make inclusive national development plans and work with civil society. They say the poorest people should be represented in politics. They also say governments should challenge difficult social norms, such as dowries and witchcraft, that can contribute to extreme poverty. They say this would mean changing the status quo and moving away from “business as usual.”

text:
Rebecka Singerer got tired of hearing the same thing again and again: that the beer she wanted was “too dark and too strong” and that she should choose something sweeter.

She said, “No, I don’t want a fruit beer. Women can drink whatever we want.”

Singerer is a childminder. She joined FemAle in Gothenburg, a group of women who like beer. They helped launch Sweden’s first beer made by women.

The beer is called We Can Do It. It is a bottled pale ale, and it is now sold in stores across Sweden. The label shows Rosie the Riveter, a picture from a US Second World War campaign that later became a symbol of women’s power at work.

FemAle was started by Elin Carlsson, 25. She paints cars at the Volvo factory near the city. She says, “We Can Do It is not a female beer. It is a beer brewed by women that anyone can drink. It’s not about feminism; it’s about equality. We wanted to show we can do it.”

FemAle is facing old prejudice in the beer world. Big brewers like Carlsberg have spent millions trying to sell beer to women by making it seem more “pink” and smaller. Beers like Carlsberg’s Eve and Copenhagen, Foster’s Radler, and Coors’s Animée were lighter and flavored, and some were even called “bloat-resistant.” But they did not sell well.

FemAle does something different. They hold women-only tastings. This helps women try beer flavors and styles they might not normally try. The group says this learning is how to “get more girls into the beer world.” They say, “Bring your mother, sister, girlfriend, aunt and grandmother so we can all learn more about beer.”

The idea for FemAle began because the women kept meeting each other at beer festivals.

We Can Do It was the idea of Felicia Nordström, a bar worker. She was tired of beer snobs saying, “What do you know about beer, sweetie?” She contacted FemAle. Together they worked with Ocean, a local independent micro-brewery. One weekend they made the recipe, and the next they brewed 1,600 litres.

Thomas Bingebo, the head brewer at Ocean, says, “This beer is not aimed at women. It’s our hoppiest brew. When big breweries target women, it usually fails. This is completely different.”

The first batch of We Can Do It sold out almost before it was brewed. Other breweries have already asked FemAle if they can make new beers together.

We Can Do It uses three malts: Maris Otter, Amber, and a Thomas Fawcett wheat malt. It uses Galaxy and Cascade hops. Its bitterness level (IBU) is 65, and its alcohol content is 4.6%.

Carlsson says, “Women choose wine because they don’t know what beer is about. They don’t know what to order. We open up new worlds to them.”

Singerer, 38, says she used to not like stout, because she only drank Pripps, a light Swedish lager. Now she says Guinness tastes like water to her. She says some imperial stouts are like drinking biscotti dipped in espresso.

These women are part of a big rise in brewing in Sweden. Many people now like “craft” ales, in bottles and on tap. In Gothenburg, the usual stor stark lager is now “almost extinct,” they say. Many pubs and bars are replacing big brands with many specialist beers.

Emma Henriksson, 22, a FemAle member who works at a garden equipment company, says, “All girls are different. There is no typical woman beer-lover. Anyone can do it.”

Singerer adds, “Every pub wants to learn how to reach women. And Elin has found the way. It’s awesome. We feel so proud.”

text:
People from all across Rwanda, and even from nearby Burundi, travel to the southern town of Butare. They go to a small shop called Inzozi Nziza (Sweet Dreams). They come to try something many have never eaten before: ice cream.

In this first ice-cream shop in Rwanda, people can buy scoops in sweet cream, passion fruit, strawberry, and pineapple. They can add toppings like fresh fruit, honey, chocolate chips, and granola. The shop also sells black tea and coffee.

The signs say “ice cream, coffee, dreams.” The manager, Louise Ingabire, says the shop is also “changing lives.” She says ice cream is new for many Rwandans, so they still need to tell more people that they will enjoy it.

Ingabire says the job helped her. She says she did not have a job before and stayed at home. Now she has a plan for the future. She makes money and can help her family.

Butare has 89,600 residents and is 135 km south of the capital, Kigali. It is home to the National University of Rwanda. Inzozi Nziza has become a popular place for tired students who want something cool and different.

Kalisa Migendo, a 24-year-old agriculture student, says it brings people together. He says if you want to go out and talk to a friend, a girl, or a boy, you come to Inzozi Nziza for ice cream.

Most ingredients come from nearby places. The milk comes from a depot in nearby Nyanza. The vanilla beans and cocoa are imported.

The theatre director Odile Gakire Katese opened Inzozi Nziza. She met Alexis Miesen and Jennie Dundas, co-founders of Blue Marble Ice Cream in Brooklyn, New York. They partnered to open the shop in 2010.

Miesen says Katese believed an ice-cream shop could help people by rebuilding spirits, hopes, and family traditions.

At first, Miesen and Dundas owned the shop together with the staff. The business is a cooperative and non-profit. They did not set money goals. After 18 months, they gave their shares to the women, who had shown they could run the business.

Fatuma Ndangiza, deputy chief executive of the Rwanda Governance Board, says many small businesses are managed by women. But very few women are in big business and compete for big tenders. She says more women are becoming entrepreneurs, in and outside Kigali.

Ndangiza says ice cream is new in Rwanda, but she likes the idea of the shop. She says it takes many skills and it changes how people think, because selling and eating ice cream is not part of their culture. She says it is interesting to introduce it and make it.

The Butare shop employs nine women. In their free time, they practise with Ingoma Nshya, Rwanda’s first and only female drumming group. Katese started it ten years ago.

The drummers are Hutu and Tutsi women. Some are survivors of the 1994 genocide, when almost a million Tutsis and moderate Hutus were killed.

Some members are widows, and some are orphans. Others were affected by the killings in different ways.

Ingabire says that in the past, Rwandan women were not allowed to drum. Many people thought the drums were too heavy for women to carry. But she says drumming brings unity.

Ingabire says her father, two siblings, and many cousins were killed in the genocide. She says some women are survivors, and some knew someone who was killed. She says when she drums with them, it gives her power because they are still alive.

The shop is shown in a documentary by Rob and Lisa Fruchtman, who are brother and sister film-makers. The film is called Sweet Dreams. It tells how the women built a hopeful life after the genocide, and it also shows the female drummers.

The film has been shown in more than a dozen countries, including the US, UK, and several African states. It premieres in Rwanda in 2014. Lisa Fruchtman, who won an Academy Award for film-editing in 1984, says the film is about resilience, hope, bravery, resourcefulness, and the ability to change your own life.

text:
In the journal Nature, Roger McKinlay, a former president of the Royal Institute of Navigation, says we may trust GPS (global positioning system) too much. He thinks this could weaken our natural sense of direction. He wrote that if we do not value these skills, they will get worse as we use smart devices more.

McKinlay says we will need a lot of money and work before navigation systems are good enough for things like self-driving cars. Until then, he says we need better research on navigation systems. He also says children should learn to find their way using older methods. He wrote that schools should teach navigation and map reading as life skills.

Ofcom (the Office of Communications) says about 66% of adults in the UK owned a smartphone in 2015, up from 39% in 2012. This makes GPS easy for many people to use. But McKinlay, who works as a satellite communication and navigation consultant, says we should be careful about letting devices do all our navigating. He wrote that navigation is a “use-it-or-lose-it” skill.

Not many scientific studies have looked at this, but a study from 2009 supports the idea. Dr Hugo Spiers, a neuroscientist at University College London, helped write the study. The researchers compared London taxi drivers who were still working with taxi drivers who had been retired for about four years. The retired drivers did worse on navigation tests than the working drivers. Spiers said this showed their skills dropped when they were not using them.

Spiers also says relying on GPS can be dangerous, especially if it leads people into unsafe places. One death linked to satnavs (satellite navigation devices) was in 2010, when a driver drove into a reservoir in Spain. Spiers said there is real risk in relying on a satnav, but the health risk of not using your brain for navigation is not known.

Spiers says the effect on our skills can depend on how we use navigation technology. If a device gives audio directions, drivers may not need to think much. But using smartphone apps like digital maps can be different. He said people still need to think hard about where they are going and interact with the device. He said modern technology does not make us less smart in every way.

McKinlay still believes navigation technology is not advanced enough for ideas like driverless cars and smart cities. He said that for very important safety uses, like landing aircraft or navigating aircraft, GPS is still not good enough.

Spiers thinks artificial intelligence using machine learning could create new navigation tools. But McKinlay is doubtful. He said machines will become smarter at specific tasks, but the big change will be when they can understand what you are thinking and what you want to do.

In the end, McKinlay says people must still be able to navigate on their own. He asked if we really want people to be unable to cope when the system fails or the battery runs out. He said technology is not magic; it is just a tool.

text:
Scarlett Johansson is suing a French novelist. She wants Û50,000 in damages. She says his fiction book makes false claims about her personal life.

The book is called La Premiere chose qu’on regarde (The First Thing We Look At). It was written by Grégoire Delacourt. It is about a French model who looks very much like Scarlett Johansson. A man in the story thinks the model is Johansson. Because she looks like her, men treat her like a sex object and women feel jealous. The model pretends to be Johansson and has many adventures. Later, people learn the truth. At the end, the model dies in a car crash.

Johansson does not like the book. Her lawyer, Vincent Toledano, says the novel illegally uses her name, reputation, and image. He also says the book has defamatory claims about her private life. They went to court to try to stop the book from being translated or made into a movie. The court case began in Paris on Wednesday afternoon. Neither Johansson nor Delacourt was there.

Toledano said freedom of expression is not the issue. He said the book was made for money, not creativity.

Delacourt said he mentioned Johansson because she is “the archetype of beauty today.” He said, “I wrote a work of fiction. My character is not Scarlett Johansson.”

On French radio, Delacourt said the lawsuit was “rather sad.” He said it scares him that judges could get involved in what happens to a character in a novel.

Delacourt is a well-known author in France. His earlier novel, My List of Desires, was translated into 47 languages and is being made into a film. He said he was “speechless” when he learned Johansson was suing him.

He said he thought she would contact him to meet for coffee. He said he did not write a novel about a celebrity. He said he wrote a love story and a tribute to women’s beauty, especially inner beauty.

He said if authors cannot mention things around them, like a beer brand, a monument, or an actor, it will be hard to write fiction. He also said it is surprising because he thinks Johansson has not read the novel, since it has not been translated yet.

Emmanuelle Allibert, a spokeswoman for the publisher JC Lattès, said the lawsuit is “crazy.” She said the novel is not about Scarlett Johansson. She said it is about a woman who is Johansson’s double.

The text says the case might be easier for Delacourt if the book had been published in the United States instead of France. Lloyd Jassin, a New York intellectual property lawyer, told Time that a U.S. court would probably not take the case because the book would be protected by the First Amendment. He said the First Amendment usually protects books if the use of a person’s name has literary meaning and merit. In France, he said, personality rights are taken more seriously.

Delacourt said he thought Johansson might send him flowers because he saw the book as a declaration of love for her, but she did not understand. He called it a strange paradox.

text:
When the children at a primary school in Stirling hear the words “daily mile,” they put down their pencils and go outside to run laps around the school field. For three-and-a-half years, all pupils at St Ninian’s Primary have walked or run one mile each day. They do it at different times during the day. They seem happy to do it. Even though childhood obesity is rising across the UK, none of the children at this school are overweight.

The daily mile has helped the children’s fitness, behaviour, and concentration in class. Because of this, many nursery and primary schools across Britain are now doing it too. Pupils leave their desks for about 15 minutes to walk or run around the school or a local park.

Elaine Wyllie, the headteacher of St Ninian’s, said she gets at least two emails a day from other schools and local authorities asking how they do it. She said it is amazing to think children across the country might run every day because of what her school started.

Figures from the Health & Social Care Information Centre say that one in ten children are obese when they start school at age four or five. In summer 2015, a study found that schoolchildren in England are the least fit they have ever been. Many primary schools have noticed the benefits of the daily mile. Schools in London, Gateshead, Wales, and other parts of Scotland have started it. Other schools planned to start during the 2015-6 academic year. In Stirling, 30 schools have started or plan to start the daily mile.

Wyllie said it is a common-sense way to improve children’s fitness, and it is free and easy. She said the most important thing is that children enjoy it, or they would not keep doing it. She said they come back looking healthy and full of energy. At St Ninian’s, teachers take pupils out of lessons and onto a special path around the playing field when it fits the day’s timetable best. Only ice or very heavy rain stops them.

Researchers from Stirling University have started a study to find clear evidence of the physical, thinking, and emotional benefits of the daily mile. Dr Colin Moran, who leads the study, said the children at St Ninian’s do not seem to have obesity problems, they seem happier, and staff say they settle into lessons faster. He said there is a lot of personal stories about the benefits, but no scientific facts yet. The pupils at St Ninian’s will be compared with children from another school in Stirling that has not started the scheme yet.

Kevin Clelland, a primary school teacher from Leeds, visited St Ninian’s and then told his colleagues it was a great idea. He said it is simple but seems to have a big effect. He said his school wants to improve pupils’ fitness beyond the two hours of PE they are expected to provide. His school is now building a track.

Active Cheshire, a group for sports and fitness in Cheshire and Warrington, is taking senior people from the local authority to Scotland to look at the results of the daily mile. They hope to bring it to all 450 schools in the region if a trial goes well.

Paralympian Tanni Grey-Thompson, chair of ukactive, said children need 60 minutes of activity every day. This can be during lessons, walking to school, or playing. She said it is good to see the daily mile growing. She said it shows strong leadership from education to improve children’s fitness and behaviour, and to help schools, teachers, parents, and young people. She said sitting still is harmful, and moving helps children learn skills they will use for life.

The Scottish government also supports it. A spokesperson said PE learning is improved by projects like the daily mile. They said it can help parents teach healthy habits to children from a young age. They said they are pleased that many Scottish schools are taking part or planning to do so.

text:
Do you want your child to do well in sport, make the school team, and maybe compete in big events one day? A study says children born in November, or in October, may have an advantage. It found that pupils born in these months are fitter than others in their class.

The study was led by Dr Gavin Sandercock from the Centre for Sports and Exercise Science at Essex University. He and his team found that children born in October and November were fitter, stronger, and more powerful than children born in the other months, especially April or June.

The research tested 8,550 boys and girls aged 10 to 16 from 26 state schools in Essex. The tests happened between 2007 and 2010. The children did three fitness tests: stamina, handgrip strength, and lower-body power. The study said the month you are born can make important differences in heart and lung fitness, muscle strength, and how quickly you can speed up. These skills matter in many sports.

Children born in November were the fittest overall. They had the most stamina and power, and they were the second strongest. Children born in October were almost as fit. They were the strongest and came third for power. Children born in December were close behind.

Sometimes the differences were large. Dr Sandercock said a boy born in November can run at least 10% faster, jump 12% higher, and be 15% more powerful than a boy of the same age born in April. The study said small differences like this can affect who gets chosen for top sports teams.

When the three test scores were added together, children born in April were the least fit, then children born in June. Dr Sandercock said this could mean these children are not picked for school teams and may do less well in sport.

The study suggests children born early in the school year may have an “autumn advantage.” They were already known to do better in school work, and now they also seem to do better in sport. Dr Sandercock said it is not only because these children are older in the school year, because the fittest children were not the tallest or the heaviest.

The authors think one possible reason is vitamin D from sunlight. They believe babies whose mothers were pregnant during summer may get more vitamin D near the end of pregnancy.

John Steele, chief executive of the Youth Sport Trust, said a good first experience of PE and sport at school can strongly affect how well a child develops in sport. He said it can help children build agility, balance, and coordination, and enjoy being active.

UK Sport could not say if many of the 1,300 athletes across 47 sports it funds were born in October and November. Natalie Dunman, head of performance pathways, said the differences seen in teenagers in junior competitions were gone in adult competitions. She said many things help make a champion, and their work has not shown that birth month is a key reason.

text:
A subway system is like a city’s veins. It carries many tiny living things, especially bacteria. These bacteria can come from food, the human body, and sometimes from germs that can cause sickness. For the first time, scientists started to count and map the bacteria in a city’s subway. They found many interesting results.

Dr Christopher Mason, a scientist at Weill Cornell Medical College, led a team in New York City. For 18 months, they used swabs to collect bacteria from turnstiles, seats, ticket booths, and stations. They found meningitis at Times Square. They also found a very small trace of anthrax on a train handhold, and bacteria that can cause bubonic plague on a rubbish bin and a ticket machine in uptown Manhattan.

The scientists said people should not worry. They said the anthrax trace was extremely small. They also said rats likely carried the plague bacteria. No one has gotten plague in or around New York for years. The study said plague and anthrax are not common, and New York residents are not at risk.

Most of the bacteria they found are not harmful. Some bacteria may even help, like bacteria that break down waste. This is like bacteria inside the human body that help with digestion.

Some findings were expected. Mason said they found some bacteria linked to fecal matter, and this should remind people to wash their hands. They also found bacteria from the same group as helpful bacteria used to make cheese.

The bacteria also seemed to match food habits in different neighbourhoods. Around the subway, they found bacteria linked to cheeses like brie, cheddar, parmesan, and mozzarella from New York pizza. Swiss cheese bacteria were more common in midtown Manhattan and the financial district. Bacteria used to ferment cabbage for kimchi and sauerkraut showed up in the financial district and Bay Ridge. The computer also found cucumber DNA all over the city.

Bacteria linked to illness were common too. They found bacteria that can cause diarrhoea and nausea. They found E. coli (mostly harmless types). They found bacteria that can cause skin infections and urinary-tract infections all over the city. The bacteria that produces tetanus appeared in Soho. Bacteria that cause dysentery appeared at one station in the Bronx and another in Harlem.

The team collected more than 1,000 samples from all 466 open subway stations. They used a DNA sequencer and a supercomputer with genetic databases. They identified 15,152 different species, and almost half were bacteria.

The researchers wrote that these possible infectious agents do not seem to be spreading disease in New York. Instead, they seem to be normal organisms living with people, and may even be important. They said this is a normal, healthy genetic picture of a city.

The researchers said the subway and the city are about as safe as people thought.

Mason said people should not worry about getting urinary-tract infections from subway seats. He said people should wash their hands, get sleep, eat salads, and go to the gym, just like before. He also said he feels more confident riding the subway now.

Other results also made sense. Busy stations like Grand Central and Times Square had more bacteria and more kinds of bacteria. The subway had many bacteria linked to human skin. The Bronx had the most variety of bacteria. Staten Island, with only three stops, had the least.

The researchers found marine bacteria at South Ferry, which flooded during Hurricane Sandy. They were surprised that some of the species are usually linked to Antarctica and fish.

Next, Mason said they will study other cities. Studies have begun in Paris, Sao Paulo, and Shanghai. They will also keep studying New York to see how the microbiome changes with the seasons. He hopes this research will give a baseline for health officials and geneticists, and help health officials be more ready to prevent and track diseases and pathogens.

text:
David Cameron said there was a “clear result” in the Scottish independence vote. Scotland voted against leaving the United Kingdom by a 10.6-point margin, keeping the 307-year-old union with England and Wales. Cameron said there should be “no disputes” and “no reruns.” He promised more powers for different parts of Great Britain. He also said there should be votes on English issues by English MPs at Westminster.

Alex Salmond, Scotland’s first minister, spoke at a Scottish National Party rally in Edinburgh. He said he accepted that Scotland had not, “at this stage,” chosen independence. He called the vote a “triumph for democratic politics.” He said he would work with Westminster for Scotland and the rest of the UK. He also warned the leaders of the three main parties to keep their promises to give Scotland more powers. He said more people had taken part in politics than before.

The yes campaign won in some places. It got 53% in Glasgow, 57% in Dundee, and 51% in North Lanarkshire. But the no campaign won in 28 areas. It won strongly in places like Edinburgh, Aberdeenshire, and Borders. It also won in the Western Isles. In the final count, no won 2,001,926 votes (55.3%), and yes won 1,617,989 votes (44.7%).

Cameron said big changes to the UK system would not happen until after the general election. He said changes for Scotland would happen along with changes in England. He said, “We have heard the voice of Scotland and, now, the millions of voices of England must be heard.”

Cameron said he was happy Scotland stayed in the UK. He said it would have “broken my heart” to see the UK end. He said the UK should now come together and move forward. He said there should be a fair plan for Scotland and also for England, Wales, and Northern Ireland.

Ed Miliband, the Labour leader, said the vote was a call for change. He said Labour would give stronger powers to a stronger Scottish parliament. He also said there should be change across England, Wales, and the whole UK.

Nick Clegg, the deputy prime minister, said the vote starts a new chapter for Scotland in the UK and also wider changes across the union. He said voting no was not a vote against change. He said the UK must deliver the new set of powers promised to Scotland.

Some Conservative MPs threatened to oppose Cameron. They were unhappy about his late promise to quickly increase the Scottish parliament’s powers while protecting its spending.

Nigel Farage, leader of the UK Independence Party, said Cameron’s plan for England did not go far enough. He said England is 86% of the population of the union. He said English people have been left out for 18 years. He said Scottish MPs can still vote on English-only issues in the House of Commons. He said English people want a fair plan.

Cameron and the Queen planned to make statements to calm tensions. Cameron planned to explain how he would increase Scotland’s devolution, including giving the Scottish Parliament more power over tax and welfare.

The Queen would make a written statement. It was understood her words would focus on bringing people together again. Cameron wanted to act fast to show the three main UK party leaders would keep their promises. Former prime minister Gordon Brown called this “home rule” within the UK.

Ministers said it was important to move quickly to avoid a repeat of the 1980 referendum in Quebec. They said behaviour in Ontario helped separatists, and the cause almost won in a second referendum in 1995.

People in the no campaign felt relieved. In the last days, polls said the vote was very close, and Yes Scotland seemed near to winning after a big rise in support.

text:
SeaWorld’s profits fell by 84% because many customers stopped going to the park. People left after claims that SeaWorld mistreated orca whales.

SeaWorld trains dolphins and killer whales to do tricks for large crowds. The company said attendance, sales, and profits went down because of ongoing problems with its brand.

SeaWorld has had a lot of news coverage since the 2013 documentary Blackfish. The film said SeaWorld’s treatment of orcas led to violent behavior and was linked to the deaths of three people. After the film came out, attendance dropped a lot. SeaWorld lost more than half of its market value on Wall Street, and its CEO left.

SeaWorld started a marketing campaign across the country to fight claims from animal rights activists. One claim is that orcas in captivity die younger than wild orcas.

SeaWorld lowered ticket prices and spent $10m on the campaign. The ads show SeaWorld veterinarians caring for whales. But CEO Joel Manby said the company is still having trouble convincing the public that it treats whales well.

Manby said the company needs time and investment to raise attendance, increase revenue, and improve results. He said early feedback on the campaign was positive, but fixing the brand problems in California will take continued effort to correct misinformation.

He also said, “We will continue to fight with the facts because the facts are on our side.”

Manby became CEO in 2015 to help SeaWorld rebuild its image. He said he would share his future plans at a special event on 6 November.

SeaWorld has plans for a new shark exhibit in Orlando. It also plans an attraction in San Antonio where customers can swim with dolphins in a “naturalistic” setting.

A financial report released on 6 August showed SeaWorld’s net income in the second quarter fell from $37.4m in 2014 to $5.8m in 2015. Revenue fell from $405m to $392m. Attendance dropped by more than 100,000, from 6.58 million to 6.48 million.

Analysts will watch sales and attendance in the third quarter. This is usually SeaWorld’s most profitable time and includes the summer holiday season. Attendance may drop more because of a new scandal from July 2015. In that case, a SeaWorld employee was accused of secretly joining animal rights protest groups.

Jared Goodman from PETA said SeaWorld is in a spying scandal, animals are dying in its tanks, and many people opposed its plan to build a new place to keep orcas. He said families do not want to buy tickets to see orcas suffering in small tanks. He said SeaWorld’s profits will not recover until SeaWorld empties its tanks and builds coastal sanctuaries.

SeaWorld’s shares were $39 in 2013, but were just under $18 in August 2015.

text:
Many people around the world celebrate William Shakespeare. London also says he belongs to them. But Stratford-upon-Avon plans to spend 2016 celebrating him as their own man. Shakespeare was born in Stratford-upon-Avon in 1564, and he died there 400 years ago.

Paul Edmondson works at the Shakespeare Birthplace Trust. He says Stratford was very important to Shakespeare all his life. Some people think Shakespeare left Stratford, went to London to get rich, and only came back to die. But Edmondson says Stratford is where Shakespeare bought land and property. It is where he kept his library. It is where he lived, read, and thought. Stratford plans to remind people that Shakespeare was a man of Stratford.

A writer named John Aubrey wrote a diary in the 1600s. He was born 11 years after Shakespeare died. Aubrey said Shakespeare was not very special. Aubrey also said Shakespeare acted very well and that people liked his plays. But the world did not agree with Aubrey. People will mark the anniversary of Shakespeare’s death across Britain and around the world. Shakespeare is the most famous and most performed playwright in the world.

There will be many events. Macbeth will open in Singapore, and Romeo and Juliet in Brussels. Shakespeare’s Globe is finishing the first world tour in theatre history. It has taken Hamlet to almost every country. North Korea has not joined yet. In London, they are also making a pop-up cinema with 37 screens. Each screen will show one of Shakespeare’s plays. It will be along the South Bank.

The National Theatre, the Royal Shakespeare Company, and almost every theatre company in the country will mark the anniversary. Some shows will be very traditional. Others will be different, like Spymonkey’s Complete Deaths. It is a funny show about the 74 deaths in Shakespeare’s plays, or 75 if you count a fly squashed in Titus Andronicus. The deaths happen in many ways, like stabbing, poisoning, smothering, and smashing. There will also be hundreds of lectures, recitals, international academic conferences, films, concerts, operas, and big exhibitions.

Even though Shakespeare was famous while he was alive, there are not many documents about his life. His plays might not have survived without his friends and fellow actors. They collected every piece they could find, like drafts, prompt scripts, scribbled actors’ parts, and 17 plays not known in any other version. They put them into the First Folio, published in 1623, seven years after Shakespeare’s death.

Actor Mark Rylance has called the First Folio his favourite book in the world. Most of the surviving First Folios will be on display. These include copies from the British Library and the Bodleian Library, and a torn copy recently found in France. Some very important documents will be shown at Somerset House in London. This exhibition is organized by the National Archives and King’s College London. It will include four of Shakespeare’s six known signatures, and they are all a little different.

The exhibition, By Me, William Shakespeare, will include his will. It will also include court papers about a bold move. Shakespeare and his fellow actors took apart a theatre on the north side of the Thames and rebuilt it as the Globe on the South Bank. The exhibition will also show records of payments from the royal treasury for Boxing Day performances for James I and Queen Anne.

Dominic Dromgoole, the outgoing Globe director, joked that Shakespeare was a true Londoner. He also admitted Stratford-upon-Avon had “some spurious claim.” But Stratford says the town made and educated Shakespeare. His old school room is being restored with a £1.4m Heritage Lottery grant. It will open as a permanent visitor attraction.

Shakespeare bought New Place, a very fine house and the second best house in the town. He died there. A story says he died on St George’s Day, 23 April, the same day as his birth. Paul Edmondson said you do not buy a house like New Place and not live there. He says many people have underestimated how important Stratford was to Shakespeare.

Edmondson believes that after Shakespeare bought the house in 1597, all his thinking time was spent there. He thinks the late plays, including The Tempest, were at least planned in Shakespeare’s library and probably written there. The Shakespeare Birthplace Trust calls New Place “the jewel in the crown of the 400th anniversary celebrations.” But today, New Place is really a big empty space.

Shakespeare’s house was pulled down 300 years ago. The next house, which may have used some of the old building, was knocked down in 1759. A clergyman named Francis Gastrell did it during an argument about taxes. Before that, he cut down Shakespeare’s mulberry tree. People said Shakespeare sat under it and worked. Gastrell was annoyed by tourists looking into his garden.

The empty space in Stratford has never been filled. But a five-year archaeology project studied the site. News spread around the world that Shakespeare’s kitchen was found in cellars that still partly survive. For the anniversary, the site is being shown again. The foundations will be marked, and the garden will be restored. Edmondson said, “Without Stratford, there would have been no Shakespeare.”
