text:
Introduction
Did you know that in the UK, restaurants do not have to give tips to staff by law?

A government review, released in May 2016, asked workers, employers, and customers what they think about tipping. After this, ministers said they wanted to change the rules so low-paid workers get the money customers leave for them. The government announced a two-month consultation with ideas to stop unfair tipping.

The review also found that some waiters are charged up to a 15% administration fee on tips paid by credit or debit card. In some restaurants, the government saw evidence that waiters were forced to give their own cash if they did not collect enough tips to cover these fees. The government said it would think about banning employers from charging this admin fee or making other deductions from tips.

The government also said customers should know that tips are voluntary. It asked for a clearer system, with restaurants showing their tipping policy clearly.

We asked waiters around the UK what they think about tipping, including how much tip money they take home, whether it is fair, and what would make it better.

1. Rodri, 37, London: “Around 50% of a waiter’s income is tips”
Average tips: £60 per eight-hour shift
I think they treat waiters best in ... the US

Things have become fairer since the tipping scandal in summer 2015. At that time, it was revealed that many restaurants were taking tips from staff as administration fees. But you still hear bad stories from new staff at some chains. I have heard of restaurants not giving chefs a share of the tronc, which is a system used to share out tips. Some restaurants take 4% of all sales to pay the chefs and as an admin fee. So if you sell £1,000 of food, the company takes £40, no matter how much you make in tips. This means if a table has a £100 bill and does not tip, the waiter has to pay £4 from their own money. This can create a culture of trying to grab money and can lead to bad service. If a table does not tip, the waiter can feel the company is taking from them and may start to resent the customers.

I average about £60 in tips per shift. On a good night it can be over £100, and on a bad night it is around £40. About 50% of a waiter’s income is tips. All restaurants should have to publicly say exactly how tipping works. In America, it is good because everyone tips.

2. Elle, 22, Edinburgh: “We never know whether it’s fair”
Average tips: £20 per eight-hour shift
I think they treat waiters best in ... France

I work three part-time jobs on zero-hours contracts in catering and hospitality. My main day job is in a cafe, where the small team works both front of house and in the kitchen, so all tips go into one pot and are shared equally. My main evening job is in a restaurant where we do not get tips, but we are paid minimum wage plus an extra £2.50 per hour. This is said to be worked out to cover average tips when tips are shared with kitchen staff. My third job is events catering, and nobody tips there.

In restaurants, many customers tip by card, so staff do not see how much is being tipped. Because of this, we do not know if we are getting a fair share. The system seems better in France, where people do not tip much, but waiting tables is seen as a real job with job security and a decent income.

3. Ashley, 22, London: “Tips go towards customer breakages”
Average tips: £10-15 per eight-hour shift
I think they treat waiters best in ... Australia

I work evenings in a pub in south-east London and day shifts in a local restaurant. In both places, tips are collected and shared at the end of the night. Money is also taken from the tip jar to pay for breakages caused by staff or customers. If someone finishes early, they often do not get their tips because tips are shared just before the pub closes.

I think it is very unfair that tips are shared, especially when one person does not work well. Also, when someone works very well, they do not get what they earned. It is also unfair that tips are used to pay for things customers break. Managers should find other ways to pay for broken glasses and plates without taking from our tips. I might make around £20 in tips in a shift, but I may only be given £10-15.

When I get good tips, it really affects my money situation. Sometimes I earn over £50 in one night, which almost doubles what I earn that day. But after tips are shared, I never get more than £30. I rely on tips because I am paid only £7 an hour.

I would rather we had a good basic wage, like in Australia, so we did not have to depend on tips to survive.

4. Tom, Manchester: “A big night of tips can help pay the rent”
Average tips: £40 per eight-hour shift
I think they treat waiters best in ... Italy

text:
On one day in August, 1 in 7 people on Earth used Facebook. That is about 1 billion people, said Facebook founder Mark Zuckerberg. In 10 years, Facebook has changed how people have relationships, how they think about privacy, how they do business, and how they get news. It has helped bring down some governments. It has even changed the meanings of common words.

“A more open and connected world is a better world,” Zuckerberg wrote when he shared the numbers. He said it can bring stronger relationships, a stronger economy with more chances, and a stronger society.

Here are some ways Facebook has changed things, for better or worse.

1 Facebook changed what “friend” means  
Now “to friend” is a verb. And it is easy to “unfriend” someone on Facebook.

Facebook also changed the feeling of words like “share” and “like.”

Reunions at school and university can feel less important now. You often already know what people are doing. You may know who broke up, and you have seen many photos of their babies. You may also already know if an ex has a new boyfriend or girlfriend because of selfies.

But Facebook does not show different levels of friendship. A person you knew long ago and your closest friend can both be “Facebook friends” in the same way.

Professor Robin Dunbar says people can only have about 150 people in their social group. He believes Facebook has not changed that. But he worries that if it is so easy to end friendships on Facebook, people may stop learning how to get along.

2 We care less about privacy  
There is a saying: if you are not paying for it, you are the product. Facebook is like that. It makes money from user information, and many users give that information willingly.

Pew Research Center found that many young people share personal details. 91% post a photo of themselves. 71% post the city or town where they live, up from 61%. More than half give email addresses. One fifth give their phone number.

Because so much life is shared online, Facebook lets people create an image of themselves and build fans. Researchers wrote about a new idea called the “Facebook self.”

More than 80% list their interests, which helps brands target them. Many younger users do limit who can see their profiles, with 60% allowing friends only.

3 Facebook created millions of jobs, but not in its own offices  
Facebook helped create a new kind of work. Many people now have jobs helping brands use Facebook.

“It is a tool like no other,” said Michael Tinmouth, a social media strategist who has worked with brands like Vodafone and Microsoft. He said marketers can learn a lot about customers through data and analytics, such as who the customers are, who their friends are, and how they interact with a brand.

Advertisers pay a lot for this. Facebook said its ad revenue rose 46% to $3.32bn.

But Facebook can also be risky for brands. Complaints used to happen on the phone or in small online forums. Now angry customers can post complaints where hundreds of friends can see, or on the brand’s own page. A story about unfair treatment can spread very fast.

4 Facebook helped organize revolutions  
The Arab Spring was sometimes called the Twitter revolution, but Facebook also changed how protests are organized. Researcher Olga Onuch from Manchester University found that Facebook was key for reaching half of the Euromaidan protesters in Ukraine.

Onuch found that Facebook posts helped start the Maidan protests after it was announced that Ukraine would not sign a free trade and association agreement with the EU. The posts helped organize real actions, not just online anger. Activist Mustafa Nayyem posted: “If you really want to do something, don’t just ‘like’ this post. Let’s meet near the monument to independence in the middle of the Maidan.”

Many people in the research said they depended on Facebook to learn what was really happening, because they did not trust traditional media.

5 Facebook makes news, breaks news, and decides what news people see  
Pew Research Center says about 71% of people aged 18 to 24 say the internet is their main news source. It also says 63% of users overall say the same. About one third of Facebook users post about politics and government.

Many people first see news through Facebook or other social media, often on mobile phones.

People may not need to leave Facebook to read news. Instant Articles lets stories appear inside Facebook. News companies can sell ads around their articles and keep 100% of that money. Facebook can also sell ads around the article, and 70% of Facebook’s ad money from that goes to the news companies.

Facebook has also changed how journalists work. Many reporters use it to find, contact, and learn about ordinary people who may suddenly become part of a big news story. Facebook also has its own newswire, sharing useful reactions from users, including pictures and videos.

6 Users are changing Facebook  
Facebook started as a site for students, and at first only elite US universities could use it.

In 2014, 10 years after it started, 56% of internet users aged 65 and older had a Facebook account. And 39% were connected to people they had never met in person.

Groups have been replaced by pages. Writing on each other’s walls is now old-fashioned. Carefully made photo albums have been replaced by quick mobile uploads. More than ever, Facebook is a gateway not only to friends but also to the rest of the internet.

David Kirkpatrick, author of The Facebook Effect, said people should get used to it. He told BBC Radio 4’s Today programme that Facebook might go away one day, but something this big takes a long time to disappear. He said Facebook has shown it can change, and it will continue to be a very major player.

text:
The UN says that if the world wasted 25% less food, there would be enough food to feed all people who are malnourished.

Each year, 1.3bn tonnes of food is wasted. That is about one third of all food produced. This includes about 45% of fruit and vegetables, 35% of fish and seafood, 30% of cereals, 20% of dairy products, and 20% of meat. At the same time, 795 million people suffer from severe hunger and malnutrition.

Many people are trying to reduce food waste. For example, France has laws that require supermarkets to give unsold food to charities. The UN says food waste is a major challenge for food security. By 2050, food production may need to increase by 60% compared with 2005 to feed a growing population. If we waste less food, we can use fewer resources.

Food waste happens in different ways in different places. In developing countries, there is a lot of “food loss.” This is unplanned waste, often caused by poor equipment, transport, and roads or storage systems. In wealthy countries, “food waste” is high. People may throw food away because they buy too much. Stores may reject food because it does not look perfect.

In developed countries, consumers and retailers throw away 30% to 40% of the food they buy. In poorer countries, only 5% to 16% is thrown away. A 2011 report says that in Europe and North America, each person wasted 95 to 115kg of edible food each year. In sub-Saharan Africa and south and south-east Asia, each person wasted only 6 to 11kg.

Robert van Otterdijk from the UN Food and Agriculture Organization’s Save Food programme says food waste happens mostly where people can afford to throw food away. He says consumers in industrialized countries waste 222m tonnes a year. This is almost the same as the total net food production of sub-Saharan Africa, which is 230m tonnes. He also says food losses are very common in developing countries because conditions are not well developed, from managing production to transport and distribution.

Food loss and waste also harm the environment. The carbon footprint of food that is produced but not eaten is about 3.3 gigatonnes of CO2. If food waste were a country, it would be the third highest producer of greenhouse gases after the US and China. About 1.4bn hectares, close to 30% of available farm land, is used to grow food that is later wasted. More surface and groundwater (“blue water”) is used to produce wasted food than any single country uses for agriculture, including India and China.

Van Otterdijk says climate change is linked to an economy that produces and consumes more than the Earth can provide. He says food production is one of the biggest sectors in the world, and wasting one third of it has a huge impact on land, water, energy, and greenhouse gas emissions.

The highest food waste is in the US, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand, where consumers waste 39% of the food they buy. Europe is next, where about 31% is thrown away. In the UK, 15m tonnes of food is lost or wasted each year. British consumers throw away 4.2m tonnes of edible food each year. This means 11.7% of all food bought is wasted even though it could be avoided. It costs each family about £700 a year, or almost £60 a month.

In the UK, the foods most often thrown away are bread, vegetables, fruit, and milk. By weight, bread is the most wasted food: 414,000 tonnes, or 22.4% of all bread bought. By percentage, lettuce and leafy salads are wasted the most: 38%, or 64,000 tonnes.

For meat, poultry is wasted the most by percentage and weight. About 13% of edible parts (not including bones) is wasted. For fruit, melon is wasted the most by percentage, with more than a quarter of each melon thrown away.

The UK has improved in the last ten years after a strong campaign to reduce waste. Van Otterdijk calls it “one of the great successes.” From 2007 to 2012, avoidable food waste in UK homes fell by 21%, from 5.3m tonnes to 4.2m tonnes, mostly because people became more aware. Less wasted food is going into landfill. In 2000–01, only 14% of household waste was recycled or composted. By 2011–12, composted food had risen to 43%.

Van Otterdijk says there is strong and growing interest in food waste, which helps local campaigns around the world. He says much more must be done, and both public and private groups must take part. He says that if the same effort continues, the world may see important results after ten years.

text:
Clay Cockrell sits in his office at Columbus Circle. Across the street is 1 Central Park West. It has Trump International Hotel and Tower. Central Park is in front of it. Cockrell does his popular “walk and talk” therapy in Central Park.

Cockrell used to work on Wall Street. Now he is a therapist. He spends a lot of time walking in Central Park. He also walks in Battery Park near Wall Street. He listens to and helps some of New York’s richest people.

He said he became an expert in wealth therapy naturally. He said the extremely wealthy, the “1% of the 1%,” often feel their problems are not real problems. But he says they are real. He says many therapists do not take their issues seriously enough.

What problems do America’s 1% have? Cockrell said some feel guilty about being rich. Some feel they must hide that they are rich. He also said being in the 1% can be lonely. The writer F Scott Fitzgerald said the very rich “are different from you and me.”

Some counsellors say things got worse after the financial crisis. They say the debate about income inequality also made things worse. Movements like Occupy Wall Street and the Fight for $15 fair wage campaign helped start this debate.

Jamie Traeger-Muney is a wealth psychologist. She founded the Wealth Legacy Group. She said Occupy Wall Street had important things to say about income inequality. But she said it pointed at the 1% and made them seem bad as a group. She also said the media partly makes rich people feel they must hide or feel ashamed.

Traeger-Muney works with inheritors, people who get money from family. She said they often get a bad reputation in the press. She said there are many negative names for inheritors, like “spoilt brat,” “born with a silver spoon in their mouth,” and “trust-fund babies.” She said it is easy to blame the rich.

She said she is sometimes shocked by what people say. She said people talk about rich people in ways they would not use for other groups.

Barbara Nusbaum is an expert in money psychology. She said it can feel very lonely to have a lot of money. She said people’s reactions can be scary. She said people are taught not to talk about money because it is not polite. She said it is harder to talk about having money than not having money. It is more accepted to say, “I am broke. Things are hard.” But you cannot easily say, “I have a ton of money.” She said rich people have to keep much of their life private.

Because of this, Cockrell said rich people often spend time with other rich Americans. He said it is not because they think they are better. It is because they want to be with people who understand their problems.

The gap between rich and poor is happening around the world. Oxfam said the richest 1% increased their share of global wealth from 44% in 2009 to 48% in 2014. Oxfam said they were on track to own more than the other 99% by 2016.

In the US, over the last three decades, the top 0.1% of households increased their share of wealth from 7% to 22%. At the same time, the wealth of the bottom 90% of households went down.

The number of extremely wealthy people is also rising. Spectrem Group said that in 2014, US households with $1m or more in assets increased by 500,000 to 10.1m. This does not count the value of their main home. In

text:
Is streaming music becoming popular with everyone?
The International Federation of the Phonographic Industry (IFPI) said that in 2014 there were only 41m people in the world paying for music streaming. Streaming is growing fast, but it is still used by a small group. Also, many people get streaming because it comes in a phone deal, so it is not clear how many are really active users.
Apple often takes something that already exists, changes it, and makes many people want it. This happened with downloads (iTunes), digital music players (the iPod), and smartphones (the iPhone). Leaked information said Apple wants 100m subscribers. If each person pays $120 per year, that would be $12bn each year. In 2014, the total value of recorded music worldwide was just under $15bn. Apple is good at making products popular, but reaching that goal would still be very hard.

Is downloading ending?
The iTunes Store started in 2003 (2004 in Europe). At that time, MP3 piracy seemed impossible to stop. Apple convinced people to pay for downloads and built a very big business, with an estimated 70% market share. Downloads were still the biggest part of digital music money in 2014. IFPI said downloads were 52% of total digital income in 2014. Apple has most of this market, and it is the biggest music retailer in the world.
But download money is falling. In the UK, download revenue peaked in 2013 at £283m and fell to £249m in 2014. The same decline reached the US in 2013. So Apple bought Beats in 2014 for $3bn. It wanted premium headphones, and it also wanted to move from music ownership (downloads) to music access (subscription streaming).
Apple and the record industry cannot end downloads yet. So streaming and downloading will both stay under the Apple brand. Many people like music, but do not want to pay $120 a year. They prefer to buy a download sometimes. Research said the average music buyer in the UK spent £39.52 in 2014. Asking most people to triple what they spend each year would be very difficult, even for Apple.

Has Apple Connect made Apple the most artist-friendly service?
Apple tried an artist-friendly platform on iTunes before. It was called Ping, and it failed. Apple Connect is different. It is like a mix of YouTube, Facebook, and SoundCloud. Artists can post music, videos, photos, and more on their profile pages.
Because of its size, and because it treats music as “art” and not just “content,” Apple has often had good relationships with the music industry and with artists. Many artists see Apple in a positive way. Spotify has been criticized by artists, from Radiohead’s Thom Yorke to Taylor Swift. Apple seems friendlier in comparison. Apple wants to be on the right side as the industry changes.

Where are the artist exclusives?
This will matter when the service starts. Exclusive deals for big albums may become very important for streaming. Spotify paid a lot of money to get Led Zeppelin and Metallica exclusively. Apple has watched this.
Apple already has AC/DC and the Beatles’ catalogues exclusively for download on iTunes. It is not clear if it can convince them to join streaming too. Apple also got the surprise Beyonce album in 2013 before anyone else, and it will want more deals like that. People also speculate that Apple wants exclusive rights to the new Adele album.
Giving iTunes a download exclusive was easier because Apple controls so much of the download market. Doing this in streaming is harder. Also, streaming now counts toward the album chart in places like the UK and US. Many artists care about chart success and will not want to limit their audience by being on only one service.

Is this going to kill Spotify?
Some people say Apple Music will destroy Spotify and other rivals. But it is not that simple. Apple is joining a market where other companies have worked for years and learned a lot. Apple has to catch up.
The winner may not be the company with the best service. It may be the company with the most money. Apple’s rivals started earlier, but they are losing a lot of money. Spotify, for example, lost Û93.1m in 2013. Apple started 2015 as the most profitable company in corporate history, with $178bn in the bank. If Apple Music loses too much money, Apple will not keep it for long. But Apple will keep investing for a while and will try to beat the competition.

text:
If we ignore big disasters like volcanoes, hurricanes, and earthquakes, two natural things can slowly damage a city or even make it disappear: water and sand.

If the Earth were getting colder, ice would also be a danger. A huge glacier can crush a city as it moves down a valley. In 2013, a rare “ice tsunami” hit the Canadian town of Ochre Beach. Wind blew a wall of melting ice from Dauphin Lake onto the shore, and it broke every house in its way.

But Ochre Beach was unusual. In most places, melting ice makes cities less safe because it raises sea levels. A century ago, Venice used to flood about ten times a year. Now its lowest point, Piazza San Marco (only three feet above sea level), is flooded about 100 times each year.

Sea levels are not the only problem. In many places, the land is also sinking. In Venice, the ground under the city was squeezed down because of industry in the area. This lowered the city by 20 cm between 1950 and 1970. Ho Chi Minh City in Vietnam is sinking by about 2 cm a year. Jakarta is sinking much faster, by 10 to 20 cm each year. In the last 30 years, Jakarta has sunk about four metres, so its 40-year-old sea wall works less well.

Jakarta has pumped out a lot of groundwater to support its people. The land above dries out and packs down, making the city like a bowl. Rivers that once flowed down through the city to the sea have had to be moved, because they cannot drain uphill.

There are many plans to save Venice. Ho Chi Minh City and Jakarta are taking the problem seriously too. But in Miami, politicians will not admit the city has a serious environmental problem.

Miami has three big problems. It is less than ten feet above sea level. More tropical storms are flooding it. And it is built on porous limestone, which soaks up seawater. The water fills the foundations and comes up through drains and pipes. This pushes sewage up and pollutes fresh water. Miami may one day become unfit for people to live in.

In the Maldives, people on whole islands are thinking about leaving their homes. The capital, Male, has a population of 153,379 and is only four feet above sea level. With Japanese investment, it built a ten-foot sea wall that cost $63 million. But in the long term, only stopping sea levels from rising will save Male and the other islands.

The sea is not the only threat. Heavy rain is also putting cities in danger. In Detroit, one morning recently brought as much rain as an entire summer. The old sewer system could not handle it. Detroit became poorer after America’s car industry collapsed, and it is struggling to build a better system. Drinking water is also affected because Detroit is very vulnerable to germs carried by water.

In Africa, desertification is making the Sahara Desert spread south by 30 miles each year. This threatens settlements in northern Mauritania. In the last 20 years, the desert grew by more than 260 acres around Chinguetti, a trading and religious centre. Its population fell from 20,000 in the mid-1900s to only a few thousand now. Trading has almost stopped because sand is piling up in the streets.

In the United States, the resort city of Rancho Mirage near Palm Springs may have to be left within the next ten years. This problem is caused less by global warming and more by people growing too much in a place with limited resources. In 1870, California had about half a million people, so farming the desert seemed possible. Now California has 38 million people. Together they own 32 million vehicles, and they expect lawns and golf courses.

In Rancho Mirage, people use more than 200 gallons of water per person each day. This has helped create a human-made drought. The city has cut water use by 25%, but this may not be enough. The resort is surrounded by sand and dust. In the long run, some desert cities in California may be abandoned, while people move into other cities.

When people leave a city, it is a final sign that the city is falling apart. If we stop caring for a city, nature quickly takes over. Japan’s Hashima Island became a concrete city in 1887 to mine coal under the sea. In 1974 the mine closed as the coal ran out, and the island was abandoned. Now sea spray is making the concrete walls fall down.

Fire is also a growing danger to cities in America. After severe storms, forest fires cause the most damage. Between 1953 and 2014, 800 major fire disasters were declared there. A report by the USDA Forest Service shows more urban places that could be hit by wildfire.

In Australia, long droughts are making trees easy to burn. Some resorts in Victoria and several suburbs of Melbourne are on a list of the state’s 52 most vulnerable bushfire places. A single forest fire is unlikely to destroy a whole city, but many fires over time could make a city unfit to live in. Abandonment is one of the strongest causes of city collapse.

Many cities are losing their fight against nature. Can we name the most vulnerable big city in the world? Natural events are hard to predict, but Male looks especially at risk. Even if its sea wall keeps working, the islands around the Maldives capital are likely to disappear before long. If they disappear, Male loses its reason for being there.

text:
How long can you hold your breath? I am trying it right now. The first 30 seconds are easy. I want to stop at 45 seconds, but I keep going, and it feels easier for a short time. But when it passes one minute, I know I cannot last much longer. My heart is beating fast. I breathe out a tiny bit, and that helps. In the end, I stop. I breathe out the old air in my lungs and take a big breath in. (Then I keep taking big breaths, and my husband asks what I am doing.) I reach one minute and 12 seconds. I feel proud of myself.

Being able to hold your breath is very important in some sports, especially freediving. In 2006, I was filming a BBC series called Don’t Die Young. It was about how the lungs work. I met Sam Amps, the captain of the UK freedive team. At a pool in Bristol, she taught me simple exercises to help me hold my breath longer while swimming underwater. By the end of the session, I think I held my breath for 90 seconds. That was enough to swim one width of the pool. Sam swam three widths easily. She could hold her breath for five minutes while swimming. Five!

I asked how she did it. She said she breathed very slowly for several minutes before each dive. Then she took one big, deep breath before going under the water. She also said training helped her fight the strong need to breathe for longer than most people.

Some people have said that being able to hold your breath means humans once had a watery part in our evolution. People have even said humans can lower the heart rate and body activity to hold the breath longer. Other things—like having little body hair, where we store body fat, and walking on two legs—have also been linked to this idea. But this “aquatic ape hypothesis” does not match the scientific evidence. It may sound nice, but it is not supported.

When scientists look at breath-holding, they find that humans are not the only land mammals that can do it. (It is hard to test this in other mammals, because they do not follow instructions.) Tests also show that the heart rate does not drop when you hold your breath on land. But in cold water, it is different. Cooling the face can slow the heart rate in most people. Still, this does not prove humans had an aquatic past, because many air-breathing animals show this effect. This slower heart rate is part of what is sometimes called the “mammalian diving reflex.” These body changes can help with diving, but they can also help an animal not drown.

Even if our breath-holding is not very special compared with other animals, it is now helping in one part of medicine. Radiotherapy for breast cancer uses radiation aimed very carefully at the tumour. This can take several minutes, so it is usually done in short bursts between breaths. But if a patient can keep her chest completely still for several minutes, doctors can give the full dose in the right place all at once. The problem is that most people, like me, cannot hold their breath for much more than a minute. Doctors at University Hospital Birmingham did careful experiments. They found that if patients breathe oxygen-rich air before the breath-hold, they can hold their breath for five-and-a-half minutes.

Surprisingly, this seems to work not by tricking the body’s normal sensors for low oxygen or high carbon dioxide in the blood, but by tricking the diaphragm. The diaphragm is a main breathing muscle. When you breathe in, you tighten this muscle and pull it flat, so your chest gets bigger and air goes into your lungs. When you hold your breath, you keep the diaphragm tight like this. If oxygen is raised and carbon dioxide is lowered before the breath-hold, as in the Birmingham experiments, the diaphragm may get tired more slowly. And—though it is not helpful if you need your chest to stay perfectly still—breathing out a little lets the diaphragm relax a little and can help you hold your breath longer, just as I found. In the end, the diaphragm’s signals become too strong, and you have to stop and breathe.

text:
Donald Trump walked on stage and said something unusually humble for a politician who seems to break many political rules.

At a campaign rally in South Carolina, he warned the crowd: “Unless we win, it doesn’t mean a damn thing.” This was even though he had finished his fourth month in a row at the top of the opinion polls. He told the crowd of 11,000 people that he wished the election were sooner: “I want to pick my date for the election. I want it next Tuesday.” The crowd showed the kind of strong support he needs to keep going until March 2016 to win the Republican nomination, and then until November’s general election.

These moments of doubt do not last long. They are quickly replaced by the loud confidence of a billionaire who presents himself as a “winner.”

Trump is not the only person wondering if his unlikely campaign can last. This is especially true because recent controversies have not hurt him in the polls. They have seemed to make his lead stronger. Many experts believe that any one of these events would have ended most politicians’ campaigns.

First, he angered prisoners of war by doubting the heroism of Vietnam veteran John McCain because McCain was captured. Then, at the first television debate, he insulted Fox News moderator Megyn Kelly because she asked him difficult questions.

He also insulted Mexican immigrants to the US. He said a Black Lives Matter protester who was violently thrown out of a rally deserved to be “roughed up.” He appeared to mock a New York Times journalist for his disability. He also falsely accused Muslim Americans of cheering for the 9/11 attackers.

Trump said the political media exaggerated many of these incidents. He claimed 70% of them are “scum.” But he refused to take back his comments. In South Carolina, he said: “I could have said, ‘Oh, I misspoke’, but I am not big on that.”

Some rivals still hope that Trump’s supporters will eventually get tired of what critics call his constant attacks, especially on minorities. One poll shows his support among Republicans down by 12 points. But at 31%, he still leads.

Other opponents say Trump does not seem to follow one clear political belief system. Former presidential rival Bobby Jindal told the Guardian, shortly before dropping out, “He is an egomaniac; he’s a narcissist. He’s not a conservative, he’s not a liberal — he believes in himself.”

Liz Mair, a Republican strategist who is organizing an anti-Trump fundraising committee, worries that comments the liberal media call mistakes may be planned to raise his support with his main followers. She said people who do not like Trump and his policies should work to actively weaken him, and “not just pray for him to commit real political suicide.”

Still, Trump is not only about shocking statements. He often says that three issues he attacks most—immigration reform, free-trade deals, and Barack Obama’s national security policy—have become some of the most important issues in the election.

His plans to deport every undocumented immigrant in the US and demand that Mexico pay for a border wall—“A real wall. A very tall wall, taller than that ceiling.”—may sound unrealistic. But they have arguably harmed the campaign hopes of Jeb Bush, who supports immigration reform.

So what could stop Trump? Opponents point to evidence that polls far before election day are often about eight percentage points off, because many people have not decided yet. Among Americans who say they are Republicans, current polls suggest Trump has about 25–30% of the vote.

It will be hard to change the minds of Trump’s passionate supporters. To reduce his lead, another candidate would need to attract Trump’s supporters without losing their own supporters. Polling experts say this is unlikely. It is hard to imagine anyone doing Trump better than Trump, or copying him without losing support.

This can be seen in answers to one question: “Are there any of these candidates you would definitely not support for the Republican nomination for president?” While about 20–30% of voters say they would support Trump, another 20–30% say they definitely would not.

Steve Deace, an Iowa conservative, said Trump’s behavior is “both a good and a bad thing.” He said it creates a loyal group that likes Trump’s style and will not leave. But it also makes it hard for Trump to win support beyond that group.

Republican pollster Frank Luntz believes Trump speaks for voters who feel, for the first time, that someone is speaking for them and listening to them. Luntz said: “Trump says what they’re thinking and, the more outrageous he is, the more they agree with him. He’s saying what no politician would say and that’s another reason they like him.”

Many regular supporters at his larger and larger events say the same.

Sandra Murray of Dubuque, Iowa, said, “I like the way he speaks. This country is a huge mess and we need to get out of this and, honestly, he could be the man to do it.”

Another supporter explained it more simply: “He’s not afraid of anybody or anything. That’s pretty cool.”

text:
Sometimes life is not fair. Mark Zuckerberg made Facebook and is now worth about $48bn. James Goodfellow invented the cash machine (ATM), which millions of people use every day. But he did not become rich. He got only £10 from the patent and has earned no more money from it.

Goodfellow says it feels strange when he sees bankers getting £1m bonuses. He wonders what they did to deserve that much money.

He is also upset because people do not see him as a good example for inventors and engineers. His invention helped make billions of pounds, but he got almost nothing. He asks why someone would want to copy his path if they only get £10.

For many years, people argued about who should be called “the inventor of the ATM.” In 2005, a man named John Shepherd-Barron got a UK honour for services to banking as the “inventor of the automatic cash dispenser.” But now the UK government says Goodfellow invented the ATM. So it seems his place in history is now secure.

In the mid-1960s, Goodfellow was a development engineer. He was asked to find a way for customers to take out cash on Saturdays. Many people could not go to the bank during the week. He worked on a machine that could give cash to a customer who was recognized. To make it work, he invented the PIN (personal identification number) and a matching coded token.

The token was a plastic card with holes in it. The patent described a system with a card reader and ten buttons on the outside wall of a bank. A customer would put in the card and press the buttons for their personal number to get banknotes.

Except for the hole-punched cards, this is similar to ATMs today. After Goodfellow showed a model, prototypes were made, and the first machines were installed in 1967.

Around the same time, Shepherd-Barron was making another cash machine. His machine did not use plastic cards. It used cheques with carbon-14, a mildly radioactive material. The machine detected the carbon-14, checked the cheque with a PIN, and then gave out cash.

Many people accept that Shepherd-Barron’s ATM was the first one installed and used by the public. The first one, in north London, opened on 27 June 1967. This was one month before Goodfellow’s ATM was shown to the public. But Goodfellow’s patent was filed on 2 May 1966, which was 14 months before the London machine started.

The rivalry became stronger when Shepherd-Barron got the official honour. Goodfellow says he did not complain until Shepherd-Barron received the OBE in 2005. He says that honour was given because Shepherd-Barron was seen as the inventor, and that upset him.

Shepherd-Barron is no longer alive to reply now, but in a 2005 interview he spoke sharply about Goodfellow. He said banks bought their system because they thought through the whole idea. He called Goodfellow’s invention “an elegant failure,” like the hovercraft.

Cash machines have become very popular around the world. New things like contactless payments, bitcoin, and wearable technology have not stopped their growth. There are now 3m ATMs worldwide, and the number was forecast to reach 4m by 2020.

Goodfellow says he did not invent the idea of a cash machine, but he did invent a way to do it. He compares this to the Wright brothers. They did not invent the idea of flying, but they made it work and got credit. He thinks he should get credit for the cash dispenser.

Now he is starting to get that credit. The website ATMInventor.com says: the idea of an ATM was from Luther George Simjian. The ATM as we know it was James Goodfellow in Scotland, because he had a patent date of 1966. The ATM design people recognize today was John D White for Docutel in the US.

Goodfellow also gets official recognition in a guidebook called Life in the United Kingdom. In a section on “great British inventions of the twentieth century,” it says: “In the 1960s, James Goodfellow (1937-) invented the cash-dispensing automatic teller machine (ATM) or ‘cashpoint’.”

So after many years, Goodfellow is now mentioned with other famous inventors, like John Logie Baird (television), Alan Turing (the Turing machine), Sir Frank Whittle (the jet engine), and Sir Tim Berners-Lee (the World Wide Web).

When asked what he did with the £10 in the 1960s, Goodfellow says he thinks he spent it on a wild night out. He says it did not change his life. But he says he had a good working life and he was happy doing his job.

story_id: WNL Why are people so wrong
story_key: wnl why are people so wrong
text:
A new international survey in 33 countries shows that many people are wrong about important facts in society.

In Britain, people think the richest 1% own 59% of the country’s wealth, but they actually own 23%. In the United States, people think 33% of the population are immigrants, but the real number is 14%.

In Brazil, people think the average age is 56, but it is 31. In Russia, people think 31% of politicians are women, but it is 14%.

In Britain, people think 43% of young adults aged 25–34 still live with their parents, but the real number is 14%. In India, people who are online think 60% of the whole country has internet access, but only 19% do.

Why are people so wrong? One reason is that many people find basic maths hard. Some people also misunderstand the questions. For example, people in many countries guess that 37% of people have no religion, but the real average is 18%. This may be because people think about who practises religion, not what people write on census forms.

Another reason is that people use quick guesses. They may think about what is easy to picture, even if it does not fit the question. For example, people often overestimate how many people live in rural areas because rural land takes up a lot of space.

People also think from their own experience. In the survey, many answers came from online users. In developing countries, online users are often richer and more connected than the whole population. Even though people with more education are often more accurate, many still guess based on their own lives and forget that they are not typical.

People can also guess with feelings, not just facts. Sometimes their answers show what they worry about. Worry can make people guess wrong, and wrong guesses can also create worry.

In Britain, this may help explain why people overestimate how much the richest own, how many young people live with parents, and how many immigrants live in the country (people guess 25%, but official estimates say 13%). These are issues people worry about, so they make the numbers seem bigger.

But the survey also shows some problems where people may not worry enough. In many countries, people underestimate how many people are overweight or obese. In Saudi Arabia, people think 28% are, but 71% are. In Britain, people think it is 44%, but the real number is 62%.

The study also shows that countries are very different from each other. The richest 1% in Russia own 70% of the nation’s wealth, but in New Zealand they own 18%. Half of Italians aged 25–34 live with their parents, but in Norway it is only 4%. The average age in India is 27, but in Japan it is 47. Only 10% of politicians are women in Brazil, Hungary, and Japan, but in Sweden it is 44%.

Because the real world is so different and sometimes surprising, it is not strange that many people get these facts wrong.

text:
James Hamblin is a senior editor at The Atlantic magazine. He tried a challenge: he stopped showering for a while. He did this for his series, “If Our Bodies Could Talk.” He wanted to see what happens when we clean our bodies too much. He took fewer showers. When he did shower, he did not use shampoo or soap.

He found something many others have found. When we use lots of soap and body wash, our skin tries harder to get back to normal. Showering can wash away the skin’s natural oil and bacteria. Hot water and cleaning products can make this happen even more.

After a shower, you might feel tight and dry. That is because your skin’s natural moisture was washed away. Our skin also has millions of helpful bacteria, like our gut does. Frequent rubbing and scrubbing can destroy these bacteria. When the bacteria grow back, the new mix may include more microbes that make smell. So showering too often may make you smell more. If you stop showering and stop using soap, your skin may go through an early adjustment period that is likely gross. After that, the skin often returns to balance, makes less oil, and healthy bacteria can grow.

After his challenge, Hamblin felt the human body can be quite lovely on its own. It is not only about smell or looks. Dermatologists suggest that using less soap can help acne and eczema. Taking fewer showers and using fewer products can also help the environment. The average shower lasts seven minutes and uses 65 litres of water. That is 65 litres of clean, drinkable water mixed with soap and sent down the drain each day, sometimes more than once.

Clean water is becoming harder to ignore as California has another summer of drought. It is becoming clear that clean water is one of the world’s most valuable commodities, and it may soon be in short supply. There is also waste from body wash bottles. These are strong reasons to let your body be more natural.

If this idea makes you feel nervous because you have been close to people who do not shower much and still smell, do not worry. Many people who reduce washing still use deodorant if they need it. People’s natural scent is different, and it can change with diet, hydration, and exercise. Also, washing your hands with soap is still recommended to help stop infectious diseases from spreading.

You do not have to stop all at once like Hamblin did. But there is a middle place between cleaning many times a day and never showering. You can reduce. Skip a few showers, use less soap, and let the helpful bacteria grow a little.

story_id: WNL Why you should start work at 10
story_key: wnl why you should start work at 10
text:
Many people do not get enough sleep. One simple idea could help: start school later.

At the British Science Festival, Dr Paul Kelley from Oxford University said schools should have different start times. He said this should match students’ natural body clocks. He said this could help students think better, get better exam results, and be healthier. Not getting enough sleep has been linked with diabetes, depression, obesity, and a weaker immune system.

In a 2014 paper, Kelley wrote that children’s natural wake-up time changes with age. Around age 10, it is about 6:30am. At 16, it is about 8am. At 18, it is about 9am. So a normal school start time may work for 10-year-olds, but not for 16- to 18-year-olds. For older teenagers, he said it may be better to start school at 11am or even later. He also said that a 7am alarm for older teenagers is like a 4:30am start for a teacher in their 50s.

Kelley said it is not as simple as telling teenagers to go to bed earlier. He said the body clock is controlled by light. The eyes have special cells that send messages to a part of the brain called the hypothalamus. This part controls our 24-hour body rhythm. Kelley said you cannot just control it by choice, like you cannot choose to control your heartbeat.

Kelley said workers could also benefit from later start times. He said the average sleep loss each night is more than two hours for ages 14 to 24. For people aged 24 to about 30 or 35, it is about an hour and a half. This can continue until about age 55, when sleep becomes balanced again. He said a 10-year-old and a 55-year-old naturally sleep and wake at the same times.

He said this may be why the traditional 9 to 5 work day is so common. Many bosses are in their mid-50s or older, and it works best for them. Kelley asked if workplaces should also have different start times. For example, people in their 50s could start at 8am, people in their 30s at 10am, and teenage interns or apprentices at 11am. He said matching work times to body clocks could improve performance, mood, and health. He said it could make people less ill, happier, and better at their jobs.

Kelley also said there could be fewer accidents because drivers would be more alert. Rush hour might become smaller if people started at different times. He said later starts could help everyone, especially families. Parents often try to wake teenagers who are waking up three hours too early, and this causes tension.

Kelley said he is 67, so he wakes naturally just after six. He said starting work is easier for him now than when he was younger.

text:
How close are we to a world where drones bring us packages? Amazon says it could happen soon. Other people are not sure. They say the next steps are not only about inventing new things. They are also about public safety.

Paul Misener, Amazon’s vice-president of global public policy, spoke at a congressional hearing. He said Amazon would be ready when all the rules were in place. He did not say exactly what the system would look like. He joked with a congressman that a basket of fresh fruit was on the way to his home to show the technology could work.

The Federal Aviation Authority (FAA) plans to have rules for commercial use of unmanned aircraft by June 2016. But the technology still has far to go before then. Bigger drones are not allowed in the air yet. The rules will cover only drones up to 25kg. The FAA also says that if many radio-controlled flying robots share the sky, they must use different radio frequencies that no one can jam or take over.

Professor Sajiv Singh from the Carnegie Mellon University Robotics Institute runs a “critical cargo delivery” company called NearEarth. He said flying a top drone can be like giving it simple steps: go to this height, do a short task, then go back home. But even short flights from a moving landing pad cause serious planning problems.

Singh said companies are not planning to fly between two empty places. They want to fly from a warehouse to a customer, often in a city or suburb. On the way, the drone will face dangers it must notice. The map may not show new terrain unless the route was mapped before. Even then, there may be new construction equipment. GPS signals may be blocked or partly blocked, so the drone may think it is in the wrong place. Singh said these problems can be solved, but it is hard.

A big problem is keeping radio contact with a drone, and deciding what happens if the contact stops. Roboticist Daniel Huber said that an off-the-shelf UAV (unmanned aerial vehicle) may just keep going and crash. Engineers can fix this, but the fix is not yet a standard in the industry.

Jay McConville, director of business development for unmanned systems at Lockheed Martin MST, said, “you can’t do everything with a 25kg aircraft.” At Lockheed Martin, much work is on making drone controls simpler, and less like complex flight simulators. He said operators want the final result, not every detail about the aircraft. They want to see vehicle status, and they want video on a handheld device or a laptop.

Huber said most of the needed technology already exists. He is working on a program to use drones to inspect things like pipelines, telephone lines, and bridges. He said they made a system where you draw a box around an area, and the drone flies around it by itself, looks at every surface, and reports back.

Huber, a senior scientist at Carnegie Mellon’s Robotics Institute who works on 3D systems imagery, talked about a program like Amazon’s. He said Amazon has said many packages are light. A drone can carry 1 kilogram for 15 minutes. If a drone can reach a neighborhood, it can deliver from a base there. You need about a 15-minute distance, and many off-the-shelf drones can do that. He said this helps keep people nearby safer. He said, “The larger you get, the more dangerous you get.”

Huber said some planning problems are being solved in dramatic ways. He said that at a recent conference, a disaster relief drone firm called SkyCatch showed a robot that could take off by itself and land on its own charging station. It would land and take off, and when its batteries got low, it would land, swap batteries, and take off again.

Safety is still a main worry. Singh said that for a commercial aircraft to be skyworthy, it must show only one serious failure in every 1m hours. He said drones are “one or two orders of magnitude away” from that level. He said the Reaper drone has one failure in 10,000 hours. An oil leak does not count as a catastrophic failure; something has to fall out of the sky. Singh said, “We’re closing the gap,” and that there is a lot of interest.

Singh said flying is naturally dangerous, so the safety standards are very high. He said that on commercial airlines, a single failed part can make the plane return to the gate. He said this airline industry has 60 years of experience, and he worries about a drone falling onto a crowded highway. Singh said part of the answer is simple backup planning: if something fails, the vehicle must do something sensible.

text:
Maria sits on a black plastic chair in a community centre on a cold Tuesday afternoon. She is waiting for her number to be called. She is number 34.

When it is her turn, Maria goes forward. She gets a brown paper bag of basic food. It has pasta, eggs, and corn flakes. She can choose butternut squash or carrots for vegetables this week.

Maria is the 34th client today at the East Hampton Food Pantry. This food pantry is only a few streets away from some of the most expensive homes in the world.

By the end of the day, the organizers expect more than 400 families to come. They will pick up their weekly food package to help them during the cold, dark winter on Long Island.

In summer, the Hamptons is a place where billionaires play. The Hamptons is a group of old towns and villages on the ocean, 100 miles from Manhattan. But after Labor Day in early September, rich people close their mansions and go back to places like Manhattan or Beverly Hills. Then life is hard for the mostly immigrant community that lives there all year.

“The people who come here are rich and famous, but we who live here are not,” Maria says. In the summer she works 14-hour days cleaning mansions. In the winter she can go for months with no work.

Maria laughs when asked if she has enough money. “There is no work in the winter, only in the summertime,” she says. Like many workers in the Hamptons, she is from Latin America. “Here, lots of people live in a single room because they can’t pay the rent.”

She says some families with up to five children live in basements. They still pay more than $1,000 a month in rent. “People come here looking for work but, in the winter, there is nothing.”

She says many friends cannot pay for heating or medicine. Many would be hungry without the East Hampton Food Pantry. It is one of several food pantries in the town.

Vicki Littman is the chairperson of the East Hampton Food Pantry. The pantry gave more than 31,000 food parcels in 2015. She says more and more people need help.

“After Labor Day, when the season is over and people’s hours are cut, our numbers go up to about 400 families a week,” she says. “On Tuesday, they get two to three days of food. Without us, they would struggle much more.”

Littman says outsiders may not see the problems in a place famous for luxury. She says the summer visitors are shocked to hear about food pantries. They think of the glamorous Hamptons with parties, beaches, and mansions.

But she says many people work in service jobs, like landscapers, nannies, and waitresses. They depend on summer pay to get through the winter. Visitors do not see that when they come on holiday.

She says some older people must choose between paying for medicine, paying bills, or buying food. She says this should not happen.

Littman says the town has lost many workers in important jobs, like teachers, police officers, and even doctors and dentists. They cannot afford to live there. The food pantry board wants to do more so people can stay.

Housing is the biggest cost in the Hamptons. The nation’s most expensive property costs $147m. It is hedge fund manager Barry Rosenstein’s 18-acre beachfront estate at 67 Further Lane, near Maidstone Golf Club.

Larry Cantwell is East Hampton’s town supervisor and a lifelong resident. He says homes often sell for more than $25m. These high prices also raise the cost of smaller apartments.

“Finding your first home is a challenge in an area like this,” Cantwell says. It is not only poor people. Working-class and middle-class families also struggle. He says if you can buy a home in East Hampton for $500,000, you are very lucky.

Cantwell says more than half the town’s homes are empty most of the year. In winter the population can drop to 10,000. In August it can be 80,000.

“It’s like two cities,” Cantwell says. There is a lot of wealth, but most of it is in second homes used only in summer. Other people live there all year.

He says there are famous rich people, and also hard-working poor people who struggle. He says the community started as a farming and fishing place, a working-class community.

Cantwell says it is almost impossible now for a farmer or fisherman to save enough to buy a first house in East Hampton. He says it is not only the poor. Police officers, teachers, young professionals, and others also struggle to find a place to live. Many cannot afford to own a home.

Being homeless in the Hamptons often means riding a bus. Different religious groups work together so homeless people have a place to sleep in winter. Churches across Long Island take turns. About 50 homeless people are bused between churches every day.

Eddie Vallone is 22 years old. He is one of the people on the bus each night. He says people think the Hamptons is rich and has no problems, but there are many problems, especially drugs.

At Maureen’s Haven, the charity that organizes the shelter program, Vallone says it is hard when summer ends. He worries about what to do in winter. He wants to work, but there is no work.

Vallone cleans pools and does small jobs on luxury estates. He says if he saves carefully and does not buy things without thinking, his summer money can last until November. But work does not start again until May or early June.

text:
It is hard to think of many things worse than walking in the sunshine on Rio’s Copacabana beach on the way to watch a World Cup match. So England fan Anthony McDowell from Liverpool did not agree with the bad news people had talked about before the tournament in Brazil. “The place is lovely. The people are great. There’s a party atmosphere,” McDowell said. “The only thing that could be better is the England team.”

McDowell and about six friends were among many fans from around the world. They turned the beach into a party area with flags, colours, and songs. Some people danced, took photos, or drank. Most people just walked around and talked about football, waiting for the next game on the nearby FanFest big screen. The last time so many people were there during the day was when the pope was visiting.

The happy and mostly peaceful mood was very different from the protests, transport problems, and stadium troubles before the World Cup. Now that the football has started, visiting fans want to enjoy it. “If I had known, when I started planning, how complicated and costly it would be, I wouldn’t have come. But, now that we’re here, it’s great,” said Brian Hill, a retired chief executive from Sunderland.

The trip has had problems. Hill travelled for more than 20 hours to reach Rio. Then there were long delays on the road from the airport at 6.30am. His son, Andrew, had his sunglasses stolen almost as soon as he sat on the beach. They were also surprised that many bars did not have big screens for the games. But they, like many fans, said they loved the atmosphere, even if the planning and travel were difficult. They said the tournament has started in a spectacular way on the pitch.

Even people who usually complain may have been excited by Robin van Persie’s diving header for the Netherlands against Spain. There have also been many goals: 28 in the first eight games, almost three times as many as at the same stage in South Africa in 2010. Also, Latin-American teams won every game they played up until the time of writing.

So far, the tournament has avoided the worst “Doomsday” predictions, but it is not free of trouble. The stadiums were finished late, and some were not fully finished. But there were no reports of building problems or trouble getting into the stadiums since the kick

Like other World Cups, ticketing has been a problem. There were many empty seats at some games, including the Netherlands against Spain. FIFA spokesman Saint-Clair Milesi confirmed that only 48,000 of the 51,900 seats were filled. FIFA is also investigating security problems after Chilean fans set off fireworks during their win over Australia.

The Globo newspaper listed several problems in the 12 host cities. Almost all had worse traffic than normal. The worst transport problems were in Natal, where bus drivers have been on strike since 12 June. In Salvador, some trips took five times longer than usual. “Traffic was already bad but this week it is chaotic,” Jecilda Mello, president of the residents’ group the Association of Friends of the Historic Centre, told the paper.

But protests have become smaller since the opening day. On that day, small protests happened in several cities, and police used tear gas and pepper spray. After that, the main security problems were small theft and fans getting too excited. A street party of Argentinian fans was broken up with pepper spray after the fans blocked roads.

Because Brazil is so large, fans had very different experiences in different places. The tournament did not change São Paulo, South America’s biggest city, very much. But far away in Manaus, the remote Amazonian city where England played Italy, visitors said World Cup fever was strong. Streets were decorated, and flags were on many cars.

The FA chairman, Greg Dyke, said the change in feeling was easy to notice. “We’ve had a really warm welcome in Manaus. It’s a big thing for them, even if it is a bit strange to spend so much on a stadium with no one to play in it. But we were in São Paulo for four or five days before the opening match and you wouldn’t have known until the last day that there was even a World Cup on. It was weird.”

text:
McDonald’s is the biggest burger chain in the world. It is also a symbol of American business. But now the company is having trouble. After many years of growing, fewer people are buying its food. McDonald’s said worldwide sales fell by 3.3% in 2013, and the results were called very bad.

McDonald’s has problems in many places. In China, sales fell by 23% after a food scare. News reports said workers at a local supplier talked about using old beef and chicken for food going to McDonald’s and KFC. In Europe, sales fell by 4%, mostly because of trouble in Ukraine and angry feelings in Russia. In Russia, health inspectors are checking about 200 of McDonald’s 450 restaurants. Ten restaurants have been closed.

The biggest problem is in the US, where McDonald’s has about 40% of its restaurants. Almost 60 years after Ray Kroc opened his first restaurant in Des Plaines, Illinois, Americans are not as interested in Big Macs and fries.

In the US, sales have gone down for 12 months in a row. Sales were down 4.1% in the latest quarter. Many younger people are choosing other places, like Chipotle Mexican Grill. Technomic said visits by 19-to-21-year-olds who go once a month fell by 13% since 2011. Visits by 22-to-37-year-olds have not grown.

McDonald’s also got bad news in a poll of more than 32,000 American diners. People voted McDonald’s hamburgers as the worst in America. They said they would rather eat burgers at Five Guys, Smashburger, or Fuddruckers. Chick-fil-A was seen as best for chicken. Many people also think McDonald’s is less healthy than rivals, especially Chipotle. Chipotle talks about antibiotic-free meat and “locally sourced, seasonal produce,” though “local” for Chipotle can mean 350 miles away.

In October, McDonald’s started a website in the US called Our Food Your Questions. It asked people to send questions. One question was, “Have you ever used pink slime in your burgers?” This meant a beef filler made from gristle and fat. It was sprayed with ammonia to make it safe to eat. This product has been banned in Europe since the BSE crisis. McDonald’s stopped using it in 2012.

Even so, many people still worry. Someone asked, “Does McDonald’s beef contain worms?” McDonald’s answered, “No. Gross. End of story.” Others asked if it uses real eggs, or if the McRib is made with the same plastic used in yoga mats. Chief Executive Don Thompson said the company must fix wrong ideas about how fresh and good its ingredients are.

At the same time, McDonald’s is losing another strength: fast service at low prices. After it added $2 items to the dollar menu, some people started to think it costs more than other fast-food places. Many people also say service is slower.

Mary Chapman at Technomic said the “expensive” idea is not true. Prices at McDonald’s have gone up, but not as fast as at many other fast-service chains. Technomic said McDonald’s prices rose 4.8% since 2009. The “quick service” sector average rose 19.4%. “Fast casual” eating, including Chipotle, rose 16.9%. US consumer prices rose 11% in the same time.

But waiting in line is a real problem. McDonald’s has a large menu with more complex items. A chicken McWrap takes 60 seconds to make. Chapman said it might be worth waiting, but someone who wants a double cheeseburger for a dollar may not want to wait.

In the UK, McDonald’s has done better. Britain is a bright spot for the company. Peter Martin of CGA Peach said a strong breakfast menu, better coffee, and free wi-fi helped. His numbers show that 56% of British adults visited McDonald’s at least once in the last six months.

In the US, executives say they will fight wrong ideas about the food. Thompson promised more organic food and custom-made burgers. But he also wants simpler menus to make lines shorter. Some analysts wonder how McDonald’s can have simpler menus and also more choices.

Mark Kalinowski at Janney Capital Markets said this plan sounds hard. He said only four of McDonald’s 14,000 US restaurants had tested “build your own burger.” He questioned how it could be used everywhere. He said he is not sure yet and wants more details.

Even with falling sales, McDonald’s is still growing around the world. By the end of 2014, it expects to open 1,400 new restaurants. Kalinowski thinks McDonald’s share of the market will keep shrinking, but he warned people not to give up on the company. He said McDonald’s will likely stay number one in total sales for many years, even decades.

story_id: WNL are you a bad boyfriend
story_key: wnl are you a bad boyfriend
text:
Dan Sullivan was late to breakfast with the Guardian. He was late because police stopped him while he was riding his motorcycle.

Sullivan says he works too much. He misses dinner dates. He forgets to give presents.

So the 27-year-old venture capitalist made a start-up to help: BetterBoyfriend.me. This service sends girlfriends and wives a present every month for about $70.

Sullivan works in finance. He has been testing the program quietly. He says about 350 boyfriends have signed up. This brings in about $17,000 each month. He says most members are his Harvard and investor friends, like venture capitalists, founders, and workers at companies like Apple, Google, and Facebook. Their girlfriends have been getting presents from Sullivan for the last year.

Each month, members pick from a list of seven gifts, such as chocolate, tea sets, and manicures. The package is sent to the boyfriend without a brand name and without a label.

Sullivan tests ideas by buying Facebook ads to see what results he gets. BetterBoyfriend.me grew quickly. Sullivan said, “BetterBoyfriend hit something. It’s a pain point everyone has.”

BetterBoyfriend.me was not his first idea. For a few days, he thought about a dog-sharing idea. He said he saw rich guys looking miserable while walking dogs. He said a dog is only valuable 5% of the time. He wondered if someone could babysit the dogs and other people could rent them.

Another start-up is called InvisibleBoyfriend. It lets users design a believable social partner who is always available to talk. The result is a “boyfriend” who can text with you all day.

Founder Kyle Tabor launched it to give users a fake boyfriend to stop unwanted advances. But he found that people became attached to their computer-made partners. He said more users want companionship through conversation, not just outside “proof” of a relationship to make people leave them alone.

For Sullivan and Tabor, a surprise was the real relationships they formed with customers. Sullivan says he has started to see himself as a kind of relationship consultant for the boyfriends.

Sullivan admits he made mistakes. At first, the packages included receipts with his name, Dan Sullivan. He said one boyfriend wrote, “Listen, dude, she’s not mad but Cynthia found out.”

Sullivan says about 50% of the women who get packages know he is involved. He says this is connected to age. He thinks that after people have been married for a while, they do not keep many secrets.

Over the year, Sullivan says he has gotten to know the boyfriends well. They even delivered a gift to a hospital delivery room. He said the key is to remember that his relationship is with the boyfriend.

When he first launched the company, he put BetterBoyfriend.me tags on flower bouquets. He went to Dolores Park, a popular place in the Mission District.

He looked for couples and gave the bouquet to the girl. But the boyfriend did not like it. Sullivan said it made sense. So he changed his plan and started giving the bouquets to the boyfriends.

text:
Opposition to Western Australia’s shark cull has grown. Thousands of people went to beaches across Australia to ask the state’s premier to stop the policy. RSPCA Australia and Virgin Atlantic owner Richard Branson also spoke against it.

The policy involves catching and killing sharks longer than three metres. It started after what the state government called an “unprecedented” number of shark attacks on Western Australia’s coast. A 35-year-old surfer was killed in November 2013. He was the sixth person to die from a shark attack in two years.

But the Shark Attack File says that across Australia, there has been an average of one shark-related death each year for the last 50 years.

Kate Faehrmann, from the Sea Shepherd Conservation Society, spoke at a protest in Perth. She said the policy will not work. She said drumlines are used to catch sharks, but they can catch and kill many animals. She said they can kill sharks of different sizes, and also dolphins, turtles, and other animals. She said this is why many people do not want the policy.

Thousands of people protested at Perth’s Cottesloe Beach and Sydney’s Manly Beach. Hundreds also protested at Glenelg in south-west Adelaide, and at beaches in Victoria and Queensland. Faehrmann said the protests showed many Australians want sharks protected. She said that even though people are often afraid of sharks, many people still respect them and do not want them killed.

Anthony Joyce, a surfer who once had his foot caught in a shark’s mouth, said killing sharks will not make a difference.

The state government has not given an ongoing count of how many sharks have been killed. There have been reports of sharks smaller than three metres being released after being caught. Drumlines are floating drums anchored to the sea bed, with bait on hooks under them.

Conservationists say there is no evidence the cull will reduce shark attacks on humans. They say no earlier cull used only drumlines. Researchers at the University of Western Australia say the recent increase in attacks may be linked to the state having the fastest-growing population in Australia, not to more sharks.

Richard Peirce, chairman of the UK charity the Shark Trust, said the cull would not work and could attract more predators to the coast. He said the drumlines can attract other predators, especially if the lines stay in the water overnight, because hooked and injured animals may draw them in.

Around the world in 2012, there were 80 unprovoked shark attacks, and seven were fatal. Humans kill nearly 100m sharks each year. RSPCA Australia said it believes the cull is not justified. It said there is no evidence the increase in attacks is because there are more sharks. It said it fits with population changes and human behaviour, meaning more people are in the water.

Richard Branson told Fairfax Radio the policy was backfiring. He said Colin Barnett may have thought it would help tourism, but he thinks it will do the opposite. Branson said it makes the problem seem bigger than it is and may stop people from wanting to visit Perth and the countryside around it. He said it will only make people worried for no good reason.

text:
Race engineer
A race engineer is the link between the driver and the mechanics.

Typical salary: New graduates start at £25,000 to £30,000. After a few years, junior engineers can earn more than £40,000. Senior race engineers earn £50,000 to £90,000. Promotion often leads to six-figure salaries.

What the job involves: Race engineer Jamie Muir says, “A race engineer acts as the interpreter between the race-car mechanics and the driver.” The engineer listens to the driver, studies the data, decides how to set up the car for the best performance, and then tells the mechanics what to do.

Qualifications: A university degree, usually in automotive engineering, mechanical engineering, or motorsport technology. Hands-on experience is very important.

To succeed as a race engineer, you need to handle pressure.

Worst thing about the job: The long hours. Chris Aylett, CEO of the Motorsport Industry Association, says, “Race engineers work 24/7.”

Ethical hacker
Typical salary: £60,000 to £90,000 at team-leader level. A newly qualified hacker can expect £35,000 to £50,000.

What the job involves: A company pays an ethical hacker to try to hack into its computer system. This tests how well the system can stop a real attack.

Qualifications: You do not always need a computer science degree. The industry accepts people with many different qualifications and skills.

To succeed as an ethical hacker, you need to love technology and details. You also need strong analytical skills and enjoy solving difficult problems.

Worst thing about the job: You may be asked to test a new customer’s network and find out they have already had a data breach.

Bomb-disposal diver
Typical salary: In the private sector, you can earn up to £100,000 by working two months out of every three.

What the job involves: You go down to the sea bed and search for unexploded ordnance, such as bombs, shells, grenades, and landmines. Then you either safely recover them or safely destroy them.

Qualifications: To dive offshore, you need diving-inspection and medical-technician qualifications. To dispose of bombs safely, you also need an explosive ordnance disposal qualification and years of experience.

To succeed as a bomb-disposal diver, you need to stay calm in stressful situations. You work alone deep underwater, with no visibility. You also need to be okay living in small, crowded spaces.

Worst thing about the job: You should expect to be away from home at least six months a year.

Social engineer
Typical salary: Between £50,000 and £80,000 on average. Graduates start on £25,000, but pay rises quickly with qualifications and experience.

The job: A company pays a social engineer to try to trick employees into giving confidential information. This can let the engineer access sensitive company data or the company’s computer network.

Qualifications: Social engineers often have a degree in IT. Knowledge of psychology is helpful. Backgrounds in marketing, teaching, and customer service can also help.

To succeed as a social engineer, you need the confidence to lie in a believable way and to fit in almost anywhere. You also need strong personal ethics and knowledge of the law.

Worst thing about the job: People may misunderstand your job. Social engineers are not spies, but many people think they are.

Power-line helicopter pilot
Typical salary: £65,000

The job: You fly a helicopter close to high-voltage power lines so the lines can be checked with a camera. The power company uses the pictures to find possible faults and problems.

Qualifications: You need a private helicopter pilot licence, a commercial pilot’s licence, and about 2,000 hours of low-level flying experience in the type of helicopter the company uses.

To succeed as a power-line helicopter pilot, you need a steady hand and a calm mind. Pilots may fly beside the power line, sometimes only 20 feet away and 30 feet above the ground.

Worst thing about the job: Robin Tutcher, chief helicopter pilot for Western Power Distribution overhead-power network, says, “There are no negatives.”

Private butler
Typical salary: £60,000 to £90,000

The job: A private butler does many tasks for an employer. This can include managing clothes, driving, and caring for pets. Other duties can include managing staff, serving meals, running errands, looking after guests, booking restaurants, keeping the house secure, cleaning, cooking, and anything else the household needs.

Qualifications: You do not need specific qualifications. You can take a course at the British Butler Academy or the British Butler Institute.

To succeed as a butler, you need to truly enjoy looking after other people.

Worst thing about the job: Long hours and an unpredictable schedule can make family life hard. Butlers can also feel isolated, face cultural differences with the employer, and have to work for people who are not always nice, says Sara Vestin, director of the British Butler Academy.
